name: HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY

topics.title: Hydrogen Economy - Decarbonisation of the Economy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Economy - Decarbonisation of the Economy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Hydrogen Economy — Formal Definition**
> The **Hydrogen Economy** is a proposed integrated energy system in which **hydrogen (H₂)** is produced from diverse primary energy sources, stored, transported, and utilised as a clean energy carrier and fuel across all major end-use sectors (power, industry, mobility, buildings) — replacing fossil fuels to enable deep **decarbonisation** of the global economy.

> [!NOTE] **Decarbonisation — Formal Definition**
> **Decarbonisation** is the strategic process of reducing **carbon dioxide (CO₂)** and other greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions associated with human economic activity, by substituting low-carbon or zero-carbon energy vectors, improving energy efficiency, and deploying carbon capture, utilisation, and storage (CCUS) technologies.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP] **Real-World Analogy: "Hydrogen is the New Electricity"**
> Imagine your house currently runs on coal (dirty, smoky, carbon-emitting). Now imagine we could build a **battery that is refuelable, store energy for months, and run trucks, ships, planes, and factories** — without producing smoke. **That "battery" is hydrogen.**
>
> Just as **electricity** is not a primary source but a *carrier* of energy (generated from coal, solar, wind, nuclear), **hydrogen** is also an *energy carrier*. We make it from water, natural gas, biomass, or electricity — then we use it where electrification alone is hard (steel, cement, heavy trucks, long-haul shipping, aviation).
>
> The "Hydrogen Economy" is the **plumbing network** — production plants, pipelines, storage tanks, refuelling stations — that allows this clean fuel to flow through the entire economy, the way gasoline flows today.

### 1.3 Why Decarbonisation? The Climate Imperative

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Global Targets (Kerala / India / World Context)**
> - **Paris Agreement (2015):** Limit global warming to **1.5 °C – 2 °C** above pre-industrial levels.
> - **India's Net-Zero target:** **2070** (announced at COP26, Glasgow, 2021).
> - **EU Net-Zero target:** **2050**.
> - **IPCC AR6 finding:** Global warming of **1.1 °C** has already occurred; CO₂ must fall by ~**43 % by 2030** (from 2019 levels) to keep 1.5 °C alive.
> - **Standard metrics:** Atmospheric CO₂ = **~421 ppm** (2023); pre-industrial = **280 ppm**.

### 1.4 The "Three Pillars" of a Hydrogen Economy

1. **Production** — Green, Blue, Pink, Grey, Brown H₂ (see Section 2).
2. **Storage & Transport** — Compressed gas, liquid H₂, LOHCs, ammonia, pipelines.
3. **End Use** — Fuel cells, combustion turbines, industrial feedstock, synthetic fuels.

> [!NOTE] **Physical Constants to Remember**
> - **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of H₂:** **142 MJ/kg**
> - **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂:** **120 MJ/kg**
> - **Energy per kg of gasoline (equivalent):** ~**44 MJ/kg**
> - **Density of liquid H₂:** **71 kg/m³**
> - **Energy density (by mass) of H₂ is ~3× gasoline**; (by volume it is ~1/10ᵗʰ).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Hydrogen? The Strategic Logic

Hydrogen is unique among clean energy options because it is the only **zero-carbon fuel** that can directly substitute fossil fuels in **hard-to-abate sectors**:

| Sector | Why Direct Electrification is Hard | Role of Hydrogen |
|---|---|---|
| **Steel** | Blast furnace needs chemical reducing agent | **Green H₂** as reductant (Direct Reduced Iron — DRI) |
| **Cement** | Process CO₂ from limestone calcination | H₂-fired kilns, CCUS |
| **Heavy trucking** | Battery weight too high for long range | **H₂ Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs)** |
| **Shipping** | Voyage energy too large for batteries | **Liquid H₂, NH₃, methanol** |
| **Aviation** | Energy density of Li-ion is too low | **e-SAF (Synthetic Aviation Fuel)** from H₂ |
| **Ammonia / Fertiliser** | Currently made from natural gas | **Green NH₃** from electrolyser H₂ |
| **Grid storage** | Seasonal, long-duration | **Power-to-Gas** (H₂ storage) |

### 2.2 The "Hydrogen Colour Code" — Production Pathways

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU-High-Yield Classification: Colours of Hydrogen**
> The colour of hydrogen is a **shorthand for the production route and its associated carbon footprint.**

| Colour | Production Route | Feedstock | CO₂ Emissions (kg CO₂ / kg H₂) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Grey** | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | Natural gas (CH₄) | **~9–10** | Commercial, dominant |
| **Brown** | Coal gasification | Coal | **~19–20** | Commercial, dirtiest |
| **Blue** | SMR + **CCUS** (Carbon Capture) | Natural gas + CO₂ capture | **~2–4** | Pilot/Commercial |
| **Green** | **Water Electrolysis** powered by renewables | H₂O + Solar/Wind | **~0–1** | Commercial, scaling fast |
| **Pink** | Electrolysis powered by nuclear | H₂O + Nuclear | **~0** | Pilot |
| **Turquoise** | Methane Pyrolysis | CH₄ → H₂ + Solid C | **Low** | R&D / Pilot |
| **White** | Natural geological deposits | Native H₂ | **~0** | Exploratory |

### 2.3 The Hydrogen Value Chain (End-to-End)

$$\text{H}_2\text{ Economy Value Chain} = \text{Production} \rightarrow \text{Conditioning} \rightarrow \text{Storage} \rightarrow \text{Transport} \rightarrow \text{End Use}$$

Each link has **energy losses (exergy destruction)**; therefore overall chain efficiency is a key metric:

$$\eta_{\text{chain}} = \eta_{\text{prod}} \times \eta_{\text{cond}} \times \eta_{\text{store}} \times \eta_{\text{trans}} \times \eta_{\text{use}}$$

Typical **well-to-wheel efficiency** for Green H₂ FCEV: **22 – 28 %** (vs. Battery EV: 70 – 80 %).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP] **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{H_2} = m_{H_2} \times \text{HHV}_{H_2}$ | Energy content of hydrogen mass | J, MJ |
| 2 | $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{E_{\text{required}}}{\text{HHV}_{H_2}}$ | Hydrogen mass needed for energy demand | kg |
| 3 | $V_{H_2,\text{liq}} = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{\rho_{LH_2}}$ | Liquid H₂ volume ($\rho = 71 \text{ kg/m}^3$) | m³ |
| 4 | $V_{H_2,\text{gas}} = \dfrac{m_{H_2} \times R \times T}{M_{H_2} \times P}$ | Ideal gas volume (R = 8.314 J/mol·K) | m³ |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{electrolyser}} = \dfrac{\text{HHV}_{H_2} \times m_{H_2}}{W_{\text{elec,in}}}$ | Electrolyser efficiency | % |
| 6 | $\text{LCOH} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{H_{2,t}}{(1+r)^t}}$ | Levelised Cost of Hydrogen ($/kg) | USD/kg |
| 7 | $\text{CO}_2\text{ avoided} = m_{H_2} \times \left(EF_{\text{fossil}} - EF_{\text{green}}\right)$ | Emission reduction vs. baseline | kg CO₂ |
| 8 | $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2$ | Electrolysis stoichiometry | — |
| 9 | $\Delta G^\circ = 237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (25 °C) | Min. electrical work for electrolysis | kJ/mol |
| 10 | $V_{\text{thermoneutral}} = 1.481 \text{ V}$ | Cell voltage at $\Delta H$ | V |
| 11 | $V_{\text{rev}} = 1.229 \text{ V}$ | Reversible cell voltage at $\Delta G$ | V |
| 12 | $W_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot V_{\text{rev}}}{M_{H_2}}$ | Min. specific energy for H₂ | kWh/kg |

Where $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$ (Faraday), $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \text{ g/mol}$, $n = 2$ electrons per H₂.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cost forecasting:** LCOH (Levelised Cost of Hydrogen) determines if Green H₂ is competitive with fossil H₂ + carbon tax.
- **Sector coupling:** Hydrogen links **electricity, heat, mobility, industry** into one integrated clean system — called **sector coupling**.
- **Energy security:** Domestic H₂ production reduces dependence on imported oil/gas.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Energy to Produce 1 kg of Hydrogen

The fundamental thermodynamic limit for splitting water is set by the **Gibbs free energy of reaction**:

$$H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow H_{2(g)} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} \quad ; \quad \Delta G^\circ_{298} = +237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 1.** Energy required per **mole** of H₂ = 237.2 kJ.

**Step 2.** Convert to **per kg** of H₂ using molar mass $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{min,electric}} &= \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{M_{H_2}} \\
&= \dfrac{237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}} \\
&= 117{,}659 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
&= 32.68 \text{ kWh/kg of H}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** If we add the **enthalpic** component (latent + sensible heat), the total thermodynamic minimum (Higher Heating Value basis) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{min,thermal}} &= \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{M_{H_2}} \\
\Delta H^\circ_{298} &= 285.8 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
W_{\text{min,thermal}} &= \dfrac{285.8}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 141{,}766 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
&= 39.38 \text{ kWh/kg of H}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** Real electrolyser efficiency (PEM / Alkaline) is **70 – 80 % LHV**, so practical electricity demand is:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{actual}} &= \dfrac{39.38}{\eta_{\text{electrolyser}}} = \dfrac{39.38}{0.75} \\
&\approx 52.5 \text{ kWh/kg H}_2
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE] **Take-away for KTU Exam:** State-of-the-art commercial electrolysers consume **~50–55 kWh/kg H₂**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — CO₂ Avoided by Switching from Grey to Green H₂

**Problem:** A refinery uses **1000 kg/day** of grey hydrogen. Calculate the daily CO₂ emissions avoided if it is replaced by green H₂.

**Given:**
- Emission factor of grey H₂ (SMR): $EF_{\text{grey}} = 9 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kg H}_2$
- Emission factor of green H₂ (renewable-powered): $EF_{\text{green}} = 0.5 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kg H}_2$

**Step 1.** Emission avoided per kg of H₂:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta EF &= EF_{\text{grey}} - EF_{\text{green}} \\
&= 9 - 0.5 = 8.5 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kg H}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2.** Total daily CO₂ avoided for 1000 kg/day:

$$\begin{aligned}
CO_{2,\text{avoided,day}} &= m_{H_2} \times \Delta EF \\
&= 1000 \times 8.5 = 8{,}500 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Annual CO₂ avoided:

$$\begin{aligned}
CO_{2,\text{avoided,year}} &= 8{,}500 \times 365 \\
&= 3{,}102{,}500 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year} \\
&\approx 3{,}102.5 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** Equivalent cars off the road (1 car ≈ 4.6 tonnes CO₂/year):

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{cars}} &= \dfrac{3{,}102.5}{4.6} \approx 674 \text{ cars/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydrogen Mass Required to Replace a Given Fossil-Fuel Energy Demand

**Problem:** A steel plant consumes **5000 tonnes of coke** per year, each tonne delivering ~**28 GJ**. Find the mass of green H₂ required to deliver the same chemical energy (as a reductant + fuel).

**Step 1.** Total annual energy:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total}} &= 5000 \text{ t} \times 28 \text{ GJ/t} \\
&= 140{,}000 \text{ GJ} = 1.4 \times 10^{11} \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2.** Mass of H₂ required (using HHV = 142 MJ/kg):

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{\text{HHV}_{H_2}} \\
&= \dfrac{1.4 \times 10^{8} \text{ MJ}}{142 \text{ MJ/kg}} \\
&\approx 985{,}915 \text{ kg} \approx 986 \text{ tonnes of H}_2/\text{year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Volume as **liquid H₂** ($\rho = 71 \text{ kg/m}^3$):

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{LH_2} &= \dfrac{985{,}915}{71} \\
&\approx 13{,}886 \text{ m}^3/\text{year} \approx 38 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** Volume as **compressed gas at 700 bar, 25 °C**:

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= \dfrac{mRT}{M \cdot P} \\
&= \dfrac{985{,}915 \times 8.314 \times 298}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \times 700 \times 10^{5}} \\
&\approx 17{,}320 \text{ m}^3/\text{year at 700 bar}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP] **Engineering insight:** The same energy takes **~1,250× more volume** as gas at 700 bar than as liquid H₂ — explaining why **LH₂ or LOHC** is preferred for intercontinental shipping.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Electrolyser Power Sizing

**Problem:** A 100 MW electrolyser operates at 75 % LHV efficiency. How much H₂ does it produce per day?

**Step 1.** Daily energy input:

$$E_{\text{in}} = 100 \text{ MW} \times 24 \text{ h} = 2400 \text{ MWh} = 2400 \times 10^3 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2.** Theoretical H₂ from energy using $39.38 \text{ kWh/kg}$:

$$m_{H_2,\text{theor}} = \dfrac{2{,}400{,}000}{39.38} \approx 60{,}945 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3.** Actual mass at 75 % efficiency:

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2,\text{actual}} &= m_{H_2,\text{theor}} \times \eta \\
&= 60{,}945 \times 0.75 \approx 45{,}709 \text{ kg/day} \\
&\approx 45.7 \text{ tonnes H}_2/\text{day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** Annual production:

$$m_{\text{annual}} = 45.7 \times 365 \approx 16{,}680 \text{ tonnes/yr} \approx 16.7 \text{ kt H}_2/\text{yr}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
