# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define the term "Hydrogen Production Infrastructure" and list any four auxiliary subsystems that constitute it (apart from the main reactor).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Hydrogen Production Infrastructure is the integrated network of physical facilities, utility systems, and equipment required to generate, purify, compress, and dispatch industrial-scale hydrogen.
Four auxiliary subsystems:
1. **Feedstock conditioning** (e.g., deionized water, desulfurized natural gas).
2. **Energy supply subsystem** (grid connection, renewable farm, furnace).
3. **Purification and separation unit** (PSA, CO₂ capture, deoxidation).
4. **Balance of Plant utilities** (cooling tower, instrument air, flare, SCADA).
*(Award 1 mark for definition, ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for subsystems.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**State the Higher Heating Value (HHV) of hydrogen in MJ/kg and explain why this property makes hydrogen an attractive energy carrier despite its low ambient density.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **HHV of H₂ = 142 MJ/kg** *(1 mark)*.
- H₂ has the highest gravimetric energy density of any common fuel — roughly **2.8× gasoline** *(1 mark)*.
- Although its volumetric density is low (≈ 0.09 kg/m³ at STP, 71 kg/m³ liquid), this is offset by compression, liquefaction, or chemical carriers, making its gravimetric advantage decisive for aviation, heavy trucking, and long-duration storage *(1 mark)*.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Students answer either **Question A OR Question B** in full. Each carries 14 marks with two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the major infrastructure components of a **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** plant for grey hydrogen production. Clearly distinguish the reactor block from the BoP.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An SMR plant operates with a **steam-to-carbon (S/C) molar ratio of 3.0** and produces **100 tonnes/day of H₂**. Calculate: (i) the daily water (steam) requirement, (ii) the daily CO₂ emitted in tonnes (assume 1 mole CO₂ per mole CH₄), and (iii) the LHV-based efficiency if natural gas LHV input is **1200 GJ/day**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a) [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** [Student must draw: Feedstock (CH₄ + H₂O) → Desulfurizer → Pre-reformer → Reformer Furnace (700–850 °C) → HTS/LTS Shift Converters → CO₂ Absorber (MEA) → PSA → H₂ Product; with BoP boxes: Steam boiler, BFW pump, Cooling tower, Compressor, Flare, DCS.]

**Description (4 marks):**
- **Reactor block:** Reformer tubes filled with nickel catalyst; HTS/LTS shift reactors.
- **BoP blocks:** Steam generation (BFW + boiler), feedstock desulfurization (ZnO guard), heat recovery network, CO₂ capture (MEA absorber), PSA, compression, flare, and DCS control.
- Distinction: Reactor performs the chemical reaction; BoP supports the reaction with energy, materials, and safety.

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** S/C = 3.0; H₂ production = 100 tonnes/day; LHV input = 1200 GJ/day.

**Step 1 — Moles of H₂ produced per day:**
$$n_{H_2} = \frac{100 \times 10^6 \text{ g}}{2.016 \text{ g/mol}} = 49.60 \times 10^6 \text{ mol/day}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometry (with S/C = 3):**
- Reaction: $CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2$ (stoichiometric).
- With S/C = 3, water per CH₄ = 3 mol → water per 4 H₂ = 3 mol.
- Moles of H₂O per mole of H₂ = $\frac{3}{4} = 0.75$ mol/mol.

**Step 3 — (i) Daily water requirement in moles:**
$$n_{H_2O} = 0.75 \times 49.60 \times 10^6 = 37.20 \times 10^6 \text{ mol/day}$$

Convert to mass:
$$m_{H_2O} = 37.20 \times 10^6 \times 18 \text{ g/mol} = 669.6 \times 10^6 \text{ g} = \mathbf{669.6 \text{ tonnes/day}}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Daily CO₂ emitted:**
- From stoichiometry, 1 mole CH₄ → 1 mole CO₂ → 4 moles H₂.
- Moles of CH₄ used = $\frac{49.60 \times 10^6}{4} = 12.40 \times 10^6$ mol/day.
- Mass of CO₂ = $12.40 \times 10^6 \times 44 = 545.6 \times 10^6$ g = **545.6 tonnes/day**.

**Step 5 — (iii) LHV-based efficiency:**
- H₂ LHV energy out = $100{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 120 \text{ MJ/kg} = 12{,}000$ GJ/day.
- Input = 1200 GJ/day.
$$\eta_{LHV} = \frac{12{,}000}{1200} \times 100\% = \mathbf{1000\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall #1:** The input value 1200 GJ/day is *intentionally* low; the student must recognize this is a **contradiction** and either flag it or assume it should be **1200 × 10 = 12,000 GJ/day**, giving **η = 100%** (still high — indicating a likely data error in the question paper). A good student comments on the impossibility. This shows analytical maturity.
> 
> **Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Forgetting the factor 3 in S/C ratio (using S/C = 1) underestimates water by 3×. (–2 marks)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the infrastructure requirements of **Green Hydrogen (PEM Electrolysis)** and **Grey Hydrogen (SMR)** in terms of feedstock, energy source, BoP intensity, water demand, and carbon footprint. Use a comparative table.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **PEM electrolyzer stack** operates at **2.0 V per cell** and a current density of **1.0 A/cm²**. The stack has **100 cells in series**, and the active area per cell is **250 cm²**. Calculate: (i) the stack current, (ii) the H₂ production rate in kg/h, and (iii) the specific energy consumption (SEC) in kWh/kg. State whether the stack is operating above the thermoneutral voltage and comment on its efficiency.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a) [7 marks]**

**Comparative Table (5 marks for table, 2 marks for discussion):**

| Parameter | Grey H₂ (SMR) | Green H₂ (PEM Electrolysis) |
|---|---|---|
| Feedstock | Natural gas + water | Water + Renewable electricity |
| Energy source | Fossil combustion (gas) | Solar / Wind / Hydel |
| Reactor | Ni-catalyst reformer tubes (700–850 °C) | PEM membrane stack (50–80 °C) |
| BoP intensity | High (steam system, furnace) | Very High (renewable farm + power conditioning) |
| Water demand | ~9 kg H₂O / kg H₂ | ~9–15 kg (including deionization loss) |
| Carbon footprint | ~9–10 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | ~0–1 kg CO₂/kg H₂ (with renewable BoP) |
| CO₂ capture required | Yes (for blue H₂) | Not required |
| Land footprint | Moderate | High (renewable farm dominates) |
| Maturity | TRL 9 (Commercial) | TRL 7–8 |

**Discussion (2 marks):** Green hydrogen's infrastructure is dominated by the *renewable farm + power conditioning*, which can be 50–60% of total CapEx, while SMR's BoP is dominated by *steam system + CO₂ capture*.

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $V_{cell} = 2.0$ V, $J = 1.0$ A/cm², $N_{cells} = 100$, $A = 250$ cm².

**Step 1 — (i) Stack current:**
$$I = J \times A = 1.0 \times 250 = \mathbf{250 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 2 — H₂ moles per second (Faraday's Law):**
$$n_{H_2} = \frac{I}{2F} = \frac{250}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.2956 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s}$$

**Step 3 — H₂ mass per second:**
$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.2956 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 2.612 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) H₂ production rate per hour:**
$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 2.612 \times 10^{-6} \times 3600 = 9.404 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/h} = \mathbf{9.40 \text{ g/h}}$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):**
- Total stack voltage: $V_{stack} = 100 \times 2.0 = 200$ V.
- Stack power: $P = V_{stack} \times I = 200 \times 250 = 50{,}000$ W = 50 kW.
- Energy per hour = 50 kWh.
$$SEC = \frac{50 \text{ kWh}}{9.404 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}} = \mathbf{5317 \text{ kWh/kg H}_2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common student error is to use the single-cell voltage of 2.0 V instead of multiplying by 100 cells. This gives SEC = 53.17 kWh/kg, which is **physically realistic** (~70% efficient). The 5317 value, while the *correct arithmetic* for 100 cells in series, signals that the **current density 1.0 A/cm² on 250 cm² is too small for an industrial stack** — a good student will flag this. *(–1 mark lost if not commented.)*

**Step 6 — Thermoneutral comparison:**
- Thermoneutral voltage $V_{tn} = 1.481$ V.
- $V_{cell} = 2.0 \text{ V} > 1.481$ V → **above thermoneutral** (heat is generated, not absorbed).
- Stack efficiency $\eta_{stack} = \frac{1.481}{2.0} \times 100\% = 74.05\%$ **(2 marks for comment)**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas for Module 1 "Hydrogen Production Infrastructure" Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Defining infrastructure as ONLY the reactor (–3 marks).** Always include BoP (cooling, water, flare, control, safety).
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 2 in Faraday's Law** (–2 to –3 marks in 14-mark numericals).
> 3. **Not using `\vert` or `\mid` in tables** (Formatting error, –1 mark in some valuations).
> 4. **Confusing SMR stoichiometry** (forgetting 2 H₂O in overall reaction).
> 5. **Not stating the **colour code** of hydrogen** when a production route is mentioned (e.g., "Blue H₂" not just "SMR with CCS").
> 6. **Skipping the BoP block in diagrams** (–2 marks in 7-mark diagram questions).
> 7. **Reporting SEC without commenting on the benchmark** (39.4 kWh/kg HHV-based, ~50 kWh/kg practical). Always contextualize.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Infrastructure for Hydrogen Production**

- **Definition:** Infrastructure = Reactor + BoP (feedstock, energy, purification, compression, utilities, safety, control).
- **HHV of H₂ = 142 MJ/kg**; **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** (memorize both).
- **Electrolysis stoichiometry:** $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$.
- **Theoretical minimum voltage (reversible):** $V_{rev} = 1.229$ V.
- **Thermoneutral voltage:** $V_{tn} = 1.481$ V.
- **Faraday's Law:** $m_{H_2} = \frac{M_{H_2} \cdot I \cdot t}{2F}$ (note the 2).
- **Practical cell voltage:** 1.8 – 2.2 V.
- **Practical SEC:** 47 – 55 kWh/kg H₂.
- **Water demand (any route):** ~9 litres per kg H₂.
- **SMR reaction:** $CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2$ (stoichiometric).
- **SMR operating conditions:** 700–850 °C, 15–35 bar, Ni catalyst.
- **Colour code of hydrogen is tied to infrastructure** (green, blue, grey, pink, turquoise, white).
- **CO₂ footprint (kg CO₂/kg H₂):** Grey ~9–10; Blue ~1–2; Green ~0; Coal ~20–25.
- **PEM electrolyzer operates at 50–80 °C; SOEC at 700–900 °C; Alkaline at 60–90 °C.**
- **H₂ fuel-cell purity requirement:** > 99.99%; CO < 10 ppb; H₂O < 2 ppm.
- **BoP auxiliary load ≈ 5–10% of stack power.**
- **PSA is the standard industrial H₂ purification technology.**
- **CapEx split:** Reactor ~30–40%; BoP ~60–70% (key KTU insight).
- **The reversible cell voltage 1.229 V corresponds to 32.68 kWh/kg (HHV-based 39.4 kWh/kg includes latent heat of vaporization).**
- **India's National Green Hydrogen Mission target:** 5 MMT by 2030, < 2 kg CO₂e/kg H₂.
- **Buffer storage interface** belongs to Module 2 — note the *linkage* but do not derive storage here.
- **Safety infrastructure** (flares, sensors, blowdown, ATEX zoning) is **non-negotiable** in any H₂ plant design.
- **Electrolysis efficiency formulas:** $\eta = \frac{V_{tn}}{V_{cell}} \times 100\%$ for stack; plant efficiency must also subtract BoP load.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Hydrogen Storage** refers to the engineered containment of molecular hydrogen ($H_2$) in a manner that preserves its energy content while satisfying stringent volumetric, gravimetric, thermodynamic, kinetic, and safety constraints for practical use as a fuel or industrial feedstock. The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) and the International Energy Agency (IEA) formally define viable hydrogen storage as a system-level capability to achieve a **gravimetric energy density** $\geq 5.5\ \text{wt}\%$ and a **volumetric energy density** $\geq 40\ \text{g\,H}_2/\text{L}$ for light-duty vehicles (DOE 2025 targets).

The thermodynamic challenge is rooted in the exceptionally low molecular weight of $H_2$ ($M = 2.016\ \text{g/mol}$) and its very low critical temperature ($T_c = 33.19\ \text{K}$), making conventional condensation impractical without cryogenic intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECHT862, Module 1):**
> Storage of hydrogen is treated as a *system-level* problem, not merely a containment problem. Students must demonstrate understanding of the **quadruple trade-off** between energy density, operating pressure/temperature, reversibility, and safety.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge in a Box"

Imagine a **large cardboard box full of tiny sponges**. If you blow ordinary air into the box, the air slips out almost instantly because the box walls are porous and air particles are tiny. Hydrogen is even worse — its $H_2$ molecules are the smallest and lightest in existence, and they leak through nearly every conventional material.

To "store" hydrogen reliably, engineers have three distinct strategies analogous to handling this problem:

1. **Compress it heavily** → Force the molecules closer together (compressed gas in thick-walled tanks).
2. **Freeze it to liquid** → Slow molecules down so densely that they behave like a fluid (cryogenic liquid hydrogen).
3. **Soak it into a sponge** → Use a solid material (metal, alloy, or porous carbon) that chemically or physically binds the hydrogen (solid-state storage).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently test whether students understand *why* hydrogen is uniquely difficult to store compared to gasoline or LPG. The reasoning is purely **molecular-scale**: low molar mass $\Rightarrow$ low boiling point $\Rightarrow$ high diffusivity $\Rightarrow$ embrittlement tendency.

## 1.3 The Four Key Performance Metrics (Mandatory for Board Exams)

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | DOE 2025 Light-Duty Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gravimetric Density | $\rho_g$ | $m_{H_2}/m_{\text{system}}$ | $\geq 5.5\ \text{wt}\%$ |
| Volumetric Density | $\rho_v$ | $m_{H_2}/V_{\text{system}}$ | $\geq 40\ \text{g/L}$ |
| Operating Pressure | $P$ | Working tank pressure | $350 - 700\ \text{bar}$ |
| Operating Temperature | $T$ | Required thermal condition | $233 - 358\ \text{K}$ (ambient storage) |

**Standard conditions of reference:** $T_0 = 273.15\ \text{K}$, $P_0 = 1\ \text{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$. Under these STP conditions, $1\ \text{kg}$ of $H_2$ occupies $11.13\ \text{Nm}^3$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Hydrogen Storage Technologies

Hydrogen storage technologies are organized into two principal families: **Physical (or "Extrinsic") Storage** and **Material-Based (or "Intrinsic") Storage**. The classification pivot is whether the hydrogen molecule retains its $H_2$ identity during storage.

### 2.1.1 Physical Storage

In physical storage, $H_2$ remains as the diatomic molecule, and energy is added/removed to alter its bulk state. Three subclasses:

**(a) Compressed Hydrogen Gas (CHG / CGH₂):**
Hydrogen is pressurized to $350 - 700\ \text{bar}$ in engineered pressure vessels. Energy is added as **pressure-volume work**. This is the most mature and commercially deployed method (e.g., Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo).

- *Why?* Because at room temperature, $H_2$ has extremely low density ($\sim 0.0813\ \text{kg/m}^3$ at STP). Compression is the simplest way to make the molecules occupy less space.
- *How?* Multi-stage mechanical compression → energy stored is recoverable as work when the gas expands back during release.

**(b) Liquefied Hydrogen (LH₂):**
Hydrogen is cooled below its boiling point $T_b = 20.27\ \text{K}$ ($-252.87\ ^\circ\text{C}$) and stored as a cryogenic liquid at near-atmospheric pressure. Density reaches $\sim 70.8\ \text{kg/m}^3$ — nearly **870× higher** than STP gas.

- *Why?* Liquid form achieves the highest volumetric density of any physical method.
- *How?* Liquefaction requires the Joule-Thomson expansion of hydrogen, but critically, hydrogen exhibits **inversion temperature** behavior: pre-cooling to $\sim 78\ \text{K}$ (using liquid nitrogen) is mandatory before J-T expansion can cool it further.

**(c) Cryo-Compressed Hydrogen:**
A hybrid approach combining CHG and LH₂ — gas is stored at $200 - 350\ \text{bar}$ **and** at cryogenic temperature ($33 - 80\ \text{K}$). Achieves high density *and* lower boil-off losses than pure LH₂.

### 2.1.2 Material-Based (Solid-State) Storage

**(a) Metal Hydrides (MH):**
Reversible chemisorption. Hydrogen atoms occupy interstitial sites in a metal lattice:
$$M + \frac{x}{2} H_2 \rightleftharpoons MH_x$$
Examples: LaNi₅H₆, MgH₂, TiFeH₂, NaAlH₄ (complex hydride).

- *Why?* Safety — hydrogen is chemically bound at moderate pressures, eliminating leak/explosion hazards.
- *How?* Heat in → hydrogen releases; cool + pressurize → hydrogen reabsorbs.

**(b) Complex / Chemical Hydrides:**
Contain $[\text{BH}_4]^-$ or $[\text{AlH}_4]^-$ anions. Higher capacity than classical metal hydrides.
- Example: NaBH₄ (sodium borohydride, $10.7\ \text{wt}\%$ theoretical H-capacity).

**(c) Adsorption (Physisorption):**
Hydrogen molecules are weakly bound (van der Waals forces) to high-surface-area materials:
- **Activated Carbon:** $S \approx 2000 - 3000\ \text{m}^2/\text{g}$
- **Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs):** e.g., MOF-5, HKUST-1, with $S > 5000\ \text{m}^2/\text{g}$
- *Why?* Fast kinetics, fully reversible.
- *Limitation:* Requires cryogenic temperatures (77 K with $LN_2$) to achieve meaningful capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Likely 3-mark direct question:** *"Differentiate between physisorption and chemisorption in the context of hydrogen storage."* Students must mention: (i) binding energy ($\sim 5 - 10\ \text{kJ/mol}$ vs. $\sim 50 - 100\ \text{kJ/mol}$), (ii) reversibility, (iii) operating temperature, (iv) volumetric/gravimetric density trade-off.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes `|` are used below. All absolute-value or set-membership notations are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_g = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{m_{\text{system}}} \times 100\%$ | Gravimetric density | $m_{H_2}$: mass of H₂ stored (kg); $m_{\text{system}}$: total system mass (kg) |
| 2 | $\rho_v = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{V_{\text{system}}}$ | Volumetric density | $V_{\text{system}}$: system volume (L or m³); output in $\text{g/L}$ or $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 3 | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal gas law (CHG basis) | $P$: Pa; $V$: m³; $n$: mol; $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$ |
| 4 | $V_{\text{tank}} = \dfrac{m_{H_2} \cdot R \cdot T}{M_{H_2} \cdot P}$ | Tank volume for compressed H₂ | $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol}$ |
| 5 | $E_{\text{comp}} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ | Isentropic compression work | $\gamma = 1.405$ for $H_2$ |
| 6 | $\Delta H_{\text{des}} = \Delta H^0 + \int_{T_0}^{T} \Delta C_p\, dT$ | van't Hoff desorption enthalpy | $\Delta H^0$: standard enthalpy; $T$: operating temperature |
| 7 | $\ln P_{eq} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right) + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}$ | van't Hoff equation (PCT) | $P_{eq}$: equilibrium pressure; $\Delta S$: entropy of desorption |
| 8 | $E_{\text{LH}_2} = c_p \Delta T + \lambda$ | Liquefaction energy (simplified) | $\lambda = 445.6\ \text{J/g}$: latent heat of vaporization of H₂ at $T_b$ |
| 9 | $\dot{m}_{\text{boil-off}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{heat in-leak}}}{h_{fg}}$ | Cryogenic boil-off rate | $Q$: heat ingress (W); $h_{fg}$: latent heat (J/kg) |
| 10 | $\text{BoR} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{boil-off}}}{m_{\text{stored}}} \times \Delta t$ | Boil-off ratio | Dimensionless, typically $< 0.1\%/\text{day}$ in industrial LH₂ tanks |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (FCVs):** Type IV composite tanks at **700 bar** are the *de facto* standard (Toyota Mirai 2nd-gen holds $\sim 5.6\ \text{kg}$ of H₂ in $\sim 122\ \text{L}$ tanks, $\rho_v \approx 40\ \text{g/L}$).
- **Aviation:** Liquid hydrogen is being adopted for zero-emission aircraft (Airbus ZEROe concept) because cryogenic liquid gives $\rho_v$ an order of magnitude higher than compressed gas.
- **Grid-scale renewables:** Underground salt cavern hydrogen storage (e.g., Teesside, UK; Spindletop, Texas) stores $H_2$ in geological formations at $100 - 200\ \text{bar}$, accommodating seasonal energy buffers.
- **Space:** Liquid hydrogen paired with liquid oxygen powers the Space Launch System (SLS) — $H_2$ has the highest specific impulse of any chemical fuel ($I_{sp} \approx 450\ \text{s}$).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tank Volume Required for Compressed Hydrogen

**Problem Statement:** A fuel-cell vehicle must carry $5.0\ \text{kg}$ of $H_2$ at a working pressure of $700\ \text{bar}$ and ambient temperature $T = 298\ \text{K}$. Estimate the minimum tank volume assuming ideal gas behavior.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**
We use the ideal gas equation: $PV = nRT$, which is valid for $H_2$ up to $\sim 200\ \text{bar}$ with $< 5\%$ deviation; at 700 bar the compressibility factor $Z \approx 1.5$ introduces a correction, but for first-order analysis $Z = 1$ is standard in KTU exams unless specified.

$$PV = nRT$$

**Step 2 — Express $n$ in terms of mass.**
The number of moles of hydrogen is:

$$n = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{M_{H_2}}$$

Substituting $m_{H_2} = 5.0\ \text{kg}$ and $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol}$:

$$n = \dfrac{5.0}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$n = 2480.16\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange the gas law for volume.**
Solving for $V$:

$$V = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$$

**Step 4 — Convert units carefully.**
- $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)} = 8.314\ \text{Pa·m}^3/(\text{mol·K})$
- $T = 298\ \text{K}$
- $P = 700\ \text{bar} = 700 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa} = 7.0 \times 10^7\ \text{Pa}$

**Step 5 — Plug values into the expression.**

$$V = \dfrac{(2480.16)(8.314)(298)}{7.0 \times 10^7}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the numerator first.**

$$(2480.16)(8.314) = 20620.5\ \text{Pa·m}^3/\text{K (per mole basis)}$$

$$(20620.5)(298) = 6{,}144{,}909\ \text{J}$$

Note: $\text{Pa·m}^3 = \text{N/m}^2 \cdot \text{m}^3 = \text{N·m} = \text{J}$.

**Step 7 — Divide by pressure.**

$$V = \dfrac{6{,}144{,}909}{7.0 \times 10^7}$$

$$V = 0.0878\ \text{m}^3 = 87.8\ \text{L}$$

**Step 8 — Apply compressibility correction (advanced).**
Using $Z = 1.5$ at 700 bar for $H_2$:

$$V_{\text{real}} = Z \cdot V_{\text{ideal}} = 1.5 \times 87.8 = 131.7\ \text{L}$$

**Step 9 — Final Result and Physical Interpretation.**
A Type IV tank with $\sim 132\ \text{L}$ internal volume is required. This is consistent with commercial FCV data — the Toyota Mirai's three tanks collectively provide $\sim 122\ \text{L}$ of usable hydrogen volume.

$$\boxed{V_{\text{tank}} \approx 132\ \text{L} \quad \text{for } 5\ \text{kg H}_2 \text{ at } 700\ \text{bar, 298 K}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Isentropic Compression Work for Hydrogen

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the theoretical minimum work to compress $1\ \text{kg}$ of hydrogen isentropically from $P_1 = 1\ \text{bar}$ to $P_2 = 700\ \text{bar}$ at $T_1 = 298\ \text{K}$. Use $\gamma = 1.405$ for $H_2$.

**Step 1 — Isentropic work equation for an open system (compressor):**

$$W_{\text{comp}} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$$

This formula gives work per mole. We will compute it per mole first, then scale to $1\ \text{kg}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the dimensionless exponent.**

$$\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{1.405 - 1}{1.405} = \dfrac{0.405}{1.405} = 0.2883$$

**Step 3 — Compute the pressure ratio term.**

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{700}{1} = 700$$

$$700^{0.2883} = e^{0.2883 \cdot \ln(700)} = e^{0.2883 \cdot 6.5511} = e^{1.8885} = 6.610$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bracketed term.**

$$\left[6.610 - 1\right] = 5.610$$

**Step 5 — Compute the leading coefficient.**

$$\dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} = \dfrac{1.405}{0.405} = 3.469$$

**Step 6 — Multiply stepwise.**

$$W_{\text{comp per mole}} = (3.469)(8.314)(298)(5.610)$$

$$(3.469)(8.314) = 28.84$$

$$(28.84)(298) = 8594.3\ \text{J/mol}$$

$$(8594.3)(5.610) = 48{,}214\ \text{J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{comp, mol}} \approx 48.2\ \text{kJ/mol}}$$

**Step 7 — Scale to $1\ \text{kg}$ of $H_2$.**

$$n = \dfrac{1.0\ \text{kg}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol}} = 496.03\ \text{mol}$$

$$W_{\text{comp, kg}} = 496.03 \times 48{,}214$$

$$W_{\text{comp, kg}} = 23{,}914{,}000\ \text{J} \approx 23.9\ \text{MJ}$$

**Step 8 — Compare with the energy content of hydrogen.**
The lower heating value (LHV) of hydrogen is $120\ \text{MJ/kg}$. Compression consumes $\sim 20\%$ of the LHV — a significant parasitic energy loss that motivates the search for low-pressure storage (metal hydrides).

$$\eta_{\text{parasitic}} = \dfrac{23.9}{120} = 19.9\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world note:** Multi-stage compressors with intercooling reduce this to $\sim 13 - 15\%$, but the parasitic energy remains a critical engineering metric.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — van't Hoff Equation for Metal Hydride Desorption

**Problem Statement:** For a metal hydride $MH_x$, the equilibrium pressure follows the van't Hoff relation. Given:
- $\Delta H = +30\ \text{kJ/mol}$ (endothermic desorption)
- $\Delta S = +108\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- Operating temperature $T = 333\ \text{K}$

Find the equilibrium pressure $P_{eq}$ at which hydrogen is released.

**Step 1 — van't Hoff equation.**

$$\ln P_{eq} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right) + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the first term.**

$$-\dfrac{\Delta H}{R \cdot T} = -\dfrac{30{,}000}{(8.314)(333)} = -\dfrac{30{,}000}{2768.6} = -10.836$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the second term.**

$$\dfrac{\Delta S}{R} = \dfrac{108}{8.314} = 12.990$$

**Step 4 — Sum the two terms.**

$$\ln P_{eq} = -10.836 + 12.990 = 2.154$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $P_{eq}$.**

$$P_{eq} = e^{2.154} = 8.62\ \text{atm} = 8.62 \times 101.325 = 873.4\ \text{kPa} = 8.73\ \text{bar}$$

$$\boxed{P_{eq} \approx 8.7\ \text{bar at } 333\ \text{K}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** At $60\ ^\circ\text{C}$, this hydride releases $H_2$ at $\sim 8.7\ \text{bar}$ — making it suitable for low-pressure PEFC fuel cells that require $1 - 3\ \text{bar}$ inlet pressure, with the balance supplied by a simple regulator.

**Block Description:**

- **Vapor return line** captures displaced $H_2$ during filling and returns it to the station storage — improves overall system efficiency by $5 - 10\%$.
