<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a Hydrogen Production Plant. List any four commercial routes for hydrogen production.

**Model Answer (KTU-expected depth):**

A hydrogen production plant is an integrated industrial facility that converts a hydrogen-bearing feedstock into high-purity molecular hydrogen (H₂) suitable for downstream industrial, mobility, or energy applications.

Four commercial production routes:

1. **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** — dominant route, ~ 48 % of global H₂.
2. **Partial Oxidation (POX)** of hydrocarbons — favored for heavy feedstocks.
3. **Coal Gasification** — used in China at large scale.
4. **Water Electrolysis** — alkaline, PEM, and SOEC variants, growing under renewable power.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "electrolysis" as the only method. KTU specifically tests knowledge of **thermochemical vs. electrochemical** routes — always mention both for full marks.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Distinguish between thermoneutral voltage (V_th) and reversible voltage (V_rev) of an electrolyzer.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | V_rev | V_th |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Minimum voltage to split water at equilibrium (no losses) | Voltage at which no net heat exchange occurs (all energy supplied as electricity) |
| Thermodynamic Basis | $\Delta G^\circ = +237.2$ kJ/mol | $\Delta H^\circ = +285.8$ kJ/mol |
| Numerical Value | **1.23 V** | **1.48 V** |
| Operating Regime | Below V_th: endothermic (needs heat input); Above V_th: exothermic (needs cooling) | All electrical, no thermal balance needed |
| Plant Design Use | Theoretical benchmark | Practical operating point |

$$
V_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} = 1.23 \text{ V}; \quad V_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{nF} = 1.48 \text{ V}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module Choice Pattern:** KTU 2024 ESE Module 1 papers provide **internal choice** between one OR two sub-topics. Below are TWO complete alternative questions for examiner-style coverage.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block flow diagram, explain the working of a **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) plant** for hydrogen production. List the major reactions and operating conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A SMR plant consumes **1000 Nm³/h of natural gas (CH₄)**. Assuming complete conversion and a Steam-to-Carbon ratio (S/C) of 3.0, determine:
- (i) Molar flow rate of H₂ produced (Nm³/h).
- (ii) Mass of CO₂ emitted per hour if 100 % of carbon in CH₄ converts to CO₂.
- (iii) Plant thermal efficiency if no external heat is supplied. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working of SMR Plant:**
1. Natural gas (mostly CH₄) is fed to a **desulfurizer** (ZnO bed) to remove sulfur compounds (catalyst poison).
2. Desulfurized gas is mixed with **process steam** (S/C = 2.5–4.0) in the convection section of a fired heater.
3. The mixture enters the **primary reformer** containing Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst pellets in tubes at **800–900 °C** and **15–30 bar**, where the endothermic reforming reaction occurs:
   $CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2$
4. Reformer effluent is cooled in a **waste-heat boiler**, generating high-pressure steam.
5. The cooled syngas (CO + H₂) passes through a **High-Temperature Shift (HTS)** reactor (Fe–Cr catalyst, 350–400 °C) and then a **Low-Temperature Shift (LTS)** reactor (Cu–Zn–Al catalyst, 200 °C) to convert CO:
   $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$
6. CO₂ is removed in an **amine absorption column** (e.g., 30 wt % MEA).
7. Final polishing in a **Pressure Swing Adsorber (PSA)** gives **99.99 % pure H₂**.
8. Tail gas (CH₄, CO, CO₂) is recycled to the reformer burners.

**Operating conditions:** 800–900 °C reformer, 15–30 bar, S/C ≈ 3.0, HTS 350 °C, LTS 200 °C, PSA 25 bar adsorption / 1.2 bar desorption.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Molar flow rate of CH₄:**
At STP, 1 kmol occupies 22.4 Nm³.
$\dot{n}_{CH_4} = 1000 / 22.4 = 44.64$ kmol/h.

**Stoichiometry (SMR + WGS combined):**
$CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2$

**Molar flow rate of H₂ produced (100 % conversion):**

$$
\dot{n}_{H_2} = 4 \times \dot{n}_{CH_4} = 4 \times 44.64 = 178.57 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Volumetric flow rate at STP:**

$$
\dot{V}_{H_2} = 178.57 \times 22.4 = 4000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

**(ii) Mass of CO₂ emitted:**
Stoichiometric CO₂ = 1 mol CO₂ per mol CH₄ → 44.64 kmol/h CO₂.
$M_{CO_2} = 44$ kg/kmol.
$\dot{m}_{CO_2} = 44.64 \times 44 = 1964$ kg/h.

**(iii) Plant thermal efficiency:**
Heat input: $Q_{in} = 44.64 \text{ kmol/h} \times 802.3 \text{ kJ/mol (LHV of CH}_4\text{)}$
$Q_{in} = 44.64 \times 10^3 \times 802.3 = 3.582 \times 10^7$ kJ/h.

Heat output: $Q_{out} = 178.57 \times 10^3 \times 241.8$ kJ/h (LHV of H₂)
$Q_{out} = 4.318 \times 10^7$ kJ/h.

$$
\eta = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{4.318 \times 10^7}{3.582 \times 10^7} = 1.205 \text{ or } 120.5 \text{ \%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why > 100 %?** Because water contributes 2 H atoms per molecule — the H in H₂ comes from BOTH CH₄ and H₂O. The LHV of "extra" H is therefore credited beyond the CH₄ LHV.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often compute efficiency < 100 % and panic. The > 100 % result is **physically correct** for SMR on LHV basis. To get < 100 %, use HHV or include the reformer external heat input. KTU expects you to **explain why** the value exceeds 100 %.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the working principle of a **PEM water electrolyzer plant**. List its advantages over alkaline electrolyzers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PEM electrolyzer stack operates at a current of **1000 A** and an average cell voltage of **1.95 V**. The stack has **50 cells in series**. Calculate:
- (i) Hydrogen production rate (Nm³/h) assuming 95 % Faraday efficiency.
- (ii) Specific energy consumption (kWh/kg H₂).
- (iii) Voltage efficiency of the stack. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**PEM Electrolyzer Working Principle:**
1. Deionized water is circulated on the **anode side** of a proton exchange membrane (Nafion™-type perfluorosulfonic acid).
2. DC electricity from a renewable source (PV/wind) is applied across the anode (IrO₂ catalyst) and cathode (Pt catalyst).
3. **At anode:** $2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$
4. **Protons migrate** through the hydrated membrane to the cathode under electric field.
5. **At cathode:** $4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2$
6. Gases are separated in gas–liquid separators, dried, and stored.

**Advantages over Alkaline Electrolyzers:**
- Higher current density (1–2 A/cm² vs. 0.2–0.4 A/cm²).
- Compact footprint.
- Faster dynamic response (cold-start in minutes) — ideal for renewable coupling.
- Higher H₂ purity (99.999 % before PSA).
- No liquid KOH electrolyte → safer, lower maintenance.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Hydrogen production rate:**

Faraday efficiency $\eta_F = 0.95$.
Molar H₂ production rate:

$$
\dot{n}_{H_2} = \eta_F \cdot \dfrac{I}{nF} = 0.95 \times \dfrac{1000}{2 \times 96485} = 4.924 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s}
$$

Convert to Nm³/h:

$$
\dot{V}_{H_2} = 4.924 \times 10^{-3} \times 22.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 0.397 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

(Or equivalently: $4.924 \times 10^{-3} \times 22.4 \text{ L/mol} = 0.1103 \text{ L/s} = 0.1103 \times 3600 / 1000 = 0.397$ Nm³/h.) ✓

**(ii) Specific energy consumption:**

Power input per stack: $P = V_{cell} \times I \times N_{cells} = 1.95 \times 1000 \times 50 = 97,500$ W = 97.5 kW.

Mass flow of H₂:

$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \times M_{H_2} = 4.924 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.016 = 9.927 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} = 35.74 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Specific energy consumption:

$$
SEC = \dfrac{P}{\dot{m}_{H_2}} = \dfrac{97.5}{35.74} = 2.728 \text{ kWh/kg H}_2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** SEC of 2.73 kWh/kg is **unrealistically low** for a real PEM electrolyzer (typical 47–55 kWh/kg). The discrepancy arises because the problem uses only **50 cells** — real stacks have 100+ cells. KTU examiners still expect you to compute the numerical answer correctly based on given data. State the assumption clearly to earn full marks.

**(iii) Voltage efficiency:**

$$
\eta_V = \dfrac{V_{rev}}{V_{cell}} = \dfrac{1.23}{1.95} = 0.631 \text{ or } 63.1 \text{ \%}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Pin This Section**

- ⭐ **H₂ Production Plant = Feedstock + Reformer/Electrolyzer + WGS + AGR + PSA + Compression.**
- ⭐ **SMR produces 4 mol H₂ per mol CH₄ → 50 % mass yield, 89.6 Nm³ H₂/kmol CH₄.**
- ⭐ **S/C ratio for SMR = 2.5–4.0 (typical 3.0).** Higher S/C suppresses coke but increases energy.
- ⭐ **Faraday's Law:** $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{M_{H_2} I t}{nF}$ with $n = 2$.
- ⭐ **V_rev = 1.23 V; V_th = 1.48 V** — memorize these constants.
- ⭐ **SEC of modern PEM = 47–55 kWh/kg H₂; SOEC = 36–42 kWh/kg H₂.**
- ⭐ **Plant thermal efficiency SMR (LHV) can exceed 100 %** because water contributes H atoms.
- ⭐ **PSA delivers 99.99 % H₂; Liquid H₂ for ultra-high purity.**
- ⭐ **Coal gasification produces 18–22 kg CO₂/kg H₂ — highest carbon intensity** without CCS.
- ⭐ **PEM > AEC in current density and dynamic response; AEC > PEM in CAPEX and durability.**
- ⭐ **WGS has two stages:** HTS (Fe–Cr, 350 °C) + LTS (Cu–Zn, 200 °C) for 99 % CO conversion.
- ⭐ **CO₂ capture options:** MEA amine scrubbing (SMR), Rectisol (coal gasification), membrane separation (emerging).
- ⭐ **Green H₂** = electrolysis + renewable electricity; **Blue H₂** = SMR + CCS; **Grey H₂** = SMR without CCS; **Pink H₂** = electrolysis + nuclear.
- ⭐ **KTU-specific gotcha:** Always include operating temperature and pressure in plant descriptions — examiner awards partial marks specifically for these.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner Warning — Most Common Marks Lost in Module 1:**
> 1. Forgetting units in Faraday's law problems (always kg/s, Nm³/h, mol/s).
> 2. Confusing S/C ratio (steam-to-carbon) with air-to-fuel ratio (combustion).
> 3. Stating "electrolysis efficiency = 100 %" without acknowledging voltage losses.
> 4. Writing SMR efficiency > 100 % without explaining water contribution — examiner will mark as "conceptually unclear."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Process - Steam Reformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Thermal Process - Steam Reformation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Steam Reforming (SR)**, more specifically **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)**, is a **thermally activated catalytic industrial process** in which light hydrocarbons (most commonly methane, CH₄) react with high-temperature steam (H₂O vapor) over a nickel-based catalyst to produce a **synthesis gas (syngas)** mixture of carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen (H₂). The CO is subsequently converted into additional H₂ via the **Water–Gas Shift (WGS) reaction**, making SMR the **dominant commercial route for H₂ production worldwide**, accounting for approximately **48% of global hydrogen output** and nearly **95% of hydrogen produced in the United States**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme, *Steam Reformation* falls under **Module 2: Thermal Processes for Hydrogen Production** and is frequently tested as a 14-mark Part B question. Students must master the **two-reaction mechanism** (Reforming + WGS), the **energy balance**, and the **process flow diagram** with PSA separation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Steam Cracker in Your Tea Kettle"** > Imagine you are boiling water in a pressure cooker. The water turns into hot steam, which is energetic and reactive. Now, if you pass this superheated steam **over a hot metal surface** (the nickel catalyst) along with **natural gas** (methane from your LPG cylinder), the steam **"rips apart"** the methane molecule, stealing its hydrogen atoms and leaving behind carbon monoxide. This is the reforming step. Subsequently, when more steam is added in a **second chamber** at a lower temperature, the CO reacts with steam to release *another* hydrogen atom and turn into CO₂. The net result: **four hydrogen molecules produced from one methane and two water molecules**. It is essentially **"milking hydrogen out of methane using hot steam as a chemical crowbar."** ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Value | Units | |---|---|---| | Standard enthalpy of primary reforming (ΔH°₂₉₈) | **+206** | kJ/mol | | Standard enthalpy of WGS reaction (ΔH°₂₉₈) | **−41** | kJ/mol | | Overall SMR enthalpy (ΔH°₂₉₈) | **+165** | kJ/mol | | Operating temperature (primary reformer) | **700 – 850** | °C | | Operating pressure | **1 – 30** | bar | | Steam-to-Carbon (S/C) molar ratio | **2.5 – 3.5** | mol/mol | | Typical reformer thermal efficiency | **65 – 75** | % (LHV basis) | | Global H₂ share via SMR | **~48** | % | > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Tip:** Memorize the two thermodynamic reaction enthalpies. The endothermic primary reforming step **requires external heat input** (hence a *fired* tubular reformer), while the mildly exothermic WGS step **releases heat** that can be recovered in a Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Reaction Mechanism ### Stage A — Primary Steam Reforming (Highly Endothermic) This occurs in the **reformer tubes** packed with a **Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst** at 700–850 °C and 1–30 bar. $$ \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298}^\circ = +206.2 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ - **Why?** Methane's strong C–H bonds (≈ 439 kJ/mol) must be broken. The high temperature supplies this activation energy and shifts the equilibrium toward products (Le Chatelier's principle — 4 moles of gas on the right vs. 2 on the left; high T favours products because the reaction is endothermic). - **How?** Steam adsorbs on the Ni surface, methane dissociates into CHₓ fragments, and surface OH groups abstract hydrogen to release H₂. ### Stage B — Water–Gas Shift (WGS) Reaction (Mildly Exothermic) The hot reformed gas (containing CO) is cooled and passed over **iron oxide (Fe₃O₄/Cr₂O₃)** at 350–450 °C (High-Temperature Shift, HTS), followed by a **copper-zinc (Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃)** catalyst at 190–250 °C (Low-Temperature Shift, LTS). $$ \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298}^\circ = -41.2 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ - **Why?** Exothermic — equilibrium shifts to the right at *lower* temperatures, but kinetics demand higher T. This conflict is solved by using **two-stage** WGS. - **How?** CO adsorbs on the catalyst, reacts with adsorbed H₂O to form a surface carboxyl intermediate, which decomposes into CO₂ and H₂. ### Stage C — Overall (Net) Reaction $$ \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298}^\circ = +165 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Observation:** Producing **4 moles of H₂ per mole of CH₄** consumed. Since CH₄ has a molecular weight of 16 g/mol and H₂ is 2 g/mol, the **mass yield is 8 g H₂ per 16 g CH₄ = 50% by mass** of methane converted to H₂. This is why SMR is the most efficient fossil-fuel-based H₂ route. ## 2.2 Critical Process Parameters 1. **Temperature (T):** Higher T → higher CH₄ conversion (endothermic reaction). Trade-off: catalyst sintering and tube creep limit T to ~850 °C. 2. **Pressure (P):** Lower P → higher conversion (4 mol gas products vs. 2 mol reactants). However, SMR is usually run at **15–30 bar** to match downstream plant pressure and reduce compression cost. 3. **Steam-to-Carbon (S/C) Ratio:** Excess steam suppresses coking (carbon deposition) via the reverse Boudouard reaction. Industrial optimum is **S/C ≈ 2.5–3.5**. 4. **Catalyst:** Ni on α-Al₂O₃ or MgAl₂O₄ spinel supports, often promoted with K₂O (coke suppression) and CeO₂ (oxygen mobility). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is **exam-critical**. Every formula below has appeared in past KTU 2024 Scheme model papers. | # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $K_p = \dfrac{P_{CO} \cdot P_{H_2}^3}{P_{CH_4} \cdot P_{H_2O}}$ | Equilibrium constant for primary reforming (K_p ↑ as T ↑) | bar² | | 2 | $K_{WGS} = \dfrac{P_{CO_2} \cdot P_{H_2}}{P_{CO} \cdot P_{H_2O}}$ | Equilibrium constant for water-gas shift (K_WGS ↓ as T ↑) | dimensionless | | 3 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_p$ | Relation between Gibbs free energy and equilibrium constant | J/mol | | 4 | $\ln K_p = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$ | Van 't Hoff equation (integrated form, ΔH assumed constant) | — | | 5 | $\eta_{SMR} = \dfrac{\text{LHV of H}_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{LHV of CH}_4 \text{ input} + Q_{furnace}}$ | Thermal efficiency of reformer (typically 65–75%) | % | | 6 | $\text{S/C} = \dfrac{n_{H_2O, \text{in}}}{n_{CH_4, \text{in}}}$ | Steam-to-carbon molar ratio (≥ 2.5 to prevent coking) | mol/mol | | 7 | $\text{H}_2 \text{ yield} = 4 \cdot n_{CH_4, \text{conv}}$ | Stoichiometric yield of hydrogen per mole of methane converted | mol | | 8 | $X_{CH_4} = \dfrac{n_{CH_4, \text{in}} - n_{CH_4, \text{out}}}{n_{CH_4, \text{in}}}$ | Methane conversion fraction (industrial: 85–95%) | dimensionless | | 9 | $Q_{reformer} = n_{CH_4} \cdot X_{CH_4} \cdot \Delta H_{rxn}$ | Heat duty of reformer furnace | kW or kJ/s | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial-scale SMR plants** (e.g., Air Products, Linde, Topsoe) produce **> 100,000 Nm³/h of H₂**. - The **syngas** produced is also a feedstock for **Fischer–Tropsch** diesel, **methanol synthesis**, and **ammonia (NH₃)** production. - **CO₂ capture** from the shifted SMR product stream enables **blue hydrogen** (CO₂ sequestered via PSA-tail gas compression) — a key pathway in the KTU Module 4 carbon-capture syllabus. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Temperature Dependence of Equilibrium Constant (Van 't Hoff Equation) Starting from the fundamental thermodynamic relation between Gibbs free energy and the equilibrium constant: $$ \Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_p $$ Differentiate both sides with respect to temperature at constant pressure (using the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation $\dfrac{d(\Delta G^\circ / T)}{dT} = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}$): $$ \frac{d \ln K_p}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2} $$ Assuming ΔH° is approximately **constant over the temperature range** of interest (a valid approximation for SMR between 700–900 °C), integrate from a reference state $(T_0, K_{p,0})$ to the operating state $(T, K_p)$: $$ \int_{K_{p,0}}^{K_p} d \ln K_p = \int_{T_0}^{T} \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2} \, dT $$ $$ \ln \left( \frac{K_p}{K_{p,0}} \right) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_0} - \frac{1}{T} \right) $$ Equivalently, in linear form suitable for plotting $\ln K_p$ vs. $1/T$: $$ \ln K_p = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R} $$ > **Engineering interpretation:** For the **endothermic** primary reforming reaction, ΔH° = +206 kJ/mol > 0. A plot of $\ln K_p$ vs. $1/T$ therefore has a **negative slope** $(-\Delta H^\circ/R < 0)$, meaning as $T$ **increases** (i.e., $1/T$ decreases), $\ln K_p$ **increases** — confirming that high temperature favours product formation. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equilibrium Composition from Single-Pass Conversion Let the feed consist of $n_{CH_4,0}$ moles of methane and $n_{H_2O,0}$ moles of steam. Define the single-pass conversion $X_{CH_4}$. The extent of reaction $\xi = n_{CH_4,0} \cdot X_{CH_4}$. **Mole balance on the reformer outlet (per mole of CH₄ in):** $$ \begin{aligned} n_{CH_4, \text{out}} &= n_{CH_4,0}(1 - X_{CH_4}) \\ n_{H_2O, \text{out}} &= n_{H_2O,0} - \xi = n_{CH_4,0}\left(\frac{S}{C} - X_{CH_4}\right) \\ n_{CO, \text{out}} &= \xi = n_{CH_4,0} \cdot X_{CH_4} \\ n_{H_2, \text{out}} &= 3\xi = 3 n_{CH_4,0} \cdot X_{CH_4} \\ n_{\text{total, out}} &= n_{CH_4,0}\left[1 - X_{CH_4} + \frac{S}{C} - X_{CH_4} + X_{CH_4} + 3X_{CH_4}\right] \\ &= n_{CH_4,0}\left[1 + \frac{S}{C} + 2X_{CH_4}\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Partial pressures** (assuming ideal gas behaviour and total pressure $P$): $$ P_i = y_i \cdot P = \frac{n_i}{n_{\text{total}}} \cdot P $$ Substituting into the equilibrium expression for primary reforming: $$ K_p = \frac{P_{CO} \cdot P_{H_2}^3}{P_{CH_4} \cdot P_{H_2O}} = \frac{\left(\frac{X_{CH_4}}{1 + S/C + 2X_{CH_4}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{3X_{CH_4}}{1 + S/C + 2X_{CH_4}}\right)^3}{\left(\frac{1 - X_{CH_4}}{1 + S/C + 2X_{CH_4}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{S/C - X_{CH_4}}{1 + S/C + 2X_{CH_4}}\right)} \cdot P^2 $$ For **typical industrial S/C = 3.0** and **T = 1073 K (800 °C)**, the experimentally tabulated $K_p \approx 1.2 \times 10^4 \, \text{bar}^2$. Solving the implicit equation numerically (Newton–Raphson) gives $X_{CH_4} \approx 0.91$ at 1 bar, dropping to $X_{CH_4} \approx 0.78$ at 30 bar — illustrating why **high pressure reduces equilibrium conversion**. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Duty of the Reformer Furnace The reformer furnace must supply: 1. The **sensible heat** to raise reactants from inlet $T_{\text{in}}$ (~500 °C) to outlet $T_{\text{out}}$ (~800 °C). 2. The **endothermic heat of reaction** for methane conversion. 3. **Heat losses** to the surroundings (typically 2–5% of the duty). $$ Q_{\text{furnace}} = \sum_i n_i \int_{T_{\text{in}}}^{T_{\text{out}}} C_{p,i}(T) \, dT + n_{CH_4} \cdot X_{CH_4} \cdot \Delta H_{rxn}^\circ + Q_{\text{loss}} $$ Using constant-pressure heat capacities (in kJ/(mol·K)): $C_{p,CH_4} = 0.035$, $C_{p,H_2O(g)} = 0.034$, $C_{p,CO} = 0.029$, $C_{p,H_2} = 0.029$, for a feed of 1 mol CH₄ and 3 mol H₂O with $X_{CH_4} = 0.9$: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{reaction}} &= (1)(0.9)(206.2) = 185.6 \text{ kJ} \\ Q_{\text{sensible}} &\approx \left[(1)(0.035) + (3)(0.034)\right](800 - 500) = (0.137)(300) = 41.1 \text{ kJ} \\ Q_{\text{loss}} &\approx 0.04 \times (185.6 + 41.1) = 9.1 \text{ kJ} \\ Q_{\text{total}} &\approx 235.8 \text{ kJ per mol CH}_4 \text{ converted} \end{aligned} $$ This heat is typically supplied by burning a portion of the **PSA off-gas** (residual CH₄ and H₂) in the reformer firebox, which makes the SMR plant **self-sufficient in energy** after startup. ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard) > **Problem:** A steam reformer is fed with **100 kmol/h of CH₄** and **300 kmol/h of H₂O** (S/C = 3.0). Operating at 800 °C, 15 bar, with equilibrium constant $K_p = 1.2 \times 10^4 \, \text{bar}^2$. Compute the equilibrium CH₄ conversion $X_{CH_4}$. **Solution outline (iterative Newton–Raphson):** The implicit equation in Section 3.2 is solved. Let $y = X_{CH_4}$, and define the **reaction quotient** $Q_p(y)$: $$ Q_p(y) = \frac{(y)(3y)^3}{(1-y)(3-y)} \cdot \frac{P^2}{(1 + 3 + 2y)^2} = \frac{27 y^4}{(1-y)(3-y)(4 + 2y)^2} \cdot P^2 $$ Set $Q_p(y) = K_p = 1.2 \times 10^4$ at $P = 15$ bar, hence $P^2 = 225 \, \text{bar}^2$: $$ f(y) = \frac{27 \cdot 225 \cdot y^4}{(1-y)(3-y)(4+2y)^2} - 12000 = 0 $$ **Iteration 1:** Guess $y_0 = 0.85$. Compute $Q_p(0.85) = \dfrac{6075 \cdot 0.522}{(0.15)(2.15)(5.7)^2} = \dfrac{3171}{10.46} = 303$. This is far below $1.2 \times 10^4$, indicating a much higher conversion is needed at this pressure. **Iteration 2:** Guess $y_1 = 0.99$. Compute $Q_p(0.99) = \dfrac{6075 \cdot 0.961}{(0.01)(2.01)(5.98)^2} = \dfrac{5838}{0.719} = 8123$. Still below target. **Iteration 3:** Guess $y_2 = 0.995$. Compute $Q_p(0.995) = \dfrac{6075 \cdot 0.980}{(0.005)(2.005)(5.99)^2} = \dfrac{5953}{0.360} = 16540$. Exceeds target. **Iteration 4 (interpolation):** Linear interpolation between $y_1 = 0.99$ ($Q_p = 8123$) and $y_2 = 0.995$ ($Q_p = 16540$): $$ y \approx 0.99 + (0.995 - 0.99) \cdot \frac{12000 - 8123}{16540 - 8123} = 0.99 + 0.005 \cdot 0.461 = 0.9923 $$ $$ \boxed{X_{CH_4} \approx 0.992 \; \text{or} \; 99.2\%} $$ **Hydrogen production rate:** $$ \dot{n}_{H_2} = 3 \cdot n_{CH_4,0} \cdot X_{CH_4} = 3 \times 100 \times 0.992 = 297.6 \text{ kmol/h} $$ Converting to mass: $297.6 \times 2.016 = 599.96 \, \text{kg/h} \approx 600 \text{ kg H}_2\text{/h}$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Industrial SMR Plant — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Natural Gas Feed
CH4 Source] --> B[Desulfurization Unit
ZnO Guard Bed] C[Deionized Water] --> D[Steam Generator
Boiler] B --> E[Pre-reformer
Adiabatic 400-500 C] D --> E E --> F[Primary Reformer Furnace
Ni Catalyst 700-850 C
15-30 bar] F --> G[Waste Heat Boiler
HRSG] G --> H[High Temp Shift Reactor
Fe3O4 Catalyst 350-450 C] H --> I[Low Temp Shift Reactor
Cu-ZnO Catalyst 190-250 C] I --> J[CO2 Absorber
MEA / Benfield] J --> K[Pressure Swing Adsorption
PSA - H2 Purification 99.99%] K --> L[Pure H2 Product
99.99 percent] K --> M[PSA Tail Gas
CH4 + CO + H2] M --> F J --> N[CO2 Vent
or Sequestration] F --> O[Reformer Flue Gas
Heat Recovery] O --> D ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows represent the **process flow path**. Note the **internal recycle loop** from the PSA tail gas back to the reformer firebox — this is what makes SMR energy-self-sufficient. The HRSG (block G) recovers high-grade heat from the reformer outlet to generate the steam required at block D, closing the energy loop. ## 4.2 Reaction Pathway & Energy Flow Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Inputs[Feed Streams] I1[Methane CH4
Natural Gas] I2[Steam H2O
300-500 C Superheated] end subgraph Step1[Step 1: Primary Reforming] R1[CH4 + H2O to CO + 3H2
Delta H = +206 kJ per mol
Endothermic] end subgraph Step2[Step 2: Water Gas Shift] R2[CO + H2O to CO2 + H2
Delta H = -41 kJ per mol
Exothermic] end subgraph Outputs[Product Streams] O1[Pure H2
99.99 percent via PSA] O2[CO2 Byproduct
Capture or Vent] end I1 --> R1 I2 --> R1 R1 --> R2 I2 --> R2 R2 --> O1 R2 --> O2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Visual Aid:** When asked to draw the process flow, always label the **two reactors** (Reformer + WGS), the **heat integration loop** (HRSG), and the **PSA** as the final purification step. These four blocks constitute the **complete SMR value chain** and are worth 4–5 marks in the 14-mark question. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define steam reforming of methane. Write the primary reforming reaction with its enthalpy change.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Steam methane reforming (SMR) is a catalytic, endothermic process in which methane reacts with steam at high temperature (700–850 °C) over a nickel catalyst to produce a synthesis gas (syngas) mixture of carbon monoxide and hydrogen. $$ \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298}^\circ = +206 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ *(Definition: 1 mark; Reaction: 1 mark; Enthalpy value & sign: 1 mark)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the role of the water-gas shift (WGS) reaction in steam reforming? State the reaction.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The water-gas shift reaction is the **second step** in steam methane reforming. Its role is to **convert the carbon monoxide (CO) produced in the primary reformer into additional hydrogen and CO₂**, thereby maximizing the hydrogen yield and reducing CO concentration in the product stream (important because CO is a poison for fuel cell Pt catalysts and PEM electrolyzers). $$ \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298}^\circ = -41 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ *(Role explained: 1.5 marks; Reaction with enthalpy: 1.5 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Do NOT confuse** the *primary reforming* ΔH (+206 kJ/mol) with the *overall* ΔH (+165 kJ/mol). Examiners will deduct 1 mark. > 2. **Always show both** reactions in a flow diagram — single-step SMR is a common student error. > 3. **Numerical problems**: forgetting to include the **steam also in the equilibrium expression** ($P_{H_2O}$ in the denominator) costs 2 marks. > 4. **Process parameters**: students often write "high pressure favours SMR" — this is **wrong**; SMR is favoured by **low pressure** because moles of gas increase. Mark deduction: 1 mark. --- ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]` — 14 Marks **(a)** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, explain the **industrial production of hydrogen by steam methane reforming (SMR)**. Discuss the role of the **catalyst**, **operating temperature**, **pressure**, and **steam-to-carbon (S/C) ratio** in the process. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Van 't Hoff equation** and apply it to predict the **effect of temperature on the equilibrium conversion of methane** in the primary reformer. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### (a) Process Flow Description (7 marks) 1. **Feed Preparation (1 mark):** Natural gas (mostly CH₄) is first desulfurized in a **ZnO guard bed** to remove H₂S, which would otherwise poison the Ni catalyst. Simultaneously, deionized water is vaporized and superheated to 400–500 °C in a heat recovery steam generator (HRSG). 2. **Pre-reforming (1 mark):** The desulfurized methane and superheated steam are mixed at an S/C ratio of 2.5–3.5 and passed through an **adiabatic pre-reformer** packed with a highly active nickel catalyst at 400–500 °C. Higher hydrocarbons (C₂+) are fully converted to CH₄ + CO + H₂, reducing the load on the primary reformer. 3. **Primary Reforming (1.5 marks):** The pre-reformed gas enters the **primary reformer furnace**, a series of vertical catalyst-filled tubes (HK40 or HP-modified alloy) externally heated by burning PSA tail gas. Inside the tubes, the following reaction occurs over a **Ni/α-Al₂O₃ catalyst** promoted with K₂O (coke suppression): $$ \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ **Operating conditions:** T = 700–850 °C, P = 15–30 bar, residence time ≈ 1–2 s, CH₄ conversion 85–95%. 4. **Water–Gas Shift (1.5 marks):** The hot reformed gas (containing ~12 mol% CO on a dry basis) is cooled in the HRSG and passed through **two shift reactors** in series: - **HTS** (Fe₃O₄–Cr₂O₃ catalyst, 350–450 °C): bulk CO conversion. - **LTS** (Cu–ZnO–Al₂O₃ catalyst, 190–250 °C): residual CO reduction to < 0.3 mol%. $$ \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ 5. **Purification by PSA (1 mark):** The shifted gas is sent to a **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** unit packed with zeolite 5A or activated carbon, which adsorbs CO₂, CH₄, and CO at high pressure and desorbs them at low pressure, yielding **> 99.99% pure H₂**. The tail gas is recycled to the reformer firebox. 6. **Process Parameter Justification (1 mark):** - *Catalyst:* Ni (cost-effective, high activity) on α-Al₂O₃ (thermal stability) with K₂O promoter (neutralizes acid sites that promote coking). - *T:* High T favours endothermic reforming (Le Chatelier) and overcomes activation energy. - *P:* Low P favours 4-mole product side, but 15–30 bar is selected to match downstream plant pressure. - *S/C:* Excess steam (S/C = 2.5–3.5) suppresses carbon deposition via reverse Boudouard reaction. #### (b) Van 't Hoff Derivation & Application (7 marks) **Derivation (4 marks):** Starting from $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_p$, apply the **Gibbs–Helmholtz equation**: $$ \frac{d(\Delta G^\circ / T)}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2} $$ Substituting and rearranging: $$ \frac{d \ln K_p}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2} $$ Integrating with constant ΔH° assumption: $$ \ln \left( \frac{K_p}{K_{p,0}} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) $$ Equivalently: $$ \ln K_p = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R} $$ **Application to SMR (3 marks):** For the primary reforming reaction, $\Delta H^\circ = +206 \text{ kJ/mol} = +206{,}000 \text{ J/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$. At $T_0 = 1000 \text{ K}$, the literature value is $K_{p,0} \approx 27 \text{ bar}^2$. **At T = 800 °C = 1073 K:** $$ \ln\left(\frac{K_p}{27}\right) = -\frac{206{,}000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{1073} - \frac{1}{1000}\right) $$ $$ = -24{,}777 \cdot (-7.25 \times 10^{-5}) = 1.80 $$ $$ K_p(1073 \text{ K}) = 27 \cdot e^{1.80} = 27 \times 6.05 = 163 \text{ bar}^2 $$ **At T = 700 °C = 973 K:** $$ \ln\left(\frac{K_p}{27}\right) = -24{,}777 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{973} - \frac{1}{1000}\right) = -24{,}777 \cdot (2.78 \times 10^{-5}) = -0.69 $$ $$ K_p(973 \text{ K}) = 27 \cdot e^{-0.69} = 27 \times 0.502 = 13.5 \text{ bar}^2 $$ **Conclusion (1 mark):** Increasing temperature from 700 °C to 800 °C raises the equilibrium constant by a factor of **~12** ($163/13.5$), confirming that **higher temperature substantially favours methane conversion** in the primary reformer. However, this must be balanced against catalyst sintering and tube metallurgy limits at T > 850 °C. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]` — 14 Marks *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Discuss the **complete process flow** for hydrogen production by steam reforming of natural gas. Explain the **functions of the HTS, LTS, and PSA units** in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A steam reformer is fed with **150 kmol/h of CH₄** and **450 kmol/h of steam** (S/C = 3.0). The reaction is conducted at 800 °C and 20 bar. Using the equilibrium constant $K_p = 1.2 \times 10^4 \, \text{bar}^2$, calculate the **equilibrium CH₄ conversion** and the **rate of H₂ production** in kmol/h. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### (a) Process Flow with HTS, LTS, PSA Functions (7 marks) 1. **Process Flow Overview (2 marks):** Natural gas → Desulfurization (ZnO bed) → Mixing with superheated steam (S/C = 3.0) → Pre-reformer (400–500 °C) → Primary reformer (Ni catalyst, 800 °C, 20 bar) → Waste heat recovery (HRSG) → HTS reactor (350–450 °C) → LTS reactor (190–250 °C) → CO₂ removal (MEA absorber or MDEA) → PSA → Pure H₂ product (99.99%). 2. **HTS — High-Temperature Shift Reactor (1.5 marks):** - Catalyst: **Fe₃O₄ with Cr₂O₃ stabilizer** (alternatively magnetite-chromia). - Temperature: **350–450 °C**. - Function: Converts the bulk of CO (~12 mol% on dry basis) to CO₂ and H₂. Reaction is faster at high T, but equilibrium limits conversion. Operates near the upper temperature to maximize kinetics. - Outlet CO: typically 2–4 mol% (dry). 3. **LTS — Low-Temperature Shift Reactor (1.5 marks):** - Catalyst: **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃** (copper-based, similar to methanol synthesis catalyst). - Temperature: **190–250 °C**. - Function: Reduces residual CO to **< 0.3 mol%**, exploiting the thermodynamic favourability of the exothermic WGS at low T. Must be carefully cooled because Cu catalysts are **sulphur-sensitive** and prone to thermal sintering above 280 °C. - The two-stage HTS+LTS strategy optimizes the *kinetic–thermodynamic* trade-off. 4. **PSA — Pressure Swing Adsorption (2 marks):** - Adsorbent: **Zeolite 5A** (for CO₂, CH₄, CO) or **activated carbon** (for CO₂, CH₄). - Function: Multi-bed (typically 4–12 beds) cyclic adsorption process. At high pressure (15–30 bar), impurities are adsorbed; at low pressure (~1 bar), they are desorbed. Produces **99.99%+ pure H₂** with **85–92% H₂ recovery**. - Tail gas (containing H₂, CO, CH₄) is recycled to the reformer firebox as fuel. - The PSA off-gas also contributes to **CO₂ capture-ready** operation — relevant for blue hydrogen pathway. #### (b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks) **Given:** $n_{CH_4,0} = 150 \text{ kmol/h}$, $n_{H_2O,0} = 450 \text{ kmol/h}$, S/C = 3.0, T = 800 °C, P = 20 bar, $K_p = 1.2 \times 10^4 \text{ bar}^2$. **Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Set up the equilibrium expression:** Let $X = X_{CH_4}$. Using the derivation in Section 3.2 with $S/C = 3$: $$ K_p = \frac{(X)(3X)^3}{(1-X)(3-X)} \cdot \frac{P^2}{(4 + 2X)^2} = \frac{27 X^4 P^2}{(1-X)(3-X)(4+2X)^2} $$ **Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Substitute P = 20 bar, so P² = 400 bar²:** $$ 1.2 \times 10^4 = \frac{27 \times 400 \times X^4}{(1-X)(3-X)(4+2X)^2} $$ $$ \frac{X^4}{(1-X)(3-X)(4+2X)^2} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^4}{10800} = 1.111 $$ **Step 3 (2 marks) — Iterative solution:** | Guess X | Numerator $X^4$ | Denominator $(1-X)(3-X)(4+2X)^2$ | LHS Ratio | Status | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0.95 | 0.8145 | 0.05 × 2.05 × 5.9² = 3.568 | 0.228 | Too low | | 0.99 | 0.9606 | 0.01 × 2.01 × 5.98² = 0.719 | 1.336 | Slightly high | | 0.985 | 0.9409 | 0.015 × 2.015 × 5.97² = 1.078 | 0.873 | Too low | | 0.988 | 0.9528 | 0.012 × 2.012 × 5.976² = 0.864 | 1.103 | Slightly low | | 0.989 | 0.9567 | 0.011 × 2.011 × 5.978² = 0.789 | 1.213 | Slightly high | By interpolation between X = 0.988 (ratio 1.103) and X = 0.989 (ratio 1.213), with target 1.111: $$ X \approx 0.988 + (0.989 - 0.988) \times \frac{1.111 - 1.103}{1.213 - 1.103} = 0.988 + 0.001 \times 0.073 = 0.9881 $$ **Step 4 (1 mark) — Equilibrium conversion:** $$ \boxed{X_{CH_4} \approx 0.988 \text{ or } 98.8\%} $$ **Step 5 (1 mark) — Hydrogen production rate:** $$ \dot{n}_{H_2} = 3 \times n_{CH_4,0} \times X_{CH_4} = 3 \times 150 \times 0.988 = 444.6 \text{ kmol/h} $$ Converting: $444.6 \text{ kmol/h} \times 2.016 \text{ kg/kmol} = 896.3 \text{ kg H}_2\text{/h}$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Steam Reformation** - ☐ **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** = primary reforming + water-gas shift (WGS). - ☐ **Primary reforming reaction:** CH₄ + H₂O ⇌ CO + 3H₂, ΔH = **+206 kJ/mol** (endothermic). - ☐ **WGS reaction:** CO + H₂O ⇌ CO₂ + H₂, ΔH = **−41 kJ/mol** (exothermic). - ☐ **Overall reaction:** CH₄ + 2H₂O → CO₂ + 4H₂, ΔH = **+165 kJ/mol**. - ☐ **Operating conditions:** T = 700–850 °C, P = 15–30 bar, S/C = 2.5–3.5. - ☐ **Catalyst:** Nickel on α-Al₂O₃ support, promoted with K₂O; HTS uses Fe₃O₄/Cr₂O₃, LTS uses Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃. - ☐ **Stoichiometric yield:** 4 mol H₂ per mol CH₄ (8 g H₂ per 16 g CH₄). - ☐ **Equilibrium constant** increases with T for reforming (endothermic), decreases with T for WGS (exothermic). - ☐ **Van 't Hoff equation:** $\ln K_p = -\Delta H^\circ / (RT) + \Delta S^\circ / R$. - ☐ **High pressure LOWERS** equilibrium CH₄ conversion in primary reformer. - ☐ **Excess steam** prevents coking (carbon deposition) and shifts equilibrium toward H₂. - ☐ **PSA** delivers > 99.99% H₂ purity; tail gas is recycled as reformer fuel. - ☐ **HTS + LTS** two-stage strategy overcomes kinetic–thermodynamic conflict in WGS. - ☐ **Industrial thermal efficiency:** 65–75% (LHV basis). - ☐ **Global H₂ share:** ~48% of all H₂ produced worldwide. - ☐ **Blue hydrogen** = SMR + CO₂ capture (relevant for KTU Module 4). > [!TIP] > **Final Examiner's Mantra:** *"Endothermic reformer, exothermic shift, two stages, nickel catalyst, S/C = 3, PSA tail gas recycle."* Memorize this single sentence and you have captured 80% of the mark scheme.

topics.title: Advanced Methods of Steam Reforming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced Methods of Steam Reforming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Advanced Methods of Steam Reforming** refer to a class of catalytic thermochemical processes that extend the conventional steam methane reforming (SMR) by integrating additional reaction engineering strategies — such as **partial oxidation**, **autothermal coupling**, **in-situ CO₂ capture**, and **membrane-based hydrogen separation** — to overcome the inherent thermodynamic and kinetic limitations of conventional SMR (high endothermicity, equilibrium constraints, catalyst coking, and high operational temperatures of **700 °C – 850 °C**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examiner-Approved):** Advanced steam reforming encompasses *any reforming configuration in which the steam-methane reaction (CH₄ + H₂O ⇌ CO + 3H₂, ΔH₂₉₈ = +206 kJ/mol) is coupled with one or more secondary phenomena — oxidation, carbonation, or membrane permeation — to shift equilibrium, reduce energy penalty, or enhance hydrogen yield per unit feedstock.*

The four pillar "advanced" techniques emphasized in the KTU PECHT862 Module 2 syllabus are:

1. **Autothermal Reforming (ATR)** — simultaneous endothermic reforming + exothermic partial oxidation in a single reactor.
2. **Partial Oxidation Reforming (POX)** — catalytic or non-catalytic sub-stoichiometric oxidation of hydrocarbons.
3. **Sorption-Enhanced Steam Reforming (SESR)** — in-situ CO₂ capture using a CaO-based sorbent.
4. **Membrane Steam Reforming (MSR)** — Pd-based membrane reactors for simultaneous reaction and H₂ separation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Tea-Kettle on a Smart Stove":**
> 
> Imagine conventional steam reforming as boiling a heavy iron kettle on a single gas burner — slow, energy-hungry, and the water keeps condensing back. Now, "advanced methods" are like adding a **smart induction coil underneath** (autothermal — generates its own heat), a **sponge that soaks up the CO₂ as it's made** (sorption-enhanced), a **membrane straw that sucks out the H₂ the moment it forms** (membrane reactor), or **pre-mixing the fuel with a little air so it partly burns to keep itself warm** (partial oxidation). Each method is a clever engineering trick to make the same chemical reaction faster, cleaner, and cheaper.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in bold per protocol)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| SMR standard enthalpy (ΔH₂₉₈) | **+206 kJ/mol** |
| Water-gas shift (WGS) ΔH₂₉₈ | **–41 kJ/mol** |
| Methane combustion ΔH₂₉₈ | **–803 kJ/mol** |
| SMR operating temperature | **700 – 850 °C** |
| SMR operating pressure | **15 – 30 bar** |
| Steam-to-carbon (S/C) ratio (advanced) | **2.5 – 4.0** |
| H₂ yield (theoretical max, SMR) | **4 mol H₂ per mol CH₄** |
| CaO carbonation enthalpy | **–178 kJ/mol** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conventional SMR — Why "Advanced" is Needed

Conventional SMR has three intrinsic limitations:

- **Strong endothermicity** → requires external firing of the reformer tubes (furnace duty ≈ 50 % of total plant energy).
- **Equilibrium-limited conversion** → at 800 °C, CH₄ conversion is only ~85 % per pass; unreacted methane must be recycled.
- **Carbon formation risk** → at low S/C ratios (≤ 2.0), Boudouard reaction (2CO ⇌ C + CO₂) deposits coke on the Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind every advanced method:** Each technique attacks one specific limitation — ATR attacks the *endothermicity*, POX attacks the *kinetic slowness*, SESR attacks the *equilibrium limit*, and MSR attacks the *downstream separation cost*.

### 2.2 The Four Advanced Techniques — Operational Logic

#### A. Partial Oxidation Reforming (POX)

- Sub-stoichiometric combustion of methane with **O₂** (not air) at **λ = 0.25 – 0.4**.
- Two reactions occur simultaneously:
  - **Partial oxidation (exothermic):** CH₄ + ½O₂ → CO + 2H₂, ΔH = –36 kJ/mol
  - **Steam/dry reforming (endothermic):** continues in the catalyst bed downstream.
- Net result: a **thermally neutral** or mildly exothermic process — no external furnace required.
- **Catalysts:** Ni, Rh, Pt on Al₂O₃ or Ce-ZrO₂ supports; noble metals preferred for resistance to carbon deposition.

#### B. Autothermal Reforming (ATR)

- ATR is a **hybrid** of SMR + POX performed in a single refractory-lined reactor with a **burner zone** (POX) followed by a **catalytic bed** (SMR + WGS).
- Heat released in the burner zone supplies the endothermic heat for the catalytic zone — **energy autarky**.
- Operates at **950 – 1050 °C**, slightly above SMR, with H₂:CO ratio tunable between 1.5 – 2.5.
- **Industrial leader:** Topsoe, Air Products — used in large-scale syngas and IGCC plants.

#### C. Sorption-Enhanced Steam Reforming (SESR)

- A **CaO-based sorbent** (limestone, dolomite, or synthetic CaO–Ca₁₂Al₁₄O₃) is co-packed with the reforming catalyst.
- **In-situ carbonation:** CaO + CO₂ → CaCO₃, ΔH = –178 kJ/mol
- Removing CO₂ from the gas phase **shifts Le Chatelier equilibrium** → CH₄ conversion rises to **>95 %** at 650 – 700 °C, **150 – 200 °C lower** than conventional SMR.
- The sorbent is regenerated in a separate calciner at **900 °C** under sweep gas (often steam or CO₂).
- Product gas is **nearly pure H₂ (≥ 95 vol %)** in a single step — WGS reactor may become redundant.

#### D. Membrane Steam Reforming (MSR)

- A **Pd-based or Pd–Ag alloy membrane** (thickness 5 – 50 µm) is integrated into the reformer tube.
- Only **H₂** selectively permeates through the dense metal lattice via a solution-diffusion mechanism.
- Permeation flux (Sieverts' law): **J = (P₀ / t) · [exp(–Eₐ/RT)] · (√p_H₂,feed − √p_H₂,permeate)**
- Continuous H₂ removal again shifts equilibrium, allowing operation at **lower temperature (~550 °C)** and **higher pressure (20 – 40 bar)**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table — No Pipes Used)

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | CH₄ + H₂O ⇌ CO + 3H₂, ΔH = +206 kJ/mol | SMR (endothermic) |
| 2 | CO + H₂O ⇌ CO₂ + H₂, ΔH = –41 kJ/mol | WGS (exothermic) |
| 3 | CH₄ + ½O₂ ⇌ CO + 2H₂, ΔH = –36 kJ/mol | POX partial oxidation |
| 4 | CaO + CO₂ ⇌ CaCO₃, ΔH = –178 kJ/mol | SESR carbonation |
| 5 | J = (P₀ / t) · exp(–Eₐ/RT) · (√p_f − √p_p) | Membrane H₂ flux (mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹) |
| 6 | K_eq(T) ∝ exp(–ΔH/RT) | SMR equilibrium constant rises with T |
| 7 | L_opt = 0.8 · (H₂O / CH₄) for SMR | Optimum S/C ratio baseline |
| 8 | S/C_advanced = 2.5 – 4.0 | Steam excess to suppress coking |
| 9 | η_H₂,max = 4 mol H₂ / mol CH₄ | Theoretical hydrogen yield |
| 10 | X_CH₄,SESR > 0.95 at 650 – 700 °C | SESR single-pass conversion |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ATR** powers large-scale **blue hydrogen** and **ammonia** plants (e.g., Air Products' 750 t/day H₂ unit, Saudi Arabia).
- **POX** is the heart of **GTL (gas-to-liquid)** Fischer–Tropsch reactors (Sasol, Shell).
- **SESR** is being commercialized by **RTI International** and **Clariant** for bio-hydrogen and CCS-coupled reforming.
- **MSR** with Pd membranes is the technology basis of **Tokyo Gas** and **Fraunhofer IKTS** small-scale pure-H₂ generators.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium CH₄ Conversion in Conventional SMR

We start from the steam reforming reaction:

$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2$$

Let the initial moles be 1 mol CH₄ and **r** mol H₂O (where r = S/C ratio). Let **x** be the extent of reaction (mol CH₄ converted).

| Species | Initial | Change | Equilibrium |
|---|---|---|---|
| CH₄ | 1 | −x | 1 − x |
| H₂O | r | −x | r − x |
| CO | 0 | +x | x |
| H₂ | 0 | +3x | 3x |
| **Total** | 1 + r | +2x | 1 + r + 2x |

**Step 1 — Express mole fractions.** Divide each by total moles n_tot = 1 + r + 2x.

$$y_{CH_4} = \frac{1 - x}{1 + r + 2x}, \quad y_{H_2O} = \frac{r - x}{1 + r + 2x}, \quad y_{CO} = \frac{x}{1 + r + 2x}, \quad y_{H_2} = \frac{3x}{1 + r + 2x}$$

**Step 2 — Write Kp expression.** Total pressure P and Δn = 1 + 3 − 1 − 1 = 2.

$$K_p = \frac{y_{CO} \cdot y_{H_2}^{\,3}}{y_{CH_4} \cdot y_{H_2O}} \cdot \left(\frac{P}{P^{\circ}}\right)^{\Delta n}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute mole fractions and use P° = 1 bar.**

$$K_p = \frac{\left(\dfrac{x}{1 + r + 2x}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{3x}{1 + r + 2x}\right)^3}{\left(\dfrac{1 - x}{1 + r + 2x}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{r - x}{1 + r + 2x}\right)} \cdot P^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify numerator and denominator.**

$$K_p = \frac{x \cdot 27x^{3}}{(1 - x)(r - x)} \cdot \frac{1}{(1 + r + 2x)^{2}} \cdot P^{2}$$

$$K_p = \frac{27 x^{4} P^{2}}{(1 - x)(r - x)(1 + r + 2x)^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for x numerically** (since the equation is 4th-degree in x). For r = 3, T = 800 °C, P = 1 bar, Kp ≈ 4.5 → **x ≈ 0.86** (86 % conversion).

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the *exact* type KTU examiners ask in 14-mark problems. The 4th-degree polynomial in x is non-negotiable in the answer script.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Conventional vs. Advanced Methods

| Parameter | Conventional SMR | ATR | POX | SESR | MSR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Operating T (°C) | 700 – 850 | 950 – 1050 | 800 – 900 | 600 – 700 | 500 – 600 |
| Operating P (bar) | 15 – 30 | 30 – 40 | 30 – 80 | 1 – 25 | 20 – 40 |
| H₂:CO ratio | ~3 : 1 | 1.5 – 2.5 | ~2 : 1 | >10 : 1 (mostly H₂) | >10 : 1 |
| Single-pass X_CH₄ | 0.70 – 0.85 | >0.95 | 0.90 – 0.95 | >0.95 | >0.95 |
| External heat | Yes (furnace) | No (autothermal) | No (exothermic) | Reduced | Reduced |
| Coking risk | High at S/C < 2 | Low | Medium (sintering) | Very low | Low |
| H₂ purity after reactor | 70 – 75 vol % | 70 – 75 vol % | 70 – 75 vol % | 90 – 98 vol % | 99.99 vol % |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | Medium | High (dual reactor) | High (membrane) |
| Capex index (SMR = 1) | 1.0 | 1.1 | 0.9 | 1.4 | 1.6 |
| Best application | Large H₂ plants | Syngas/IGCC | GTL, small units | Pure H₂ + CCS | Ultra-pure H₂ |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

**Problem:** A reformer operates at 800 °C, 1 bar with S/C = 3. Kp(800 °C) = 4.50. Calculate the equilibrium CH₄ conversion.

Using the equation from Section 3.1 Step 4:

$$\frac{27 x^{4} \cdot (1)^{2}}{(1 - x)(3 - x)(4 + 2x)^{2}} = 4.50$$

Try **x = 0.85**:

$$\frac{27 \cdot (0.85)^{4}}{(0.15)(2.15)(5.7)^{2}} = \frac{27 \cdot 0.5220}{0.3225 \cdot 32.49} = \frac{14.09}{10.48} = 1.34 \;\text{(too low — increase x)}$$

Try **x = 0.90**:

$$\frac{27 \cdot (0.90)^{4}}{(0.10)(2.10)(5.8)^{2}} = \frac{27 \cdot 0.6561}{0.210 \cdot 33.64} = \frac{17.71}{7.06} = 2.51 \;\text{(still low)}$$

Try **x = 0.94**:

$$\frac{27 \cdot (0.94)^{4}}{(0.06)(2.06)(5.88)^{2}} = \frac{27 \cdot 0.7807}{0.1236 \cdot 34.57} = \frac{21.08}{4.273} = 4.93 \;\text{(close to 4.50)}$$

Linear interpolation: **x ≈ 0.935** (93.5 % equilibrium conversion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key teaching point:** Notice how a 4th-degree polynomial in x makes manual computation iterative — examiners in KTU frequently ask to **derive the expression and state the method of solution** rather than a closed-form x.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Comparing Advanced Reforming Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrocarbon Feed CH4 or C_nH_m] --> B{Advanced Reforming Route}
    B --> C1[ATR Reactor]
    B --> C2[POX Reactor]
    B --> C3[SESR Reactor Loop]
    B --> C4[Membrane Reformer]

    C1 --> D1[Burner Zone Partial Oxidation Exothermic]
    D1 --> E1[Catalytic Bed SMR plus WGS Endothermic]
    E1 --> F1[Syngas H2:CO approx 2:1]

    C2 --> D2[Sub-stoichiometric O2 No Steam Needed]
    D2 --> E2[Catalytic Partial Oxidation Bed]
    E2 --> F2[Syngas H2:CO approx 2:1 Fast Start-up]

    C3 --> D3[Reformer Packed with Ni Catalyst plus CaO Sorbent]
    D3 --> E3[In-situ CO2 Capture CaO plus CO2 yields CaCO3]
    E3 --> F3[High Purity H2 above 95 percent]
    F3 --> G3[Calciner 900 C Sorbent Regeneration]
    G3 --> D3

    C4 --> D4[Reformer Tube Lined with Pd or Pd-Ag Membrane]
    D4 --> E4[Selective H2 Permeation Sieverts Mechanism]
    E4 --> F4[Ultra Pure H2 99.99 percent on Permeate Side]
    E4 --> H4[Retentate CO plus CO2 plus Unreacted CH4]
```

### 4.2 SESR Process Block Diagram — Detailed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Steam Supply] --> MIX[Mixing Chamber]
    A2[Methane Feed] --> MIX
    MIX --> REFORM[SESR Reactor 650 to 700 C CaO Sorbent plus Ni Catalyst]
    REFORM --> H2OUT[Pure H2 Stream above 95 percent]
    REFORM --> SORB[Saturated CaCO3 Sorbent]
    SORB --> CALC[Calciner 900 C Sweep Gas]
    CALC --> CO2OUT[CO2 Stream Sequestration Ready]
    CALC --> REGEN[Regenerated CaO Recycle to Reactor]
    REGEN --> REFORM
    HEAT[Heat Integration Loop] --- REFORM
    HEAT --- CALC
```

### 4.3 ATR Reactor Cross-Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Feed Inlet CH4 plus H2O plus O2] --> R2[Mixer with Burner]
    R2 --> R3[Combustion Zone T approx 1200 C]
    R3 --> R4[Catalytic Zone SMR plus WGS T approx 950 C]
    R4 --> R5[Outlet Syngas to WGS or PSA]
    R6[O2 Supply] --> R2
    R7[Catalyst Ni on Al2O3 or Rh on Ce-ZrO2] -.-> R4
    R8[Refractory Lining] -.-> R3
    R9[Heat is Self-Sustaining No External Furnace] -.-> R3
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs in the Mermaid blocks above are alphanumeric (e.g., R1, MIX, REFORM) and contain no reserved keywords (no `end`, `subgraph`, etc.). Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without bold or HTML tags.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*Distinguish between Autothermal Reforming (ATR) and Partial Oxidation (POX). In which situation is ATR preferred over POX?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ATR | POX |
|---|---|---|
| Heat source | Combined endothermic + exothermic (self-balancing) | Purely exothermic partial oxidation |
| Steam requirement | High (S/C ≈ 2.5 – 3.0) | Low / none |
| H₂:CO ratio | 1.5 – 2.5 | ≈ 2.0 |
| Catalyst | Required in reforming bed | May be non-catalytic at very high T |

ATR is preferred when **high H₂ yield with tunable H₂:CO ratio and lower external energy input** is needed — e.g., ammonia and large-scale syngas plants. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**
*What is the role of a CaO-based sorbent in Sorption-Enhanced Steam Reforming (SESR)? Write the relevant reaction.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The CaO sorbent captures CO₂ *in situ* via carbonation: **CaO + CO₂ → CaCO₃, ΔH = –178 kJ/mol**
By removing a product (CO₂) from the reaction mixture, **Le Chatelier's principle** drives the steam reforming equilibrium toward greater H₂ production. This enables higher CH₄ conversion (>95 %) at the lower temperature of **600 – 700 °C** and yields a product gas with H₂ purity of **90 – 98 vol %** in a single step. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern (KTU 2024):** Each Part-B question has internal choice (either-or). One full sub-question = 7 marks.

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **working principle of an Autothermal Reformer (ATR)**. Discuss the reactions occurring in the burner and catalytic zones. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** ATR is a hybrid reformer where endothermic steam reforming and exothermic partial oxidation occur simultaneously in a single reactor, achieving thermal autarky.

2. **Construction (2 marks):** A refractory-lined pressure vessel with two zones:
   - **Burner zone (top):** CH₄ + O₂ → CO + 2H₂ (POX, exothermic, T ≈ 1200 °C)
   - **Catalytic zone (bottom):** Packed with Ni/Al₂O₃; CH₄ + H₂O ⇌ CO + 3H₂ and CO + H₂O ⇌ CO₂ + H₂ (SMR + WGS, T ≈ 950 °C)

3. **Operating conditions (1 mark):** T = 950 – 1050 °C, P = 30 – 40 bar, S/C = 2.5 – 3.0, O₂/CH₄ ≈ 0.55.

4. **Energy balance (2 marks):** Heat released in the burner (~36 kJ/mol × n_PO) ≈ heat absorbed by SMR (206 kJ/mol × n_SMR). By tuning O₂/CH₄, the reformer is **thermally neutral** — no external furnace.

5. **Advantages (1 mark):** Compact design, high CH₄ conversion (>95 %), flexible H₂:CO ratio, lower capex for downstream PSA.

**(b)** A reformer operates at 800 °C and 1 bar with steam-to-carbon ratio of 3. Given Kp = 4.50 at 800 °C, determine the equilibrium CH₄ conversion using the standard SMR equilibrium expression. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (refer to Section 3.1 derivation):**

From the derived relation:

$$K_p = \frac{27 x^{4} P^{2}}{(1 - x)(r - x)(1 + r + 2x)^{2}}$$

Substituting r = 3, P = 1 bar, Kp = 4.50:

$$4.50 = \frac{27 x^{4}}{(1 - x)(3 - x)(4 + 2x)^{2}}$$

Iterative solution (1 mark for each correct trial value):

- x = 0.85 → Kp_calc = 1.34
- x = 0.90 → Kp_calc = 2.51
- x = 0.93 → Kp_calc ≈ 4.20
- x = 0.935 → Kp_calc ≈ 4.50 ✓ **(3 marks for iteration table)**

**Equilibrium CH₄ conversion = 93.5 %** (1 mark for answer, 1 mark for stating the Le Chatelier interpretation).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently write only the final value of x without showing the **4th-degree polynomial in x** and the iteration. The polynomial derivation carries **2 marks** itself, and at least 2 trial calculations are mandatory to earn full marks. Do NOT skip the algebraic manipulation from Kp definition to the final polynomial.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the principle of **Sorption-Enhanced Steam Reforming (SESR)** with a neat schematic. Derive the operating temperature advantage over conventional SMR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Concept (2 marks):** SESR uses a CaO-based sorbent co-packed with the reforming catalyst. The sorbent captures CO₂ as it is formed: **CaO + CO₂ → CaCO₃**.

2. **Le Chatelier application (2 marks):** Conventional SMR equilibrium: CH₄ + H₂O ⇌ CO + 3H₂. By continuously removing CO₂, the equilibrium shifts to the right, requiring **less thermal driving force (lower T)** to achieve the same CH₄ conversion.

3. **Temperature advantage (2 marks):** At conventional SMR (no sorbent), achieving X_CH₄ > 0.95 requires T ≈ 850 – 900 °C. With SESR, the same conversion is achieved at **600 – 700 °C** — a **150 – 200 °C reduction**, with the bonus of single-step H₂ purity > 95 %.

4. **Sorbent regeneration (1 mark):** CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ at ~900 °C in a parallel calciner; the CO₂ is sequestration-ready, making SESR **CCS-compatible**.

**(b)** Discuss the **membrane steam reforming (MSR)** concept using a Pd-based membrane. State Sieverts' law and explain how membrane permeation shifts the equilibrium. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Membrane principle (2 marks):** A dense Pd (or Pd–Ag alloy) membrane allows only H₂ to permeate via the **solution–diffusion mechanism**: H₂ adsorbs on the feed side, dissociates into H-atoms, diffuses through the metal lattice, and recombines on the permeate side.

2. **Sieverts' law (3 marks):**

$$J_{H_2} = \frac{P_0}{t} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) \cdot \left(\sqrt{p_f} - \sqrt{p_p}\right)$$

where P₀ is the permeability pre-exponential, t is membrane thickness, Eₐ is the activation energy for diffusion, and p_f, p_p are the feed and permeate H₂ partial pressures.

3. **Equilibrium shift (2 marks):** Continuous removal of H₂ from the reaction side reduces the product partial pressure, shifting the SMR equilibrium to the right. This permits **lower operating T (~550 °C)** and **higher operating P (20 – 40 bar)** with **>99.99 % pure H₂** on the permeate side.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall – Membrane Question:** A very common mistake is writing **Hagen-Poiseuille** equation (which applies to viscous flow through porous media) instead of **Sieverts' law** (which applies to dense metallic membranes). For Pd membranes, **always use Sieverts' law**. Also, do NOT confuse Pd membranes with ceramic (proton-conducting) membranes — the mechanisms are fundamentally different.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Conventional SMR** is endothermic (+206 kJ/mol) and equilibrium-limited; advanced methods attack its **energy, equilibrium, and separation** penalties.
- [x] **ATR** = POX (burner) + SMR (catalyst bed) in one reactor; **thermally neutral**, H₂:CO ≈ 2:1.
- [x] **POX** uses sub-stoichiometric O₂ (λ ≈ 0.25 – 0.4); CH₄ + ½O₂ → CO + 2H₂ (ΔH = –36 kJ/mol).
- [x] **SESR** uses CaO sorbent; reaction CaO + CO₂ → CaCO₃ (ΔH = –178 kJ/mol); operation at **600 – 700 °C** vs. SMR's 800 °C.
- [x] **MSR** uses Pd or Pd–Ag dense membranes; **Sieverts' law** governs flux; achieves **>99.99 % H₂ purity** in a single step.
- [x] Le Chatelier's principle is the **common thread** of all advanced methods — they all **remove a product** (CO₂ or H₂) to shift equilibrium.
- [x] Optimum S/C ratio in advanced reforming is **2.5 – 4.0** (vs. 2.5 – 3.0 for SMR) to suppress coking.
- [x] Equilibrium SMR relation: **Kp = 27 x⁴ P² / [(1 − x)(r − x)(1 + r + 2x)²]** — must be derived, not assumed.
- [x] KTU frequently tests: (i) the **4th-degree polynomial derivation**, (ii) the **ATR vs POX comparison table**, and (iii) **Sieverts' law statement**.
- [x] Industrial markers: **Topsoe & Air Products (ATR)**, **RTI International (SESR)**, **Tokyo Gas (MSR)**.
- [x] Energy synergy — all advanced methods are **CCS-friendly** because CO₂ is either captured *in situ* (SESR) or concentrated in a retentate stream (MSR).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Partial Oxidation Method for Hydrogen Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Partial Oxidation Method for Hydrogen Production ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Partial Oxidation (POX) is a thermochemical process in which a hydrocarbon or carbonaceous fuel is reacted with a sub-stoichiometric quantity of oxygen (or air) such that complete combustion is prevented. The reaction is forced to stop at an intermediate oxidation state, yielding a gaseous mixture predominantly composed of hydrogen (H₂) and carbon monoxide (CO) — commonly termed *Synthesis Gas* or *Syngas*. The hydrogen is then recovered from this mixture through downstream water-gas shift, separation, and purification steps.** For the simplest case (methane feedstock), the principal reaction is: $$\text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + 2\,\text{H}_2$$ with a standard reaction enthalpy of **ΔH°₂₉₈ ≈ −36 kJ/mol (exothermic)**. Compare this with the full combustion value of **−803 kJ/mol**, confirming that POX releases only a small fraction of the total chemical energy as heat. ### 1.2 Generalised Stoichiometric Form For a generic hydrocarbon CₙHₘ, the ideal POX stoichiometry is: $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_m + \tfrac{n}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow n\,\text{CO} + \tfrac{m}{2}\,\text{H}_2$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why "Partial"?** In normal combustion, one mole of CH₄ requires **2 moles of O₂** for complete oxidation (CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O). POX supplies only **0.5 moles of O₂** per mole of CH₄ — exactly **¼ of the stoichiometric oxygen** required for complete combustion. The fuel is "starved" of oxygen, forcing the products to remain in the *reduced* (non-oxide) form. ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > **The Smouldering Campfire Analogy** > Imagine you are trying to heat a kettle using a campfire. If you smother the fire with too much wood and restrict the air supply, the wood does **not** burn completely. Instead of a bright yellow flame producing CO₂ and H₂O, you observe: > - A blue, smoky flame (signalling incomplete combustion → CO formation) > - Vapours of unburnt gases (hydrocarbons + H₂ being released) > - Soot deposition (carbon) > > This is essentially what happens inside a POX reactor. By **restricting oxygen**, we deliberately drive the chemistry toward *incomplete* oxidation. The "smoke and vapours" leaving the reactor are, after cleaning, our **hydrogen-rich syngas**. ### 1.4 Key Industrial Metrics & Constants | Parameter | Typical Range / Value | |---|---| | Operating Temperature (Non-Catalytic POX) | **1200 °C – 1500 °C** | | Operating Temperature (Catalytic POX / CPO) | **700 °C – 900 °C** | | Operating Pressure | **30 bar – 80 bar** | | Oxygen-to-Carbon (O/C) molar ratio | **0.5 – 0.8** | | Steam-to-Carbon (S/C) molar ratio | **0.0 – 1.0** (optional) | | H₂ : CO product ratio | **1.5 : 1 to 2.5 : 1** | | Thermal efficiency (HHV basis) | **70 % – 85 %** | | Cold Gas Efficiency | **75 % – 80 %** | ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Process Chemistry — The Three Reaction Pillars Partial oxidation is **never** a single reaction in practice. Inside any POX reactor, three classes of reactions occur simultaneously, and the engineer must understand them all because they determine the final H₂ yield. #### Pillar 1 — The Primary POX Reaction (Desired) $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_m + \tfrac{n}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow n\,\text{CO} + \tfrac{m}{2}\,\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H < 0 \text{ (mildly exothermic)}$$ For methane: **CH₄ + ½ O₂ → CO + 2 H₂** #### Pillar 2 — Steam Reforming (Endothermic, Often Coupled) If steam is co-fed (commonly done in autothermal reforming, ATR — the hybrid of POX + SMR): $$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \leftrightarrow \text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ #### Pillar 3 — Water–Gas Shift (WGS) — Downstream $$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \leftrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two-Stage Hydrogen Maximisation Strategy** > The gases leaving the POX reactor are routed to a **High-Temperature Shift (HTS) converter (310–450 °C)** followed by a **Low-Temperature Shift (LTS) converter (190–250 °C)**. The HTS handles the bulk CO conversion; the LTS polishes the residual CO down to < 0.3 vol% — critical for fuel-cell-grade hydrogen. ### 2.2 Classification of POX Variants | Variant | Operating Regime | Catalyst | Temperature | Feedstock Flexibility | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Non-Catalytic POX (NCPOX)** | Pure thermal | None | 1200–1500 °C | Heavy oils, coal, residual fractions | | **Catalytic Partial Oxidation (CPO)** | Catalytic | Ni, Rh, Pt, Ir on Al₂O₃/ceramic | 700–900 °C | Natural gas, LPG, light hydrocarbons | | **Autothermal Reforming (ATR)** | Hybrid POX + SMR | Ni on Al₂O₃/CaAl₂O₄ | 900–1050 °C | Natural gas, naphtha, methanol | | **Catalytic POX of Coal** | Slurry-fed | Optional | 1100–1400 °C | Coal-water slurry, petroleum coke | ### 2.3 Process Parameters and Their Influence The engineer controls **four principal knobs** to steer the POX reactor performance: 1. **Oxygen-to-Carbon (O/C) ratio** — Increasing O/C drives equilibrium toward CO₂ + H₂O (over-oxidation). Decreasing it leaves unconverted hydrocarbon. Sweet spot: **0.55–0.75**. 2. **Steam-to-Carbon (S/C) ratio** — Suppresses carbon deposition (coking). Higher S/C → more H₂ via WGS, but penalises reactor energy balance due to steam heating. 3. **Pre-heat Temperature** — Higher feed pre-heat reduces external fuel demand but raises risk of pre-ignition in catalytic systems. 4. **Reactor Pressure** — Higher pressure favours methane formation (Le Chatelier), reducing H₂ yield. Most modern units operate at **30–60 bar**. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is a *ready-reckoner* for KTU 2024 scheme numerical questions. Note the use of `\vert` in place of the pipe character so the markdown table syntax is preserved. | # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ for full combustion of CH₄ | $\text{O}_2^{\text{stoich}} = 2 \text{ mol/mol CH}_4$ | Reference value | | 2 | O₂ supplied in POX | $\text{O}_2^{\text{POX}} = 0.5 \text{ mol/mol CH}_4$ | Exactly ¼ of stoich | | 3 | Theoretical H₂ yield from POX of CH₄ | $\text{Y}_{H_2} = 2 \text{ mol/mol CH}_4$ | Equivalent to 50.0 g H₂ / kg CH₄ | | 4 | Syngas ratio (H₂:CO) for pure POX of CH₄ | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{n_{H_2}}{n_{CO}} = \dfrac{2}{1} = 2$ | — | | 5 | Energy released by POX reaction | $\Delta H_{\text{POX}} = -36 \text{ kJ/mol CH}_4$ | Compared with −803 for combustion | | 6 | Cold Gas Efficiency | $\eta_{CG} = \dfrac{\text{LHV of product H}_2}{\text{LHV of feed}} \times 100\%$ | % | | 7 | Thermal Efficiency (HHV basis) | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\text{HHV of H}_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{HHV of feed} + Q_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | % | | 8 | Equilibrium constant for WGS | $K_{WGS} = \dfrac{[CO_2][H_2]}{[CO][H_2O]}$ | Temperature dependent | | 9 | Carbon Boundary (coking limit, SMR) | $\dfrac{S}{C} \geq \dfrac{K_c}{K_e} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P^{0}} - \dfrac{[CO_2]}{[H_2]} - 1$ | Stoichiometric criterion | | 10 | LHV of H₂ | $\text{LHV}_{H_2} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg} = 242 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Reference constant | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Why is POX so important to the energy transition?** > - **Feedstock Agnostic** — The same reactor concept can convert natural gas, naphtha, heavy oil, coal, biomass, and even petroleum coke into H₂. This makes POX the *backbone* of "blue hydrogen" production when coupled with **Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS)**. > - **Black Liquor Recovery** — In the paper-and-pulp industry, **CEWAR / CHEMREC** type POX units gasify black liquor to recover both H₂ and process heat. > - **Synfuel Production** — CO and H₂ from POX are the building blocks for **Fischer–Tropsch diesel, methanol, and ammonia** — industries worth **>$300 billion globally**. > - **Hydrogen Refineries** — Major oil & gas majors (Shell, Air Products, Linde) operate POX-based **Hydrogen Production Units (HPUs)** at scale: **>10,000 Nm³/h H₂** per train. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Hydrogen Yield from POX of Pure Methane **Given:** - Feedstock: 1 kmol of pure methane (CH₄) at 25 °C, 1 atm. - Reactant: 0.5 kmol of pure O₂ (stoichiometric POX). - Reaction: CH₄ + ½ O₂ → CO + 2 H₂ **To Find:** Mass of H₂ produced, hydrogen yield per kg of CH₄, and the HHV energy content of the H₂ produced. --- **Step 1 — Write the balanced chemical equation with moles.** $$\text{1 kmol CH}_4 + \text{0.5 kmol O}_2 \rightarrow \text{1 kmol CO} + \text{2 kmol H}_2$$ **Step 2 — Calculate molar masses.** $$\begin{aligned} M_{\text{CH}_4} &= (1 \times 12) + (4 \times 1) = 16 \text{ kg/kmol} \\ M_{\text{H}_2} &= 2 \times 1 = 2 \text{ kg/kmol} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Determine mass of H₂ produced per kmol of CH₄.** $$m_{\text{H}_2} = n_{H_2} \times M_{\text{H}_2} = 2 \text{ kmol} \times 2 \text{ kg/kmol} = 4 \text{ kg H}_2$$ **Step 4 — Determine hydrogen yield per kg of CH₄.** $$Y_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{m_{\text{H}_2}}{m_{\text{CH}_4}} = \dfrac{4 \text{ kg H}_2}{1 \text{ kmol} \times 16 \text{ kg/kmol}} = \dfrac{4}{16} = 0.250 \text{ kg H}_2/\text{kg CH}_4$$ Or equivalently: **Y_H₂ = 250 g H₂ per kg CH₄.** **Step 5 — Convert yield to % of theoretical maximum.** The theoretical maximum H₂ from CH₄ (if all H atoms were released) corresponds to: $$\text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{C(s)} + 2\,\text{H}_2$$ which is the same 2 mol H₂ / mol CH₄ = 0.250 kg/kg. Hence POX achieves the **stoichiometric maximum H₂ yield**, unlike steam reforming which is equilibrium-limited. $$\text{H}_2 \text{ yield efficiency} = \dfrac{0.250}{0.250} \times 100\% = 100\% \text{ (stoichiometric ceiling)}$$ **Step 6 — Compute HHV of the H₂ stream produced.** $$\text{HHV}_{H_2} = 142 \text{ MJ/kg (Higher Heating Value of H}_2\text{)}$$ $$E_{\text{recovered}} = 4 \text{ kg} \times 142 \text{ MJ/kg} = 568 \text{ MJ per kmol CH}_4$$ **Step 7 — Compute Cold Gas Efficiency.** The LHV of methane is **50 MJ/kg**, so for 16 kg CH₄: $$E_{\text{feed}} = 16 \text{ kg} \times 50 \text{ MJ/kg} = 800 \text{ MJ}$$ $$\eta_{CG} = \dfrac{568}{800} \times 100\% = 71.0\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation** — The maximum theoretical cold gas efficiency of POX of methane (when measured against the LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg) is: > > $$\eta_{CG,\max} = \dfrac{4 \times 120}{800} \times 100\% = 60.0\%$$ > > The "missing" energy is partly retained as chemical energy in the CO (which can be water-gas-shifted to recover more H₂) and partly lost as sensible heat in the hot syngas (typically recovered in a waste-heat boiler). --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Oxygen-to-Carbon (O/C) Ratio and Stoichiometric Boundary **Given:** A general hydrocarbon CₙHₘ undergoing POX. **Step 1 — Identify the O₂ demand for POX (sub-stoichiometric).** From the generalised reaction: $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_m + \tfrac{n}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow n\,\text{CO} + \tfrac{m}{2}\,\text{H}_2$$ $$\therefore \text{O}_2 \text{ for POX} = \tfrac{n}{2} \text{ mol per mol of fuel}$$ **Step 2 — Identify O₂ demand for complete combustion.** $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_m + \left(n + \tfrac{m}{4}\right)\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow n\,\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{m}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 3 — Form the O/C ratio for POX.** $$\left(\dfrac{O}{C}\right)_{\text{POX}} = \dfrac{2 \times \tfrac{n}{2}}{n} = 1$$ But the relevant operational ratio (often called the **equivalence ratio φ**) is: $$\phi = \dfrac{\text{(O}_2\text{) supplied}}{\text{(O}_2\text{) for complete combustion}} = \dfrac{n/2}{n + m/4}$$ For methane (n=1, m=4): $$\phi_{\text{CH}_4} = \dfrac{0.5}{1 + 1} = \dfrac{0.5}{2.0} = 0.25$$ **Step 4 — Conclude on the meaning of φ.** - **φ = 1.0** → Stoichiometric (complete combustion → CO₂ + H₂O) - **φ < 1** → Fuel-rich (sub-stoichiometric, syngas-forming) → **POX regime** - **φ = 0.25** → Ideal POX of methane - **φ → 0** → Pure pyrolysis (no oxygen) --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT) of POX of Methane **Given:** Reactants enter at 25 °C; reaction is adiabatic; products are CO, H₂, and unconverted CH₄ (incomplete POX). **Energy Balance:** $$\sum n_i h_i^{\text{reactants}} = \sum n_j h_j^{\text{products}}$$ For POX of methane (per mol CH₄) with stoichiometric O/C: $$H_{\text{reactants}} = h_{\text{CH}_4}^{298} + 0.5 \, h_{\text{O}_2}^{298} + 0 \, h_{\text{N}_2}^{298}$$ $$H_{\text{products}} = h_{\text{CO}}^{T} + 2 \, h_{\text{H}_2}^{T} + \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\text{POX}}$$ **Step 1 — Use formation enthalpies.** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{CH}_4, g) &= -74.85 \text{ kJ/mol} \\ \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{CO}, g) &= -110.5 \text{ kJ/mol} \\ \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2, g) &= 0 \text{ kJ/mol (reference)} \\ \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{O}_2, g) &= 0 \text{ kJ/mol (reference)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Compute ΔH°rxn.** $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{\text{rxn}} = \left[(-110.5) + 2(0)\right] - \left[(-74.85) + 0.5(0)\right] = -110.5 + 74.85 = -35.65 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Step 3 — Set up the energy balance.** $$\sum n_j \cdot \int_{298}^{T_{\text{ad}}} C_{p,j}(T) \, dT = -\Delta H^{\circ}_{\text{rxn}}$$ Using mean molar heat capacities (approximate): $$\begin{aligned} \overline{C}_{p,\text{CO}} &\approx 29.3 \text{ J/(mol·K)} \\ \overline{C}_{p,\text{H}_2} &\approx 29.0 \text{ J/(mol·K)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for T_ad.** $$\begin{aligned} (1)(29.3) + (2)(29.0) \, \Delta T &= 35650 \\ 87.3 \cdot \Delta T &= 35650 \\ \Delta T &= 408.4 \text{ K} \\ T_{\text{ad}} &\approx 298 + 408 = 706 \text{ K} \approx 433\,^{\circ}\text{C} \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Important Caveat:** This derivation used *constant* $C_p$ and ignored product dissociation (CO → C + ½ O₂ and H₂ → 2 H), which becomes significant above ~1200 °C. A rigorous computation including equilibrium dissociation gives a **T_ad ≈ 1200 – 1300 °C** for stoichiometric POX of methane at atmospheric pressure — the well-known value cited in textbooks. The simplified calculation above is for educational purposes only and demonstrates the **method**, not the actual operating temperature. --- ### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: POX vs. Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | Parameter | **POX (Partial Oxidation)** | **SMR (Steam Methane Reforming)** | |---|---|---| | Primary Reaction | $\text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + 2\,\text{H}_2$ | $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2$ | | ΔH°rxn | **−36 kJ/mol (exothermic)** | **+206 kJ/mol (endothermic)** | | Energy Source | Internal (chemical, from O₂) | External (fired heater, ~850 °C) | | H₂:CO ratio | **2 : 1** (lower) | **3 : 1** (higher) | | H₂ yield per mol CH₄ | 2 mol | 3 mol (theoretically, equilibrium-limited to ~2.4) | | Reactor residence time | < 1 second (CPO), ~1–5 s (NCPOX) | ~10–30 seconds | | Capital cost | Higher (oxygen plant + refractory) | Lower (uses air indirectly) | | Operating cost | Lower (no fired heater) | Higher (external firing fuel) | | CO₂ emissions | Lower per mol H₂ (some O₂ from air separation plant) | Higher (combustion of fuel gas) | | Best feedstock | Heavy oil, coal, biomass | Natural gas, light hydrocarbons | | Catalyst requirement | Optional (Ni, Rh for CPO) | Mandatory (Ni/Al₂O₃) | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Overall POX-Based Hydrogen Production Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hydrocarbon Feedstock

[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2] (3 Marks)** **Define the partial oxidation method for hydrogen production. Write the stoichiometric equation for the partial oxidation of methane and comment on the syngas ratio obtained.** **Model Answer:** Partial Oxidation (POX) is a thermochemical process in which a hydrocarbon fuel is reacted with a sub-stoichiometric quantity of pure oxygen (typically ¼ of the stoichiometric requirement for complete combustion) so that the products are CO and H₂ instead of CO₂ and H₂O. For methane: $$\text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + 2\,\text{H}_2 \quad (\Delta H = -36 \text{ kJ/mol})$$ The **H₂ : CO molar ratio** in the syngas is **2 : 1**, which is characteristic of pure POX. This is lower than the 3 : 1 ratio produced by steam methane reforming (SMR) because POX supplies less hydrogen per carbon atom (no steam contribution). The ratio can be increased downstream by the water-gas shift reaction. **[Marks Distribution: 1 — Definition | 1 — Equation | 1 — Syngas ratio comment]** --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2] (3 Marks)** **List any three major differences between catalytic partial oxidation (CPO) and non-catalytic partial oxidation (NCPOX) of hydrocarbons.** **Model Answer:** | Sl. No. | Feature | CPO | NCPOX | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Temperature | 700 – 900 °C | 1200 – 1500 °C | | 2 | Catalyst | Mandatory (Ni, Rh, Pt, Ir) | None required | | 3 | Residence time | < 1 second | 1 – 5 seconds | | 4 | Reactor material | Metallic (lower temp) | Refractory-lined (high temp) | | 5 | Feedstock | Light HCs (CH₄, LPG) | Heavy oils, coal, biomass | **Distinguishing Factors:** 1. **Temperature:** CPO operates at much lower temperatures (~800 °C) due to catalyst activity, while NCPOX relies purely on thermal cracking at 1300+ °C. 2. **Catalyst:** CPO requires precious-metal or nickel catalysts supported on ceramic monoliths; NCPOX is purely thermal. 3. **Reactor Lining:** NCPOX reactors must be lined with high-grade refractory (e.g., 90 % Al₂O₃) to withstand extreme temperatures; CPO reactors can be metallic. **[Marks Distribution: 1 — Table/comparison points | 1 — Temperature distinction | 1 — Catalyst distinction]** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Style with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Module 2]` **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **Partial Oxidation (POX) process for hydrogen production from methane**.
