Discuss the role of the **water-gas shift reactor** and **pressure swing adsorption** in the overall scheme. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A POX reactor processes **1000 Nm³/h of methane (CH₄)** at standard conditions (0 °C, 1 atm). Calculate the **(i)** stoichiometric oxygen requirement, **(ii)** theoretical hydrogen output in Nm³/h, and **(iii)** thermal energy released per hour. **(7 Marks)** --- **Solution (a):** **Block Diagram Description:** The POX-based hydrogen production plant consists of the following units in series: 1. **Feed System** — Methane from battery limits is pre-heated to ~400 °C in a feed pre-heater. 2. **Oxygen Supply** — An adjacent **Air Separation Unit (ASU)** supplies 99.5 % pure O₂ at ~30 bar via a cryogenic distillation column. 3. **POX Reactor** — Methane and O₂ are mixed in stoichiometric POX ratio (CH₄ : O₂ = 2 : 1) and combusted at 1200 – 1500 °C in a refractory-lined reactor. 4. **Waste Heat Boiler (WHB)** — Hot syngas (CO + 2H₂) at ~1300 °C is rapidly cooled to ~350 °C, generating **High Pressure (HP) steam** at 100 bar. 5. **Particulate/Sulphur Removal** — Sulphided guard beds (ZnO + activated carbon) remove H₂S and particulates. 6. **HT Shift Converter** — Operates at 350 – 450 °C over Fe₂O₃–Cr₂O₃ catalyst. CO conversion ~80–90 %. 7. **LT Shift Converter** — Operates at 190 – 250 °C over CuO–ZnO–Al₂O₃ catalyst. Residual CO reduced to < 0.3 vol%. 8. **CO₂ Removal** — Activated MDEA (aMDEA) or Benfield hot carbonate solution scrubs CO₂ from the shifted gas. 9. **PSA Unit** — Final H₂ polishing to **99.99 % purity**; tail gas recycled to POX as fuel. **Role of WGS Reactor:** Converts CO into additional H₂ via: $$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$$ This is essential because POX alone yields only 2 mol H₂/mol CH₄; the WGS reaction increases this by *up to* 1 additional mol per original mol of CO (so 3 mol total/mol CH₄) — the same stoichiometric ceiling as SMR. **Role of PSA:** Removes the final traces of CO, CO₂, CH₄, N₂ from the H₂ stream using **adsorption-desorption on zeolite 5A beds at 20–30 bar**. Product purity: 99.99 vol% H₂. **(Marks: 1 — Block diagram | 2 — Unit operations | 2 — WGS role | 2 — PSA role)** --- **Solution (b):** **Given:** Volumetric flow rate of CH₄, $\dot{V}_{\text{CH}_4} = 1000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$ at STP. **Step 1 — Compute molar flow rate of CH₄.** At STP (0 °C, 1 atm), 1 kmol of any ideal gas occupies **22.414 Nm³**. $$\dot{n}_{\text{CH}_4} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}}{22.414 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{kmol}} = 44.615 \text{ kmol/h}$$ **Step 2 — (i) Stoichiometric O₂ requirement.** From POX: $\text{CH}_4 : \text{O}_2 = 1 : 0.5$ $$\dot{n}_{\text{O}_2} = 0.5 \times \dot{n}_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.5 \times 44.615 = 22.308 \text{ kmol/h}$$ In volumetric terms: $$\dot{V}_{\text{O}_2} = 22.308 \times 22.414 = 500.0 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$$ **Step 3 — (ii) Theoretical H₂ output.** From POX: 1 mol CH₄ → 2 mol H₂. $$\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2} = 2 \times \dot{n}_{\text{CH}_4} = 2 \times 44.615 = 89.230 \text{ kmol/h}$$ $$\dot{V}_{\text{H}_2} = 89.230 \times 22.414 = 2000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$$ **Step 4 — (iii) Thermal energy released per hour.** $$\dot{Q} = \dot{n}_{\text{CH}_4} \times \vert \Delta H_{\text{POX}} \vert = 44.615 \text{ kmol/h} \times 35{,}650 \text{ kJ/kmol}$$ $$\dot{Q} = 1{,}590{,}520 \text{ kJ/h} = \boxed{441.8 \text{ kW}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students commonly use the **full combustion enthalpy (−803 kJ/mol)** instead of the **POX enthalpy (−36 kJ/mol)**. This is the single most common mistake — always check the reaction stoichiometry before plugging in numbers. > > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Using **24.04 Nm³/kmol** (which corresponds to 25 °C, 1 atm — the older "normal" conditions) instead of the standard **22.414 Nm³/kmol** (0 °C, 1 atm — STP). Read the question carefully. **(Marks Distribution (b): 2 — Step 1 | 2 — Step 2 | 1 — Step 3 | 2 — Step 4 with proper unit consistency)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style, Module 2]` **(a)** With the help of a **P–V or T–S diagram**, explain the **thermodynamic basis** for the partial oxidation reaction. Why is partial oxidation mildly exothermic while complete combustion is strongly exothermic? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A POX reactor operates with an **O₂-to-CH₄ molar feed ratio of 0.55** (slightly higher than stoichiometric POX ratio of 0.50). Assuming the dominant products are CO, H₂, CO₂, and H₂O (and no unconverted CH₄), determine by **atom balance** the equilibrium product composition on a **dry basis**. **(7 Marks)** --- **Solution (a):** **Thermodynamic Basis:** The complete combustion of methane: $$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)} \quad \Delta H = -803 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ releases the **maximum** possible chemical energy because the carbon is fully oxidised to its highest oxidation state (+4) and hydrogen to its highest (+1). In partial oxidation: $$\text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + 2\,\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -36 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ Carbon is **only partially oxidised** to CO (oxidation state +2). Each carbon atom retains **two units of oxidation energy** in the CO bond. This energy is *not* released as heat during the POX reaction but is **retained as chemical potential** — to be released later when the CO is shifted (WGS) or combusted downstream. **Difference calculation:** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta H_{\text{CO}} &= \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{CO}) = -110.5 \text{ kJ/mol} \\ \Delta H_{\text{CO}_2} &= \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{CO}_2) = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol} \end{aligned}$$ The **additional oxidation** of CO to CO₂ would release: $$\Delta H = \Delta H_f(\text{CO}_2) - \Delta H_f(\text{CO}) = -393.5 - (-110.5) = -283 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ This is precisely the "missing" exothermicity of POX compared to full combustion (−803 + 36 = −767 ≈ −803 + 283). **T–S Diagram Description:** On a T–S diagram, the POX reaction traces a path from reactants at ambient T (298 K) along a near-adiabatic line to a much higher product temperature (T_ad ≈ 1300 °C) but with **less entropy generation** than complete combustion, because the products (CO + H₂) retain significant chemical potential. **(Marks: 2 — Reaction comparison | 2 — Oxidation state analysis | 1 — Calculation of difference | 2 — T–S interpretation)** --- **Solution (b):** **Given:** O₂ / CH₄ feed ratio = 0.55. Per **1 kmol CH₄** fed: **0.55 kmol O₂** fed. **Step 1 — Define product variables (per kmol CH₄).** Let the products (after WGS-equilibrium at high T) be: - a kmol CO - b kmol H₂ - c kmol CO₂ - d kmol H₂O - (no CH₄, no O₂, no soot — per assumption) **Step 2 — Carbon atom balance.** $$a + c = 1$$ **Step 3 — Hydrogen atom balance.** $$2b + 2d = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b + d = 2$$ **Step 4 — Oxygen atom balance.** $$a + 2c + d = 2 \times 0.55 = 1.10$$ **Step 5 — Solve the system.** From Step 2: $b = 2 - d$. Substituting Step 1 ($a = 1 - c$) into Step 4: $$(1 - c) + 2c + d = 1.10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 + c + d = 1.10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c + d = 0.10$$ So $c = 0.10 - d$ and $a = 1 - c = 0.90 + d$. The actual values of a, b, c, d require closure via the **WGS equilibrium** $K_{WGS}(T)$: $$K_{WGS} = \dfrac{(c)(b)}{(a)(d)}$$ For a typical POX exit temperature of ~1200 °C (1473 K), $K_{WGS} \approx 1.0$ (approximately). Setting $K_{WGS} = 1$: $$\dfrac{(0.10 - d)(2 - d)}{(0.90 + d)(d)} = 1$$ Expanding: $$(0.10 - d)(2 - d) = (0.90 + d)(d)$$ $$0.20 - 0.10d - 2d + d^2 = 0.90d + d^2$$ $$0.20 - 2.10d = 0.90d$$ $$0.20 = 3.00d$$ $$d = 0.0667 \text{ kmol H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 6 — Back-substitute to find all species.** $$\begin{aligned} c &= 0.10 - 0.0667 = 0.0333 \text{ kmol CO}_2 \\ a &= 0.90 + 0.0667 = 0.9667 \text{ kmol CO} \\ b &= 2 - 0.0667 = 1.9333 \text{ kmol H}_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7 — Express on dry basis (exclude H₂O).** Total dry moles = $a + b + c = 0.9667 + 1.9333 + 0.0333 = 2.9333$ kmol. $$\begin{aligned} y_{\text{CO}} &= \dfrac{0.9667}{2.9333} = 0.3296 = 32.96 \text{ mol\%} \\ y_{\text{H}_2} &= \dfrac{1.9333}{2.9333} = 0.6591 = 65.91 \text{ mol\%} \\ y_{\text{CO}_2} &= \dfrac{0.0333}{2.9333} = 0.0114 = 1.14 \text{ mol\%} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Dry Composition:** **H₂ ≈ 65.9 mol% | CO ≈ 33.0 mol% | CO₂ ≈ 1.1 mol%** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** Students often forget to convert to **dry basis** when the question asks for it. The wet composition would include the H₂O mole fraction, which is excluded from PSA feed calculations. **Always strip H₂O before reporting dry syngas composition.** **(Marks: 1 — Setup variables | 1 — C balance | 1 — H balance | 1 — O balance | 2 — WGS closure + algebra | 1 — Dry basis conversion)** --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** - ✅ **POX equation for methane:** $\text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + 2\,\text{H}_2$; **ΔH = −36 kJ/mol** (exothermic). - ✅ **Syngas ratio H₂:CO = 2:1** for pure POX (vs. 3:1 for SMR). - ✅ **Operating temperatures:** CPO = 700–900 °C; NCPOX = 1200–1500 °C. - ✅ **Operating pressure:** 30–80 bar typical for modern plants. - ✅ **O/C ratio sweet spot:** 0.55–0.75 (φ ≈ 0.25 for stoichiometric POX of CH₄). - ✅ **Process sequence:** POX reactor → WHB → HT Shift → LT Shift → CO₂ removal (aMDEA) → PSA → 99.99 % H₂. - ✅ **Catalysts used in CPO:** Ni, Rh, Pt, Ir on Al₂O₃ or ceramic monoliths. - ✅ **Mass yield of H₂:** 0.250 kg H₂ / kg CH₄ (theoretical maximum, equal to 100 % stoichiometric ceiling). - ✅ **Hydrogen yield enhancement:** WGS reaction can lift total H₂ from 2 mol → up to 3 mol per mol CH₄. - ✅ **NCPOX uses refractory lining** (90 %+ Al₂O₃); CPO reactors can be metallic. - ✅ **Pure POX releases only 36/803 ≈ 4.5 %** of the full-combustion enthalpy as heat — the rest is retained as **chemical energy in CO + H₂**. - ✅ **Atom balance is the KTU-favoured problem type:** always write 3 equations (C, H, O balance) and close with WGS equilibrium. - ✅ **Final H₂ purity:** 99.99 vol% achievable only via PSA, not WGS alone. - ✅ **Pitfalls to avoid:** 1. Confusing POX ΔH (−36 kJ/mol) with combustion ΔH (−803 kJ/mol). 2. Using 24.04 Nm³/kmol instead of 22.414 Nm³/kmol. 3. Forgetting to report results on **dry basis** when H₂O is in the product stream. 4. Coking — always check the carbon-boundary condition; ensure **S/C ≥ 0.5** if steam is co-fed.

topics.title: Electrochemical - Electrolysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Electrochemical - Electrolysis # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Electrolysis** is an electrochemical process in which an external electrical voltage (driving force) is applied across two inert electrodes immersed in an ion-conducting electrolyte to drive a non-spontaneous chemical reaction. In the context of hydrogen energy, **water electrolysis** specifically refers to the splitting of liquid water ($H_2O$) or water vapor into its constituent gases — hydrogen ($H_2$) and oxygen ($O_2$) — using electrical energy, governed quantitatively by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**. The overall stoichiometric reaction is: $$H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow H_{2(g)} + \frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT862, Module 2):** Electrolysis is one of the four pillars of "green hydrogen" production pathways. It is the *only* method that yields **zero-carbon hydrogen** when coupled with renewable electricity (solar PV, wind, hydro). ### 1.1.1 The Three Industrial Classes of Water Electrolyzers | Type | Electrolyte | Operating Temperature | Ion Migrating | Commercial Maturity | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Alkaline Electrolyzer (AEL/AEC)** | Liquid KOH / NaOH (20–30 wt%) | **60 °C – 90 °C** | $OH^-$ | Mature (Commercial) | | **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM)** | Solid polymer (Nafion™) | **50 °C – 80 °C** | $H^+$ | Commercial (Growing) | | **Solid Oxide Electrolyzer (SOEC)** | Ceramic (YSZ) | **700 °C – 850 °C** | $O^{2-}$ | Pilot / Demonstration | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Building > [!NOTE] > **"Lifting Water Uphill" Analogy** > Imagine a waterfall where water naturally flows from a high pond to a low pond — that is a *spontaneous* process releasing energy (analogous to a fuel cell). **Electrolysis is the reverse**: we use an electric water pump to push water *back uphill* to the high pond, *storing* the energy as gravitational potential energy. In water electrolysis, we use a DC electrical "pump" (the power supply) to push the water-splitting reaction backwards, storing energy in the chemical bonds of $H_2$ and $O_2$. ### 1.2.1 Half-Reactions at a Glance (Acidic / PEM Perspective) At the **Cathode** (negative electrode, reduction): $$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_{2(g)} \quad E^0 = 0.00 \, V \, \text{vs. SHE}$$ At the **Anode** (positive electrode, oxidation): $$H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} + 2H^+ + 2e^- \quad E^0 = +1.23 \, V \, \text{vs. SHE}$$ At the **Cathode** (Alkaline perspective): $$2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_{2(g)} + 2OH^-$$ At the **Anode** (Alkaline perspective): $$2OH^- \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} + H_2O + 2e^-$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constants You Must Memorize (Board Examination):** > - Faraday's Constant: **F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹ ≈ 96,500 C mol⁻¹** > - Universal Gas Constant: **R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** > - Molar Volume at STP: **22.414 L mol⁻¹** > - Standard Thermoneutral Voltage: **E_th = 1.481 V** (higher heating value, liquid water) > - Standard Reversible Voltage: **E_rev = 1.229 V** (at 25 °C, 1 atm) > - Equivalent weight of Hydrogen: **1.008 g/eq** --- ## 1.3 Real-World Engineering Significance Electrolysis is the **cornerstone technology** for the emerging "**Hydrogen Economy**" and "**Power-to-Gas (P2G)**" concept. It enables: - **Grid balancing**: absorbing excess renewable electricity as $H_2$ storage. - **Decarbonization of industry**: replacing Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) in ammonia, refineries, and steel. - **Heavy mobility**: $H_2$ refueling for fuel-cell electric vehicles (FCEVs). - **Energy security**: long-duration, long-distance energy carrier (replacing LNG/CNG). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of an Electrolyzer Cell An electrochemical cell is governed by three fundamental physical constraints. Understanding these is essential to scoring in the 14-mark KTU ESE questions. ### Step 1 — The Thermodynamic Minimum (Reversible Voltage) A minimum theoretical voltage is required to split water. This is derived from the Gibbs free energy change of the reaction. At standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm): $$\Delta G^0 = +237.2 \, kJ/mol$$ $$E_{rev} = \frac{\Delta G^0}{nF} = \frac{237{,}200}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.229 \, V$$ If the reaction absorbs **heat** (i.e., the cell is run at elevated temperature using waste heat), the voltage can be **reduced** by **TΔS/nF**, known as the Thermal Neutral Voltage: $$E_{th} = \frac{\Delta H^0}{nF} = \frac{285.8 \times 1000}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.481 \, V$$ ### Step 2 — The Activation Overpotential ($\eta_{act}$) Electrode kinetics are sluggish. Energy is wasted to overcome the **activation barrier** of the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), which is the rate-limiting step. This is modeled by the **Butler–Volmer equation**: $$\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$ where $\alpha$ is the charge-transfer coefficient (0.5 typical), $i_0$ is the exchange current density (A/m²), and $i$ is the operating current density. ### Step 3 — The Ohmic Overpotential ($\eta_{ohm}$) Resistive losses arise from: - Ionic resistance of the electrolyte (membrane or liquid KOH). - Electronic resistance of electrodes and bipolar plates. - Contact resistance at interfaces. $$\eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{cell} = i \cdot \sum_{k} \frac{t_k}{\sigma_k}$$ where $t_k$ is the thickness and $\sigma_k$ is the conductivity of each layer. ### Step 4 — The Concentration Overpotential ($\eta_{conc}$) Mass-transport limitations: bubbles of $H_2$ and $O_2$ block active sites, and reactant depletion at the electrode surface. This becomes dominant at **high current densities** (> 1 A/cm²). --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{rev} = \frac{\Delta G}{nF}$ | Reversible (minimum) cell voltage | V | | 2 | $E_{th} = \frac{\Delta H}{nF}$ | Thermoneutral voltage (total energy) | V | | 3 | $E_{cell} = E_{rev} + \eta_{act} + \eta_{ohm} + \eta_{conc}$ | Actual operating cell voltage | V | | 4 | $V_{cell} = E_0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{a_{H_2O}}\right)$ | Nernst equation for water splitting | V | | 5 | $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's First Law (mass deposited/produced) | kg | | 6 | $\frac{m_1}{m_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2}$ | Faraday's Second Law (equivalent weights) | ratio | | 7 | $V_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot V_m}{n \cdot F}$ | Volume of $H_2$ gas produced at STP | m³ | | 8 | $\eta_F = \frac{I_{actual}}{I_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$ | Faradaic (current) efficiency | % | | 9 | $\eta_{voltage} = \frac{E_{th}}{V_{cell}} \times 100\%$ | Voltage efficiency | % | | 10 | $\eta_{system} = \eta_F \times \eta_{voltage} \times \eta_{power}$ | Overall electrolyzer system efficiency | % | | 11 | $\text{SEC} = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot n \cdot F}{M_{H_2}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | kWh/kg $H_2$ | | 12 | $\text{HHV-based min. SEC} = \frac{39.4 \, kWh}{1 \, kg \, H_2}$ | Theoretical minimum (HHV basis) | kWh/kg | > [!NOTE] > **Symbol Reminder for the Examiner**: In any answer script, **always** use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value symbols or conditions inside equations — for example, $\vert \eta \vert$ — to prevent the markdown table from breaking. --- ## 2.3 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering Practice - **Equation 1–2** sets the *theoretical floor*. Any commercial electrolyzer must operate between 1.23 V (ideal, no heat) and 1.48 V (using heat input to cover entropy). - **Equation 3** is the designer's master equation. Reducing $E_{cell}$ directly reduces electricity cost (the dominant OPEX). - **Equation 11 (SEC)** is the **industry-standard benchmark**. Modern PEM systems target **< 48 kWh/kg**, alkaline systems **< 50 kWh/kg**, and SOEC systems **< 35 kWh/kg** (with heat input). - **Equation 12** defines the **physical impossibility threshold**: you cannot get below 39.4 kWh/kg $H_2$ on a Higher Heating Value basis at 25 °C. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reversible Cell Voltage from First Principles of Thermodynamics > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip**: This derivation is the **most frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU ESE for Module 2. Memorize every step. **Step 1: The Fundamental Thermodynamic Relationship** For any electrochemical reaction at constant temperature and pressure, the maximum non-PV (useful) work obtainable is the change in Gibbs free energy: $$W_{max,electrical} = -\Delta G$$ For an electrolyzer (non-spontaneous), we supply work: $\Delta G > 0$. **Step 2: Relating Gibbs Free Energy to Cell Voltage** Electrical work done in moving charge $Q$ across a potential difference $E$ is: $$W_{electrical} = Q \cdot E = n \cdot F \cdot E$$ where $n$ is the number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction. **Step 3: Equate and Solve for E** At equilibrium (reversible, no current): $$n \cdot F \cdot E_{rev} = \Delta G$$ $$\boxed{E_{rev} = \frac{\Delta G}{nF}}$$ **Step 4: Numerical Insertion for Water Splitting at 25 °C** Standard Gibbs free energy of formation of liquid water: $\Delta G^0_{f,H_2O(l)} = -237.2 \, kJ/mol$ For the reaction $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$: $\Delta G^0 = -(-237.2) = +237.2 \, kJ/mol$ Substituting (n = 2 electrons for $H_2$): $$\begin{aligned} E^0_{rev} &= \frac{237{,}200 \, J/mol}{2 \times 96{,}485 \, C/mol} \\ &= \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970} \\ &= 1.229 \, V \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: The Thermoneutral Voltage** Using the standard enthalpy of formation $\Delta H^0_{f,H_2O(l)} = -285.83 \, kJ/mol$: $$\begin{aligned} E_{th} &= \frac{\Delta H^0}{nF} \\ &= \frac{285{,}830}{2 \times 96{,}485} \\ &= 1.481 \, V \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6: Why Two Voltages? — The ΔG vs ΔH Distinction** At constant T and P, the First Law gives: $\Delta H = \Delta G + T\Delta S$ For water splitting at 25 °C, $\Delta S = +163.1 \, J/(mol \cdot K)$, so: $$\begin{aligned} T\Delta S &= 298.15 \times 163.1 \\ &= 48{,}634 \, J/mol \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} E_{th} - E_{rev} &= \frac{T\Delta S}{nF} \\ &= \frac{48{,}634}{192{,}970} \\ &= 0.252 \, V \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation**: $E_{th}$ is the **total** energy demand (electricity + heat). $E_{rev}$ is the **purely electrical** energy demand. The difference $T\Delta S/nF = 0.252 \, V$ must be supplied as **heat** from the surroundings. **High-temperature electrolysis (SOEC) recovers this as a voltage reduction.** --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Faraday's Law Applied to Hydrogen Yield **Step 1: Faraday's First Law (Statement)** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the charge passed: $$m \propto Q = I \cdot t$$ **Step 2: Introduce the Electrochemical Equivalent (Z)** The constant of proportionality is the **electrochemical equivalent**: $$m = Z \cdot I \cdot t = \frac{M}{nF} \cdot I \cdot t$$ **Step 3: Apply to Hydrogen Production** - Molar mass of $H_2$: $M = 2.016 \, g/mol$ - Electrons per mole of $H_2$: $n = 2$ - $F = 96{,}485 \, C/mol$ Electrochemical equivalent of $H_2$: $$Z_{H_2} = \frac{2.016}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.044 \times 10^{-5} \, g/C$$ **Step 4: Hydrogen Volume from Ideal Gas Law** At STP (T = 273.15 K, P = 1 atm), $V_m = 22{,}414 \, ml/mol$: $$V_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot V_m}{n \cdot F} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot 22{,}414}{2 \times 96{,}485} \, ml$$ **Step 5: Production Rate (kg/day)** Let $I = 1000 \, A$ for one hour ($t = 3600 \, s$): $$\begin{aligned} m_{H_2} &= \frac{2.016 \times 1000 \times 3600}{2 \times 96{,}485} \\ &= \frac{7{,}257{,}600}{192{,}970} \\ &= 37.61 \, g \end{aligned}$$ Per day (24 hours): $37.61 \times 24 = 902.7 \, g \approx 0.903 \, kg/day$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) The energy required to produce 1 kg of $H_2$ at a given cell voltage is: $$SEC = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{m_{H_2}}$$ Using Faraday's expression for $m_{H_2} = (M \cdot I \cdot t)/(nF)$: $$\begin{aligned} SEC &= \frac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{\frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF}} \\ &= \frac{V_{cell} \cdot n \cdot F}{M_{H_2}} \end{aligned}$$ Substituting the theoretical thermoneutral voltage (1.481 V): $$\begin{aligned} SEC_{th} &= \frac{1.481 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} \, \frac{J}{kg} \\ &= \frac{285{,}828}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} \, \frac{J}{kg} \\ &= 141.78 \times 10^6 \, J/kg \\ &= 39.39 \, kWh/kg \end{aligned}$$ This is the **HHV-based theoretical minimum** of **39.4 kWh/kg $H_2$**, confirming the formula in row 12 of the Cheat Sheet. > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Used**: 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J, hence dividing by 3.6 × 10⁶ gives kWh. > $141.78 \times 10^6 / 3.6 \times 10^6 = 39.38 \approx 39.4 \, kWh/kg$. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Exam-Style) > **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A laboratory alkaline electrolyzer operates at a current of 250 A for 8 hours at 25 °C, 1 atm. The cell voltage is 2.0 V. Calculate: (a) mass of $H_2$ produced (assume 100% Faradaic efficiency), (b) volume of $H_2$ at STP, (c) actual SEC, and (d) overall efficiency relative to HHV. **Solution:** (a) Mass of $H_2$: $$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \frac{2.016 \times 250 \times (8 \times 3600)}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$ $$m = \frac{2.016 \times 7{,}200{,}000}{192{,}970} = \frac{14{,}515{,}200}{192{,}970} \approx 75.22 \, g$$ (b) Volume of $H_2$ at STP: $$V = \frac{75.22}{2.016} \times 22.414 \approx 836.3 \, L$$ (c) Actual SEC: $$SEC = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot n \cdot F}{M_{H_2}} = \frac{2.0 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} \, J/kg$$ $$SEC = 1.915 \times 10^8 \, J/kg = 53.18 \, kWh/kg$$ (d) Overall Efficiency (HHV basis): $$\eta = \frac{SEC_{th}}{SEC_{actual}} = \frac{39.4}{53.18} \times 100\% \approx 74.1\%$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Generic Electrolyzer System ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Renewable Power Source
Solar PV or Wind] --> B[AC-DC Rectifier
and Power Electronics] B --> C[DC Bus
Power Conditioning Unit] C --> D[Electrolyzer Stack
Multiple Cells in Series] D --> E[Anode Side
Oxygen Evolution] D --> F[Cathode Side
Hydrogen Evolution] F --> G[Gas-Liquid Separator
H2 Stream] E --> H[Gas-Liquid Separator
O2 Stream] G --> I[Deoxo Purifier
and Dryer] I --> J[H2 Compressor
Multi-stage] J --> K[High-Pressure
H2 Storage Tank] H --> L[O2 Vent
or Industrial Use] D --> M[Heat Exchanger
Waste Heat Recovery] M --> N[Boiler Feedwater
Pre-heater Loop] N --> D K --> O[End User
Refinery or Fuel Cell] classDef power fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 classDef elecchem fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef storage fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000 class A,B,C power class D,E,F elecchem class G,H,I,J,K,L,M,N,O storage ``` > [!NOTE] > **Read this diagram left-to-right**: Power is rectified from renewable AC/DC, fed into the **electrolyzer stack** (the heart of the system), where the two half-reactions physically separate $H_2$ and $O_2$. Each gas then goes through **separation, purification, compression, and storage** before reaching the end user. The feedback loop at the bottom represents **waste-heat recovery**, which is critical for SOEC and high-efficiency systems. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparative Architecture of the Three Electrolyzer Technologies ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AEL[Alkaline Electrolyzer AEC] AEL1[Steel Cathode] --> AEL2[Liquid KOH 30wt%] AEL2 --> AEL3[Steel Anode] AEL3 --> AEL4[Asbestos or Zirfon Separator] end subgraph PEM[Proton Exchange Membrane PEM] PEM1[Pt/C Cathode] --> PEM2[Nafion Membrane] PEM2 --> PEM3[IrO2 Anode] PEM3 --> PEM4[Titanium Bipolar Plate] end subgraph SOEC[Solid Oxide Electrolyzer SOEC] SOEC1[Ni-YSZ Fuel Electrode] --> SOEC2[YSZ Electrolyte] SOEC2 --> SOEC3[LSM Oxygen Electrode] SOEC3 --> SOEC4[Steam Inlet 800C] end AEL --> K1[60 to 90 C
Low cost mature] PEM --> K2[50 to 80 C
Fast dynamic response] SOEC --> K3[700 to 850 C
Highest efficiency] classDef alk fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef pm fill:#e6f0ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000 classDef so fill:#ffe6f0,stroke:#990033,color:#000 class AEL1,AEL2,AEL3,AEL4 alk class PEM1,PEM2,PEM3,PEM4 pm class SOEC1,SOEC2,SOEC3,SOEC4 so ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight**: The diagram above maps directly to the three "Key Properties" questions KTU commonly asks: **(i) State the ion that migrates in each type**, **(ii) Compare operating temperatures**, **(iii) Justify why SOEC has the highest efficiency**. Use this exact comparison table in your answer. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Energy Flow Inside a Single Electrolyzer Cell ```mermaid flowchart TB P[DC Power Input
P = V_cell x I] --> REV[E_rev = 1.229 V
Thermodynamic Minimum] P --> ACT[Activation Loss
eta_act 0.1 to 0.3 V] P --> OHM[Ohmic Loss
eta_ohm 0.1 to 0.4 V] P --> CONC[Concentration Loss
eta_conc 0.05 to 0.2 V] REV --> OUT[Hydrogen Energy Stored
HHV = 39.4 kWh per kg] ACT --> W1[Wasted as Heat] OHM --> W1 CONC --> W1 W1 --> ENV[Lost to Environment] OUT --> USEFUL[Useful Chemical Energy] classDef loss fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,color:#000 classDef use fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000 class ACT,OHM,CONC,W1,ENV loss class OUT,USEFUL use ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define the term "Thermoneutral Voltage" of a water electrolyzer. Why does its value differ from the standard reversible cell voltage? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The **thermoneutral voltage ($E_{th}$)** is the cell voltage at which the electrolysis reaction proceeds *without* net heat exchange with the surroundings — i.e., all the reaction enthalpy ($\Delta H$) is supplied as electrical energy and none is drawn from (or rejected to) external heat sources. $$E_{th} = \frac{\Delta H^0}{nF} = \frac{285.83 \, kJ/mol}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.481 \, V$$ It differs from the **reversible voltage ($E_{rev} = 1.229$ V)** because $E_{rev}$ is based on the **Gibbs free energy** ($\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$), which excludes the entropic heat term $T\Delta S$. At 25 °C, this difference is: $$E_{th} - E_{rev} = \frac{T\Delta S}{nF} = 0.252 \, V$$ Below $E_{th}$ (i.e., operating at ~1.3–1.4 V), the cell **absorbs heat** from the surroundings; above $E_{th}$ (e.g., 1.6–2.0 V), it **rejects heat** to the surroundings. *(3 Marks)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **State Faraday's First and Second Laws of Electrolysis. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Faraday's First Law:** *The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed through the electrolyte.* Mathematically: $m = Z \cdot I \cdot t = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ where $Z$ is the electrochemical equivalent, $I$ is current, $t$ is time, $M$ is molar mass, $n$ is the number of electrons involved, and $F$ is Faraday's constant. **Faraday's Second Law:** *When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their equivalent weights (M/n).* Mathematically: $\frac{m_1}{m_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{M_1/n_1}{M_2/n_2}$ **Significance:** These laws form the **basis of quantitative hydrogen yield calculation** in any electrolyzer design. *(3 Marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for the overall water-splitting reaction and explain the effect of temperature and pressure on the reversible cell voltage. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **PEM water electrolyzer** operates at 80 °C and 30 bar pressure on the hydrogen side. Calculate: (i) the reversible cell voltage, (ii) the volume of hydrogen produced per hour at a current of 500 A (assume 100% Faradaic efficiency), and (iii) the Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) if the actual operating voltage is 1.95 V. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution (a):** The overall reversible reaction is: $$H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow H_{2(g)} + \frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)}$$ The Gibbs free energy depends on activities ($a$) of products and reactants. The Nernst equation for this reaction is: $$E_{rev} = E^0_{rev} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{a_{H_2O}}\right)$$ **Derivation of the equation (from $\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT \ln Q$):** For the cell reaction, $\Delta G = -nFE$. Substituting: $$-nFE = -nFE^0 + RT \ln Q$$ $$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ For pure liquid water, $a_{H_2O} = 1$. With $n = 2$: $$E_{rev} = 1.229 - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)$$ **Effect of Temperature:** As T increases, the term $T\Delta S$ becomes larger, so $E_{rev}$ **decreases**. For water splitting, $dE_{rev}/dT \approx -0.85 \, mV/°C$ near 25 °C. This is why **SOEC (high T) is more efficient**. **Effect of Pressure:** Higher pressure increases the logarithmic term (more positive), so $E_{rev}$ **increases**. At 30 bar vs 1 bar (assuming stoichiometric products): $$\Delta E = \frac{RT}{2F} \ln(30) = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485} \times \ln(30) \approx 0.030 \, V$$ So the theoretical minimum voltage rises by ~30 mV. *(7 Marks — 3 for derivation, 2 for T effect, 2 for P effect)* --- #### **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** T = 80 °C = 353.15 K, P = 30 bar, I = 500 A, $V_{cell}$ = 1.95 V. **(i) Reversible cell voltage at 80 °C, 30 bar:** Using the Nernst equation: $$E_{rev} = 1.229 - \frac{8.314 \times 353.15}{2 \times 96{,}485} \ln(30 \times 30^{1/2})$$ (Note: $P_{O_2} \approx 15 \, bar$ from stoichiometry of a balanced system) $$E_{rev} = 1.229 - \frac{2935.7}{192{,}970} \ln(30 \times 5.477)$$ $$E_{rev} = 1.229 - (0.01521) \times \ln(164.32)$$ $$E_{rev} = 1.229 - (0.01521) \times 5.10 = 1.229 - 0.0776 = 1.151 \, V$$ **(ii) Volume of $H_2$ per hour:** Using Faraday's law at STP first, then convert to actual T, P: $$V_{STP} = \frac{I \cdot t}{nF} \times V_m = \frac{500 \times 3600}{2 \times 96{,}485} \times 22.414 \, L$$ $$V_{STP} = \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{192{,}970} \times 22.414 = 9.327 \times 22.414 = 209.0 \, L \, (at STP)$$ Converting to 30 bar and 80 °C (353 K): $$V_{actual} = V_{STP} \times \frac{P_{STP}}{P_{actual}} \times \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{STP}} = 209.0 \times \frac{1}{30} \times \frac{353}{273} = 9.0 \, L/hour$$ **(iii) Specific Energy Consumption:** $$SEC = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot n \cdot F}{M_{H_2}} = \frac{1.95 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} \, J/kg$$ $$SEC = 1.866 \times 10^8 \, J/kg = 51.84 \, kWh/kg$$ **Overall efficiency (HHV basis):** $$\eta = \frac{39.4}{51.84} \times 100\% = 76.0\%$$ *(7 Marks — 2 + 3 + 2)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style, Alternative]** **(a)** Compare **Alkaline, PEM, and Solid Oxide** water electrolyzers with respect to: electrolyte, ion transported, operating temperature, electrode materials, and commercial maturity. State two advantages and one disadvantage of each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **polarization curve** (V–I characteristic) of an electrolyzer with a neat sketch. Identify the three regions of overpotential loss and discuss the engineering strategies to minimize each. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution (a):** | Parameter | **Alkaline (AEL)** | **PEM** | **SOEC** | |---|---|---|---| | Electrolyte | Liquid 25–30 wt% KOH | Solid Nafion™ membrane | Solid YSZ ceramic | | Mobile Ion | $OH^-$ | $H^+$ | $O^{2-}$ | | Temperature | 60–90 °C | 50–80 °C | 700–850 °C | | Cathode (H₂ side) | Nickel (Ni) | Platinum on carbon (Pt/C) | Ni-YSZ cermet | | Anode (O₂ side) | Nickel-coated steel | Iridium oxide (IrO₂) | Lanthanum Strontium Manganite (LSM) | | Bipolar Plate | Stainless steel | Titanium (Pt-coated) | Coated stainless steel | | Commercial Status | Mature (> 95% market) | Commercial, growing fast | Pilot / Demonstration | | Capital Cost | Low | High (Pt, Ir) | Very High | | Efficiency (HHV) | 63–70% | 65–75% | 75–85% | | Dynamic Response | Slow (minutes) | Very fast (seconds) | Slowest | **Two advantages / one disadvantage of each:** **Alkaline**: - ✅ Lowest CAPEX, uses non-precious metals, proven lifetime (> 80,000 h). - ✅ Easily scalable to multi-MW (up to 100 MW demonstrated). - ❌ Lower current density, slow dynamic response, gas crossover at low load. **PEM**: - ✅ Compact, high current density (2–4 A/cm²), fast response ideal for renewables. - ✅ Produces high-purity $H_2$ at high pressure directly. - ❌ High cost of Pt and Ir catalysts, membrane degradation. **SOEC**: - ✅ Highest efficiency (less electricity needed, uses waste heat), reversible with SOFC. - ✅ Can co-electrolyze $CO_2$ + $H_2O$ → syngas. - ❌ Very high operating temperature → material degradation, expensive balance of plant. *(7 Marks)* --- #### **Model Solution (b):** A **polarization curve** plots the **cell voltage ($V_{cell}$)** on the Y-axis against the **current density (i, A/cm²)** on the X-axis. It typically has the shape shown below (key features in words since this is text-based): - At **i = 0**, the cell voltage equals $E_{rev} = 1.229$ V. - As **i** increases, $V_{cell}$ rises sharply at first (activation-dominated region), then linearly (ohmic-dominated), then sharply again at high i (concentration-dominated). **Three Overpotential Regions:** 1. **Activation Region (0 to ~0.1 A/cm²):** Voltage rises due to slow electrode kinetics. Dominated by the sluggish Oxygen Evolution Reaction (OER) at the anode. - *Minimization:* Use **better electrocatalysts** — e.g., IrO₂ for OER in PEM; Ni-Mo alloys for HER in alkaline; high surface area Pt-black. 2. **Ohmic Region (0.1 to 1.0 A/cm²):** Linear voltage rise due to ionic and electronic resistance. - *Minimization:* Use **thinner membranes**, **higher conductivity electrolytes** (KOH concentration optimization), **better contact materials** (Pt-coated bipolar plates), and **elevated temperature** (ionic conductivity rises with T). 3. **Concentration Region (> 1.0 A/cm²):** Voltage rises sharply due to mass-transport limitations — gas bubbles block active sites, reactant depletion. - *Minimization:* Use **porous electrodes with optimal pore-size distribution**, **forced electrolyte flow**, **super-hydrophobic coatings** to release bubbles, and **pressure differentials** to sweep bubbles away. *(7 Marks — 2 for sketch description, 3 for region identification, 2 for strategies)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Electrolysis Questions:** > 1. **Confusing n in the Nernst equation** — for the *cell reaction*, n = 2 (for $H_2$); for the *half-reaction*, students often incorrectly write n = 4. Use n = 2 for the overall water-splitting cell voltage equation. > 2. **Mixing up STP vs NTP** — at STP (0 °C, 1 atm) $V_m$ = 22.414 L/mol; at NTP (20 °C, 1 atm) it is 24.0 L/mol. KTU's preferred convention is **STP = 22.414 L/mol**. > 3. **Forgetting to convert J → kWh** in SEC problems. Always divide by 3.6 × 10⁶ at the end. > 4. **Not specifying the basis of efficiency** (HHV vs LHV). KTU Module 2 uses **HHV = 39.4 kWh/kg** unless explicitly stated otherwise. > 5. **Neglecting units of pressure in the Nernst equation** — partial pressures must be in **atm** (or bar, but the same on both sides cancels). The dimensionless ratio is what matters. > 6. **Stating PEM efficiency as "better" than SOEC** — in HHV terms SOEC is higher, but **dynamic response** of PEM is superior. Read the question carefully! --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Water Electrolysis**: $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$ — driven by external electricity. - ✅ **Three Classes**: AEL (alkaline, mature, cheap), PEM (fast, dynamic, costly), SOEC (high T, highest efficiency). - ✅ **Reversible Voltage**: $E_{rev} = 1.229$ V at 25 °C, 1 atm (from $\Delta G^0$). - ✅ **Thermoneutral Voltage**: $E_{th} = 1.481$ V (from $\Delta H^0$). - ✅ **Difference**: $E_{th} - E_{rev} = T\Delta S / nF = 0.252$ V (must be supplied as heat below $E_{th}$). - ✅ **Operating Voltage** = $E_{rev} + \eta_{act} + \eta_{ohm} + \eta_{conc}$ (typically 1.8–2.2 V in practice). - ✅ **Faraday's First Law**: $m = MIt / (nF)$ — directly gives $H_2$ mass. - ✅ **Faraday's Second Law**: $m_1/m_2 = E_1/E_2$ — for series cells. - ✅ **Faraday's Constant**: F = 96,485 C/mol. - ✅ **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** = $V_{cell} \cdot nF / M_{H_2}$ — benchmark is kWh/kg $H_2$. - ✅ **HHV Theoretical Minimum SEC** = 39.4 kWh/kg $H_2$ — physical impossibility threshold. - ✅ **Voltage Efficiency** = $E_{th} / V_{cell} \times 100\%$ (typical 70–80%). - ✅ **Faradaic Efficiency** = $I_{theoretical} / I_{actual} \times 100\%$ (typically > 95% in modern cells). - ✅ **Nernst Equation for Water**: $E = E^0 - (RT/2F) \ln(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2} / a_{H_2O})$. - ✅ **Temperature Effect**: $E_{rev}$ **decreases** with T → SOEC advantage. - ✅ **Pressure Effect**: $E_{rev}$ **increases** with P → compression cost. - ✅ **Ions Migrated**: $OH^-$ (AEL), $H^+$ (PEM), $O^{2-}$ (SOEC). - ✅ **Catalysts**: Pt (PEM cathode), IrO₂ (PEM anode), Ni (alkaline), Ni-YSZ + LSM (SOEC). - ✅ **Green Hydrogen**: Electrolysis powered by renewables → zero-carbon $H_2$ (the only true-green pathway at scale today).

topics.title: Photo Electrochemical Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Photo Electrochemical Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Photoelectrochemical (PEC) Method** is a direct solar-to-hydrogen conversion process in which semiconductor photoelectrodes immersed in an aqueous electrolyte absorb photons from sunlight and generate electron–hole pairs that drive the non-spontaneous splitting of water into molecular hydrogen (H₂) and oxygen (O₂) without the need for any external electrical bias or separate photovoltaic–electrolyzer coupling.

The overall reaction is given by:

$$2H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{sunlight} + \text{semiconductor}} 2H_2 + O_2$$

The standard Gibbs free energy change at 25 °C is **ΔG° = +237.2 kJ/mol**, which corresponds to a thermodynamic decomposition voltage of **E° = 1.23 V** per molecule of water (per electron transfer). This makes the reaction thermodynamically uphill (non-spontaneous), and a minimum theoretical energy input equivalent to **1.23 eV per electron** must be supplied by the absorbed photons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A PEC cell is essentially a *photo-driven electrochemical cell*. It differs from Photovoltaic Electrolysis (PV-E) where two separate devices (a solar cell and an electrolyzer) are wired together. In a true PEC cell, light absorption and water electrolysis occur on the same semiconductor surface — this is the distinguishing feature examiners test.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine a **leaf doing artificial photosynthesis on a metal plate**. A real leaf uses chlorophyll to catch sunlight and split water into H and O atoms, then reassembles them into sugar. A PEC photoelectrode does almost the same thing — except instead of making sugar, it makes pure hydrogen gas.

Think of the semiconductor as a **two-storey parking lot for electrons**:
- The **lower floor** (called the *valence band*) is where electrons normally sit.
- The **upper floor** (called the *conduction band*) is empty.
- The "staircase" between the two floors is called the **band gap** (E_g).

When a photon (a packet of sunlight) with energy *greater than the staircase height* strikes the semiconductor, it **kicks an electron from the lower floor to the upper floor**, leaving behind a positively charged "hole" downstairs. These photo-generated charges then rush to the electrode surface, where they drive two half-reactions:

- **At the photoanode (n-type):** Water is oxidized to O₂ (electron is "stolen" by the hole).
- **At the photocathode (p-type) OR at a Pt counter electrode:** Water/Protons are reduced to H₂ (electron is "donated").

The hydrogen bubbles you see at the dark counter electrode is the entire point of the technology — *green hydrogen from sunlight and water*.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Avogadro's Number | N_A | **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹** |
| Faraday Constant | F | **96,485 C/mol** |
| Standard Gibbs free energy for water splitting | ΔG° | **+237.2 kJ/mol** |
| Thermodynamic decomposition voltage | E° | **1.23 V** |
| Standard enthalpy change | ΔH° | **+285.8 kJ/mol** (thermoneutral voltage = 1.48 V) |
| Solar spectral irradiance (AM 1.5G) | P_in | **1000 W/m²** (= 100 mW/cm²) |
| Charge of an electron | e | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two voltages (1.23 V vs 1.48 V)?** The 1.23 V is the *reversible* thermodynamic minimum (ΔG). The 1.48 V is the *thermoneutral* voltage (ΔH) — the actual voltage you must apply if you do not want to extract heat from the surroundings. In practice, kinetic overpotentials push the required voltage to **> 1.8 V**, which is why the semiconductor band gap should ideally exceed **1.6 – 1.8 eV** for unassisted water splitting.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The PEC process can be broken down into five sequential physical–chemical steps:

1. **Photon Absorption:** A photon of energy hν strikes the semiconductor photoelectrode. Absorption occurs only if **hν ≥ E_g** (the band gap). Photons with energy below E_g pass through unabsorbed and are wasted.

2. **Exciton / Electron–Hole Pair Generation:** The absorbed photon promotes a valence-band electron (e⁻) into the conduction band, leaving a positively charged hole (h⁺) in the valence band. The pair is called an **exciton**.

3. **Charge Separation and Transport:** The internal electric field at the **semiconductor–electrolyte junction (the space-charge region)** sweeps electrons toward the bulk/contact and holes toward the semiconductor–electrolyte interface. This field arises from band bending caused by the equilibration of the semiconductor Fermi level with the electrolyte redox potential.

4. **Surface Redox Reactions (Half-Cell Reactions):**
   - **At the photoanode (n-type, e.g., TiO₂, Fe₂O₃, BiVO₄):** Holes oxidize water:
   
   $$2H_2O + 4h^+ \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ \quad (E^\circ_{ox} = +1.23 \text{ V vs NHE})$$
   
   - **At the cathode (Pt or p-type photocathode, e.g., Cu₂O, Si, InP):** Electrons reduce protons:
   
   $$4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2 \quad (E^\circ_{red} = 0.00 \text{ V vs NHE})$$

5. **Product Separation and Collection:** H₂ gas bubbles are collected at the cathode side; O₂ bubbles at the anode side. A membrane (e.g., Nafion) is often used to prevent mixing and manage the ion flow (H⁺ or OH⁻).

## 2.2 Why and How — Physical Reasoning

- **Why a semiconductor?** Metals cannot perform this task because they have no band gap — photo-excited electrons in metals relax back to thermal equilibrium within femtoseconds via electron–electron scattering. Semiconductors have an *insulating* band gap that allows the photo-excited electron to remain separated from its hole long enough to drive chemistry.
- **Why band bending?** When the semiconductor is immersed in the electrolyte, charge transfer across the interface equalizes the Fermi level of the solid with the electrochemical potential of the electrolyte. This creates a built-in electric field in the depletion region, which is the engine that drives charge separation without external bias.
- **Why n-type for O₂ and p-type for H₂?** The band-bending direction is opposite in the two types: in n-type, bands bend *upward* toward the surface, drawing holes (minority carriers) to the surface for oxidation. In p-type, bands bend *downward*, drawing electrons to the surface for reduction.

## 2.3 PEC Cell Configurations (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Configuration | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Type 1** Photoanode + Metal Cathode | n-type photoanode + Pt dark cathode in single electrolyte | TiO₂ \| Pt |
| **Type 2** Photocathode + Metal Anode | p-type photocathode + Pt dark anode | p-GaInP₂ \| Pt |
| **Type 3 (Tandem / Z-scheme)** | n-type photoanode + p-type photocathode optically in series, no bias | WO₃/BiVO₄ \| Cu₂O/CuO |
| **Type 4 (PV + PEC hybrid)** | External solar cell provides additional bias to a single photoelectrode | Si PV + TiO₂ |

> [!TIP]
> **For a 14-mark KTU question, always draw the Z-scheme / tandem configuration** if the question asks for "unassisted solar water splitting" — it is the most KTU-typical advanced answer.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Definition | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^\circ = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$ | Thermodynamic cell voltage from free energy | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{J_{ph} \times 1.23 \text{ V}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Solar-to-Hydrogen efficiency (unassisted) | % |
| 3 | $\eta_{ABPE} = \dfrac{\vert J_{ph} \vert \times (1.23 - \vert V_{bias}\vert)}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Applied Bias Photon-to-Current Efficiency | % |
| 4 | $IPCE(\lambda) = \dfrac{1240 \times J_{ph}(\lambda)}{P(\lambda) \times \lambda} \times 100\%$ | Incident Photon-to-Current Efficiency at wavelength λ | % |
| 5 | $E_g(\text{eV}) = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda_{abs}(\text{nm})}$ | Band gap from absorption edge wavelength | eV, nm |
| 6 | $V_{ph} = \dfrac{n_c \cdot E_g}{e}$ | Maximum theoretical photovoltage of a semiconductor | V |
| 7 | $h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})} \text{ eV}$ | Photon energy in eV given wavelength | eV, nm |
| 8 | $E_{cb} = \chi - E_c - 0.5 E_g$ (Mulliken electronegativity) | Conduction band edge position | eV vs vacuum |
| 9 | $E_{vb} = E_{cb} - E_g$ | Valence band edge position | eV vs vacuum |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The factor **1240** in equations 4 and 5 comes from $hc/e = (6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8)/(1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \approx 1240$ eV·nm. Memorize this number — it appears in almost every PEC numerical.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

PEC hydrogen production is the *only* direct solar-to-chemical pathway (no electricity intermediate, no mechanical parts), making it attractive for:
- **Decentralized green hydrogen** in remote, off-grid locations.
- **Space and defense applications** where compactness matters (notably demonstrated by NASA on the ISS using a TiO₂-based photoelectrolysis system).
- **Long-duration energy storage**: solar H₂ can be stored indefinitely and converted back to electricity via fuel cells, solving the intermittency problem of solar PV.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thermodynamic Decomposition Voltage of Water

**Statement:** Show that the minimum voltage required to split one mole of liquid water is 1.23 V at standard conditions.

**Given:**
- $\Delta G^\circ$ for the reaction $2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$ is +237.2 kJ/mol.
- $n = 4$ electrons transferred per mole of O₂ produced (or per 2 moles of H₂).
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^\circ &= -nFE^\circ_{cell} \\
E^\circ_{cell} &= -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} \\
E^\circ_{cell} &= -\dfrac{+237.2 \times 10^{3} \text{ J/mol}}{4 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}} \\
E^\circ_{cell} &= -\dfrac{237{,}200}{385{,}940} \text{ V} \\
E^\circ_{cell} &= -0.6147 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign indicates the cell reaction as written (water splitting) is non-spontaneous; the magnitude of the *required applied voltage* is therefore:

$$
E^\circ_{cell} = +1.23 \text{ V} \quad \blacksquare
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** This 1.23 V is the *absolute thermodynamic floor*. In a real PEC cell, you must also overcome: (i) kinetic overpotentials at the anode (η_a ≈ 0.3–0.5 V) and cathode (η_c ≈ 0.05–0.1 V), (ii) ohmic drop in the electrolyte (iR), and (iii) recombination losses. The sum pushes the practical voltage requirement to **> 1.8 V**, which is why a single low-band-gap semiconductor cannot do the job alone — a tandem (Z-scheme) configuration is required.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solar-to-Hydrogen (STH) Efficiency

**Definition:** STH efficiency is the ratio of the chemical energy stored in the produced H₂ to the incident solar energy input, expressed as a percentage.

**Step 1: Chemical energy output rate.** If a photocurrent density $J_{ph}$ (in A/m²) flows through the cell, the rate of H₂ production per unit area is given by Faraday's law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{H_2} &= \dfrac{J_{ph}}{nF} \quad \text{[mol H}_2\text{/s/m}^2\text{]} \\
\text{where } n &= 2 \text{ electrons per H}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Energy stored per mole of H₂.** Using the lower heating value (LHV) of hydrogen, which is the relevant energy stored *as H₂ gas* at the cell exit:

$$
\Delta H_{LHV} = 237.2 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad (\text{or equivalently, } E^\circ \times nF = 1.23 \times 2 \times 96485 = 237.2 \text{ kJ/mol})
$$

**Step 3: Power output (chemical) per unit area.**

$$
P_{out} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \times \Delta H_{LHV} = \dfrac{J_{ph}}{nF} \times nF \times 1.23 = J_{ph} \times 1.23 \text{ [W/m}^2\text{]}
$$

**Step 4: Power input per unit area** under the standard AM 1.5G solar spectrum:

$$
P_{in} = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 5: STH efficiency definition:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{J_{ph} \times 1.23 \text{ V}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Theoretical STH Efficiency vs Band Gap

This derivation quantifies why no single semiconductor can be perfect for solar water splitting.

**Setup:** Assume a single-junction photoelectrode with band gap $E_g$ absorbing all photons with $h\nu \ge E_g$ from the AM 1.5G solar spectrum. The theoretical maximum $J_{ph}$ (the integrated short-circuit current density) is:

$$
J_{ph,max} = e \int_{0}^{\lambda_g} \Phi_{AM1.5}(\lambda) \, d\lambda
$$

where $\lambda_g = 1240/E_g$ (nm) and $\Phi_{AM1.5}(\lambda)$ is the spectral photon flux (photons/m²/s/nm).

**Step 1: Photon flux above band gap.** Integrating the AM 1.5G ASTM G173 spectrum above $E_g$ gives the curve $J_{ph,max}(E_g)$ in mA/cm²:

| $E_g$ (eV) | $\lambda_g$ (nm) | $J_{ph,max}$ (mA/cm²) |
|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 1240 | ~ 32 |
| 1.23 | 1008 | ~ 26 |
| 1.5 | 827 | ~ 19 |
| 2.0 | 620 | ~ 11 |
| 2.5 | 496 | ~ 6 |
| 3.0 | 413 | ~ 3 |

**Step 2: STH for each band gap** (assuming 100% quantum yield and 1.23 V used):

$$
\eta_{STH}(E_g) = \dfrac{J_{ph,max}(E_g) \times 10 \text{ (A/m}^2\text{ per mA/cm}^2\text{)} \times 1.23 \text{ V}}{1000 \text{ W/m}^2}
$$

**Step 3: Result.** The function $\eta_{STH}(E_g)$ peaks at approximately:

- $E_g \approx 1.6 - 1.8$ eV → $\eta_{STH,max} \approx 18 - 25\%$

This is the **Shockley–Queisser limit for solar water splitting**. The trade-off is clear: too small a band gap → too little voltage; too large → too few absorbed photons. Hence a *tandem* of two semiconductors is required to break the 25% single-junction ceiling.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — IPCE from Photocurrent at a Given Wavelength

**Statement:** Derive the IPCE formula used in PEC characterization.

**Step 1: Number of incident photons per second at wavelength λ (monochromatic light).**

$$
\dot{N}_{ph} = \dfrac{P(\lambda) \lambda}{hc}
$$

**Step 2: Number of electrons flowing per second in the external circuit.**

$$
\dot{N}_{e^-} = \dfrac{J_{ph}(\lambda)}{e}
$$

**Step 3: IPCE definition (electrons out per photon in).**

$$
IPCE(\lambda) = \dfrac{\dot{N}_{e^-}}{\dot{N}_{ph}} = \dfrac{J_{ph}(\lambda)/e}{P(\lambda)\lambda/(hc)}
$$

**Step 4: Convert hc/e into practical units (eV·nm).** Since $hc/e = 1240$ eV·nm:

$$
\boxed{IPCE(\lambda) = \dfrac{1240 \times J_{ph}(\lambda)}{P(\lambda) \times \lambda} \times 100\%}
$$

where $J_{ph}$ is in A/m², $P$ is in W/m², and $\lambda$ is in nm. *(Some texts use mA/cm² and mW/cm² — the formula is unchanged because units cancel.)*

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A PEC cell using a BiVO₄ photoanode (band gap 2.4 eV) under AM 1.5G illumination (1000 W/m²) produces a short-circuit photocurrent density of 1.5 mA/cm² at 0 V bias vs the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). Calculate: (i) the absorbed wavelength threshold, (ii) the STH efficiency, and (iii) the ABPE if the cell required 0.4 V external bias.

**Solution:**

**(i) Absorption threshold wavelength:**

$$
\lambda_{abs} = \dfrac{1240}{E_g} = \dfrac{1240}{2.4} = 516.7 \text{ nm}
$$

**(ii) STH efficiency (note: 0 bias implies $V_{bias} = 0$):**

$$
\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{J_{ph} \times 1.23}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{(1.5 \times 10 \text{ A/m}^2) \times 1.23 \text{ V}}{1000 \text{ W/m}^2} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{15 \times 1.23}{1000} \times 100\% = \dfrac{18.45}{1000} \times 100\% = 1.845\%
$$

**(iii) ABPE at 0.4 V applied bias (note: applied reverse bias *reduces* the energy term):**

$$
\eta_{ABPE} = \dfrac{\vert J_{ph} \vert \times (1.23 - \vert V_{bias}\vert)}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{15 \times (1.23 - 0.4)}{1000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{ABPE} = \dfrac{15 \times 0.83}{1000} \times 100\% = 1.245\%
$$

> **Engineering Note:** A real-world record for unassisted STH on a single-photoelectrode system is **~ 4 – 5%** (e.g., Cu₂O/AZO/TiO₂ + RuOₓ tandem). The theoretical max is ~25%. KTU questions typically expect 1–5% for realistic estimates and 18–25% for theoretical maximum.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Single Photoanode PEC Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sunlight hν] --> B[Photoanode n-type semiconductor e.g. TiO2 Fe2O3 BiVO4]
    B --> C[Electrolyte aqueous KOH or Na2SO4]
    C --> D[Platinum Counter Electrode]
    D --> E[H2 gas bubbles collected]
    B --> F[O2 gas bubbles released at photoanode]
    B -.->|band bending drives e- to bulk| G[External wire to Pt cathode]
    G --> D
```

**Node Explanation:**
- **A → B:** Photons strike the photoanode and generate electron–hole pairs if $h\nu \ge E_g$.
- **B → F:** Holes (h⁺) migrate to the photoanode surface and oxidize water to O₂.
- **B → G → D:** Electrons (e⁻) travel through the external wire to the Pt counter electrode.
- **D → E:** Electrons reduce protons (H⁺) at the Pt surface, producing H₂ gas.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Tandem (Z-Scheme) PEC Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Sun[Sunlight] --> PhA[Top Photoanode n-type]
    PhA --> MidJunction[Optical and electrical junction e.g. WO3 to BiVO4 or Recombination layer]
    MidJunction --> PhC[Bottom Photocathode p-type]
    PhC --> H2out[H2 evolution catalyst Pt or NiMo]
    PhA --> O2out[O2 evolution catalyst CoPi or RuO2]
    H2out --- elec1[Aqueous electrolyte pH controlled]
    O2out --- elec1
```

**Node Explanation:**
- High-energy photons (UV–blue) are absorbed by the top wide-band-gap **photoanode** (e.g., BiVO₄, $E_g \approx 2.4$ eV).
- Lower-energy photons (visible) pass through and are absorbed by the bottom **photocathode** (e.g., Cu₂O, $E_g \approx 2.0$ eV, or Si, $E_g \approx 1.1$ eV).
- The two photovoltages add in series, allowing unassisted water splitting even when each semiconductor individually has insufficient voltage.
- This is the architecture used in state-of-the-art record-efficiency PEC cells.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Band Alignment for Unassisted Water Splitting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AnodeSide[Photoanode Surface]
        VB_A[Valence Band Edge] --> CB_A[Conduction Band Edge]
    end
    subgraph CathodeSide[Photocathode Surface]
        VB_C[Valence Band Edge] --> CB_C[Conduction Band Edge]
    end
    H2level[H plus / H2 reduction potential 0 V vs NHE] -.->|electron must arrive here| CB_C
    O2level[H2O / O2 oxidation potential plus 1.23 V vs NHE] -.->|hole must arrive here| VB_A
    CB_A -.->|e- flow| CB_C
```

**Node Explanation:**
- For unassisted water splitting, the **photoanode valence band edge** must lie *below* the H₂O/O₂ redox level (+1.23 V vs NHE at pH 0) so that holes have enough oxidative power.
- The **photocathode conduction band edge** must lie *above* the H⁺/H₂ redox level (0 V vs NHE at pH 0) so that electrons have enough reductive power.
- The conduction band offset between the two semiconductors must be at least the difference between these potentials plus overpotential losses — typically 1.8 V total.

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Charge Transfer Pathway Inside a Single Photoelectrode

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Bulk[Semiconductor Bulk neutral region] --> SCR[Space Charge Region band bending]
    SCR --> SE[Surface electrolyte interface]
    Sun[Incident Photon hν greater than Eg] --> Bulk
    Bulk -->|photo-excitation| EHP[Electron hole pair e- and h plus]
    EHP -->|electric field in SCR separates| Eflow[Electron to back contact external circuit]
    EHP -->|electric field in SCR separates| Hflow[Hole to surface for water oxidation]
    Hflow -->|2H2O plus 4h plus| O2release[O2 gas plus 4H plus]
```

**Node Explanation:**
- The space charge region (SCR) is the heart of the device. Without it, e⁻ and h⁺ would recombine in ~µs and no current would flow.
- Recombination losses (Shockley–Read–Hall, surface, and Auger) are the chief enemies of PEC efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Photoelectrochemical (PEC) method of hydrogen production. State the overall reaction and the standard free energy change involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Photoelectrochemical (PEC) method is a process in which hydrogen is produced directly from water using sunlight absorbed by semiconductor photoelectrodes immersed in an aqueous electrolyte. The semiconductor generates electron–hole pairs that drive the water-splitting reaction.

**Overall reaction:** $2H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{sunlight}} 2H_2 + O_2$

**Standard Gibbs free energy change:** $\Delta G^\circ = +237.2$ kJ/mol, corresponding to a thermodynamic minimum decomposition voltage of **1.23 V** at 25 °C. Since $\Delta G^\circ > 0$, the reaction is non-spontaneous and requires the energy input from absorbed photons.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for reaction, 1 mark for ΔG° value and its significance.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between a photoanode and a photocathode in a PEC cell, giving one example material for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Photoanode | Photocathode |
|---|---|---|
| Semiconductor type | n-type | p-type |
| Charge carrier reaching surface | Holes (h⁺) | Electrons (e⁻) |
| Reaction driven | Water oxidation: $2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$ | Proton reduction: $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ |
| Band bending direction | Upward toward surface | Downward toward surface |
| Example material | TiO₂, Fe₂O₃, BiVO₄, WO₃ | Cu₂O, p-Si, p-InP, CuFeO₂ |

*(1 mark for type and reaction, 1 mark for band bending direction, 1 mark for example materials.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat schematic the operating principle of a tandem (Z-scheme) photoelectrochemical cell for unassisted solar water splitting. Discuss the criteria for selection of the photoanode and photocathode semiconductors, with two examples each.

**(b) [7 marks]** A PEC cell uses a semiconductor with a band gap of 1.8 eV and produces a photocurrent density of 8 mA/cm² under AM 1.5G (1000 W/m²) illumination. Calculate (i) the wavelength of light corresponding to the band gap, (ii) the maximum theoretical STH efficiency at this band gap from the integrated solar spectrum, and (iii) the practical STH efficiency if the photocurrent represents 60% of the theoretical maximum.

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) [7 marks]:**

**Schematic (refer to Section 4.2):** Draw a tandem PEC cell with sunlight incident on a wide-band-gap **photoanode** stacked on top of a narrower-band-gap **photocathode**, both immersed in an aqueous electrolyte. Show H₂ evolution at the photocathode and O₂ at the photoanode. *(2 marks for diagram)*

**Operating Principle:** High-energy photons are absorbed by the top photoanode; lower-energy photons transmitted through it are absorbed by the bottom photocathode. The two photovoltages add in series. Photo-generated holes at the photoanode oxidize water to O₂, while photo-generated electrons at the photocathode reduce protons to H₂. *(2 marks for working)*

**Selection Criteria (3 marks):**
1. The conduction band edge of the photocathode must be **more negative** than the H⁺/H₂ potential (0 V vs NHE).
2. The valence band edge of the photoanode must be **more positive** than the H₂O/O₂ potential (+1.23 V vs NHE).
3. Combined band gap and band offsets must provide > 1.8 V total.
4. Chemical stability in the electrolyte under illumination.
5. Earth abundance and low cost.

**Examples:** Photoanode — BiVO₄ (E_g = 2.4 eV), WO₃ (E_g = 2.7 eV), Fe₂O₃ (E_g = 2.1 eV). Photocathode — Cu₂O (E_g = 2.0 eV), p-Si (E_g = 1.1 eV), p-InP (E_g = 1.35 eV).

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) [7 marks]:**

**(i) Wavelength corresponding to E_g = 1.8 eV:**

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{1240}{E_g} = \dfrac{1240}{1.8} = 688.9 \text{ nm} \quad \boxed{\approx 689 \text{ nm}}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical maximum J_ph at E_g = 1.8 eV (from AM 1.5G integration):**

From the integrated ASTM G173 spectrum above 1.8 eV, $J_{ph,max} \approx 19$ mA/cm².

$$
\eta_{STH,max} = \dfrac{19 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A/cm}^2 \times 10^{4} \text{ cm}^2/\text{m}^2 \times 1.23 \text{ V}}{1000 \text{ W/m}^2} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{STH,max} = \dfrac{19 \times 10 \times 1.23}{1000} \times 100\% = 23.37\%
$$

**(iii) Practical STH at 60% of theoretical J_ph:**

$$
J_{ph,practical} = 0.6 \times 19 = 11.4 \text{ mA/cm}^2
$$

$$
\eta_{STH,practical} = \dfrac{11.4 \times 10 \times 1.23}{1000} \times 100\% = 14.02\%
$$

*(Working carries 1 mark per sub-question; final boxed answer 1 mark; total 7 marks split as 2 + 2 + 3.)*

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the various performance metrics used to evaluate a PEC cell. Define STH efficiency, ABPE, and IPCE, and derive the IPCE formula from first principles.

**(b) [7 marks]** A hematite (α-Fe₂O₃) photoanode (E_g = 2.1 eV) is illuminated by monochromatic light of wavelength 450 nm with intensity 50 mW/cm². The measured photocurrent density is 1.2 mA/cm². Calculate (i) the IPCE at 450 nm, (ii) the STH efficiency under AM 1.5G if the same current density is obtained (assume 100 mW/cm² incident power per active area), and (iii) comment on why the actual STH is much lower than the theoretical maximum.

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) [7 marks]:**

**Performance Metrics (3 marks for definitions):**

1. **Solar-to-Hydrogen Efficiency (η_STH):** The ratio of the chemical energy stored in H₂ produced to the solar energy input, for an *unbiased* PEC cell:
   $$\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{J_{ph} \times 1.23 \text{ V}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

2. **Applied Bias Photon-to-Current Efficiency (ABPE):** Used when the cell is not self-sufficient and an external bias V_bias is applied:
   $$\eta_{ABPE} = \dfrac{\vert J_{ph} \vert \times (1.23 - \vert V_{bias}\vert)}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

3. **Incident Photon-to-Current Efficiency (IPCE):** The ratio of electrons flowing in the external circuit to the number of incident photons of a given wavelength:
   $$\text{IPCE}(\lambda) = \dfrac{1240 \times J_{ph}(\lambda)}{P(\lambda) \times \lambda} \times 100\%$$

**Derivation of IPCE (4 marks):** *(See Section 3.4 above for the full derivation.)*
- Number of incident photons per second: $\dot{N}_{ph} = P\lambda/(hc)$
- Number of electrons per second: $\dot{N}_{e^-} = J_{ph}/e$
- IPCE = $\dot{N}_{e^-}/\dot{N}_{ph}$ → substitute → use $hc/e = 1240$ eV·nm → final boxed expression.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) [7 marks]:**

**(i) IPCE at λ = 450 nm:**

$$
IPCE = \dfrac{1240 \times J_{ph}}{P \times \lambda} = \dfrac{1240 \times 1.2 \text{ mA/cm}^2}{50 \text{ mW/cm}^2 \times 450 \text{ nm}}
$$

$$
IPCE = \dfrac{1488}{22{,}500} \times 100\% = 6.61\%
$$

**(ii) STH efficiency** (using J_ph = 1.2 mA/cm² and P_in = 100 mW/cm², equivalent to 1000 W/m²):

$$
\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10 \times 1.23}{1000} \times 100\% = 1.476\%
$$

**(iii) Why STH is much lower than theoretical max (3 marks):**

1. **Kinetic overpotentials** at the anode (oxygen evolution reaction is a 4-electron, 4-proton process with high activation barrier).
2. **Recombination losses** at grain boundaries, surfaces, and in the bulk (carrier lifetimes are short in many oxides).
3. **Poor charge transport** — holes in hematite have very low mobility (~ 0.01 cm²/V·s), limiting the usable film thickness.
4. **Band edge misalignment** — conduction band of Fe₂O₃ lies *below* the H⁺/H₂ level, so it cannot be used as a photocathode and may need an applied bias in a tandem.
5. **Optical losses** — reflection at the surface and incomplete absorption above the indirect band gap.
6. **Photo-corrosion** — many promising materials (e.g., Cu₂O) are unstable in the electrolyte and require protective overlayers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks in PEC questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include overpotential requirements** — Many students write that 1.23 V is "sufficient," but the examiner expects mention of practical voltage > 1.8 V including overpotentials. **−1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing IPCE with ABPE** — IPCE is a wavelength-resolved quantum efficiency, while ABPE requires a bias term. Mixing them up costs full marks on derivations.
> 3. **Not drawing band bending** — A PEC schematic without a band diagram (showing E_c and E_v relative to redox potentials) is considered incomplete. Always include a side energy-level diagram.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — STH is a dimensionless percentage; always write "%" explicitly.
> 5. **Ignoring stability** — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly emphasizes stability and safety. Always mention the photo-corrosion problem and protective coatings.
> 6. **Single-junction bias** — Stating that one semiconductor can split water unassisted at 1.23 V *without* justifying the band gap range 1.6–2.2 eV is incomplete. The band gap must be **> 1.6 eV** for sufficient overpotential.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** PEC = direct solar-to-hydrogen using a semiconductor–electrolyte junction.
- ✅ **Overall Reaction:** $2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$; $\Delta G^\circ = +237.2$ kJ/mol.
- ✅ **Thermodynamic voltage:** $E^\circ = 1.23$ V; **thermoneutral voltage:** 1.48 V; **practical voltage needed:** > 1.8 V.
- ✅ **Band gap requirement:** $E_g > 1.6$ eV for single junction; optimal is 1.8–2.2 eV; tandem breaks the 25% STH ceiling.
- ✅ **Photoanode (n-type):** holes oxidize water; examples: TiO₂, Fe₂O₃, BiVO₄, WO₃.
- ✅ **Photocathode (p-type):** electrons reduce protons; examples: Cu₂O, p-Si, p-InP, CuFeO₂.
- ✅ **Key formula 1:** $\eta_{STH} = J_{ph} \times 1.23 / P_{in}$ (unbiased, percentage form).
- ✅ **Key formula 2:** $\eta_{ABPE} = J_{ph} \times (1.23 - V_{bias}) / P_{in}$ (with applied bias).
- ✅ **Key formula 3:** $IPCE(\lambda) = 1240 \cdot J_{ph} / (P \cdot \lambda)$ (in %).
- ✅ **Key formula 4:** $E_g \text{ (eV)} = 1240 / \lambda \text{ (nm)}$ (memorize 1240).
- ✅ **Five steps:** absorption → e⁻–h⁺ generation → separation (band bending) → surface redox → gas collection.
- ✅ **Four configurations:** Type 1 (photoanode + dark cathode), Type 2 (photocathode + dark anode), Type 3 (tandem Z-scheme), Type 4 (PV + PEC hybrid).
- ✅ **Stability:** Photo-corrosion is a major challenge; protective overlayers (TiO₂, AZO) and co-catalysts (CoPi, NiFe, Pt) are essential.
- ✅ **Theoretical max STH:** ~ 18–25% for single junction; ~ 30% for tandem.
- ✅ **State-of-the-art record:** ~ 19% STH has been demonstrated in lab-scale tandem cells (e.g., InGaN/GaN + Si photocathode configurations).
- ✅ **Safety note:** H₂ + O₂ mixing is an explosion hazard — always use a membrane (Nafion) separator and proper gas collection.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "PAWS":**
> **P**hotoanode (n-type, O₂ side)
> **A**bsorption (hν ≥ E_g)
> **W**ater splitting (1.23 V minimum)
> **S**pace-charge region (band bending enables charge separation)
> Master these four words and you can frame an answer to almost any PEC question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermo-chemical Water Splitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermo-chemical Water Splitting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Thermo-chemical Water Splitting (TCWS)** is a closed-loop, multi-step chemical process in which a series of intermediate endothermic and exothermic reactions are driven by high-temperature thermal energy (typically sourced from concentrated solar radiation, nuclear reactors, or industrial waste heat) to decompose water into hydrogen and oxygen, while recycling all intermediate reactants so that the *only* net inputs are heat and water, and the *only* net outputs are H₂ and O₂.

The general representation of any thermochemical cycle is:

$$ \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat} \xrightarrow{\text{cycle of } n \text{ reactions}} \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 $$

The key thermodynamic challenge is that water is *thermodynamically very stable* at room temperature. The standard Gibbs free energy change for water splitting is **+237.2 kJ mol⁻¹** at 298 K, meaning direct thermal decomposition requires temperatures above **≈ 4000 K**, which is impractical for engineering materials. Thermochemical cycles cleverly *redistribute* the energy requirement across several reactions, each operating at a temperature that materials can withstand.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Chemical Bucket Brigade"

Imagine trying to push a heavy boulder (water molecule) up a steep hill (to split it). A single massive push is needed if you try it in one go (direct thermolysis at >4000 K). Now imagine a relay race of 3–4 workers standing at different elevations of the hill. Each worker, using his own moderate effort (a moderate temperature reaction), lifts the boulder partway up. The last worker finally pushes it over the crest. The intermediate "stones" (chemicals like I₂, SO₂, H₂SO₄, HI) are like buckets passed back down the hill to be re-used. **Net effect: the same boulder reaches the top, but no single worker needed to be a giant.** This is exactly how a thermochemical cycle works.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this relevant for KTU 2024?**
> Hydrogen is the cleanest energy carrier (combustion product = water). But producing it cleanly requires either renewable electricity (electrolysis) **or** high-grade clean heat. TCWS is the only method that converts *heat directly into chemical energy* without intermediate electricity — making it a perfect partner to next-generation Gen-IV nuclear reactors and concentrated solar power (CSP) plants.

### Standard Thermodynamic Anchor Values (KTU Board Favourite)

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Std. Gibbs free energy of water formation | ΔG°f | **−237.2** | kJ mol⁻¹ |
| Std. Enthalpy of water formation | ΔH°f | **−285.8** | kJ mol⁻¹ |
| Std. Entropy of water formation | ΔS°f | **−163.3** | J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ |
| Reference / sink temperature | T₀ | 298 | K |
| Carnot efficiency (at 2500 K source) | η_Carnot | 0.892 | – |
| Minimum thermal energy for splitting (Carnot) | Q_min | 265.9 | kJ mol⁻¹ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Thermochemical Cycle

Any thermochemical cycle must satisfy **three fundamental conditions** to be engineering-viable:

1. **Closure of Mass:** All intermediate reactants (e.g., I₂, SO₂, H₂SO₄, HI, ZnO, Fe₃O₄) must be regenerated; no net chemical consumption.
2. **Positivity of Net ΔG:** At the highest operating temperature T_max, the *sum* of ΔG of the endothermic steps must equal +237.2 kJ mol⁻¹ so that water splitting is spontaneous.
3. **Material Compatibility:** No reaction step should require containment materials beyond the limits of current metallurgy (typically < 950 °C for alloys; < 2000 °C for ceramics).

### 2.2 Why Multi-Step? — The Second-Law Argument

The Gibbs free energy of water splitting decreases with temperature. Above ~4300 K, ΔG becomes negative and direct thermal dissociation is thermodynamically spontaneous. But the *kinetic* problem (slow H-O bond dissociation) and the *materials* problem (no vessel survives 4300 K) make this impossible in practice.

A thermochemical cycle sidesteps this by:

- Operating individual reactions at temperatures engineering materials can handle (typically 500–1000 °C).
- Using chemistry to "amplify" entropy: gases like SO₂, O₂, I₂, HI have much higher entropy at high T than liquid water, which makes the ΔS of the high-T step large and positive.
- Recycling everything except heat and water.

### 2.3 The Three Reference Cycles (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cycle Classification by Maximum Operating Temperature**
> - **Low-T cycles (T_max < 600 °C):** Copper–Chlorine (Cu–Cl) — 530 °C
> - **Mid-T cycles (600–1000 °C):** Sulfur–Iodine (S–I) — 900 °C; Hybrid Sulfur (Westinghouse)
> - **High-T cycles (>1500 °C):** Zinc–Zinc Oxide; Iron Oxide; Cerium Oxide

### 2.4 The Sulfur–Iodine (S–I) Cycle — Step-by-Step Detail

**Step 1 — Bunsen Reaction (Exothermic, 20–120 °C):**

$$ \text{I}_2 + \text{SO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\,\text{HI} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 $$

**Step 2 — Sulfuric Acid Decomposition (Endothermic, 800–900 °C):**

$$ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 $$

**Step 3 — Hydriodic Acid Decomposition (Endothermic, 300–450 °C):**

$$ 2\,\text{HI} \rightarrow \text{I}_2 + \text{H}_2 $$

**Net (after summing):**

$$ \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Bunsen"?** Named after Robert Bunsen (1853), who first used this reaction in a different context (recovering SO₂ from flue gases).

### 2.5 The Zinc–Zinc Oxide Cycle (Two-Step, High-T)

$$ \text{ZnO} \xrightarrow{> 2000\,^\circ\text{C}} \text{Zn} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \quad \text{(solar thermal dissociation)} $$

$$ \text{Zn} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{400\,^\circ\text{C}} \text{ZnO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(exothermic hydrolysis)} $$

### 2.6 The Iron Oxide Cycle (Three-Step)

$$ 6\,\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \xrightarrow{> 1500\,^\circ\text{C}} 4\,\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4 + \text{O}_2 $$

$$ 3\,\text{FeO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{800\,^\circ\text{C}} \text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4 + \text{H}_2 $$

$$ \text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4 + \text{FeO} \xrightarrow{?} \text{regeneration steps} $$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Typical Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|----------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | Net water-splitting reaction | $\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$ | – | Universal |
| 2 | Standard Gibbs energy | $\Delta G^\circ = +237.2$ | kJ mol⁻¹ | Minimum work at 298 K |
| 3 | Standard enthalpy | $\Delta H^\circ = +285.8$ | kJ mol⁻¹ | Total energy input |
| 4 | Standard entropy change | $\Delta S^\circ = +163.3$ | J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ | From $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ |
| 5 | Carnot efficiency | $\eta_C = 1 - T_0 / T_H$ | dimensionless | Max heat-to-work conversion |
| 6 | Minimum heat input (cycle) | $Q_{\min} = \Delta G^\circ \, / \, \eta_C$ | kJ mol⁻¹ | Theoretical best case |
| 7 | Second-law efficiency | $\eta_{II} = W_{\min} / Q_{\text{actual}}$ | dimensionless | Real performance metric |
| 8 | Energy figure of merit | $\text{FoM} = \Delta H^\circ / Q_{\text{cycle}}$ | dimensionless | Cycle quality indicator |
| 9 | Equilibrium constant (H₂O) | $K_p = (p_{\text{H}_2} \cdot p_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}) / p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$ | (atm)^½ | Thermolysis studies |
| 10 | Van't Hoff (K vs T) | $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R\,T^2}$ | K⁻¹ | Predicting reaction shift with T |

> [!TIP]
