> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The most commonly asked formula on TCWS is the relation between minimum heat and Carnot efficiency: $Q_{\min} = \Delta G / \eta_C$. Memorize the values 237.2 kJ/mol and 285.8 kJ/mol — they appear in almost every numerical.

### 2.8 Engineering Real-World Utility

Thermochemical water splitting is the backbone of several demonstration plants worldwide:

- **General Atomics (USA):** Pilot S–I cycle coupled to a high-temperature gas-cooled nuclear reactor.
- **JAEA (Japan):** S–I loop completed 150 hours of continuous H₂ production in the HTTR project.
- **ANU (Australia):** Zn–ZnO "SynMet" solar reactor (solar concentrator > 3000 suns).
- **Atomic Energy of Canada Limited (AECL):** Cu–Cl cycle pilot plant at Chalk River, designed for 460–530 °C operation, well-matched to Canada’s CANDU supercritical water reactor.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Heat Required for a Thermochemical Cycle

**Problem Statement:** Given a high-temperature thermal source at temperature $T_H$ and ambient sink at $T_0 = 298\,\text{K}$, find the minimum quantity of heat $Q_{\min}$ that must be supplied to split one mole of water.

**Step 1 — State the requirement.**

The water-splitting reaction is non-spontaneous at ambient conditions; the *minimum* useful work needed is the Gibbs free energy:

$$ W_{\min} = \Delta G^\circ_{298} = +237.2\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

**Step 2 — Relate work output to heat input via Carnot efficiency.**

The maximum possible conversion of supplied heat $Q$ into useful work is given by the Carnot efficiency, which depends only on the source temperature $T_H$ and the sink temperature $T_0$:

$$ \eta_C = \frac{W_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{T_0}{T_H} $$

**Step 3 — Equate maximum work obtainable to minimum work needed.**

For a reversible (ideal) cycle, the work obtained from $Q_{\min}$ equals the work required for splitting:

$$ W_{\min} = \eta_C \cdot Q_{\min} $$

Substituting the two equations:

$$ \Delta G^\circ = \left(1 - \frac{T_0}{T_H}\right) \cdot Q_{\min} $$

**Step 4 — Solve for $Q_{\min}$.**

$$ Q_{\min} = \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{1 - \dfrac{T_0}{T_H}} = \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{\eta_C} $$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation at $T_H = 2500\,\text{K}$ (typical solar receiver temperature).**

$$ \eta_C = 1 - \frac{298}{2500} = 1 - 0.1192 = 0.8808 $$

$$ Q_{\min} = \frac{237.2}{0.8808} = 269.3\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation at $T_H = 1000\,\text{K}$ (Cu–Cl cycle temperature).**

$$ \eta_C = 1 - \frac{298}{1000} = 0.702 $$

$$ Q_{\min} = \frac{237.2}{0.702} = 337.9\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

**Step 7 — Interpretation.**

A higher source temperature reduces the heat required (Carnot efficiency rises), but demands exotic materials. The Cu–Cl cycle is favoured for its moderate temperature (≈ 530 °C, well within stainless-steel capability).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Second-Law Efficiency of a Practical S–I Cycle

**Problem Statement:** A pilot S–I plant operates with $T_H = 900\,^\circ\text{C}$ and consumes $Q_{\text{actual}} = 850\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ of heat. The plant also uses 30 kJ mol⁻¹ of electrical work for pumps and gas-separation. Compute the second-law efficiency.

**Step 1 — Compute Carnot efficiency at $T_H = 1173\,\text{K}$.**

$$ \eta_C = 1 - \frac{298}{1173} = 1 - 0.2541 = 0.7459 $$

**Step 2 — Compute the total primary-energy input in "heat-equivalent" terms.**

The electrical work, if generated from the same thermal source, would require heat input of $W_{\text{el}}/\eta_C$. So the equivalent total heat is:

$$ Q_{\text{eq}} = Q_{\text{actual}} + \frac{W_{\text{el}}}{\eta_C} $$

$$ Q_{\text{eq}} = 850 + \frac{30}{0.7459} = 850 + 40.2 = 890.2\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the second-law efficiency.**

$$ \eta_{II} = \frac{W_{\min}}{Q_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{237.2}{890.2} = 0.2664 \approx 26.6\% $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

A second-law efficiency of 26.6% means only about one-quarter of the *quality* of the supplied heat is converted into the chemical potential of hydrogen. The remaining ~73% is degraded — primarily by irreversibilities in heat exchangers, finite-temperature-difference heat transfer, and the auxiliary electric power. State-of-the-art S–I cycles in Japan have achieved η_II ≈ 35–40%.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Energy Figure of Merit of the Zn–ZnO Cycle

**Problem Statement:** Compute the energy figure of merit $\text{FoM} = \Delta H^\circ / Q_{\text{cycle}}$ for the Zn–ZnO cycle, where the net cycle heat $Q_{\text{cycle}} = 530\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ (literature average).

**Step 1 — Recall the standard enthalpy of water splitting.**

$$ \Delta H^\circ = +285.8\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of FoM.**

$$ \text{FoM} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{Q_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{285.8}{530} = 0.539 $$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

A FoM of 0.539 means the cycle uses about 1.85× more energy than the thermodynamic minimum. This is acceptable for a solar-driven two-step cycle where solar energy is essentially free, but unattractive when heat is expensive (e.g., electricity-derived resistance heating).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cycle Comparison (KTU Board Reference)

| Parameter | S–I | Cu–Cl | Zn–ZnO | Fe-Oxide | CeO₂/Ce₂O₃ |
|-----------|-----|-------|--------|----------|-------------|
| No. of reaction steps | 3 | 4–5 | 2 | 3 | 2 |
| $T_{\max}$ (°C) | 900 | 530 | >2000 | >1500 | >2000 |
| $T_{\min}$ (°C) | 20 | <100 | 400 | 800 | ~500 |
| Cycle efficiency η_II (target %) | 40–50 | 40–45 | 30–40 | 35–45 | 30–40 |
| Technology readiness | Pilot | Lab | Lab–Pilot | Lab | Lab |
| Key advantage | High T efficiency | Low T, uses waste heat | Simplicity | Uses cheap iron | Uses cheap ceria |
| Key challenge | Corrosive acids (H₂SO₄, HI) | CuCl₂ handling | Quench of Zn vapour | High T heat source | High T heat source |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sulfur–Iodine (S–I) Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Solar Concentrator OR Nuclear Reactor T 900 C] --> Q1[High Temperature Heat Q H]
    Q1 --> R1[Reactor 1 H2SO4 Decomposition 800 to 900 C]
    R1 --> P1[P1 SO2 plus H2O plus 0.5 O2]
    P1 --> S1[Separator O2 removed]
    S1 --> R2[Reactor 2 Bunsen Reaction 120 C with I2 plus H2O]
    R2 --> P2[P2 H2SO4 plus HI acid mixture]
    P2 --> S2[Separator 1 H2SO4 fraction]
    S2 --> S3[Separator 2 HI fraction]
    S3 --> R3[Reactor 3 HI Decomposition 300 to 450 C]
    R3 --> O1[Output H2 to Storage]
    O1 --> O2[Output O2 to Vent or Use]
    R2 -.->|recycle I2| S3
    S2 -.->|recycle H2SO4| R1
    A2[Water Feed H2O] --> R2
    A2 --> R3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed recycle loops in the Mermaid diagram are the **key** to a thermochemical cycle: all intermediate chemicals (I₂, H₂SO₄) are returned to upstream reactors. Only heat, water, H₂, and O₂ cross the system boundary.

### 4.2 Process Block Diagram — Two-Step Zn–ZnO Solar Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Sun[Solar Dish Concentrator 3000 to 5000 suns] --> Recv[Windowless Cavity Receiver 2000 C plus]
    Air[Ambient Air Quench] --> Recv
    Recv --> Dis[Dissociation ZnO to Zn plus 0.5 O2]
    Dis --> Mix[Mixing with Quench Gas]
    Mix --> Sep[Separator Zn Powder and O2 Stream]
    Sep --> Zn1[Zn Powder to Hydrolyser]
    Sep --> O2out[O2 Storage]
    Zn1 --> Hyd[Hydrolyser 400 C with H2O vapor]
    Hyd --> Prod[Products ZnO plus H2]
    Prod --> Sep2[Separator ZnO recycled]
    Sep2 --> H2out[H2 to Storage or Pipeline]
    Sep2 -.->|solid recycle| Dis
```

### 4.3 Thermodynamic State-Point Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H0[Point 0 H2O liquid 298 K 1 bar] --> H1[Point 1 H2O vapor superheated to T H]
    H1 --> H2[Point 2 Endothermic Reaction ΔG positive 0 with T increase]
    H2 --> H3[Point 3 Reaction Products high T gas]
    H3 --> H4[Point 4 Heat Rejection to 298 K]
    H4 --> H5[Point 5 Final H2 and 0.5 O2 separated]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the state-point diagram:** A thermochemical cycle is essentially a *chemical heat pump*. The H₂O absorbs heat $Q_H$ at high temperature, climbs the chemical-potential "hill," then rejects heat $Q_0$ at low temperature. The *net* energy stored in H₂ is the *Gibbs free energy difference* ($\Delta G$), exactly as in a mechanical heat engine. This analogy is the heart of second-law analysis in KTU paper 2.

### 4.4 Cycle Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Selecting a Thermochemical Cycle] --> Q1{Available Heat Source Temperature}
    Q1 -->|Below 600 C| L[Low T Cycle]
    Q1 -->|600 to 1000 C| M[Mid T Cycle]
    Q1 -->|Above 1500 C| H[High T Cycle]
    L --> CuCl[Cu Cl Cycle 530 C]
    M --> SI[S I Cycle 900 C]
    H --> ZnO[Zn ZnO Cycle 2000 C]
    H --> FeO[Fe Oxide Cycle 1500 C]
    H --> CeO[Ce Oxide Cycle 2000 C]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Define a thermochemical water-splitting cycle. Why is a multi-step cycle preferred over direct thermal dissociation of water at very high temperature?

**Model Answer:**

A thermochemical water-splitting cycle is a closed-loop sequence of chemical reactions, driven by thermal energy, that decomposes water into hydrogen and oxygen while regenerating all intermediate reactants. Multi-step cycles are preferred over direct thermal dissociation because:

1. Direct thermolysis of H₂O requires temperatures above ~4300 K (where ΔG becomes negative), which exceeds the capability of any known engineering material for containment.
2. In a multi-step cycle, the energy demand is *redistributed* across several reactions, each occurring at a temperature compatible with metallic or ceramic reactors (typically 500–1000 °C).
3. The intermediate reactions exploit large entropy changes of gases (e.g., SO₂, O₂, I₂) to drive the conversion at lower temperatures, while keeping mass balance closed.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> List any three thermochemical cycles with their maximum operating temperatures.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Cycle | $T_{\max}$ (°C) |
|---|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Copper–Chlorine (Cu–Cl) | 530 |
| 2 | Sulfur–Iodine (S–I) | 900 |
| 3 | Zinc–Zinc Oxide (Zn–ZnO) | > 2000 |
| 4 | Iron Oxide (Fe-Oxide) | > 1500 |
| 5 | Cerium Oxide (CeO₂/Ce₂O₃) | > 2000 |

(Any three with their temperatures — full marks.)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** With neat reaction steps, explain the Sulfur–Iodine (S–I) thermochemical cycle for hydrogen production. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A thermochemical cycle operates with a heat source at 1000 K. Compute (i) the Carnot efficiency, (ii) the minimum heat required per mole of H₂ produced, and (iii) the second-law efficiency if the actual heat input is 400 kJ mol⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The S–I Cycle (7 marks):**

The S–I cycle, originally proposed by General Atomics, is a 3-step closed cycle. The reactions are:

**Step 1 — Bunsen Reaction (Exothermic, 20–120 °C):**

$$ \text{I}_2 + \text{SO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\,\text{HI} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 $$

**Step 2 — Sulfuric Acid Decomposition (Endothermic, 800–900 °C):**

$$ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 $$

**Step 3 — Hydriodic Acid Decomposition (Endothermic, 300–450 °C):**

$$ 2\,\text{HI} \rightarrow \text{I}_2 + \text{H}_2 $$

**Net (sum of all three):**

$$ \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 $$

Process flow: SO₂ and I₂ are reacted with water in the Bunsen reactor to produce two immiscible acid phases — a lighter HI phase and a heavier H₂SO₄ phase. The H₂SO₄ phase is vaporised and catalytically decomposed at ~850 °C in the presence of a solid catalyst (e.g., platinum or vanadium pentoxide) to release O₂ and regenerate SO₂. The HI phase is concentrated and decomposed at ~400 °C over a Pt catalyst to release H₂ and regenerate I₂. Both SO₂ and I₂ are recycled to the Bunsen reactor, closing the loop.

The key engineering challenge is the highly corrosive nature of concentrated H₂SO₄ and HI at high temperatures, requiring exotic materials (e.g., silicon carbide, tantalum-clad vessels).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T_H = 1000\,\text{K}$, $T_0 = 298\,\text{K}$, $\Delta G^\circ = 237.2\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, $Q_{\text{actual}} = 400\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$.

**(i) Carnot efficiency:**

$$ \eta_C = 1 - \frac{T_0}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{298}{1000} = 1 - 0.298 = 0.702 $$

$$ \boxed{\eta_C = 0.702 \text{ or } 70.2\%} $$

**(ii) Minimum heat required:**

$$ Q_{\min} = \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{\eta_C} = \frac{237.2}{0.702} = 337.9\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

$$ \boxed{Q_{\min} = 337.9\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}} $$

**(iii) Second-law efficiency:**

$$ \eta_{II} = \frac{W_{\min}}{Q_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{237.2}{400} = 0.593 $$

$$ \boxed{\eta_{II} = 0.593 \text{ or } 59.3\%} $$

**Interpretation:** A second-law efficiency of 59.3% is excellent — among the highest reported in pilot S–I plants. A practical upper bound of ~60% is the thermodynamic target for commercial S–I deployment.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)**

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the Zinc–Zinc Oxide (Zn–ZnO) two-step thermochemical cycle. What are its main advantages and disadvantages? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A research reactor delivers heat at 2000 K. For the water-splitting reaction, calculate the minimum quantity of heat required per kg of H₂ produced. Take $\Delta G^\circ_{298} = 237.2\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ and $M_{\text{H}_2} = 2\,\text{g mol}^{-1}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Zn–ZnO Cycle (7 marks):**

The Zinc–Zinc Oxide cycle is the simplest thermochemical cycle, consisting of only two reaction steps:

**Step 1 — Solar Thermal Dissociation (Endothermic, T > 2000 °C):**

$$ \text{ZnO(s)} \xrightarrow{\text{solar}} \text{Zn(g)} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2\text{(g)} $$

This reaction is carried out in a windowless cavity receiver subjected to concentrated solar radiation (typically 3000–5000 suns). At temperatures above 2000 °C, the dissociation of ZnO becomes thermodynamically favourable.

**Step 2 — Hydrolysis (Exothermic, 400–700 °C):**

$$ \text{Zn(g or l)} + \text{H}_2\text{O(g)} \rightarrow \text{ZnO(s)} + \text{H}_2\text{(g)} $$

The Zn vapour (or quenched Zn droplets) reacts with steam to regenerate ZnO and produce H₂.

**Net reaction:**

$$ \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 $$

**Advantages:**
- Only two steps — simple reactor design.
- Uses widely available, non-toxic, inexpensive materials (ZnO is a commodity chemical).
- High theoretical efficiency (~45%).
- Naturally paired with concentrated solar power (CSP).

**Disadvantages:**
- The dissociation step requires extremely high temperature (>2000 °C), demanding advanced ceramic receivers and effective thermal insulation.
- Zn vapour must be rapidly quenched to prevent re-oxidation (back-reaction) — a major engineering challenge.
- Solar-only — not suitable for baseload operation without thermal storage.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T_H = 2000\,\text{K}$, $T_0 = 298\,\text{K}$, $\Delta G^\circ = 237.2\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, $M_{\text{H}_2} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg mol}^{-1}$.

**Step 1 — Compute Carnot efficiency:**

$$ \eta_C = 1 - \frac{T_0}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{298}{2000} = 1 - 0.149 = 0.851 $$

**Step 2 — Compute minimum heat per mole of H₂:**

$$ Q_{\min} = \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{\eta_C} = \frac{237.2}{0.851} = 278.7\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

**Step 3 — Convert to per kilogram of H₂:**

The number of moles in 1 kg of H₂:

$$ n = \frac{1\,\text{kg}}{2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg mol}^{-1}} = 500\,\text{mol} $$

Therefore:

$$ Q_{\min} \text{ per kg} = 278.7 \times 500 = 139\,350\,\text{kJ} = 1.394 \times 10^{5}\,\text{kJ kg}^{-1} $$

Equivalently:

$$ Q_{\min} \text{ per kg} = \frac{1.394 \times 10^{5}}{3600} \approx 38.7\,\text{kWh kg}^{-1} $$

$$ \boxed{Q_{\min} \approx 1.39 \times 10^{5}\,\text{kJ per kg of H}_2 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 38.7\,\text{kWh kg}^{-1}} $$

**Sanity check:** A typical alkaline electrolyser requires ~50–55 kWh kg⁻¹ of electricity. A thermochemical cycle using 2000 K heat matches that order of magnitude — confirming that thermochemical splitting is competitive when abundant high-grade heat is available (e.g., from Gen-IV reactors or solar concentrators).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 1000 in unit conversion** when going from kJ mol⁻¹ to kJ kg⁻¹. Many students write 278.7 as the final answer; the correct per-kg value is **278.7 × 500 = 1.39 × 10⁵ kJ**.
> 2. **Confusing ΔG with ΔH.** Use $\Delta G$ (237.2 kJ mol⁻¹) for *minimum work / Carnot-limited heat*. Use $\Delta H$ (285.8 kJ mol⁻¹) only for *total enthalpy change* or figure of merit. KTU examiners **always** deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 3. **Forgetting the recycle of intermediates.** When writing net reactions in Part (a), explicitly *list* the regenerated species (I₂, SO₂, ZnO). Omitting the recycle clause costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong reaction temperature assignment.** Step 1 of S–I cycle is exothermic and low-T (~120 °C). The endothermic, high-T step is the **sulfuric acid decomposition**, not the Bunsen reaction. Reversing this is a frequent error.
> 5. **Not stating units explicitly in numerical answers.** Always write "kJ mol⁻¹" or "kJ kg⁻¹" — units-free answers attract an automatic 0.5 mark deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A thermochemical water-splitting cycle is a *closed-loop* set of reactions using *only heat and water* to produce H₂ and O₂, regenerating all intermediates.
- **Net reaction (universal):** $\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$.
- **Key constant (memorize):** $\Delta G^\circ_{298} = +237.2\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$; $\Delta H^\circ_{298} = +285.8\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$.
- **Why multi-step?** Direct thermolysis needs T > 4300 K; thermochemical cycles reduce this to 500–2000 °C, compatible with materials.
- **Three reference cycles:**
  - *S–I (Sulfur–Iodine):* 3 steps, $T_{\max} = 900\,^\circ\text{C}$, highest η_II target (~50%), corrosive.
  - *Cu–Cl (Copper–Chlorine):* 4–5 steps, $T_{\max} = 530\,^\circ\text{C}$, paired with Gen-IV SMR/CANDU reactors.
  - *Zn–ZnO (Zinc–Zinc Oxide):* 2 steps, $T_{\max} > 2000\,^\circ\text{C}$, solar-only, simplest design.
- **Carnot-limited heat formula (high-yield):** $Q_{\min} = \Delta G^\circ / \eta_C = \Delta G^\circ / (1 - T_0/T_H)$.
- **Second-law efficiency (definition):** $\eta_{II} = W_{\min} / Q_{\text{actual}}$.
- **Three conditions for any viable cycle:** mass closure, $\Sigma \Delta G = +237.2\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ at the highest T, and material compatibility.
- **Material challenges:** Concentrated H₂SO₄/HI (S–I) and molten CuCl₂ (Cu–Cl) require silicon-carbide or tantalum-clad reactors.
- **Global flagship projects:** General Atomics (USA), JAEA/HTTR (Japan), AECL/Chalk River (Canada), ANU SynMet (Australia).
- **KTU exam focus areas:** (1) Writing the 3 S–I reactions and net; (2) Carnot + minimum heat numericals; (3) Second-law efficiency; (4) Cycle selection by temperature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biological Process: Hydrogen Production from Microbial Biomass Conversion - Gasification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Biological Process: Hydrogen Production from Microbial Biomass Conversion - Gasification # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Microbial Biomass Conversion – Gasification** is defined as a thermochemical conversion process in which organic biomass of microbial origin (such as microalgae, cyanobacteria-derived biowaste, sewage sludge, biogas digestate, and lignocellulosic microbial residues) is subjected to partial oxidation at elevated temperatures, typically in the range of **700 °C – 1200 °C**, in a controlled oxygen-deficient (sub-stoichiometric) environment using a gasifying agent (air, pure oxygen, steam, or CO₂) to produce a hydrogen-rich synthesis gas (syngas). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > Biomass gasification is the *partial oxidation* of carbonaceous biomass at high temperatures using limited oxygen (or steam) to produce a combustible gas mixture predominantly containing **H₂, CO, CO₂, CH₄**, and trace higher hydrocarbons, called **producer gas** or **syngas**. Hydrogen is then enriched through the Water-Gas Shift (WGS) reaction. The generalized representation of a microbial biomass feedstock with elemental composition $C_n H_a O_b N_c S_d$ undergoing gasification yields: $$C_n H_a O_b N_c S_d + \text{Gasifying Agent} \xrightarrow{\Delta, \ 700\text{-}1200^\circ C} \text{Syngas } (H_2 + CO + CO_2 + CH_4 + N_2 + H_2O + \text{tar})$$ --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Burnt Toast Logic" > [!NOTE] > **Think of it like making burnt toast on a controlled stove.** When bread (biomass) is completely burned with plenty of oxygen, it becomes ash (full combustion: CO₂ + H₂O). But if you **restrict the oxygen** (turn the gas stove to "low") and apply **high heat**, the bread does not fully combust — instead, it partially chars and releases flammable vapors (CO + H₂). This is exactly what gasification does: **starve the fuel of oxygen but feed it heat** so that it breaks down into useful combustible gases rather than wasting its energy as heat of complete combustion. - **Microbial biomass** = the "toast" (energy-rich organic matter). - **Limited oxygen / steam** = the "low-flame setting". - **High temperature (~800 °C)** = the "toaster heat". - **Syngas (H₂ + CO)** = the "useful vapors" released. - **Ash / char** = the "charred residue". This analogy helps internalize the core idea: gasification is **incomplete combustion** engineered to maximize gaseous fuel production. --- ## 1.3 Why Hydrogen from Microbial Biomass Gasification? Microbial biomass (especially microalgae *Chlorella vulgaris*, *Spirulina platensis*, and bacterial cellulose residues) is attractive because: - It is **carbon-neutral** (CO₂ absorbed during growth). - It does **not compete with food crops** (unlike corn/soy). - It contains **high lipid/protein fractions** that give higher H₂ yield per tonne. - It can be grown on **wastewater, saline water, or industrial flue gas**. - Has very **short growth cycles** (~24–72 hours doubling time). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Metric (Memorize):** Theoretical maximum hydrogen yield from microalgae gasification is approximately **167 g H₂ per kg of dry biomass**, which is significantly higher than lignocellulosic biomass (~110 g H₂/kg) due to higher hydrogen-to-carbon atomic ratio. --- ## 1.4 Microbial Biomass Classification | Class | Examples | Typical H/C Atomic Ratio | H₂ Yield Potential | |---|---|---|---| | Microalgae | *Chlorella vulgaris*, *Spirulina* | 1.7 – 2.0 | High | | Cyanobacteria | *Anabaena*, *Synechococcus* | 1.8 – 2.1 | Very High | | Bacterial cellulose | *Acetobacter xylinum* | 1.6 – 1.9 | High | | Sewage sludge | Municipal / Industrial | 1.4 – 1.7 | Moderate | | Biogas digestate | Anaerobic digester residue | 1.2 – 1.5 | Moderate | > [!NOTE] > The **higher the H/C atomic ratio** in the feedstock, the greater the theoretical H₂ yield, because more hydrogen atoms are intrinsically available in the molecular structure for release during gasification. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Stages of Biomass Gasification Gasification proceeds through **four sequential thermo-chemical stages**. Each stage is critical for hydrogen yield optimization. ### Stage 1 — Drying (Zone 1: 100 °C – 200 °C) - **Goal:** Remove free and bound moisture. - **Process:** Endothermic evaporation. - **Energy demand:** ~2260 kJ per kg of water vaporized (latent heat). - Moisture content should be reduced below **15 % (wt)** before entering the reaction zone. $$H_2O_{(l)} \xrightarrow{100^\circ C} H_2O_{(g)} \quad \Delta H_{vap} = +40.65 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ ### Stage 2 — Pyrolysis / Devolatilization (Zone 2: 200 °C – 700 °C) - **Goal:** Thermal decomposition of biomass into char, tar, and volatile gases. - **Process:** Endothermic at lower T, exothermic at higher T. - Major products released: **CO, CO₂, H₂, CH₄, light hydrocarbons, tars, water vapor**. $$C_n H_a O_b N_c S_d \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Char} + \text{Tar} + \text{Gases}(CO + CO_2 + H_2 + CH_4)$$ ### Stage 3 — Gasification / Char Reactions (Zone 3: 700 °C – 1200 °C) The heart of the process. Heterogeneous reactions between char and gasifying medium occur here. ### Stage 4 — Combustion (Partial Oxidation Zone: 800 °C – 1500 °C) A small fraction of char is combusted to **supply the process heat** required for endothermic Stages 1–3. This is what makes the process **autothermal** when operated correctly. --- ## 2.2 Core Gasification Reactions (THE HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET) > [!IMPORTANT] > **These seven reactions are the spine of KTU Module-2 questions. Memorize the stoichiometry, sign of ΔH, and reaction name.** | # | Reaction Name | Stoichiometric Equation | ΔH°₂₉₈ (kJ/mol) | Type | |---|---|---|---|---| | R1 | **Boudouard Reaction** | $C + CO_2 \rightleftharpoons 2CO$ | **+172** kJ/mol | Endothermic | | R2 | **Water–Gas Reaction (Primary)** | $C + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + H_2$ | **+131** kJ/mol | Endothermic | | R3 | **Water–Gas Shift (WGS)** | $CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2$ | **–41** kJ/mol | Exothermic | | R4 | **Methanation** | $C + 2H_2 \rightleftharpoons CH_4$ | **–75** kJ/mol | Exothermic | | R5 | **Steam Reforming of Methane** | $CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2$ | **+206** kJ/mol | Endothermic | | R6 | **Partial Oxidation** | $C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO$ | **–111** kJ/mol | Exothermic | | R7 | **Complete Combustion** | $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ | **–394** kJ/mol | Exothermic | --- ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Why Each Reaction Matters - **R1 (Boudouard):** Critical at high temperatures (>700 °C) — drives carbon conversion to CO, the building block of syngas. Higher T shifts equilibrium to the right (Le Chatelier). - **R2 (Water-Gas):** This is the **direct hydrogen-producing reaction** from solid carbon. Steam is the gasifying agent. Endothermic → needs heat input. - **R3 (WGS):** The **hydrogen amplification reaction**. One mole of CO + one mole of H₂O gives one mole of CO₂ + one mole of H₂. **Net H₂ gain!** Operated in two stages (HT-WGS at 350 °C and LT-WGS at 200 °C) industrially. - **R4 (Methanation):** Undesirable when targeting H₂, because it **consumes H₂**. Must be suppressed by operating at high T and using catalysts that selectively suppress CH₄ formation. - **R5 (Steam Reforming):** Dominant when biomass contains high CH₄ (e.g., from anaerobic digestate). Each mole of CH₄ produces 3 moles of H₂. - **R6 (Partial Oxidation):** Provides process heat. Controlled oxygen feed (ER < 0.3) is critical. - **R7 (Complete Combustion):** Should be minimized because it produces no H₂. > [!NOTE] > **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** is the most important operating parameter: > $$ER = \frac{\text{Actual Oxygen Supplied}}{\text{Stoichiometric Oxygen for Complete Combustion}}$$ > Optimal ER for hydrogen-rich syngas: **0.20 – 0.30**. Beyond ER = 0.4, combustion dominates and H₂ yield collapses. --- ## 2.4 Gasifying Agents — Comparative Analysis | Agent | Syngas H₂ Content (vol %) | Calorific Value (MJ/Nm³) | Operating T (°C) | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | Air | 10 – 16 % | 4 – 7 | 700 – 900 | Low-cost, N₂ dilution reduces H₂ fraction | | Pure O₂ | 25 – 35 % | 10 – 18 | 800 – 1200 | Higher H₂, no N₂, expensive O₂ plant | | Steam | 30 – 60 % | 12 – 20 | 700 – 1100 | Highest H₂ yield, endothermic process | | CO₂ | 20 – 30 % | 8 – 12 | 800 – 1000 | Emerging option, utilizes captured CO₂ | | Steam-O₂ mixture | 35 – 50 % | 14 – 19 | 850 – 1100 | Industrial best-practice (auto-thermal) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Steam gasification** produces the highest hydrogen yield (50–60 vol %) because steam directly feeds the water-gas (R2) and WGS (R3) reactions. However, it is **highly endothermic** and requires external heat input or coupling with partial oxidation. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Shell, Denmark** operates a 50 MWth biomass gasification pilot integrated with a steam reformer + WGS unit producing **green H₂** for refinery use. - **Pacific Northwest National Lab (PNNL)** has demonstrated **supercritical water gasification** of microalgae at 600 °C / 25 MPa achieving >95 % carbon-to-gas conversion and ~55 % H₂ in syngas. - **India (IIT Bombay, TERI)** has piloted air–steam gasification of rice straw and *Chlorella* biomass for decentralized H₂ production. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Theoretical Hydrogen Yield from Biomass Elemental Composition **Given:** A microbial biomass feedstock with ultimate (elemental) analysis given as: $$C_n H_a O_b N_c S_d$$ where the coefficients are the **mole numbers per mole of biomass** (or per kg basis). For gasification in pure steam with no oxygen input, we apply atomic balances. **Step 1 — Carbon Balance** Every carbon atom ends up either in **CO** or **CO₂**. Let $x$ = moles of CO produced per mole of biomass; $y$ = moles of CO₂ produced. $$n = x + y \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1)$$ **Step 2 — Hydrogen Balance** Hydrogen atoms end up in H₂ (produced), H₂O (unreacted steam), and CH₄. Let $m$ = moles of H₂ and $p$ = moles of CH₄ produced. $$\frac{a}{2} + \text{moles of steam} = m + 2p + \text{moles of unreacted steam} \quad (2)$$ For maximum H₂ yield, we set $p = 0$ (suppress methanation). For pure steam gasification with no air ingress, the oxygen atoms are supplied entirely by H₂O. **Step 3 — Oxygen Balance** O atoms in biomass ($b$) + O atoms from steam = O atoms in CO + 2×O atoms in CO₂ + O atoms in unreacted steam. $$b + \text{moles of steam} = x + 2y + \text{moles of unreacted steam} \quad (3)$$ **Step 4 — Solving for H₂ Yield** Let $S$ = moles of steam supplied. Equating (2) and (3) under the constraint $p = 0$: $$\frac{a}{2} + S = m + S_{\text{unreacted}}$$ $$b + S = x + 2y + S_{\text{unreacted}}$$ Subtracting: $$\frac{a}{2} - b = m - (x + 2y) = m - n \quad \text{(using (1))}$$ Therefore, **the theoretical maximum H₂ yield** is: $$\boxed{m_{\text{max}} = \frac{a}{2} - b + n}$$ **Step 4 (Verification):** For pure cellulose $(C_6 H_{10} O_5)$, $n=6$, $a=10$, $b=5$: $$m_{\text{max}} = \frac{10}{2} - 5 + 6 = 5 - 5 + 6 = 6 \text{ mol } H_2$$ This matches the well-known reaction: $$C_6 H_{10} O_5 + H_2 O \rightarrow 6 CO + 6 H_2$$ Mass yield: $(6 \times 2) = 12$ g H₂ from 162 g cellulose = **74 g H₂ / kg cellulose**. ✓ --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Gasification Efficiency Definitions (KTU-Favorite) ### Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE) $$CGE = \frac{\text{Energy content of produced syngas (LHV basis)}}{\text{Energy content of biomass feed (HHV basis)}} \times 100\%$$ $$CGE = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{syngas}} \times LHV_{\text{syngas}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{biomass}} \times HHV_{\text{biomass}}} \times 100\%$$ Typical CGE for air–steam gasification: **70 – 85 %**. ### Carbon Conversion Efficiency (CCE) $$CCE = \frac{\text{Mass of C in syngas (as CO + CO}_2 + CH_4)}{\text{Mass of C in biomass feed}} \times 100\%$$ Typical CCE for microalgae at 850 °C with steam: **88 – 96 %**. ### Hydrogen Yield Efficiency (HYE) $$HYE = \frac{\text{Actual } H_2 \text{ produced (mol)}}{\text{Theoretical maximum } H_2 \text{ (per stoichiometry)}} \times 100\%$$ --- ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — *Chlorella vulgaris* Steam Gasification **Given Data:** - Feedstock: 100 kg/hr of dry *Chlorella vulgaris* - Ultimate analysis (dry, wt %): C = 49.5 %, H = 7.0 %, O = 33.0 %, N = 8.5 %, S = 2.0 % - Operating conditions: 850 °C, 1 atm, steam-to-biomass ratio (S/B) = 1.2 - Steam gasification, ER = 0 **Step 1 — Convert wt% to moles (per kg basis, M = 1 kg = 1000 g)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Moles of C} &= \frac{495}{12} = 41.25 \text{ mol} \\ \text{Moles of H} &= \frac{70}{1} = 70.00 \text{ mol} \\ \text{Moles of O} &= \frac{330}{16} = 20.625 \text{ mol} \\ \text{Moles of N} &= \frac{85}{14} = 6.07 \text{ mol} \\ \text{Moles of S} &= \frac{20}{32} = 0.625 \text{ mol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Moles of steam supplied (per kg biomass)** $$ S = \frac{S/B \times 1000}{18} = \frac{1.2 \times 1000}{18} = 66.67 \text{ mol} $$ **Step 3 — Theoretical maximum H₂** Using the formula derived in 3.1: $$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{max}} &= \frac{a}{2} - b + n \\ &= \frac{70}{2} - 20.625 + 41.25 \\ &= 35 - 20.625 + 41.25 \\ &= 55.625 \text{ mol } H_2 \text{ per kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Mass yield** $$ \text{Mass } H_2 = 55.625 \times 2 = 111.25 \text{ g } H_2 \text{ / kg dry biomass} $$ **Step 5 — For 100 kg/hr feed** $$ H_2 \text{ production rate} = 111.25 \times 100 = 11.125 \text{ kg/hr } = 11,125 \text{ g/hr} $$ **Step 6 — Convert to Nm³/hr** (at STP, molar volume = 22.414 L/mol = 0.022414 Nm³/mol) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Molar flow rate} &= \frac{11125}{2} = 5562.5 \text{ mol/hr} \\ \text{Volume flow rate} &= 5562.5 \times 0.022414 = 124.7 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer for the worked example:** The theoretical H₂ yield is **111.25 g H₂ / kg dry *Chlorella vulgaris***, equivalent to **124.7 Nm³/hr** for a 100 kg/hr feed. Actual yields with WGS will be 10–20 % higher (~135–145 g/kg). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Sensitivity | Parameter | Direction of Change | Effect on H₂ Yield | Effect on CO Yield | Effect on Tar | |---|---|---|---|---| | Temperature ↑ | Increase | ↑↑ Strong increase | ↑ Increase | ↓↓ Decrease | | Steam/Biomass ratio ↑ | Increase | ↑↑ Increase | ↓ Decrease | ↓ Decrease | | Equivalence Ratio ↑ | Increase | ↓↓ Strong decrease | ↓ Decrease | ↓ Decrease | | Pressure ↑ | Increase | ↓ Slight decrease | ↑ Slight increase | ↑ Increase | | Residence time ↑ | Increase | ↑ Moderate increase | ↑ Moderate increase | ↓ Decrease | | Catalyst (Ni-based) | Add | ↑↑ Significant increase | ↓ Decrease | ↓↓ Strong decrease | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Mnemonic:** "**T**emperature, **S**team, **C**atalyst → **T**hree pillars for maximum H₂" (increase all three; control ER low). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Biomass Gasification System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Microbial Biomass Feedstock
Microalgae, Cyanobacteria, Sewage Sludge] --> B[Drying Unit
100 to 200 C] B --> C[Size Reduction
Crusher and Grinder
less than 2 mm] C --> D[Gasifier Reactor
700 to 1200 C] D --> E[Heat Source
Partial Oxidation or External Furnace] E --> D D --> F[Syngas Outlet
H2 CO CO2 CH4 N2 H2O Tar] F --> G[Cyclone Separator
Particulate Removal] G --> H[Quench and Scrubber
Tar and Moisture Removal] H --> I[Compressor
1 to 30 bar] I --> J[High Temperature WGS Reactor
350 C, Fe Cr Catalyst] J --> K[Low Temperature WGS Reactor
200 C, Cu Zn Catalyst] K --> L[CO2 Removal Unit
PSA or MEA Scrubbing] L --> M[Pressure Swing Adsorption
99.99 percent H2 Purity] M --> N[Pure Hydrogen Output
Storage or Utilization] M --> O[Offgas Tail
Recycle to Gasifier] F --> P[Ash and Char Collection
Bottom Discharge] H --> Q[Tar Condensate
Treatment or Recycle] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Four Reaction Zones Inside the Gasifier ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Zone1[Drying Zone 100 to 200 C] Z1[Biomass Moisture
Released as H2O Vapor] end subgraph Zone2[Pyrolysis Zone 200 to 700 C] Z2[Volatile Release
CO, CO2, H2, CH4, Tar, Char] end subgraph Zone3[Reduction Zone 700 to 1100 C] Z3[Char Gasification
Boudouard and Water Gas Reactions] end subgraph Zone4[Combustion Zone 800 to 1500 C] Z4[Partial Oxidation
Heat Generation ER less than 0.3] end Z1 --> Z2 Z2 --> Z3 Z3 --> Z4 Z4 -. Heat Recycle .-> Z2 Z4 -. Heat Recycle .-> Z3 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Logic — Choice of Gasifying Agent ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Choose Gasifying Agent] --> Q1{Priority Maximum H2 Yield?} Q1 -- Yes --> Steam[Steam Gasification
H2 50 to 60 percent
Endothermic] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Autothermal Operation Needed?} Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{N2 Dilution Acceptable?} Q3 -- Yes --> Air[Air Gasification
H2 10 to 16 percent
Low Cost] Q3 -- No --> Oxy[Oxygen Gasification
H2 25 to 35 percent
No N2 Dilution] Q2 -- No --> Q4{CO2 Utilization Goal?} Q4 -- Yes --> CO2[CO2 Gasification
H2 20 to 30 percent
Carbon Capture Synergy] Q4 -- No --> Mix[Steam plus O2 Mixture
H2 35 to 50 percent
Industrial Best Practice] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Catalytic Tar Reforming & WGS Integration ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Syngas from Gasifier
CO 25 percent, H2 30 percent
CO2 15 percent, CH4 5 percent
Tar 5 g per Nm3] --> B[Catalytic Tar Reformer
Ni or Dolomite
800 to 900 C] B --> C[Tar Reduced Syngas
Tar less than 0.05 g per Nm3] C --> D[HT WGS Reactor
350 C, Fe3O4 Cr2O3] D --> E[LT WGS Reactor
200 C, CuO ZnO Al2O3] E --> F[CO2 Scrubbing
MEA or Selexol] F --> G[PSA Hydrogen Purification
99.99 percent H2] G --> H[Final Product Pure H2] G --> I[Tail Gas Recycle
Back to Gasifier] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Diagram Tip:** When asked "draw a block diagram of biomass-to-H₂ system," always include the following sequential blocks in order: **Drying → Gasifier → Gas Cleaning → WGS → CO₂ Removal → PSA H₂ Purification**. Marks are awarded for completeness of the chain. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define biomass gasification. List the four sequential reaction zones inside a downdraft gasifier.** **Model Answer:** Biomass gasification is a thermochemical conversion process in which carbonaceous biomass is partially oxidized at elevated temperatures (700–1200 °C) in a controlled oxygen-deficient environment using air, oxygen, steam, or CO₂ as gasifying agents to produce a hydrogen-rich syngas (H₂ + CO + CO₂ + CH₄ + N₂ + H₂O + tar). The four sequential reaction zones are: 1. **Drying Zone** (100–200 °C): Removal of free and bound moisture. 2. **Pyrolysis / Devolatilization Zone** (200–700 °C): Thermal decomposition to char, tar, and gases. 3. **Reduction / Gasification Zone** (700–1100 °C): Heterogeneous char–gas reactions (Boudouard, Water–Gas). 4. **Combustion / Oxidation Zone** (800–1500 °C): Partial oxidation supplying process heat. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often miss the **pyrolysis zone** or wrongly list it as a separate process. Pyrolysis is **embedded inside** the gasifier as Zone 2 — it is NOT a separate pre-treatment step in a single-reactor downdraft design. Marks lost: 1/3. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Explain the role of the Water–Gas Shift (WGS) reaction in biomass-to-hydrogen systems. Why is it conducted in two stages?** **Model Answer:** The Water–Gas Shift reaction is: $$CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Role:** It converts the CO produced during gasification into **additional H₂**, thereby nearly doubling the hydrogen yield. Since gasifier syngas is rich in CO (~20–30 vol %) and the desired product is H₂, WGS is the critical hydrogen-amplification step. **Why two stages?** - **High-Temperature WGS (HT-WGS, 350 °C, Fe₃O₄–Cr₂O₃ catalyst):** Fast kinetics, handles high CO concentrations, but equilibrium-limited (~85 % CO conversion). - **Low-Temperature WGS (LT-WGS, 200 °C, CuO–ZnO–Al₂O₃ catalyst):** Lower operating temperature pushes equilibrium toward higher H₂ yield (~95–98 % CO conversion), but kinetics are slower. - Together, they achieve **>99 % CO conversion** and **syngas with H₂ content >70 vol %**. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A — Option A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024] **(a)** With neat reaction stoichiometry, derive the theoretical maximum hydrogen yield expression $m_{max} = \frac{a}{2} - b + n$ for a biomass feedstock of formula $C_n H_a O_b N_c S_d$ gasified in pure steam at maximum H₂ yield conditions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A microbial biomass *Spirulina platensis* has the following ultimate analysis (dry basis): C = 46.5 %, H = 6.8 %, O = 31.5 %, N = 12.5 %, S = 2.7 %. Calculate (i) theoretical maximum H₂ yield in g/kg, (ii) H₂ production rate in Nm³/hr for a 200 kg/hr feed in a steam gasifier operating at S/B = 1.5, ER = 0. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — Derivation:** Given biomass formula: $C_n H_a O_b N_c S_d$ gasified in pure steam. **Step 1:** Assume all nitrogen and sulfur exit as N₂ and H₂S (they do not contribute to H₂ or CO yield). **Step 2:** Carbon atoms end up in CO and CO₂. Let $x$ = moles of CO, $y$ = moles of CO₂. $$n = x + y \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Step 3:** Hydrogen atoms end up in H₂ (target), H₂O (unreacted steam), CH₄. For maximum H₂, set CH₄ = 0. **Step 4:** Oxygen atoms are supplied by biomass ($b$ atoms) and steam. They end up in CO ($x$ atoms), CO₂ ($2y$ atoms), and unreacted H₂O. **Step 5:** Writing the hydrogen balance: $$\frac{a}{2} + S_{\text{in}} = m + S_{\text{unreacted}} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 6:** Writing the oxygen balance: $$b + S_{\text{in}} = x + 2y + S_{\text{unreacted}} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ **Step 7:** Subtracting (iii) from (ii): $$\frac{a}{2} - b = m - (x + 2y)$$ Substituting $n = x + y$ from (i) into this expression: $$\frac{a}{2} - b = m - (n + y)$$ But $y$ represents CO₂ — for **maximum** H₂, we want $y = 0$ (all C as CO). Therefore: $$\boxed{m_{max} = \frac{a}{2} - b + n}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** Feed = 200 kg/hr *Spirulina platensis*, S/B = 1.5, ER = 0. **Step 1 — Elemental composition (per kg basis):** $$ \begin{aligned} n_C &= \frac{465}{12} = 38.75 \text{ mol} \\ n_H &= \frac{68}{1} = 68.00 \text{ mol} \\ n_O &= \frac{315}{16} = 19.6875 \text{ mol} \\ n_N &= \frac{125}{14} = 8.9286 \text{ mol} \\ n_S &= \frac{27}{32} = 0.84375 \text{ mol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{max} &= \frac{a}{2} - b + n \\ &= \frac{68}{2} - 19.6875 + 38.75 \\ &= 34 - 19.6875 + 38.75 \\ &= 53.0625 \text{ mol } H_2 \text{ per kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Mass yield:** $$ H_2 \text{ yield} = 53.0625 \times 2 = \boxed{106.125 \text{ g } H_2 \text{ per kg dry biomass}} $$ **Step 4 — For 200 kg/hr feed:** $$ H_2 \text{ rate (mass)} = 106.125 \times 200 = 21,225 \text{ g/hr} = 21.225 \text{ kg/hr} $$ **Step 5 — Molar and volumetric rates:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Molar flow} &= \frac{21225}{2} = 10,612.5 \text{ mol/hr} \\ \text{Volumetric flow at STP} &= 10612.5 \times 0.022414 \\ &= \boxed{237.87 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Steam supplied (for completeness):** $$ S = \frac{1.5 \times 1000}{18} = 83.33 \text{ mol per kg biomass} \ (\text{total, including unreacted}) $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall for Question A(b):** Many students forget to convert **wt % to moles using atomic masses**. Always divide by atomic mass (C=12, H=1, O=16, N=14, S=32), not molecular weight. Marks lost: 2/7. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B — Option B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** With the help of a process flow diagram, explain the working of a **steam-blown downdraft biomass gasifier** for hydrogen production. Include the four reaction zones and the role of the throat section. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **steam gasification, air gasification, and oxygen gasification** of microbial biomass on the basis of: (i) syngas H₂ content, (ii) calorific value, (iii) operating temperature, (iv) tar yield, (v) economic viability, and (vi) H₂ yield per kg biomass. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — Steam-Blown Downdraft Gasifier:** A downdraft gasifier is a **co-current** reactor in which biomass and gasifying agent (steam) flow **downward in the same direction**, passing through four distinct reaction zones stacked vertically. **Working (Zone-wise):** 1. **Drying Zone (Top):** Wet biomass enters and is dried by rising hot gases; moisture exits as steam. 2. **Pyrolysis Zone:** Dried biomass is heated to 200–700 °C and decomposes into char + volatile gases + tar. 3. **Reduction Zone (Throat Section):** This is the **narrowest cross-section** of the gasifier, located just above the combustion zone. Here, hot steam meets the descending char. The **water–gas reaction** ($C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$) dominates. The throat forces steam to pass through the hottest, most reactive char bed, maximizing contact time and conversion. **This is the heart of the downdraft design.** 4. **Combustion / Oxidation Zone:** A controlled air/oxygen inlet near the throat combusts a fraction of the char to supply process heat. ER is held between **0.20 – 0.30**. **Downstream:** Syngas exits at the bottom, passes through a **cyclone** (particulate removal), **scrubber** (tar + moisture), **WGS reactors** (H₂ amplification), **CO₂ scrubber** (amine/PSA), and finally **PSA** for pure H₂. **Role of Throat:** - Restricts cross-section → increases **gas velocity** through the char bed. - Forces intimate **steam–char contact** → maximizes H₂ yield. - Allows precise **temperature control** by positioning of the oxidation zone above it. **Part (b) — Comparative Table:** | Parameter | Air Gasification | Oxygen Gasification | Steam Gasification | |---|---|---|---| | (i) H₂ content (vol %) | 10–16 | 25–35 | 30–60 | | (ii) Calorific value (MJ/Nm³) | 4–7 | 10–18 | 12–20 | | (iii) Operating T (°C) | 700–900 | 800–1200 | 700–1100 | | (iv) Tar yield (g/Nm³) | 0.5–2.0 | 1.0–5.0 | 5.0–30.0 (needs reformer) | | (v) Economic viability | High (cheap O₂ source) | Moderate (cryogenic O₂ cost) | Low–Moderate (heat demand) | | (vi) H₂ yield (g/kg biomass) | 60–90 | 100–140 | 110–160 | **Engineering Synthesis:** - **Air gasification** is preferred for **small-scale, decentralized, cost-sensitive** applications (e.g., village-level H₂ for fuel cells). - **Oxygen gasification** is preferred for **medium-scale industrial** plants where higher syngas quality justifies cryogenic O₂ cost. - **Steam gasification** is the **best for maximum H₂ yield** but requires coupling with **partial oxidation** (auto-thermal steam–O₂ mixture) or external heat sources, plus a downstream **catalytic tar reformer**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall for Question B(a):** Students frequently confuse **updraft** and **downdraft** gasifiers. In **updraft**, syngas exits cold (low tar cracking); in **downdraft**, syngas exits hot and tar-cracked through the high-temperature throat. Marks lost: 1.5/7. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (memorize for KTU ESE)** ### 🔹 Core Definitions - [ ] Biomass gasification = **partial oxidation** at 700–1200 °C, sub-stoichiometric O₂. - [ ] Syngas = mixture of **H₂, CO, CO₂, CH₄, N₂, H₂O, tar**. - [ ] Producer gas = synonym for air-blown syngas (low heating value). - [ ] Equivalence Ratio (ER) = Actual O₂ / Stoichiometric O₂; **optimal = 0.20–0.30** for H₂. ### 🔹 Seven Master Reactions - [ ] **R1:** $C + CO_2 \rightleftharpoons 2CO$ — **+172 kJ/mol** — Boudouard (endothermic). - [ ] **R2:** $C + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + H_2$ — **+131 kJ/mol** — Water–Gas (endothermic). - [ ] **R3:** $CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2$ — **–41 kJ/mol** — WGS (exothermic, H₂ amplifier). - [ ] **R4:** $C + 2H_2 \rightleftharpoons CH_4$ — **–75 kJ/mol** — Methanation (suppress!). - [ ] **R5:** $CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2$ — **+206 kJ/mol** — Steam Reforming. - [ ] **R6:** $C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO$ — **–111 kJ/mol** — Partial Oxidation. - [ ] **R7:** $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ — **–394 kJ/mol** — Complete Combustion. ### 🔹 Key Numerical Formula - [ ] **Theoretical H₂ yield:** $m_{max} = \frac{a}{2} - b + n$ (mol H₂ per mol biomass). - [ ] For *Chlorella vulgaris*: ~111 g H₂/kg dry; for *Spirulina*: ~106 g H₂/kg. - [ ] For cellulose: 74 g H₂/kg (theoretical). ### 🔹 Four Reaction Zones (Downdraft) - [ ] **Zone 1** — Drying (100–200 °C). - [ ] **Zone 2** — Pyrolysis (200–700 °C). - [ ] **Zone 3** — Reduction / Gasification (700–1100 °C) — **throat**. - [ ] **Zone 4** — Combustion / Partial Oxidation (800–1500 °C). ### 🔹 Process Parameter Mnemonic - [ ] **"T-S-C → maximize H₂":** ↑Temperature, ↑Steam/Biomass, ↑Catalyst → ↑H₂ yield. - [ ] **"E-P-T → minimize tar & CH₄":** low ER, optimized Pressure, sufficient residence Time. ### 🔹 Gasifying Agent Hierarchy - [ ] **Steam > O₂ > Air** in terms of syngas H₂ concentration (vol %). - [ ] **Air < O₂ < Steam** in terms of economic cost (ascending). ### 🔹 Process Chain (must appear in block-diagram answers) - [ ] Drying → Gasifier → Cyclone → Scrubber → Compressor → HT-WGS → LT-WGS → CO₂ Removal → PSA → Pure H₂.

topics.title: Pyrolysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pyrolysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PYROLYSIS** is the **thermochemical decomposition of organic carbonaceous feedstocks (biomass, coal, municipal solid waste, plastics)** at elevated temperatures (typically **400 °C – 900 °C**) in the **complete absence (or near absence) of oxygen**, producing a combustible gas mixture rich in **H₂, CO, CO₂, CH₄**, along with condensable organic vapours (tars/bio-oil) and a solid carbonaceous residue (char).

In the strict context of **Hydrogen Energy** (Module 2 of PECHT862), pyrolysis is classified as a **second-generation thermochemical hydrogen production route** — distinct from steam methane reforming (SMR), partial oxidation, and gasification — because the *primary* intent is to **thermally crack long-chain hydrocarbons and oxygenates into smaller, hydrogen-rich molecules**, with the secondary intent of producing a hydrogen-enriched syngas fraction after downstream reforming/water-gas-shift stages.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Cooking in a Sealed Pressure Cooker"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine baking a piece of bread inside a perfectly sealed, oxygen-free pressure cooker over a high flame.**
> - The bread cannot *burn* (no O₂ → no combustion).
> - Instead, it **thermally disintegrates** into:
>   - a **black solid** (charcoal),
>   - a **dark oily liquid** dripping down (bio-oil/tar),
>   - and **gases** escaping through a valve (H₂, CO, CH₄, CO₂).
> - The *flame* is just heat — **not oxygen**.
> - By carefully controlling *how fast* you heat it (heating rate) and *how long* it stays in the cooker (residence time), you can tune the relative amounts of solid, liquid, and gas you obtain.

This perfectly mirrors industrial pyrolysis:
- The **feedstock** = the bread.
- The **absence of O₂** = the sealed cooker.
- The **heat** = the externally fired heater (no flame inside the reactor).
- The **three product phases** (char, bio-oil, gas) are separated downstream by condensation and filtration.

### 1.3 Position in the H₂ Production Hierarchy

| Pathway | Oxygen Level | Primary Product | H₂ Yield Potential |
|---|---|---|---|
| Combustion | Excess O₂ | CO₂ + H₂O | **None** (oxidative) |
| Gasification | Limited O₂ (sub-stoichiometric) | Syngas (H₂ + CO) | **High** |
| **Pyrolysis** | **No O₂ (inert atmosphere)** | **Char + Bio-oil + Gas** | **Moderate–High** (with reforming) |
| Steam Reforming | External H₂O | Syngas (H₂ + CO) | **Highest** |

### 1.4 Why Pyrolysis Matters for Hydrogen Energy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pyrolysis is **carbon-negative-friendly** when paired with biochar sequestration, allows **simultaneous waste valorization** (plastics, MSW, agricultural residue), and produces a **renewable hydrogen stream** without consuming fossil methane — making it a key pillar of the **green / blue-grey hydrogen** ecosystem covered in this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Pyrolysis Classification

Every pyrolysis process is uniquely defined by **three operating parameters**. Mastering these is the single most important step for KTU exam scoring.

#### Pillar 1 — Heating Rate (β)
$$\beta = \frac{dT}{dt} \quad \left[\frac{^{\circ}\text{C}}{\text{s}}\right]$$

#### Pillar 2 — Final Reaction Temperature (T)
$$T_{\text{peak}} \in [400^{\circ}\text{C},\; 900^{\circ}\text{C}]$$

#### Pillar 3 — Vapour Residence Time (τ)
$$\tau = \frac{V_{\text{reactor}}}{\dot{V}_{\text{carrier gas}}} \quad [\text{s}]$$

### 2.2 Four Canonical Modes of Pyrolysis

| Mode | Heating Rate (°C/s) | Peak Temp (°C) | Residence Time | Primary Product | H₂ Yield (vol% dry gas) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slow Pyrolysis** (Carbonisation) | 0.1 – 1 | 400 – 600 | Hours – Days | **Char (≈ 35 %)** | 5 – 15 |
| **Fast Pyrolysis** | 10 – 200 | 500 – 650 | 0.5 – 2 s | **Bio-oil (≈ 70 %)** | 15 – 25 |
| **Flash Pyrolysis** | > 1000 | 800 – 1000 | < 0.5 s | **Gas (≈ 80 %)** | **30 – 45** |
| **Vacuum Pyrolysis** | 0.1 – 1 | 350 – 500 | Long | Bio-oil (≈ 60 %) | 10 – 20 |

> [!NOTE]
> **For maximum H₂ yield** → **Flash Pyrolysis at the highest practical temperature with minimum residence time**, followed by a downstream catalytic reformer and water-gas-shift (WGS) reactor.

### 2.3 Governing Reaction Network

The pyrolysis reaction set is complex; the **principal pathways** are:

#### (a) Primary Decomposition (devolatilisation)
$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z \xrightarrow{\Delta,\; \text{no O}_2} \text{Char (C)} + \text{Tar (C}_n\text{H}_m\text{O}_p) + \text{Gas (H}_2, \text{CO, CO}_2, \text{CH}_4, \text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{)}$$

#### (b) Secondary Cracking of Tar (homogeneous)
$$\text{Tar} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Lighter Gases} \quad (\text{esp. at } T > 700^{\circ}\text{C})$$

#### (c) Char Gasification (heterogeneous, requires H₂O/CO₂ — externally supplied)
$$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +131.4 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{CO} \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \;\texttext{kJ/mol} \;\; \text{(Boudouard)}$$

#### (d) Water-Gas Shift (WGS) — *in-situ* if steam is present
$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41.2 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

#### (e) Methanation (low-T) / Steam Methane Reforming (high-T)
$$\text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -206.3 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206.3 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Quantity | Symbol | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heating rate | $\beta = dT/dt$ | °C/s | Classifies process |
| 2 | Vapour residence time | $\tau = V_{\text{reactor}} / \dot{V}_{\text{carrier}}$ | s | Tunes product slate |
| 3 | Mass yield (char) | $Y_{\text{char}} = m_{\text{char}}/m_{\text{feed}}$ | kg/kg | Material balance |
| 4 | Gas yield | $Y_{\text{gas}} = V_{\text{gas}}/m_{\text{feed}}$ | Nm³/kg | H₂ potential |
| 5 | H₂ mass balance | $\dot{n}_{H_2,\text{out}} = Y_{\text{gas}} \cdot x_{H_2} \cdot \rho_{H_2}^{-1}$ | mol/s | Production rate |
| 6 | HHV of syngas (per Nm³) | $\text{HHV} = \sum_i x_i \cdot \text{HHV}_i$ | MJ/Nm³ | Energy content |
| 7 | Cold Gas Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{CG}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{gas}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{\text{gas}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{\text{feed}}} \cdot 100\%$ | % | Process efficiency |
| 8 | H₂ selectivity | $S_{H_2} = \dfrac{n_{H_2,\text{produced}}}{n_{\text{C,converted}}}$ | — | Reactor figure-of-merit |
| 9 | Char–gas H₂O endothermic | $\Delta H = +131.4$ | kJ/mol | Energy duty |
| 10 | WGS exothermic | $\Delta H = -41.2$ | kJ/mol | Energy recovery |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) management** — pyrolysis of non-recyclable plastics yields H₂ + char (used as soil amendment).
- **Biomass-to-H₂** — agricultural residues (rice husk, bagasse) converted via fast pyrolysis + secondary reformer.
- **Carbon-negative H₂** — when biochar is permanently sequestered, the net CO₂ footprint of the H₂ produced can be **negative**.
- **Plastic upcycling** — pyrolysis of mixed polyolefins yields H₂ + hydrocarbon monomers for circular economy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — H₂ Mass Produced from Pyrolysis Gas

**Problem Statement:** A biomass pyrolysis reactor processes **100 kg/hr** of dry sawdust (proximate: fixed carbon 18 %, volatiles 78 %, ash 4 % by mass). The dry product gas has the following molar composition: **H₂ = 32 %, CO = 28 %, CO₂ = 18 %, CH₄ = 14 %, C₂H₄ = 5 %, N₂ = 3 %**. The total dry gas yield is **0.65 Nm³ per kg of feed**. Compute the **mass flow rate of H₂ produced**.

#### Step 1 — Total volumetric flow rate of dry gas
$$\dot{V}_{\text{gas}} = 0.65 \;\frac{\text{Nm}^3}{\text{kg}_{\text{feed}}} \times 100 \;\frac{\text{kg}_{\text{feed}}}{\text{hr}} = 65 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Logic:** Multiply specific gas yield by feed flow rate.

#### Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate of pure H₂ in the gas
$$\dot{V}_{H_2} = \dot{V}_{\text{gas}} \times x_{H_2} = 65 \times 0.32 = 20.8 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Logic:** The mole fraction (32 %) is the volumetric fraction (ideal gas law, identical partial volumes).

#### Step 3 — Convert volume to moles using standard molar volume
$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{\dot{V}_{H_2}}{V_m} = \frac{20.8 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}}{22.414 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{mol}}$$

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{20.8}{0.022414} = 927.95 \;\text{mol/hr}$$

**Logic:** At **STP (273.15 K, 1 atm)**, **Vₘ = 22.414 L/mol = 0.022414 Nm³/mol**.

#### Step 4 — Convert to mass using molar mass of H₂
$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \times M_{H_2} = 927.95 \;\text{mol/hr} \times 2.016 \;\text{g/mol}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1870.6 \;\text{g/hr} \approx 1.871 \;\text{kg/hr}$$

#### Step 5 — Final Answer
$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{H_2} \approx 1.87 \;\text{kg H}_2 \text{ per hour (from 100 kg/hr feed)}}$$

#### Step 6 — Specific H₂ yield
$$Y_{H_2,\text{specific}} = \frac{1.871 \;\text{kg}}{100 \;\text{kg}} = 18.71 \;\text{g H}_2 / \text{kg}_{\text{feed}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cold Gas Efficiency of Pyrolysis-Derived Syngas

**Problem Statement:** Using the same data as above, the LHV of the product gas components (in MJ/Nm³) are: H₂ = 10.79, CO = 12.64, CH₄ = 35.88, C₂H₄ = 59.44, CO₂ = 0, N₂ = 0. The feed (sawdust) LHV = **17 MJ/kg**. Compute cold gas efficiency.

#### Step 1 — Compute LHV of product gas per Nm³
$$\text{LHV}_{\text{gas}} = \sum_i x_i \cdot \text{LHV}_i$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LHV}_{\text{gas}} &= (0.32)(10.79) + (0.28)(12.64) + (0.14)(35.88) + (0.05)(59.44) \\
&= 3.4528 + 3.5392 + 5.0232 + 2.9720 \\
&= 14.987 \;\text{MJ/Nm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Weighted average by volume fraction; CO₂ and N₂ have zero combustion energy.

#### Step 2 — Energy output rate
$$\dot{E}_{\text{out}} = \dot{V}_{\text{gas}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{gas}} = 65 \times 14.987 = 974.2 \;\text{MJ/hr}$$

#### Step 3 — Energy input rate
$$\dot{E}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{feed}} = 100 \times 17 = 1700 \;\text{MJ/hr}$$

#### Step 4 — Cold Gas Efficiency
$$\eta_{\text{CG}} = \frac{\dot{E}_{\text{out}}}{\dot{E}_{\text{in}}} \times 100\% = \frac{974.2}{1700} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{CG}} \approx 57.3 \;\%}$$

**Note:** Char retains the remaining energy (its LHV ≈ 28 MJ/kg). A complete energy balance would partition energy into char (≈ 30 %) + sensible heat losses (≈ 10 %) + gas (≈ 57 %) + minor losses.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — H₂ Selectivity from Char Conversion

**Problem Statement:** 50 kg/hr of plastic waste (assume polyethylene, **C₂H₄** monomer equivalent, carbon content = 85.7 % by mass) is pyrolysed. The gas contains 28 mol% H₂, and the char produced is 8 kg/hr with 90 % fixed carbon. Compute **H₂ selectivity** (moles H₂ produced per mole of carbon converted to gas).

#### Step 1 — Total carbon input
$$\dot{m}_C^{\text{in}} = 50 \times 0.857 = 42.85 \;\text{kg C/hr}$$

#### Step 2 — Carbon retained in char
$$\dot{m}_C^{\text{char}} = 8 \times 0.90 = 7.20 \;\text{kg C/hr}$$

#### Step 3 — Carbon converted to gas (to all C-bearing species)
$$\dot{m}_C^{\text{gas,total}} = 42.85 - 7.20 = 35.65 \;\text{kg C/hr}$$

$$\dot{n}_C^{\text{gas,total}} = \frac{35.65 \times 10^3}{12.011} = 2967.8 \;\text{mol/hr}$$

**Logic:** Moles of C = mass / atomic mass of C (12.011 g/mol).

#### Step 4 — Total H₂ produced (assume specific gas yield = 0.8 Nm³/kg)
$$\dot{V}_{\text{gas}} = 0.8 \times 50 = 40 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{40 \times 0.28}{0.022414} = \frac{11.2}{0.022414} = 499.6 \;\text{mol/hr}$$

#### Step 5 — H₂ Selectivity
$$S_{H_2} = \frac{\dot{n}_{H_2}}{\dot{n}_C^{\text{gas,total}}} = \frac{499.6}{2967.8}$$

$$\boxed{S_{H_2} \approx 0.168 \;\text{mol H}_2 \text{ per mol C gasified}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pyrolysis vs Gasification

| Parameter | Pyrolysis | Gasification |
|---|---|---|
| O₂ supply | None | Sub-stoichiometric |
| Temperature | 400 – 900 °C | 700 – 1200 °C |
| Primary product | Char + bio-oil + gas | Syngas (H₂ + CO) |
| H₂ in dry gas | 5 – 45 vol% | 30 – 60 vol% |
| Tar yield | High (esp. fast) | Low (oxidative cracking) |
| ER ratio | 0 | 0.2 – 0.4 |
| Cold gas efficiency | 50 – 65 % | 70 – 85 % |
| Air-separation unit | Not needed | Often needed (for O₂-blown) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Biomass Pyrolysis to H₂

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedA[Feedstock Preparation Drying Grinding Sieving] --> reactB[Pyrolysis Reactor 400 to 900 C Inert N2]
    reactB --> sepC[Primary Cyclone Solid Char Separation]
    sepC --> charOut[Char Quenching Storage Sequestration]
    sepC --> condD[Quench Condenser Tar and Bio oil Recovery]
    condD --> tarOut[Bio oil Tank]
    condD --> gasE[Secondary Thermal Cracking 800 to 1000 C Tar Reforming]
    gasE --> reformF[Catalytic Steam Reformer 800 C Ni Catalyst]
    reformF --> wgsG[Water Gas Shift Reactor Fe Cr or Cu Zn]
    wgsG --> h2SepH[PSA Pressure Swing Adsorption H2 Purification 99.99 percent]
    h2SepH --> h2Tank[H2 Compression and Storage 350 bar or 700 bar]
    reformF --> co2Out[CO2 Vent or Capture]
    wgsG --> co2Out
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**
- **Feedstock Preparation** removes moisture (target < 10 wt%) because water consumes heat (latent heat 2260 kJ/kg).
- **Pyrolysis Reactor** is the heart — externally heated, **no oxygen**; produces char + vapour.
- **Primary Cyclone** separates hot char (inert solid) from vapour by centrifugal force.
- **Quench Condenser** rapidly cools vapour from 500 °C → < 100 °C, condensing heavy tars into bio-oil.
- **Secondary Cracking** breaks residual tars into lighter gases (↑ H₂ yield).
- **Steam Reformer** converts CH₄ and light hydrocarbons into additional H₂ + CO.
- **WGS Reactor** shifts CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂, doubling H₂ from the CO pool.
- **PSA** separates 99.99 % pure H₂ from the off-gas (tail gas recycled as reformer fuel).

---

### 4.2 Process Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Pyrolysis Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Define Objective] --> q1{Desired Product?}
    q1 -->|Solid Char| slowB[Slow Pyrolysis Low T Long Tau]
    q1 -->|Liquid Bio oil| fastC[Fast Pyrolysis Moderate T Short Tau]
    q1 -->|Gas H2 Rich| flashD[Flash Pyrolysis High T Very Short Tau]
    slowB --> endZ[Reactor Selection]
    fastC --> endZ
    flashD --> endZ
    endZ --> q2{Feedstock Type?}
    q2 -->|Biomass| r1[Auger Reactor or Fluidised Bed]
    q2 -->|Plastic| r2[Screw Kiln or Fixed Bed]
    q2 -->|Coal| r3[Moving Bed Reactor]
    q2 -->|MSW| r4[Rotary Kiln Reactor]
```

---

### 4.3 Heat & Mass Balance Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inM[Feedstock m_feed kg hr] --> rx[Pyrolysis Reactor]
    inE[External Heat Q_ext kW] --> rx
    inN[Inert Carrier N2 Nm3 hr] --> rx
    rx --> outG[Non condensable Gas V_gas Nm3 hr]
    rx --> outC[Char m_char kg hr]
    rx --> outL[Bio oil m_oil kg hr]
    rx --> outW[Water Vapour m_H2O kg hr]
    outG --> split[Composition H2 CO CO2 CH4 CnHm]
```

---
