## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT862]`
**Define pyrolysis. How does it fundamentally differ from combustion and gasification for hydrogen production?**

**Model Answer:**
Pyrolysis is the **thermal decomposition of organic feedstocks in the complete absence of oxygen** at temperatures between **400 °C and 900 °C**, yielding char, bio-oil, and a non-condensable gas mixture containing H₂, CO, CO₂, and CH₄.

| Process | Oxygen | Temp Range | Primary Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| Combustion | Excess | 800 – 1200 °C | CO₂ + H₂O (no H₂) |
| Gasification | Sub-stoichiometric | 700 – 1200 °C | Syngas (H₂ + CO) |
| **Pyrolysis** | **Zero (inert)** | **400 – 900 °C** | **Char + Bio-oil + Gas** |

Pyrolysis is **distinct** because it is purely **thermal cracking** — no oxidation reactions take place; hence the energy released is only from cracking endothermicities, not from combustion. This allows retention of carbon as char rather than full conversion to CO₂.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECHT862]`
**List the four modes of pyrolysis and state the operating parameter that MOST strongly influences the product distribution.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Slow Pyrolysis** — β = 0.1–1 °C/s; τ = hours; favours **char**.
2. **Fast Pyrolysis** — β = 10–200 °C/s; τ = 0.5–2 s; favours **bio-oil**.
3. **Flash Pyrolysis** — β > 1000 °C/s; τ < 0.5 s; favours **gas** (including H₂).
4. **Vacuum Pyrolysis** — sub-atmospheric pressure; favours **bio-oil**.

**Dominant parameter:** The **heating rate (β)** combined with **vapour residence time (τ)** controls which product dominates (Le Chatelier-style kinetic selectivity — high β + low τ suppresses secondary char-forming reactions).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT862]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the chemistry of biomass pyrolysis with the major reactions involved in hydrogen formation. Derive the role of water-gas shift reaction in enhancing H₂ yield.

**Model Answer:**

The chemistry proceeds in **four sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Drying (T < 200 °C):** Physically bound moisture evaporates. Endothermic; consumes ~2260 kJ/kg of water.

**Stage 2 — Devolatilisation (200 – 500 °C):** Cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin decompose, releasing volatiles.

**Stage 3 — Primary Pyrolysis (500 – 700 °C):** Char + Tar + Gas formation via the master reaction:

$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z \longrightarrow a\,\text{Char (C)} + b\,\text{CO} + c\,\text{CO}_2 + d\,\text{CH}_4 + e\,\text{H}_2 + f\,\text{Tar}$$

**Stage 4 — Secondary Reactions (T > 700 °C):**
- **Tar cracking:** $\text{Tar} \rightarrow \text{Lighter gases} + \text{H}_2$
- **Char gasification (with externally injected steam):** $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$
- **Boudouard:** $\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$
- **Methane reforming:** $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$

**Role of WGS in enhancing H₂ yield:**

The WGS reaction:
$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41.2 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

- Converts **every mole of CO into one mole of additional H₂**, effectively doubling the H₂ obtainable from the carbon pool.
- **Exothermic**, hence favoured at low temperatures thermodynamically — but kinetically requires a catalyst (Fe-Cr at 350–500 °C for HTS; Cu-Zn at 200–250 °C for LTS).
- In practice, two-stage WGS (HTS + LTS) can convert **> 95 %** of CO to H₂, raising syngas H₂:CO ratio from ~1 to ~3 — ideal for downstream H₂ separation by PSA.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A pyrolysis plant processes 200 kg/hr of rice husk. The dry gas yield is 0.55 Nm³/kg with composition (vol%): H₂ = 28, CO = 30, CO₂ = 20, CH₄ = 14, N₂ = 8. Calculate: (i) H₂ mass produced per hour, (ii) cold gas efficiency, given LHV values (MJ/Nm³): H₂ = 10.79, CO = 12.64, CH₄ = 35.88, CO₂ = 0, N₂ = 0; feed LHV = 15 MJ/kg.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Mass of H₂ produced:**

Total gas flow:
$$\dot{V}_{\text{gas}} = 0.55 \times 200 = 110 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

H₂ volume flow:
$$\dot{V}_{H_2} = 110 \times 0.28 = 30.8 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

H₂ moles:
$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{30.8}{0.022414} = 1374.2 \;\text{mol/hr}$$

H₂ mass flow:
$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1374.2 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 2.770 \;\text{kg/hr}$$

**(ii) Cold Gas Efficiency:**

LHV of product gas:
$$\text{LHV}_{\text{gas}} = (0.28)(10.79) + (0.30)(12.64) + (0.14)(35.88) = 3.021 + 3.792 + 5.023 = 11.836 \;\text{MJ/Nm}^3$$

Energy output:
$$\dot{E}_{\text{out}} = 110 \times 11.836 = 1301.9 \;\text{MJ/hr}$$

Energy input:
$$\dot{E}_{\text{in}} = 200 \times 15 = 3000 \;\text{MJ/hr}$$

$$\eta_{\text{CG}} = \frac{1301.9}{3000} \times 100\% = 43.4\%$$

**Final Answers:** $\dot{m}_{H_2} \approx \mathbf{2.77 \;\text{kg/hr}}$ and $\eta_{\text{CG}} \approx \mathbf{43.4 \;\%}$.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECHT862]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, describe the various reactors used for biomass pyrolysis. Compare fluidised bed and auger reactor performance.

**Model Answer:**

**Common Pyrolysis Reactors:**

1. **Fixed Bed (Batch)** — Simplest; biomass heated externally; low heat transfer; used for laboratory / small-scale.
2. **Auger (Screw) Reactor** — Continuous; biomass conveyed by screw through a heated tube; τ = 5–30 s; good for mixed/wet feedstocks.
3. **Fluidised Bed Reactor** — Sand bed fluidised by inert gas; excellent heat transfer (h ≈ 200–500 W/m²K); industry standard for fast pyrolysis.
4. **Rotating Cone Reactor** — Biomass + sand mixed on rotating cone; centrifugal transport; compact; high biomass-to-sand ratio.
5. **Entrained Flow Reactor** — Co-current gas-solid flow; very short τ (< 1 s); used for flash pyrolysis.
6. **Plasma Pyrolysis** — Ultra-high T (> 3000 °C); for MSW / hazardous waste.

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Fluidised Bed | Auger Reactor |
|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer coefficient | High (200–500 W/m²K) | Low–moderate (50–100 W/m²K) |
| Carrier gas requirement | High (N₂ fluidising) | Low |
| Scale-up | Difficult (uniform fluidisation) | Easy (modular) |
| Particle size tolerance | 1–3 mm | Up to 6 mm |
| Bio-oil yield | 70–75 % wt | 50–60 % wt |
| Operating temperature | 500–550 °C | 400–600 °C |
| Capital cost | High | Low |

**Conclusion:** Fluidised bed gives **higher bio-oil yield** and better temperature uniformity; auger is **cheaper and more robust** for variable feedstock.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the role of catalysts in enhancing H₂ yield during catalytic pyrolysis of biomass. Discuss the effect of temperature on H₂ production with reference to Le Chatelier's principle.

**Model Answer:**

**Catalysts in Pyrolysis:**

Catalysts accelerate secondary cracking of tars and shift product selectivity toward H₂. Major catalyst families:

- **Ni-based** (Ni/Al₂O₃, Ni-Mg-O) — promote tar reforming and methane cracking. Most widely studied.
- **Zeolites** (HZSM-5, HY) — deoxygenate biomass vapours; produce aromatics + H₂O + CO + H₂.
- **Dolomite / Olivine** — cheap natural catalysts for tar cracking; lower activity than Ni.
- **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** catalysts — promote secondary cracking.

**Catalytic reactions enhancing H₂:**
$$\text{Tar} \xrightarrow{\text{Ni}} \text{H}_2 + \text{CO} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{CH}_4$$

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Ni}} \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$$

**Effect of Temperature (Le Chatelier's Principle):**

Consider the **endothermic char–steam reaction**:
$$\text{C}_{(s)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_{(g)} + \text{H}_{2(g)} \quad \Delta H = +131.4 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

By **Le Chatelier's principle**, raising T shifts equilibrium **rightward** → ↑ CO and ↑ H₂ production.

Consider the **exothermic WGS reaction**:
$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41.2 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

Lower T favours **forward** WGS → ↑ H₂, but kinetics require T > 200 °C.

**Optimal Strategy:** **High T (800–900 °C) for tar/char conversion** → followed by **two-stage WGS at lower T (HTS 400 °C, LTS 220 °C)** for thermodynamic + kinetic optimum.

**Quantitative trend (typical):**

| Temperature (°C) | H₂ vol% (dry gas) |
|---|---|
| 500 | 18 |
| 700 | 28 |
| 850 | 38 |
| 1000 | 45 |

Thus, **H₂ yield rises monotonically with temperature**, plateauing near 1000 °C due to thermodynamic equilibrium constraints of the WGS reaction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating gasification and pyrolysis** — A common error: stating that gasification *is* a type of pyrolysis. They are **distinct** — gasification has sub-stoichiometric O₂; pyrolysis has none.
> 2. **Forgetting the WGS step** — Many students omit the WGS reaction in H₂-yield derivations, missing ~50 % of the H₂ produced.
> 3. **Wrong molar volume** — At STP, Vₘ = **22.414 L/mol** = 0.022414 m³/mol; at NTP (293 K), it is 24.04 L/mol. Always state the reference conditions explicitly.
> 4. **Unit errors in efficiency** — Cold Gas Efficiency = (Energy out / Energy in) × 100 %; not the ratio without ×100.
> 5. **Ignoring sensible heat** — In real KTU problems, ~5–10 % marks are reserved for sensible-heat losses; mention them in the energy balance.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Pyrolysis (Module 2):**

- ✅ **Pyrolysis = thermal decomposition in the ABSENCE of oxygen**; T = 400–900 °C.
- ✅ **Three products:** Char (solid), Bio-oil (liquid), Non-condensable gas (H₂, CO, CO₂, CH₄, light HCs).
- ✅ **Four modes** — Slow (char), Fast (bio-oil), Flash (gas), Vacuum (bio-oil).
- ✅ **Three governing parameters:** Heating rate (β), Peak T (T), Vapour residence time (τ).
- ✅ **High β + low τ → gas dominated → high H₂ yield.**
- ✅ **Key reactions:** Char–steam (endothermic, +131.4 kJ/mol); Boudouard (+172.5 kJ/mol); WGS (exothermic, -41.2 kJ/mol); SMR (+206.3 kJ/mol).
- ✅ **WGS doubles H₂** from CO; downstream **PSA** purifies H₂ to 99.99 %.
- ✅ **Standard molar volume** at STP = **22.414 L/mol**.
- ✅ **Cold Gas Efficiency** = (LHV of gas × gas flow) / (LHV of feed × feed flow) × 100 %.
- ✅ **Major reactors:** Fluidised bed (best heat transfer), Auger (cheapest), Rotating cone (compact), Plasma (high T).
- ✅ **Catalysts:** Ni-based (tar reforming), Zeolites (deoxygenation), Dolomite (cheap tar cracking).
- ✅ **Pyrolysis ≠ Gasification** — different O₂ levels; different primary products; different ER ratios.
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** MSW valorisation, plastic upcycling, biomass-to-H₂, biochar sequestration.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Storage Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** General Storage Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — General Storage Methods

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT862 / Module 3):**
> *General Storage Methods* in hydrogen energy encompass the engineering strategies, thermodynamic pathways, and material-based technologies used to retain produced hydrogen in a usable form (molecular H₂, liquid H₂, or chemically bonded H) until it is required by the end-user. The choice of storage method directly determines the **round-trip energy efficiency**, **gravimetric/volumetric density**, **safety profile**, and **economic feasibility** of any hydrogen value chain.

### 1.1 The Four Pillars of Hydrogen Storage

Hydrogen is the lightest and smallest molecule in the universe (molecular weight **2.016 g/mol**, density at STP **0.0899 kg/m³**). Because of its extremely low ambient density, storing it in useful quantities is one of the grand challenges of the hydrogen economy. KTU 2024 categorizes all storage methods into four broad families:

| # | Storage Family | Physical State of H | Typical Storage Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Compressed Gaseous Hydrogen (CGH₂)** | Gas | 350 – 700 bar (industrial: up to **1000 bar**) |
| 2 | **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂ / Cryo-compressed)** | Liquid | 20.3 K (cryogenic) |
| 3 | **Solid-State (Metal / Complex Hydrides, Adsorbents)** | Solid | Ambient / mild temperature |
| 4 | **Underground Storage (Caverns, Aquifers, Depleted Reservoirs)** | Gas | 50 – 200 bar geological |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Performance Metrics Used in KTU Valuation:**
> - **Gravimetric Hydrogen Density** $\rho_g = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{m_{system}}$ (mass fraction, wt %)
> - **Volumetric Hydrogen Density** $\rho_v = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{V_{system}}$ (kg H₂ / m³)
> - **Energy Density** $E_d = \rho_g \times \text{LHV}_{H_2}$ where **LHV = 120 MJ/kg**, **HHV = 142 MJ/kg**

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Water in Different Containers" Analogy**
> Think of hydrogen as **water** that you need to deliver to a village, but the pipes leak if the pressure is too high, the water freezes into ice at −253 °C, and you can also hide it inside sponges (metal hydrides). The four storage methods are simply four different "containers":
> - **CGH₂** → squeezing water into a pressurized scuba tank (high pressure, gaseous).
> - **LH₂** → freezing water into ice in a thermos flask (cryogenic liquid, boil-off losses).
> - **Solid-State** → soaking water into a sponge (reversible chemical/physical binding).
> - **Underground** → filling a giant underground swimming pool with water (geological scale).
>
> The "best" container depends on **how much water, how far, and how long** you need to store it.

### 1.3 Why Hydrogen Storage is Hard — The Physics Bottleneck

- **Very low critical temperature** $T_c = 33.2$ **K** → hydrogen is a *non-condensable* gas at room temperature; it cannot be liquefied by pressure alone.
- **Very low critical pressure** $P_c = 13.0$ **bar** → supercritical fluid above 33.2 K.
- **Boiling point at 1 atm** = **20.27 K** (≈ −252.87 °C).
- **High diffusivity** → embrittles metals and leaks through seals.
- **Wide flammability range** in air: **4 – 75 vol %** (the widest of any common fuel).

These properties dictate why no single "one-size-fits-all" storage method exists.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Compressed Gaseous Hydrogen (CGH₂)

In CGH₂, hydrogen is pressurized into cylindrical or spherical vessels. The thermodynamics of real compressed hydrogen is governed by the **Real Gas Equation**:

$$PV = ZnRT$$

where $Z$ is the **compressibility factor** (for ideal gas, $Z = 1$; for real hydrogen at 700 bar, $Z \approx 1.45$).

### Types of High-Pressure Vessels (KTU frequently tested)

| Type | Construction | Pressure Rating | $\rho_g$ (wt %) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I** | All-metal (steel/aluminium) | 200 – 300 bar | 1 – 2 | Industrial, stationary |
| **Type II** | Metal liner + hoop-wrapped fibre | 200 – 450 bar | 2 – 4 | CNG-style |
| **Type III** | Metal liner + fully wrapped composite | 450 – 700 bar | 4 – 6 | Vehicle on-board |
| **Type IV** | Polymer liner + fully wrapped composite | 700 bar | 5 – 10 | Fuel-cell vehicles (FCV) |
| **Type V** | All-composite (no liner) | > 700 bar | 8 – 12 | R&D, aerospace |

### Thermodynamic Compression Work

The minimum (reversible/isentropic) work required to compress hydrogen from $P_1$ to $P_2$ assuming ideal gas and isothermal conditions:

$$W_{comp} = nRT \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

For real hydrogen, the **isothermal compression work with variable Z** is:

$$W_{comp,real} = nRT \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{Z}{P}\,dP$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Approximately **10 – 15 % of LHV** of hydrogen is consumed just to compress it from 200 bar to 700 bar. This is a recurring 3-mark question.

## 2.2 Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂) — Cryogenic Storage

LH₂ is stored at its normal boiling point of **20.27 K** in super-insulated dewars. The major challenge is the **boil-off loss (BOL)** caused by heat ingress $Q_{in}$:

$$BOL = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{m_{LH_2} \cdot \Delta h_{vap}}$$

where $\Delta h_{vap} = 445.6$ **kJ/kg** is the latent heat of vaporization of H₂.

The **liquefaction energy** of hydrogen is among the highest of any gas:

$$W_{liq} \approx 0.35 \times \text{LHV}_{H_2} = 0.35 \times 120 \approx 42 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

This means **~35 % of the energy content is consumed in liquefaction alone**, making LH₂ economically justified only for aviation, space, and long-distance transport.

## 2.3 Solid-State Hydrogen Storage

### 2.3.1 Metal Hydrides (e.g., LaNi₅H₆, MgH₂, TiFeH₂)

The fundamental thermodynamic relation governing absorption/desorption is the **van't Hoff equation**:

$$\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T} + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}$$

| Parameter | Typical Range for Metal Hydrides |
|---|---|
| $\Delta H$ (enthalpy of desorption) | 20 – 80 kJ/mol H₂ |
| $\Delta S$ (entropy of desorption) | 80 – 130 J/(mol·K) |
| Operating $T$ (1 bar equilibrium) | 250 – 600 K |

### 2.3.2 Complex / Chemical Hydrides

- **Borohydrides** (e.g., NaBH₄, LiBH₄) — very high theoretical $\rho_g$ (up to **18.5 wt %** for LiBH₄).
- **Alanates** (e.g., NaAlH₄) — reversible with Ti-catalyst doping.
- **Ammonia (NH₃)** — 17.8 wt % H, decomposed via Haber–Bosch reverse.

### 2.3.3 Adsorption (Physisorption)

Materials like **MOFs (Metal-Organic Frameworks)**, **CNTs**, and **activated carbon** store H₂ via weak van der Waals forces. Requires **cryogenic temperatures (77 K LN₂)** to achieve meaningful capacities. Binding energy: **4 – 10 kJ/mol**.

## 2.4 Underground Hydrogen Storage (UHS)

Stores large quantities of gaseous H₂ in:
- **Salt caverns** (most common, e.g., Teesside, UK; Spindletop, USA).
- **Aquifers** (porous rock + cap rock).
- **Depleted oil/gas reservoirs**.

Adiabatic compression of gas in a fixed-volume cavern:

$$P_1 V = Z_1 n_1 R T \quad ; \quad P_2 V = Z_2 n_2 R T$$

Mass of H₂ stored per cycle: $\Delta m = m_{in} - m_{out} = M_{H_2}(n_{in} - n_{out})$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Consolidated)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Real gas law | $PV = ZnRT$ | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) |
| 2 | Ideal isothermal work | $W = nRT \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | Minimum compression work |
| 3 | Gravimetric density | $\rho_g = m_{H_2}/m_{sys}$ | wt %, dimensionless |
| 4 | Volumetric density | $\rho_v = m_{H_2}/V_{sys}$ | kg/m³ |
| 5 | Energy density | $E_d = \rho_g \times 120$ | MJ/kg-system |
| 6 | Boil-off rate | $BOL = Q_{in}/(m_{LH_2}\Delta h_{vap})$ | fraction/day |
| 7 | van't Hoff | $\ln P = -\Delta H/(RT) + \Delta S/R$ | Plot $\ln P$ vs $1/T$ |
| 8 | Joule-Thomson inversion | $\mu_{JT} = (2a/RT - b)/C_p$ | Real gas cooling on throttling |
| 9 | Liquefaction work | $W_{liq} \approx 0.35 \cdot \text{LHV}$ | ~42 MJ/kg |
| 10 | Compressibility | $Z = PV/(nRT)$ | $Z = 1$ ideal |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a problem gives a hydrogen tank with pressure and volume, immediately write the real gas law with $Z$ to get full marks. Forgetting $Z$ costs 1 – 2 marks in valuation.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Fuel-cell vehicles (FCVs)** — Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo: **Type IV 700 bar CGH₂**.
- **Aviation / Space** — NASA, ISRO: **LH₂** as rocket propellant.
- **Grid-scale seasonal storage** — **Salt cavern UHS** (Spindletop holds 2.6 million kg H₂).
- **Portable electronics / drones** — **Metal hydrides** and **chemical hydrides** (e.g., NH₃).
- **Renewable integration** — Solid-state and UHS act as long-duration buffers for solar/wind.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrogen Mass Stored in a Type IV 700 bar Cylinder

**Given:**
- Cylinder volume $V = 0.122$ m³ (typical FCV tank)
- Storage pressure $P = 700$ bar $= 700 \times 10^5$ Pa
- Temperature $T = 298$ K
- Compressibility factor at 700 bar, 298 K: $Z = 1.45$

**Find:** Mass of H₂ stored.

### Step 1 — Start with the Real Gas Equation

We begin with the universal real gas equation that incorporates the compressibility factor $Z$:

$$PV = ZnRT$$

This equation is the starting point because, at the high pressure of 700 bar, hydrogen deviates significantly from ideal-gas behaviour, and $Z$ must be retained.

### Step 2 — Solve for Number of Moles

Rearranging the real gas equation algebraically to isolate the number of moles $n$:

$$n = \dfrac{PV}{ZRT}$$

This step simply performs division: the term $PV$ is in the numerator, while the product $ZRT$ (compressibility × gas constant × temperature) goes to the denominator.

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

Substituting $P = 700 \times 10^5$ Pa, $V = 0.122$ m³, $Z = 1.45$, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T = 298$ K:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{(700 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa})(0.122\,\text{m}^{3})}{(1.45)(8.314)(298)} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{8.54 \times 10^{6}}{3590.66} \\[6pt]
&= 2378.4 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

The numerator computes to $8.54 \times 10^6$ J (since Pa·m³ = J). The denominator computes to approximately $3590.66$ J/mol. Their ratio yields the number of moles stored.

### Step 4 — Convert Moles to Mass Using Molar Mass

Using the molar mass of H₂, $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= n \times M_{H_2} \\[6pt]
&= 2378.4 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \\[6pt]
&= 4.795 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying moles by molar mass yields mass. This is the actual hydrogen payload in the tank.

### Step 5 — Compute Volumetric and Gravimetric Densities

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_v &= \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{V_{sys}} = \dfrac{4.795}{0.122} = 39.3 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\[6pt]
\rho_g &= \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{m_{system}}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $m_{system} = 95$ kg (typical Type IV tank mass):

$$
\rho_g = \dfrac{4.795}{95} = 0.0505 = 5.05\,\text{wt \%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Reference Value:** Modern Type IV tanks achieve **~5 – 6 wt %** at 700 bar, matching the 5.05 % we just derived. This is the current 2024 industry benchmark for FCVs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Isothermal Compression Work for 1 kg of H₂ from 1 bar to 700 bar

**Given:** $m_{H_2} = 1$ kg, $T = 298$ K, $P_1 = 1$ bar, $P_2 = 700$ bar.

### Step 1 — Convert Mass to Moles

$$
n = \dfrac{m}{M_{H_2}} = \dfrac{1\,\text{kg}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg/mol}} = 496.03 \text{ mol}
$$

This is a direct division: 1 kg of hydrogen contains approximately 496 moles.

### Step 2 — Apply the Ideal Isothermal Compression Formula

For an isothermal, reversible (minimum-work) process on an ideal gas:

$$W_{min} = nRT \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

The log-pressure ratio appears because the work integral of $V\,dP$ for an ideal gas at constant $T$ reduces to a natural logarithm of the pressure ratio.

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{min} &= (496.03)(8.314)(298)\ln\left(\dfrac{700}{1}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 1.229 \times 10^6 \times \ln(700) \\[6pt]
&= 1.229 \times 10^6 \times 6.551 \\[6pt]
&= 8.05 \times 10^6 \text{ J} \\[6pt]
&= 8.05 \text{ MJ per kg of H}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

The arithmetic is straightforward: take the natural log of 700, which is 6.551, then multiply by the product $nRT$.

### Step 4 — Express as Percentage of LHV

$$
\eta_{comp} = \dfrac{W_{min}}{\text{LHV}_{H_2}} \times 100 = \dfrac{8.05}{120} \times 100 = 6.71\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *theoretical minimum* (reversible isothermal). Real multi-stage compressors with cooling approach **10 – 12 %**. The 3.3 % gap is the **irreversibility / entropy generation** of the actual process.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Boil-Off Loss for a 50 m³ LH₂ Storage Tank

**Given:** $V_{LH_2} = 50$ m³, $\rho_{LH_2} = 70.8$ kg/m³, $Q_{in} = 1.2$ kW (typical heat ingress), $\Delta h_{vap} = 445.6$ kJ/kg.

### Step 1 — Compute Mass of LH₂

$$
m_{LH_2} = V \times \rho_{LH_2} = 50 \times 70.8 = 3540 \text{ kg}
$$

The density of liquid hydrogen at 1 atm is fixed by its saturation properties.

### Step 2 — Compute Boil-Off Mass Flow Rate

$$
\dot{m}_{BOL} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{\Delta h_{vap}} = \dfrac{1.2 \text{ kW}}{445.6 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 2.693 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

This is a direct energy balance: each kg of liquid that boils absorbs 445.6 kJ.

### Step 3 — Convert to Daily Boil-Off Fraction

$$
BOL_{day} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{BOL} \times 86400}{m_{LH_2}} = \dfrac{2.693 \times 10^{-3} \times 86400}{3540} = 0.0657 = 6.57\,\% \text{ per day}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **6.57 %/day is high.** Modern aerospace dewars achieve **< 0.1 %/day** using multi-layer insulation (MLI) with 30 – 60 layers of aluminized Mylar. The KTU 2024 expected answer is therefore **0.05 – 0.5 %/day** for well-designed systems.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Van't Hoff Equation for a Metal Hydride

For the reaction $\text{MH}_n \rightleftharpoons \text{M} + \dfrac{n}{2}\text{H}_2$, the equilibrium plateau pressure is:

$$
\ln P_{eq} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T} + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}
$$

**Given:** For MgH₂, $\Delta H = 74$ kJ/mol H₂, $\Delta S = 130$ J/(mol·K).

### Step 1 — Compute Desorption Temperature at 1 bar

Setting $P_{eq} = 1$ bar, so $\ln P_{eq} = 0$:

$$
0 = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{\Delta H}{\Delta S} = \dfrac{74\,000}{130} = 569.2 \text{ K} = 296\,^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

This shows the **major drawback of MgH₂**: it requires ~300 °C to release hydrogen at 1 bar.

### Step 2 — Compute Plateau Pressure at 300 °C (573 K)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln P &= -\dfrac{74\,000}{8.314 \times 573} + \dfrac{130}{8.314} \\[6pt]
&= -15.53 + 15.64 \\[6pt]
&= 0.11 \\[6pt]
P_{eq} &= e^{0.11} = 1.12 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This confirms MgH₂ operates in the 1 – 10 bar range only at **550 – 600 K**, which is why nanostructuring and catalyst doping (e.g., Nb₂O₅) are active KTU 2024 research topics.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU 2024 Frequently Asked)

| Parameter | CGH₂ (700 bar) | LH₂ (20 K) | Metal Hydride (LaNi₅) | MOF (77 K) | Salt Cavern |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho_g$ (wt %) | 5 – 6 | ~100 (pure) | 1 – 2 | 1 – 7 (theor.) | n/a (large scale) |
| $\rho_v$ (kg/m³) | 40 | 70.8 | 50 – 100 | 20 – 50 | 5 – 15 (reservoir) |
| Energy to store | 10 – 12 % LHV | 30 – 40 % LHV | < 5 % LHV | 20 – 30 % LHV | 1 – 2 % LHV |
| Boil-off / losses | Slow leak | 0.1 – 1 %/day | None (reversible) | Slow desorption | None |
| Cost (USD/kg H₂ stored) | $15 – 20 | $20 – 30 | $50 – 100 | $80+ | < $1 |
| Maturity | Commercial (FCV) | Commercial (aviation) | R&D / niche | Lab scale | Commercial (3 sites) |
| Safety | High pressure | Cryo + asphyxiation | Mild | Cryo sorbent | Geological safe |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Taxonomy of Hydrogen Storage Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["H2 STORAGE METHODS"] --> B["GASEOUS STATE"]
    A --> C["LIQUID STATE"]
    A --> D["SOLID STATE"]
    A --> E["UNDERGROUND"]

    B1 --> B1a["Type I All Metal"]
    B1 --> B1b["Type II Hoop Wrap"]

    B1 --> B1e["Type V All Composite"]

    C --> C1["Liquid Hydrogen LH2"]
    C1 --> C1a["Stationary Dewar"]
    C1 --> C1b["Transport Trailer"]
    C1 --> C1c["Cryo Compressed 350 bar"]

    D --> D1["Metal Hydrides"]
    D1 --> D1a["AB5 e.g. LaNi5H6"]
    D1 --> D1b["AB2 e.g. TiZrV"]
    D1 --> D1c["Mg Based e.g. MgH2"]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Functional Block Architecture of an On-Board CGH₂ Storage System (FCV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INLET["H2 Inlet from Refueling Nozzle"] --> FILT["Particulate Filter 10 micron"]
    FILT --> REG["Pressure Regulator 700 to 20 bar"]
    REG --> TANK["Composite Tank Type IV 700 bar"]
    TANK --> SHUT["Solenoid Shutoff Valve"]
    SHUT --> PRU["Pressure Reduction Unit 20 to 10 bar"]
    PRU --> FCSTK["Fuel Cell Stack"]
    FCSTK --> BATT["Li Ion Buffer Battery"]
    FCSTK --> MOTOR["Traction Motor"]
    BATT --> MOTOR
    TANK --> TC["Thermal Management  LN2 or Air Cool"]
    TC --> TANK
    TANK --> SENS["Pressure and Temp Sensor Array"]
    SENS --> ECU["Vehicle ECU and Safety Interlock"]
    ECU --> SHUT
    ECU --> REG

    style INLET fill:#c5e0b4,color:#000000
    style TANK fill:#ffd966,color:#000000,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px
    style FCSTK fill:#f4b183,color:#000000
    style MOTOR fill:#b4c7e7,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Type III and Type IV compressed hydrogen storage vessels with respect to construction, pressure rating, and gravimetric density.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Type III | Type IV |
|---|---|---|
| Liner material | Metal (aluminium) | Polymer (HDPE) |
| Wrapping | Full composite over metal | Full composite over polymer |
| Pressure rating | 450 – 700 bar | 700 bar |
| Gravimetric density | 4 – 6 wt % | 5 – 10 wt % |
| Weight | Heavier | Lighter (~70 % of Type III) |
| Application | Early FCV prototypes | Current FCVs (Mirai, Nexo) |

**(1 mark per key difference + concluding line stating that Type IV dominates current FCV market.)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three advantages and three disadvantages of liquid hydrogen storage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. Very high volumetric density (**70.8 kg/m³** vs. 39 kg/m³ for 700 bar CGH₂).
2. Zero pressure hazard (stored at ~1 atm in insulated dewar).
3. High purity (impurities freeze out during liquefaction).

**Disadvantages:**
1. Boil-off losses (**0.1 – 1 %/day**) due to heat ingress.
2. Very high liquefaction energy (~**35 % of LHV**).
3. Extreme cryogenic temperature (**20.27 K**) requires multi-layer insulation (MLI).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 3]**

**A.(a)** With neat sketches, classify the different methods of hydrogen storage and explain compressed gas storage in detail, including types of pressure vessels and their relative merits. **(7 marks)**

**A.(b)** A fuel-cell vehicle uses a Type IV hydrogen tank of internal volume **0.122 m³** pressurized to **700 bar** at **298 K**. Using the compressibility factor $Z = 1.45$, calculate: (i) the mass of hydrogen stored, (ii) the volumetric density, and (iii) the energy stored in kWh. Take $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), LHV = 120 MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution A.(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Classification:**
Storage methods are classified into:
- Gaseous (compressed gas CGH₂)
- Liquid (cryogenic LH₂)
- Solid (metal hydrides, complex hydrides, adsorbents)
- Underground (salt caverns, aquifers)

A labeled block diagram should be drawn (refer Section 4.1 Mermaid block).

**Step 2 (3 marks) — CGH₂ Detail:**
Hydrogen gas is compressed into high-pressure cylinders. Pressure ranges from 350 bar (industrial) to 700 bar (FCV). The real gas law $PV = ZnRT$ governs storage, with $Z > 1$ at high pressures. Compression work $W = nRT \ln(P_2/P_1)$.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Vessel Types:**
- **Type I:** All metal, low cost, heavy → industrial use.
- **Type II:** Metal liner + hoop wrap → CNG.
- **Type III:** Metal liner + full composite → mid-range FCV.
- **Type IV:** Polymer liner + full composite → modern FCV (best $\rho_g$).
- **Type V:** No liner, all composite → R&D.

Merits: Type IV has the highest gravimetric density (5 – 10 wt %) and is the current industry standard.

---

### Model Solution A.(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Compute moles using real gas law:**

$$
n = \dfrac{PV}{ZRT} = \dfrac{(700 \times 10^{5})(0.122)}{(1.45)(8.314)(298)} = 2378.4 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute mass:**

$$
m = n \times M = 2378.4 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 4.795 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Volumetric density:**

$$
\rho_v = \dfrac{4.795}{0.122} = 39.3 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Energy in kWh:**

$$
E = m \times \text{LHV} = 4.795 \times 120 \times 10^6 = 5.754 \times 10^8 \text{ J}
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{5.754 \times 10^8}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 159.8 \text{ kWh}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Forgetting $Z = 1.45$ → loses 1 mark (use real gas law).
> 2. Converting bar to Pa incorrectly → loses 1 mark (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa).
> 3. Not converting J to kWh at the end → loses 1 mark.
> 4. Using $M = 2$ g/mol instead of 2.016 g/mol → minor deduction (½ mark).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 3]**

**B.(a)** Explain the concept of solid-state hydrogen storage using metal hydrides. Derive the **van't Hoff equation** and discuss how the plateau pressure varies with temperature. For MgH₂, given $\Delta H = 74$ kJ/mol H₂ and $\Delta S = 130$ J/(mol·K), calculate the temperature at which the desorption pressure becomes 1 bar. **(7 marks)**

**B.(b)** A 50 m³ cryogenic tank stores liquid hydrogen at 20.27 K. Heat ingress into the tank is **1.2 kW**. The latent heat of vaporization of H₂ is **445.6 kJ/kg** and the density of LH₂ is **70.8 kg/m³**. Calculate: (i) the mass of LH₂ stored, (ii) the boil-off rate in kg/s, and (iii) the percentage boil-off per day. Comment on how boil-off can be reduced. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution B.(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Concept:**
Metal hydrides store hydrogen via reversible chemical bonding: $\text{M} + \dfrac{n}{2}\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{MH}_n$. Examples: LaNi₅H₆, MgH₂, TiFeH₂.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Derivation of van't Hoff equation:**

At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0 = \Delta H - T\Delta S$. Also $\Delta G = -RT \ln K_p$ where $K_p = P_{H_2}^{n/2}$. For simplicity, writing for the standard form:

$$
\ln P_{H_2} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Trend:** A plot of $\ln P$ vs $1/T$ gives a straight line with slope $-\Delta H/R$ and intercept $\Delta S/R$. Higher $T$ → higher plateau pressure.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Calculation:**
Setting $P = 1$ bar ⇒ $\ln P = 0$:

$$
T = \dfrac{\Delta H}{\Delta S} = \dfrac{74\,000}{130} = 569.2 \text{ K} = 296\,^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This high temperature (~300 °C) is the main reason MgH₂ is not commercial yet; catalyst doping with Nb₂O₅ and nanostructuring reduce this to ~250 °C.

---

### Model Solution B.(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Mass of LH₂:**

$$
m_{LH_2} = V \times \rho = 50 \times 70.8 = 3540 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Boil-off rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_{BOL} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{\Delta h_{vap}} = \dfrac{1.2}{445.6} = 2.693 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Percentage per day:**

$$
BOL\% = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{BOL} \times 86\,400}{m_{LH_2}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2.693 \times 10^{-3} \times 86\,400}{3540} \times 100 = 6.57\,\%/\text{day}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Reduction strategies:**
- Use **multi-layer insulation (MLI)** with 30 – 60 layers of aluminized Mylar.
- Use **vacuum-jacketed dewars** to eliminate convective heat ingress.
- **Re-liquefy** boil-off gas using on-site cryocooler.
- Use **cryo-compressed** storage at 200 – 350 bar to reduce BOL.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often forget to convert seconds to days in the boil-off percentage calculation. Show the unit conversion step explicitly (86 400 s/day) to earn full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — General Storage Methods (Module 3)**

- ☐ **Four families of H₂ storage:** gaseous (CGH₂), liquid (LH₂), solid (hydrides/adsorbents), underground (caverns).
- ☐ **Real gas law $PV = ZnRT$** is mandatory for high-pressure problems; $Z \approx 1.45$ at 700 bar, 298 K.
- ☐ **Type IV tanks** (polymer liner + full composite) are the current FCV standard with **~5 – 6 wt %** at 700 bar.
- ☐ **Volumetric density of 700 bar CGH₂** ≈ **39 kg/m³**; of LH₂ = **70.8 kg/m³**.
- ☐ **Liquefaction energy** of H₂ ≈ **35 % of LHV** (~42 MJ/kg), making LH₂ expensive.
- ☐ **LH₂ boil-off** is driven by heat ingress $Q_{in}$: $BOL = Q_{in}/\Delta h_{vap}$; mitigated by MLI + vacuum jacket.
- ☐ **Metal hydride equilibrium** is governed by **van't Hoff equation**: $\ln P = -\Delta H/(RT) + \Delta S/R$.
- ☐ **MgH₂** requires **~300 °C** to release H₂ at 1 bar (key disadvantage; high $\Delta H$).
- ☐ **Gravimetric density** $\rho_g$ is the system-level mass fraction; **DOE 2025 target = 6.5 wt %**.
- ☐ **Volumetric density** $\rho_v$ is system-level mass per unit volume; **DOE 2025 target = 50 kg/m³**.
- ☐ **Salt cavern UHS** is the only method commercially proven for **GWh-scale** seasonal storage.
- ☐ **MOF and CNT adsorption** require **77 K (LN₂)** for meaningful capacities; binding energy 4 – 10 kJ/mol (physisorption).
- ☐ **Critical constants of H₂:** $T_c = 33.2$ K, $P_c = 13.0$ bar — remember these for Part A definitions.
- ☐ **Normal boiling point** of H₂ = **20.27 K** (≈ −252.87 °C).
- ☐ **Safety flammability range in air** = **4 – 75 vol %** (widest of any fuel).
- ☐ **Always convert bar → Pa** (× 10⁵) before substituting in SI units.
- ☐ **Always convert J → kWh** by dividing by $3.6 \times 10^6$ when energy is asked in kWh.
- ☐ **Always show the unit step** for BOL calculations: 86 400 s/day.
- ☐ **Remember $\Delta S$** of desorption is typically **80 – 130 J/(mol·K)** for metal hydrides.
- ☐ **DOE / IEA target** for onboard storage by 2030: 8 wt % system-level gravimetric density.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressed Storage - Composite Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compressed Storage - Composite Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Composite Cylinder** for compressed hydrogen storage is a high-pressure pressure vessel consisting of a load-bearing non-metallic reinforcement (typically carbon fiber, aramid fiber, or glass fiber embedded in an epoxy or thermoplastic matrix) overwrapping a non-load-bearing or semi-load-bearing thin liner, designed to safely contain hydrogen at service pressures of **350 bar (≈35 MPa)** or **700 bar (≈70 MPa)** with a typical design life of **15 to 20 years** and a fatigue cycle rating of **up to 11,250 fill cycles** as per ISO 11439 and EC 79 (UN Regulation No. 79) for hydrogen-powered vehicles.

Composite cylinders are classified by the Compressed Gas Association (CGA) and ISO 11439 into four structural types:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification (CGA C-12 / ISO 11439):**
> - **Type I**: All-metal (steel/aluminum) — baseline reference only.
> - **Type II**: Metal liner with hoop-wrapped fiber composite overwrap.
> - **Type III**: Metal (aluminum) liner fully overwrapped with fiber composite.
> - **Type IV**: Non-metallic (high-density polyethylene, PA6, or PVDF) liner fully overwrapped with fiber composite.

For mobile and on-board vehicle applications in KTU 2024 syllabus, only **Type III and Type IV** composite cylinders are commercially relevant.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine trying to hold water inside a thin paper cup under high pressure — the paper would burst instantly because paper is weak. Now wrap the paper cup tightly with many layers of strong, unidirectional nylon thread (fiber) glued in place. The threads carry the load, and the paper only acts as a leak barrier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy Mapping:**
> - The **paper cup** = the **liner** (aluminum for Type III, polymer for Type IV). Its only job is to prevent hydrogen gas from leaking out, not to bear the pressure.
> - The **wrapped thread** = the **fiber-reinforced composite** (carbon-fiber + epoxy matrix). It carries **almost all** of the mechanical load.
> - The **glue** = the **epoxy/thermoplastic matrix** that locks fibers in place and transfers stress between them.

This layered design enables composite cylinders to be **4 to 5 times lighter** than equivalent Type I steel cylinders, while safely holding hydrogen at pressures **4 to 7 times higher** than the standard CNG cylinders used in city gas vehicles.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (Bold-Formatted for Quick Recall)

> - **Service Pressure (Automotive):** **350 bar (35 MPa)** and **700 bar (70 MPa)**
> - **Test Pressure:** **1.5 × Service Pressure** = 52.5 MPa (350-bar) / 105 MPa (700-bar)
> - **Burst Pressure (Minimum):** **2.25 × Service Pressure**
> - **Design Service Life:** **15 years** (EC 79) to **20 years** (ISO 11439 typical)
> - **Fatigue Life:** **11,250 cycles** (refuelling cycles) at ambient temperature
> - **Working Temperature:** **-40 °C to +85 °C**
> - **Hydrogen Permeation Limit (Type IV):** **≤ 1.0 × 10⁻⁴ mbar·L/s** per cylinder (per ECE R110)
> - **Composite Fiber Volume Fraction (V_f):** typically **0.55 to 0.70**
> - **Gravimetric Density Achieved:** **5.7 wt%** (Type IV, 700 bar, system level target)
> - **Volumetric Density Achieved:** **≈ 25–30 g H₂/L** (Type IV, 700 bar)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Layer-by-Layer Load Path

The pressure load follows a clearly defined path through the cylinder wall. Understanding this path is essential for both design and KTU exam-style analysis.

- **Step 1 — Internal Pressure Acting on the Liner:**
  Hydrogen gas at pressure $P$ pushes radially outward on the inner surface of the liner. The liner experiences a biaxial stress state consisting of **hoop stress** (circumferential) and **longitudinal stress** (axial). In a thin-walled cylinder, the hoop stress is twice the longitudinal stress:
  
  $$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot r}{t} \quad ; \quad \sigma_{long} = \frac{P \cdot r}{2t}$$

- **Step 2 — Strain Transfer at the Liner–Composite Interface:**
  As the liner expands elastically under internal pressure, it strains the inner fibers of the composite overwrap. The epoxy matrix bonds the two, so both layers must share a compatible radial expansion at the interface. This is governed by the **iso-strain (compatibility) condition** for bonded cylinders:

  $$\varepsilon_{hoop,\,liner} = \varepsilon_{hoop,\,composite}$$

- **Step 3 — Composite Overwrap Carries the Bulk of the Hoop Load:**
  Because the fiber's ultimate tensile strength is typically **3,000–7,000 MPa** (carbon fiber T700/T800/T1000) versus aluminum liner's yield strength of ≈ 300 MPa, the composite takes **>90%** of the load once strain compatibility is established. The liner is sized only for permeation, fatigue crack resistance, and burst redundancy (a "fail-safe" liner).

- **Step 4 — Helical Winding Geometry Resolves the Biaxial Load:**
  Fibers cannot be wound purely circumferentially without leaving a structural weakness in the axial direction. Composite cylinders therefore use **two winding angles**:
  
  > [!NOTE]
  > - **Hoop layers (≈ 80–90°):** Resist the dominant hoop stress.
  > - **Helical (low-angle, ≈ ±10° to ±20°):** Resist axial and shear stresses.
  > - Typical volume split: **60–70% hoop**, **30–40% helical**.

- **Step 5 — Matrix Phase Prevents Fiber Buckling:**
  The epoxy/thermoplastic matrix holds fibers in alignment under compressive loading (e.g., during impact or external crushing). Without the matrix, the slender high-strength fibers would micro-buckle instantly. This is why **fiber volume fraction** ($V_f$) is engineered to be **0.55–0.70** — high enough for strength, low enough for matrix continuity.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Equation | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use |
|---|----------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Hoop stress (thin-walled) | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$ | Liner check; baseline sizing |
| 2 | Longitudinal stress | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2t}$ | Liner axial check |
| 3 | Effective composite thickness (homogenized) | $t_c = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{V_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \eta}$ | Overwrap thickness |
| 4 | Fiber volume fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{A_{fiber}}{A_{total}}$ | Composite layup control |
| 5 | Iso-strain compatibility | $\dfrac{P \cdot r_{liner}}{t_{liner} \cdot E_{liner}} = \dfrac{P \cdot r_c}{t_c \cdot E_c}$ | Load-sharing between liner and composite |
| 6 | Rule of mixtures (axial modulus) | $E_c = V_f E_f + (1 - V_f) E_m$ | Effective composite modulus |
| 7 | Burst pressure (fiber-dominated) | $P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_c \cdot \sigma_{f,u} \cdot V_f}{r}$ | Safety margin calculation |
| 8 | Volumetric storage density | $\rho_V = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{V_{cylinder}} \left[\dfrac{g}{L}\right]$ | Compare with liquid H₂ (≈ 70 g/L) |
| 9 | Gravimetric storage density | $\rho_m = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{m_{cylinder}} \times 100\,[\%]$ | On-board efficiency |
| 10 | Ideal-gas mass of stored H₂ | $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{P \cdot V \cdot M_{H_2}}{R_u \cdot T}$ | Filling calculations |
| 11 | Compressibility factor (real gas) | $Z = \dfrac{P V_m}{R_u T}$ | High-pressure correction |
| 12 | Safety factor (KTU typical) | $SF = \dfrac{P_{burst}}{P_{service}} \geq 2.25$ | Code compliance (ISO 11439) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Conditions to Use in KTU Numericals:**
> - $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ **kg/mol**
> - $R_u = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)**
> - $T_{ref} = 298.15$ **K (NTP, 25 °C)**
> - $T_{fill} = 288$ **K (15 °C)** for vehicle fill
> - $P_{service}$ = 35 MPa (350 bar) or 70 MPa (700 bar)

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Composite cylinders are the **backbone of fuel-cell electric vehicle (FCEV) commercialization**. As of 2024, every commercially produced FCEV (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo, Hyundai XCIENT Fuel Cell trucks, heavy-duty trucks) uses Type IV composite cylinders for 700-bar on-board storage. Stationary backup-power applications (telecom towers, data centers) increasingly use larger Type III 350-bar vessels for grid-scale hydrogen buffering. The same technology is now being scaled for **Type V (linerless all-composite) cylinders** under development by Composites Adv. Tech. and Hexagon Purus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Pressure Vessel

**Statement:** A long cylindrical pressure vessel of inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$, and wall thickness $t = r_o - r_i \ll r_i$ is subjected to internal pressure $P$. Derive the expression for the hoop (circumferential) stress.

**Step 1 — Free-Body Cut:**
Cut the cylinder along a longitudinal plane and isolate one half. The internal pressure acts on the projected area, balanced by the hoop stress in the two cut walls.

The projected area carrying pressure = $2 r_i L$, where $L$ is the cylinder length.
The resisting force = $2 t L \sigma_h$.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium Equation:**

$$P \cdot (2 r_i L) = 2 t L \sigma_h$$

**Step 3 — Simplify by Cancelling Common Terms:**

$$P \cdot 2 r_i L = \sigma_h \cdot 2 t L$$

Cancel $2L$ from both sides:

$$P \cdot r_i = \sigma_h \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Hoop Stress:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot r}{t}}$$

**Step 5 — Longitudinally (Cap-End Equilibrium):**
Cap area = $\pi r^2$; axial stress force = $2 \pi r t \sigma_L$. Equate and solve:

$$\boxed{\sigma_L = \frac{P \cdot r}{2t}}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca, KTU-favoured):**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_L}{2} = \frac{P \cdot r}{4t}$$

This is a **3-mark derivation** frequently asked in KTU Module-3 exams.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Composite Overwrap Thickness for Type IV Cylinder

**Statement:** A Type IV cylinder has a polymer liner of inner radius $r_i = 175$ mm and negligible load-bearing capacity. Hydrogen is stored at 700 bar (70 MPa). Carbon-fiber T700 has ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{f,u} = 4900$ MPa. The design fiber volume fraction is $V_f = 0.60$, and a winding efficiency $\eta = 0.90$ accounts for fiber misalignment and tow gap. Calculate the minimum composite overwrap thickness.

**Step 1 — Identify Load to Be Carried by Composite:**
In a Type IV design, the liner does not contribute to load-bearing. The composite alone resists the full internal pressure:

$$\sigma_c \cdot t_c = P \cdot r$$

**Step 2 — Express Composite Stress in Terms of Fiber Stress:**
The composite is fiber-dominated, so:

$$\sigma_c = V_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \eta$$

where $\eta$ is the **Krenchel/Kelly efficiency factor** for the winding angle.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Equilibrium Equation:**

$$V_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \eta \cdot t_c = P \cdot r$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t_c$:**

$$t_c = \frac{P \cdot r}{V_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \eta}$$

**Step 5 — Insert Numerical Values:**
$P = 70 \times 10^6$ Pa, $r = 0.175$ m, $V_f = 0.60$, $\sigma_f = 4.9 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\eta = 0.90$.

$$t_c = \frac{(70 \times 10^6)(0.175)}{(0.60)(4.9 \times 10^9)(0.90)}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate Numerator and Denominator Separately:**

$$N = (70 \times 10^6)(0.175) = 12.25 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$

$$D = (0.60)(4.9 \times 10^9)(0.90) = 2.646 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 7 — Final Calculation:**

$$t_c = \frac{12.25 \times 10^6}{2.646 \times 10^9} = 4.63 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t_c \approx 4.6 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical 700-bar Type IV cylinder actually has a **20–30 mm** total composite wall (helical + hoop plies), but only a portion of the layers (those near the hoop direction) carry the dominant hoop load. The rest carry axial and shear. The "effective" $t_c$ from the simple fiber-strength model gives the **minimum** required.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Burst Pressure of a Type III Cylinder with Aluminum Liner

**Statement:** A Type III cylinder has an aluminum liner (yield $S_y = 280$ MPa) of inner radius $r = 0.150$ m and liner thickness $t_{Al} = 2.5$ mm. The composite overwrap is $t_c = 12$ mm thick with effective hoop strength $\sigma_c = 1800$ MPa. Determine the burst pressure assuming (a) plastic flow of the liner occurs at yield, and (b) the composite remains elastic.

**Step 1 — Stress Equilibrium (Total Wall):**
For a bonded cylinder at burst, total wall must carry:

$$\sigma_{total} \cdot (t_{Al} + t_c) = P_{burst} \cdot r$$

**Step 2 — Treat Liner as Yielded (Plastic Plateau):**
At burst, aluminum has yielded and is at $S_y$:

$$\sigma_{Al} = 280 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Composite Stress at Liner Yield (Iso-Strain):**
The aluminum and composite are bonded, so equal hoop strain:

$$\varepsilon_{hoop} = \frac{\sigma_{Al}}{E_{Al}} = \frac{\sigma_c}{E_c}$$

For aluminum, $E_{Al} \approx 70$ GPa, and at $\sigma_{Al} = 280$ MPa:

$$\varepsilon_{hoop} = \frac{280 \times 10^6}{70 \times 10^9} = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Composite Stress at This Strain:**
Take effective $E_c \approx 70$ GPa (typical hybrid carbon/glass at $V_f \approx 0.55$):

$$\sigma_c = E_c \cdot \varepsilon_{hoop} = (70 \times 10^9)(4.0 \times 10^{-3}) = 280 \text{ MPa}$$

This is **far below** composite ultimate, so the composite is elastic — consistent with our assumption.

**Step 5 — Insert into Equilibrium:**

$$P_{burst} = \frac{\sigma_{Al} \cdot t_{Al} + \sigma_c \cdot t_c}{r}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Numerical Values:**

$$P_{burst} = \frac{(280)(2.5) + (280)(12)}{150} \text{ MPa·mm/mm}$$

$$P_{burst} = \frac{700 + 3360}{150} = \frac{4060}{150} = 27.07 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{burst} \approx 27.1 \text{ MPa} \approx 271 \text{ bar}}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Safety Factor at 70 MPa Service:**

$$SF = \frac{P_{burst}}{P_{service}} = \frac{27.1}{70} = 0.39$$

This is **below** 1.0 — the design is unsafe! In a real Type III 700-bar cylinder, the composite must dominate, with aluminum only acting as a permeation barrier. This calculation illustrates the **importance of the iso-strain assumption** in real design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a properly designed Type III cylinder, the aluminum liner is intentionally **sized to be below yield at the design pressure** by limiting $t_{Al}$ and ensuring the composite takes the bulk of the load. The liner's role is **permeation and fatigue resistance**, not pressure containment.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Type III vs. Type IV Comparison

| Performance Parameter | Type III (Al Liner) | Type IV (Polymer Liner) |
|----------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Liner material | Aluminum 6061-T6 | HDPE / PA6 / PVDF |
| Liner weight fraction | ~30% of cylinder mass | ~10% of cylinder mass |
| 700-bar gravimetric density | ≈ 3.5–4.0 wt% | ≈ 5.7 wt% (DOE 2020 target met) |
| Hydrogen permeation | Near-zero (metal) | Non-zero; barrier coatings needed |
| Fatigue life | Excellent | Excellent with proper liner design |
| Burst safety factor | 2.25–2.5 | 2.25–2.8 |
| Cost (2024 USD) | ~$3,000–$4,000/system | ~$2,500–$3,500/system |
| Manufacturing complexity | Filament winding + metal forming | Filament winding + polymer blow-molding |
| Recyclability / End-of-life | Difficult (Al/composite mix) | Moderate (polymer recyclable) |
| Dominant failure mode | Liner fatigue crack → composite delamination | Liner microcrack → slow leak / fatigue |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Layered Architecture of a Type IV Composite Cylinder (Block-Level Functional Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Inner Surface - Hydrogen Contact]:::layer1
    B[Polymer Liner - HDPE or PA6 - 3 to 6 mm]:::layer2
    C[Inner Barrier Coating - EVOH or Metallized Layer 50 to 100 micron]:::layer3
    D[Helical Composite Layers - Carbon Fiber Epoxy - 10 to 20 deg wind angle]:::layer4
    E[Hoop Composite Layers - Carbon Fiber Epoxy - 85 to 90 deg wind angle]:::layer5
    F[Outer Protective Coating - Polyurethane or Epoxy Gelcoat 0.5 mm]:::layer6
    G[External Surface - Impact and UV Resistant]:::layer7

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G

    H[Internal Pressure Load Path: P from Gas]:::load
    I[Hoop Stress Sigma h Carried by Hoop Fibers]:::load
    J[Axial and Shear Stress Carried by Helical Fibers]:::load
    K[Permeation Barrier Maintained by Liner and EVOH Coating]:::load

    H --> I
    H --> J
    H --> K
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define a Type IV composite cylinder for compressed hydrogen storage. List its main components and state the typical service pressures used in fuel-cell vehicles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Type IV composite cylinder is a high-pressure vessel in which a non-metallic polymer liner (typically high-density polyethylene HDPE, polyamide PA6, or polyvinylidene fluoride PVDF) is fully overwrapped with a fiber-reinforced plastic (FRP) composite, usually carbon fiber embedded in an epoxy matrix. **(1 mark)**

Main components: (i) polymer liner — leak-tight barrier, (ii) inner permeation barrier coating (EVOH/metallized layer), (iii) helical-wound composite layers, (iv) hoop-wound composite layers, (v) outer protective gelcoat/UV shield, (vi) integrated valve boss with PRD. **(1 mark)**

Service pressures used in fuel-cell vehicles: **350 bar (≈ 35 MPa)** for older/light-duty systems and **700 bar (≈ 70 MPa)** as the current global standard (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo). **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the role of hoop-wound and helical-wound composite layers in a Type IV cylinder.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Hoop-wound layers are wound at a high winding angle (85°–90°) relative to the cylinder axis. They carry the dominant **hoop (circumferential) stress** generated by internal hydrogen pressure, which is twice the longitudinal stress in a thin-walled cylinder. **(1 mark)**

Helical-wound layers are wound at a low angle (10°–20°) to the cylinder axis. They carry the **axial stress, shear stress, and torsional load**, and also provide balanced stiffness to prevent asymmetric bulging. **(1 mark)**

The two layers are interleaved during filament winding, with hoop layers forming roughly 60–70% of the total composite volume and helical layers the remaining 30–40%. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module-3 Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for the hoop stress and longitudinal stress in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel of inner radius $r$ and wall thickness $t$ subjected to internal pressure $P$. Show that the hoop stress is twice the longitudinal stress. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Type IV composite cylinder has an inner radius of **0.18 m**, a polymer liner of thickness **5 mm** (load-bearing capacity negligible), and is designed for a service pressure of **70 MPa**. The carbon-fiber overwrap has a fiber volume fraction $V_f = 0.65$, fiber ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{f,u} = 5,000$ MPa, and a Kelly-Krenchel winding efficiency $\eta = 0.88$. Calculate: (i) the minimum effective composite thickness required, (ii) the minimum burst pressure, and (iii) the safety factor achieved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks) — Already shown in Section 3.1 above.**
Key steps to write down:
- Free-body cut along longitudinal plane (2 marks)
- Equilibrium: $P \cdot 2rL = 2tL \cdot \sigma_h$ → $\sigma_h = Pr/t$ (3 marks)
- Cap-end equilibrium: $\sigma_L = Pr/2t$ (1 mark)
- Conclude $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_L$ (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

(i) **Minimum composite thickness (3 marks):**

$$t_c = \frac{P \cdot r}{V_f \cdot \sigma_{f,u} \cdot \eta}$$

$$t_c = \frac{(70 \times 10^6)(0.18)}{(0.65)(5000 \times 10^6)(0.88)}$$

Numerator = $12.6 \times 10^6$ N/m
Denominator = $(0.65)(5 \times 10^9)(0.88) = 2.86 \times 10^9$ N/m

$$t_c = \frac{12.6 \times 10^6}{2.86 \times 10^9} = 4.41 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = \boxed{4.41 \text{ mm}}$$

(ii) **Minimum burst pressure (2 marks):**

$$P_{burst} = \frac{V_f \cdot \sigma_{f,u} \cdot \eta \cdot t_c}{r} = \frac{2.86 \times 10^9 \times 4.41 \times 10^{-3}}{0.18}$$

$$P_{burst} = 12.6 \times 10^6 / 0.18 = \frac{12.6}{0.18} \times 10^6 = 70 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

Wait — this is just the service pressure. That's because at this point $\sigma_{f,u}$ has been exhausted. The burst pressure is reached exactly when the fiber reaches $\sigma_{f,u}$. Therefore the answer is:

$$P_{burst} = 70 \text{ MPa} = 700 \text{ bar}$$

This is the *minimum* — meaning the actual burst is higher because the equation assumed $\sigma_f = \sigma_{f,u}$.

(iii) **Safety factor (2 marks):**

$$SF = \frac{P_{burst}}{P_{service}} = \frac{70}{70} = 1.0$$

This is **NOT acceptable** for real design. Codes require $SF \geq 2.25$. **Engineering interpretation:** in practice, the composite wall is **2.25× thicker** than the calculated minimum, which is why real Type IV walls are 15–30 mm thick.

For a real cylinder with **actual** $t_c = 12$ mm:

$$P_{burst,real} = \frac{2.86 \times 10^9 \times 0.012}{0.18} = 190.7 \text{ MPa}$$

$$SF_{real} = \frac{190.7}{70} = 2.72 \quad (\text{Code compliant!})$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Compare Type III and Type IV composite cylinders with respect to liner material, weight, hydrogen permeation, gravimetric density, and typical application. State which type is preferred for 700-bar fuel-cell vehicles and justify. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Type III cylinder uses an aluminum 6061-T6 liner of inner radius $r = 0.150$ m and liner thickness $t_{Al} = 3.0$ mm. The composite overwrap is $t_c = 18$ mm with effective longitudinal modulus $E_c = 70$ GPa and effective hoop modulus $E_{c,h} = 110$ GPa. Aluminum has $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa, $\nu_{Al} = 0.33$, and yield strength $S_y = 280$ MPa. The cylinder is pressurized to $P = 35$ MPa. Determine: (i) the hoop stress in the aluminum liner, (ii) the hoop stress in the composite overwrap, and (iii) comment on whether the liner has yielded. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**
Provide a structured table (already presented in Section 3.4 above) covering:
- Liner material (1 mark)
- Weight and gravimetric density (2 marks)
- Hydrogen permeation (1 mark)
- Cost and application (1 mark)
- Justification for 700-bar FCEV use: Type IV is preferred because the polymer liner reduces mass by ~20% versus aluminum, enabling the 5.7 wt% system target. (2 marks)

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

(i) **Hoop stress in aluminum liner (3 marks):**
For a thin-walled vessel, the total wall must satisfy:

$$\sigma_h \cdot t_{total} = P \cdot r$$

The load is shared between liner and composite **based on stiffness (iso-strain)**. Equal hoop strain in both:

$$\frac{\sigma_{Al}}{E_{Al}} = \frac{\sigma_c}{E_{c,h}}$$

So $\sigma_c = \sigma_{Al} \cdot \dfrac{E_{c,h}}{E_{Al}} = \sigma_{Al} \cdot \dfrac{110}{70} = 1.571 \, \sigma_{Al}$

Total wall thickness: $t_{total} = 3.0 + 18 = 21$ mm = 0.021 m

The equilibrium of total wall:

$$P \cdot r = \sigma_{Al} \cdot t_{Al} + \sigma_c \cdot t_c$$

Substitute $\sigma_c$:

$$P \cdot r = \sigma_{Al} \cdot t_{Al} + 1.571 \, \sigma_{Al} \cdot t_c = \sigma_{Al} (t_{Al} + 1.571 \, t_c)$$

$$(35 \times 10^6)(0.150) = \sigma_{Al} (0.003 + 1.571 \times 0.018)$$

$$5.25 \times 10^6 = \sigma_{Al} (0.003 + 0.02828) = \sigma_{Al} \cdot 0.03128$$

$$\sigma_{Al} = \frac{5.25 \times 10^6}{0.03128} = 167.8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = \boxed{167.8 \text{ MPa}}$$

(ii) **Hoop stress in composite (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_c = 1.571 \times 167.8 = \boxed{263.6 \text{ MPa}}$$

(iii) **Yield check (2 marks):**
$\sigma_{Al} = 167.8$ MPa $<$ $S_y = 280$ MPa. **The liner has NOT yielded** (Factor of Safety on liner = $280/167.8 = 1.67$). The cylinder is operating within elastic design limits — consistent with Type III design philosophy where the aluminum remains elastic and serves primarily as a permeation/impact barrier.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply iso-strain (load-sharing) condition** — students often incorrectly assign all the load to the composite only, which is wrong for Type III where liner is semi-load-bearing.
> 2. **Unit mismatch** — converting 35 MPa to 35 × 10⁶ Pa is essential; mixing bar and MPa loses marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 1.5 in test pressure** — ISO 11439 requires hydrostatic test at 1.5× service. Don't confuse with burst pressure (2.25×).
> 4. **Neglecting helical layers** in the composite stress calculation — the effective modulus used must reflect the volume-weighted average of hoop and helical plies.
> 5. **Reporting burst pressure as just the service pressure** when the calculation is done at $\sigma_f = \sigma_{f,u}$ — always state clearly that this is the *minimum theoretical* burst, with real designs having SF > 2.25.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compressed Storage — Composite Cylinders: 1-Page Cheat Sheet**
>
> - **Two main types in syllabus:** Type III (aluminum liner) and Type IV (polymer liner). Type IV is the modern standard for 700-bar FCEVs.
> - **Two service pressures:** 35 MPa (350 bar) and 70 MPa (700 bar). 700 bar is global FCEV standard.
> - **Four governing standards to remember:** ISO 11439, ECE R110, EC 79 (UN R79), CGA C-12.
> - **Three key tests:** Hydrostatic test (1.5×), Burst test (2.25× minimum), Cycling test (≥ 11,250 cycles).
> - **Two classic stress formulas:** $\sigma_h = Pr/t$ and $\sigma_L = Pr/(2t)$. Ratio is exactly 2:1.
> - **One critical relationship:** $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_L$ — this appears in nearly every KTU Part-A question on this module.
> - **Composite overwrap minimum thickness:** $t_c = Pr/(V_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \eta)$. Always insert $\eta$ (winding efficiency, ≈ 0.85–0.90).
> - **Typical winding angle split:** 60–70% hoop (high angle) + 30–40% helical (low angle). This split is what makes the cylinder balanced.
> - **Design life:** 15–20 years; cycling life ≥ 11,250 fill–empty cycles.
> - **Hydrogen permeation target (Type IV):** ≤ $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ mbar·L/s per cylinder, achieved by adding an EVOH or metallized barrier layer.
> - **Storage density achieved (Type IV, 700 bar):** ≈ 5.7 wt% (system) and ≈ 25–30 g H₂/L — compare to liquid H₂ (70.8 g/L) and LH₂ energy density.
> - **Real failure modes (KTU expects you to know at least 3):** (1) Liner fatigue cracking, (2) Fiber–matrix debonding, (3) Hydrogen embrittlement of metallic boss, (4) Impact damage to outer surface, (5) Over-pressurization (PRD activation).
> - **Pressure Relief Device (PRD):** Mandatory component, designed to vent hydrogen safely in fire to prevent BLEVE-like rupture. Two types: thermally-activated (TPRD) and burst-disc type.
> - **Composite weight fraction:** Carbon fiber dominates (≈ 70% of mass), with epoxy matrix and liner making up the rest. Type IV is the lightest because the polymer liner is denser than aluminum? No — polymer is *less* dense (HDPE ≈ 0.95 g/cm³ vs. Al ≈ 2.7 g/cm³), so Type IV wins on weight.
> - **Key fibre types and moduli to remember:** Carbon T700 (~230 GPa), T800 (~290 GPa), T1000 (~640 GPa). E-glass ≈ 70 GPa. Aramid (Kevlar 49) ≈ 130 GPa.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carbon-based Materials for Hydrogen Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Carbon-based Materials for Hydrogen Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carbon-based hydrogen storage** refers to the reversible adsorption and desorption of molecular hydrogen (H₂) within the structural framework of engineered carbonaceous sorbents — including **activated carbons (ACs)**, **carbon nanotubes (CNTs)**, **graphene**, **fullerenes**, **carbon nanofibers (CNFs)**, and **templated carbons** — through predominantly **physisorption (van der Waals interaction)** with supplemental chemisorption pathways. The performance is quantified by **gravimetric capacity (wt%)**, **volumetric capacity (g H₂/L)**, and **binding energy (kJ/mol)**.

### 1.2 The KTU 2024 Syllabus Scope

The KTU Module 3 framework demands a comparative study of:
1. **Carbon nanotubes (single-walled & multi-walled)** for H₂ occlusion
2. **Activated carbon** as the commercial benchmark sorbent
3. **Graphene, fullerenes, and doped carbons** for advanced tuning
4. **Storage mechanisms** — physisorption vs. chemisorption vs. spillover
5. **DOE 2025 Targets**: **5.5 wt%** gravimetric and **40 g/L** volumetric capacity

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a **sponge city** built entirely of carbon. Microscopic H₂ molecules are like tiny cars parking in the city's vast network of tunnels, alleyways, and underground parking lots. The more tunnels (high surface area), the more cars fit. Some tunnels are coated with special magnets (dopants) that hold cars more tightly — these are the chemisorption sites. The carbon "city" must release its cars easily when needed (desorption) but hold them firmly enough at low pressures. Carbon nanotubes are like **multi-lane highways** with cars parked between lanes and inside the tubes. Graphene is a **flat parking plaza** with cars adsorbed on top and bottom surfaces.

### 1.4 Why Carbon? The Material Advantage

| Property | Engineering Significance |
|:---|:---|
| **Low atomic mass (12.011 u)** | Enables high theoretical wt% capacity |
| **Tunable allotropes** | 0D (fullerene) → 1D (CNT) → 2D (graphene) → 3D (activated carbon) |
| **High specific surface area (SSA)** | ACs reach **3000 m²/g**; CNTs reach **1315 m²/g** |
| **Chemical stability** | Resistant to poisoning, recyclable over thousands of cycles |
| **Low cost (especially AC)** | $1–5/kg for biomass-derived activated carbon |
| **Ease of functionalization** | Dopants (Li, K, Pd, Pt, N) tune binding energy |

---

### 1.5 Categorization of Carbon Sorbents

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Classification:**
> - **Class A — Microporous Carbons:** Activated Carbon (AC), Activated Carbon Fibers (ACF), Super-activated Carbon (SAC)
> - **Class B — Nanostructured Carbons:** Single-Walled Carbon Nanotubes (SWCNTs), Multi-Walled Carbon Nanotubes (MWCNTs)
> - **Class C — 2D/0D Carbons:** Graphene, Graphite Nanofibers (GNF), Fullerenes (C₆₀)
> - **Class D — Hybrid/Doped Carbons:** N-doped, B-doped, metal-decorated carbons

---

### 1.6 The Central Engineering Challenge

The fundamental limitation: **pure physisorption on pristine carbon** yields binding energies of only **4–6 kJ/mol** — far below the DOE requirement of **20–30 kJ/mol** for near-ambient operation. This is the **thermodynamic gap** that doping and spillover engineering aim to close.

---

### 1.7 Hydrogen Interaction Energies — Context

> [!NOTE]
> For context, the binding energy of H₂ on:
> - **Bare graphene/CNT:** ~**5 kJ/mol** (too weak)
> - **Pd-decorated graphene:** ~**15–25 kJ/mol** (acceptable)
> - **Li-doped CNT:** ~**20 kJ/mol** (within DOE range)
> - **Ti-decorated graphene:** ~**30–40 kJ/mol** (strong but often irreversible)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Primary Storage Mechanisms

#### Mechanism A — Physisorption (Physical Adsorption)

**Physics:** H₂ molecules are held by **London dispersion forces (induced dipole–induced dipole)** onto the carbon surface. The interaction is non-dissociative and fully reversible.

**Characteristics:**
- Binding energy: **4–10 kJ/mol** (pristine carbon)
- Operates at cryogenic temperatures (**77 K, LN₂**) or moderately high pressure
- Fully reversible over thousands of cycles
- Storage scales with **specific surface area (SSA)** and **pore size distribution**

**Operational Logic Flow:**
1. H₂ molecules approach carbon surface
2. Electron cloud fluctuations induce temporary dipoles
3. Induced dipoles create attractive potential well
4. Molecule binds within potential well of depth $E_b$
5. On heating or pressure drop, molecule escapes (desorbs)

---

#### Mechanism B — Chemisorption (Chemical Adsorption)

**Physics:** H–H bond dissociates (or weakens significantly), forming C–H bonds at edge/defect sites or via Kubas-type interaction on transition-metal centers.

**Characteristics:**
- Binding energy: **50–300 kJ/mol** (often too strong, requires high desorption T)
- Spillover mechanism: H atoms migrate from metal catalyst onto carbon support
- Higher wt% possible but reversibility issues

**Operational Logic:**
1. Metal nanoparticle (Pd, Pt, Ni) dissociates H₂ → 2H*
2. H atoms spill over onto carbon support
3. H atoms bind to C surface (chemisorption)
4. Desorption requires elevated T (>200°C) for high binding energies

---

#### Mechanism C — Kubas Interaction (Hybrid Physisorption)

**Physics:** H₂ binds to a transition metal center via σ-donation and π-back-donation without H–H dissociation.

**Characteristics:**
- Binding energy: **20–50 kJ/mol** (ideal for near-ambient storage)
- Requires metal decoration: Ti, Sc, V, Ni, Pd on carbon
- Reversible at moderate T (50–150°C)

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | Validity / Notes |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\theta = \dfrac{KP}{1 + KP}$ | **Langmuir isotherm** — surface coverage θ vs pressure P | dimensionless | Monolayer, identical sites |
| 2 | $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | **BET equation** — multilayer adsorption | — | Used for SSA measurement |
| 3 | $V = V_0 \exp\left[-\left(\dfrac{A}{\beta E_0}\right)^n\right]$ | **Dubinin–Astakhov (DA)** — micropore filling | — | Activated carbon best fit |
| 4 | $\text{wt\%} = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{m_{H_2} + m_{sorbent}} \times 100$ | **Gravimetric capacity** | wt% | DOE target ≥ 5.5 wt% |
| 5 | $\rho_v = \dfrac{\rho_s \cdot \text{wt\%}}{1 - \text{wt\%}}$ | **Volumetric capacity** | g H₂/L | DOE target ≥ 40 g/L |
| 6 | $E_b = \dfrac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2m_{H_2} d^2}$ | **Quantum confinement energy** (1D tube) | J or eV | SWCNT diameter $d$ |
| 7 | $d_{SWCNT} = \dfrac{a_0 \sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}}{\pi}$ | **Chirality-to-diameter** relation | nm | $a_0 = 0.246$ nm |
| 8 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | **Gibbs free energy** of adsorption | kJ/mol | Determines spontaneity |
| 9 | $V_{H_2} = \dfrac{n_{H_2}}{n_C} \cdot \dfrac{M_{H_2}}{M_C}$ | **Stoichiometric H-uptake** | dimensionless | Molecular formula basis |
| 10 | $\Delta V = V_{ads} \left[1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{t}{RC}\right)\right]$ | **Kinetics** — pressure-driven charging | volume | Time constant $RC$ |

---

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic: How Physisorption Capacity Scales

**Step 1:** The volume of H₂ adsorbed (V) is proportional to the **specific surface area (S)**:
$$V \propto S \cdot \rho_{H_2,\text{adsorbed}}$$

**Step 2:** However, not all surface is equal — pore geometry matters. Micropores (< 2 nm) **enhance** adsorption via overlapping potential wells from opposite walls:
$$E_{pore} = E_{flat} \cdot f\!\left(\dfrac{d_{pore}}{\sigma_{H_2}}\right)$$

where $\sigma_{H_2} = 0.289$ nm is the H₂ kinetic diameter.

**Step 3:** The optimal pore diameter for H₂ physisorption is **0.6–0.7 nm** (≈ 2 × kinetic diameter of H₂).

**Step 4:** Temperature strongly affects capacity — the Clausius–Clapeyron relation gives:
$$\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + \text{const}$$

Lower T → higher adsorption at given P.

---

### 2.4 Why Specific Materials Fail or Succeed

| Material | SSA (m²/g) | Pore Structure | H₂ wt% (77 K, ~1 bar) | Engineering Verdict |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Activated Carbon (AC)** | 1000–3000 | Microporous (<2 nm) | 1.5–5.0 | **Commercial benchmark** |
| **Super-Activated Carbon (SAC)** | 3000–4000 | Ultra-micropores | 4.0–7.0 | **Highest reported physisorption** |
| **SWCNT (pristine)** | 400–1300 | 1D channels | 0.5–2.0 | Limited without doping |
| **MWCNT (pristine)** | 200–500 | Concentric channels | 0.1–1.0 | Lower than AC |
| **Graphene (pristine)** | 2600 (theor.) | 2D surface | ~0.4 at 1 bar | Restacking problem |
| **Fullerene C₆₀** | ~10 | 0D cage | <0.5 | Poor for bulk storage |
| **Graphite Nanofibers** | 50–200 | Stacked platelets | 0.5–3.0 | Historical interest |
| **MOF-177 (comparison)** | 4500 | 3D framework | 7.5 (77 K) | Best physisorber overall |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application Context:**
> - **Vehicle onboard storage (700 bar Type IV tanks)** uses **activated carbon** as a heat-sink / pre-cooling adsorbent layer in advanced tank designs.
> - **Stationary storage** at H₂ refueling stations uses **AC-filled adsorbent beds** for buffer storage.
> - **Sub-sea and aerospace** favor **CNT composites** for cryo-compressed H₂.
> - **DOE Hydrogen Program** has tested >40 carbon materials; activated carbon (AX-21, Kansai Maxsorb) remains the cost leader.
