## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**Q1. Why does hydrogen combustion produce NOx emissions despite being a "zero-carbon" fuel? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Hydrogen combustion in air produces thermal NOx due to the high adiabatic flame temperature (~2400 K). At such temperatures, atmospheric N₂ reacts with O₂ through the Zeldovich mechanism: O + N₂ → NO + N. Although H₂ has no carbon and no fuel-bound nitrogen, the high temperature drives nitrogen fixation. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. State the Zeldovich mechanism for thermal NOx formation. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The Zeldovich mechanism comprises two primary reactions:
- O + N₂ → NO + N (rate-limiting, E_a ≈ 318 kJ/mol)
- N + O₂ → NO + O
An extended third reaction: N + OH → NO + H.
The first reaction is highly temperature-dependent, making thermal NOx dominant above ~1800 K. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the various **NOx formation mechanisms** in hydrogen combustion. Compare the relative contributions of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three mechanisms are:
1. **Thermal NOx** — Zeldovich mechanism, dominant at T > 1800 K.
2. **Prompt NOx** — Fenimore mechanism via HCN/H intermediate; minor in pure H₂.
3. **Fuel NOx** — Negligible for H₂ (no N in fuel).

For hydrogen, **thermal NOx dominates** (>90%) due to the high adiabatic flame temperature (~2400 K). Prompt NOx contributes <10%, and fuel NOx = 0.

Sketch: Show three pathways and indicate reaction coordinates. (7 marks)

---

**(b)** Discuss the **flame temperature reduction techniques** to control NOx in hydrogen engines. Provide quantitative justification. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Primary techniques:
1. **Lean burn operation** (λ > 1.5): dilutes O₂, reduces peak T.
2. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)**: 20–40% EGR drops T by 200–400 K.
3. **Water/steam injection**: latent heat absorption reduces peak T.
4. **Premixed combustion**: avoids stoichiometric hotspots.
5. **Two-stage (staged) combustion**: fuel-rich primary, fuel-lean secondary.

Quantitative: From the Zeldovich rate equation, reducing T from 2400 K to 1800 K cuts NOx rate by ~200× (computed in §3.3). (7 marks)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Describe the working principle of **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** for post-combustion NOx control in hydrogen systems. State the typical operating conditions and reduction efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**SCR Principle:** A reductant (NH₃ or urea) is injected into the exhaust stream over a catalyst bed (typically V₂O₅/TiO₂ or zeolite-based). NOx is reduced to N₂ and H₂O.

**Key reactions:**
- 4 NH₃ + 4 NO + O₂ → 4 N₂ + 6 H₂O
- 2 NH₃ + NO + NO₂ → 2 N₂ + 3 H₂O

**Operating window:** 300–450 °C for vanadia catalyst; 200–500 °C for zeolites.
**Efficiency:** 80–95% NOx reduction.
**For hydrogen systems:** catalyst selection must avoid sulfur poisoning and handle higher water content. (7 marks)

---

**(b)** Compare **premixed vs diffusion flame** combustion in the context of hydrogen NOx emissions. Why are catalytic combustion and micromix burners preferred for hydrogen turbines? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Premixed | Diffusion |
|---|---|---|
| Peak T | Lower (uniform) | Higher (local stoichiometric pockets) |
| NOx | Lower | Higher |
| Stability | Sensitive (flashback risk with H₂) | Stable |
| Application | Gas turbines | Industrial boilers |

**Why catalytic combustion?**
- Operates at T < 1600 K → no thermal NOx.
- Uniform temperature distribution.
- Suitable for hydrogen's wide flammability range.

**Why micromix burners?**
- Many small premixed jets → uniform mixing.
- Sub-millimeter jet spacing prevents flashback.
- Used in GE/Hydrogenics fuel-flex turbines. (7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Forgetting that hydrogen produces NO fuel-NOx** — students often over-discuss prompt/fuel NOx. Focus on thermal NOx (Zeldovich).
> 2. **Misapplying EGR percentage** — EGR dilutes O₂ AND adds heat capacity. State both effects.
> 3. **Sketching without labeling** — KTU examiners deduct marks for unlabeled axes/curves.
> 4. **Confusing Φ and λ** — Φ = (F/A)_actual/(F/A)_stoich; λ = 1/Φ. Lean burn = λ > 1 = Φ < 1.
> 5. **Ignoring flashback risk** — H₂'s high flame speed causes flashback in premixed systems; mention it for full marks.
> 6. **Writing "zero emission"** — hydrogen in air is NOT zero NOx. Always clarify.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 3 — NOx Control**

- ✅ **H₂ flame T ≈ 2400 K** → thermal NOx dominant; thermal NOx negligible below **1600 K**.
- ✅ **Zeldovich mechanism**: O + N₂ → NO + N (rate-limiting); N + O₂ → NO + O; N + OH → NO + H.
- ✅ **Hydrogen produces**: thermal NOx (major), prompt NOx (minor), **no fuel NOx**.
- ✅ **NOx ∝ exp(-E_a/RT)** → exponential sensitivity to temperature.
- ✅ **Primary control**: reduce peak flame temperature via lean burn, EGR, water injection, premixing.
- ✅ **Secondary control**: SCR (NH₃/urea, 80–95% efficient, 300–450 °C).
- ✅ **Catalytic combustion** keeps T < 1600 K → near-zero NOx.
- ✅ **Micromix burners** use many small premixed jets for uniform mixing without flashback.
- ✅ **Lean Direct Injection (LDI)** is the standard for hydrogen gas turbines.
- ✅ **For fuel cells**: cathode exhaust recirculation minimizes NO formation (low T operation).
- ✅ **Hydrogen's wide flammability range (4–75%)** demands careful flashback prevention.
- ✅ **Euro VI / EPA Tier 4**: hydrogen engines must meet these NOx limits → SCR often essential.
- ✅ **EGR 20–40%** reduces T by 200–400 K → cuts NOx by ~100–200× (use Zeldovich).
- ✅ **Staged combustion**: fuel-rich primary zone (low O₂, low NOx) + fuel-lean secondary zone.
- ✅ **Exam-ready line:** *"Thermal NOx in hydrogen systems is controlled primarily by limiting peak combustion temperature rather than modifying fuel composition."*
- ✅ **Numerical tool:** $k_1 \propto \exp(-38370/T)$ — remember this constant for derivation-based questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Transportation via Hydrogen Pipelines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hydrogen Transportation via Hydrogen Pipelines # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hydrogen Pipeline Transportation** is defined as the bulk conveyance of gaseous hydrogen (GH₂) through a network of high-pressure fixed piping infrastructure — comprising trunk lines, distribution mains, service lines, valve stations, compressor stations, metering skids, and safety interlocks — connecting production/import terminals to industrial off-takers, refueling stations, and end-use consumers. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PECHT862, Module 3), hydrogen pipelines are classified into three operational categories: | Category | Operating Pressure | Typical Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Low-Pressure Distribution** | **≤ 1 MPa (10 bar)** | Short-distance, urban last-mile delivery to refueling stations | | **Medium-Pressure Transmission** | **1 – 10 MPa (10–100 bar)** | Regional pipelines connecting production hubs to industrial clusters | | **High-Pressure Trunk Lines** | **> 10 MPa, typically 7–10 MPa (70–100 bar)** | Long-distance, cross-country bulk hydrogen transport analogous to natural gas trunk lines | > [!NOTE] > **Salient Point for KTU Examinations:** For the 2024 syllabus, students must distinguish between **dedicated (pure) hydrogen pipelines** (100% H₂) and **blended hydrogen pipelines** (H₂ mixed with natural gas, e.g., 5–20% H₂ by volume in existing CH₄ grids). Examiners frequently test this distinction. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine the municipal water supply system in your city. Water is pumped from a treatment plant (analogous to a hydrogen production plant) through large trunk mains (high-pressure pipelines), then branched into smaller distribution pipes, finally reaching your home tap at usable pressure. Hydrogen pipelines work almost identically — except the "water" is invisible gaseous hydrogen, and the pipes must withstand hydrogen's peculiar behavior of making metals **brittle** (a phenomenon called *hydrogen embrittlement*) and its tendency to **leak through microscopic pores** because H₂ is the **smallest molecule in the universe**. **Why Pipelines Matter (Big Picture):** For large-scale hydrogen economies, pipelines are projected to be the **lowest unit-cost transportation mode** when distances exceed **~1,500–2,000 km and annual volumes exceed several hundred thousand tonnes**. Below these thresholds, truck-based compressed gaseous hydrogen (CGH₂) or liquid hydrogen (LH₂) tanker transport is generally more economical. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Metrics (KEEP MEMORIZED):** > - Lower Heating Value of H₂ (LHV): **120 MJ/kg** (or **10.8 MJ/Nm³**) > - Density of H₂ at STP (0°C, 1 atm): **0.0899 kg/m³** > - Density of H₂ at 70 bar, 15°C: **≈ 5.5 kg/m³** > - Flammability range in air: **4 – 75 vol%** > - Detonation range in air: **18.3 – 59 vol%** # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Hydrogen Pipeline System A hydrogen pipeline is **not a single pipe** — it is an engineered system with the following critical subsystems: ### A. Compressor Stations (The "Heart" of the Pipeline) - H₂ has **low molecular weight (M = 2.016 g/mol)** and **low volumetric energy density**, so achieving economic mass flow rates demands **high pressures**. - Reciprocating compressors (most common for H₂) or centrifugal compressors (for very large flows) are placed at intervals of typically **80–160 km**. - Each station must compensate for **frictional pressure losses** along the preceding pipe segment. ### B. Pipe Material and Embrittlement Mitigation - **Hydrogen Embrittlement** (HE): Atomic H diffuses into the steel lattice, reducing ductility and fracture toughness. - Common material choices: - **API 5L X42–X70 carbon steel** (with internal epoxy or polymer liners) — economic for large diameters. - **Austenitic stainless steels (304L, 316L)** — for high-pressure sections and fittings. - **Fiber-Reinforced Polymer (FRP) / composite pipes** — emerging for new installations. ### C. Valves, Fittings, and Safety Devices - **Emergency Shutdown Valves (ESDV)** at section boundaries. - **Pressure Safety Valves (PSV)** and **Thermal Relief Valves**. - **Hydrogen Flame Detectors** + **LEL sensors** + **thermal imaging cameras** at stations. ## 2.2 Energy Penalty — The "Compression Tax" Transporting hydrogen by pipeline is **not free** — significant electrical energy is consumed at compressor stations. The specific energy required depends on: - The **pressure ratio** across each compressor. - The **isothermal vs adiabatic** compression efficiency. - The **number of molecules** (since H₂ has 2 atoms, polytropic index k ≈ 1.41 for diatomic gas). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use | Typical Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho}$ | Volumetric flow rate from mass flow rate | m³/s | | 2 | $\rho_{H_2}(P,T) = \dfrac{P \cdot M_{H_2}}{Z \cdot R_u \cdot T}$ | Ideal gas density (with compressibility Z ≈ 1.0006 for H₂ at moderate P) | kg/m³ | | 3 | $W_{comp} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot Z \cdot R_u \cdot T_1 \cdot M_{H_2}^{-1}}{\eta} \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}} - 1\right]$ | Compressor power per unit mass flow (adiabatic) | W | | 4 | $\dfrac{k-1}{k} = \dfrac{1.41 - 1}{1.41} = 0.291$ | Polytropic exponent factor for H₂ (diatomic) | dimensionless | | 5 | $\Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \cdot v^2}{2}$ | Darcy–Weisbach frictional pressure drop | Pa | | 6 | $v = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{A_{pipe}} = \dfrac{4\dot{V}}{\pi D^2}$ | Average fluid velocity in pipe | m/s | | 7 | $f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}$ | Friction factor via Colebrook equation (turbulent) | dimensionless | | 8 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for flow regime check | dimensionless | | 9 | $P_{max,design} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow} \cdot t}{D}$ | Hoop stress-based maximum design pressure (thin-wall) | Pa | | 10 | $\eta_{LHV,cogen} = \dfrac{E_{H_2,delivered}}{E_{LHV,H_2,injected} + W_{comp,electric}}$ | Overall energy efficiency of H₂ pipeline transport | dimensionless | | 11 | $Q_{leak} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot k_{H_2}}{\delta} \cdot \Delta P$ | Permeation leakage rate (Fick's law approximation) | kg/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Symbols Glossary:** $M_{H_2}$ = molar mass = **2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol**; $R_u$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**; $k$ = specific heat ratio = **1.41** for H₂; $D$ = inner diameter; $L$ = pipe length; $\varepsilon$ = roughness; $\eta$ = compressor efficiency (typically 0.70–0.85); $\sigma_{allow}$ = allowable hoop stress. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility Hydrogen pipeline infrastructure is **not theoretical**. Existing operational examples that KTU examiners cite: - **USA — Gulf Coast Hydrogen Network:** ~1,600 km of dedicated H₂ pipelines (mostly serving refineries and ammonia plants), operating at **34–70 bar**, in service since the 1980s. - **Europe — HyPipe Finland (2024+):** Planned 3,000 km conversion of natural gas pipelines to H₂. - **Netherlands — HyNetwork (HYNET):** Re-purposing ~1,200 km of existing gas grid for H₂ blending. - **India — National Hydrogen Mission (2023):** Targeting pipeline infrastructure under the green hydrogen mission, with **GAIL, IOCL, and HPCL** exploring blending in CGD networks. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Compressor Power for Hydrogen Pipeline **Problem Setup:** A hydrogen pipeline segment requires a compressor to boost pressure from $P_1 = 20 \text{ bar}$ at the inlet to $P_2 = 70 \text{ bar}$ at the outlet. The mass flow rate of H₂ is $\dot{m} = 5{,}000 \text{ kg/hour}$. The compressor is adiabatic with isentropic efficiency $\eta = 0.75$, inlet temperature $T_1 = 298 \text{ K}$, and the gas behaves as an ideal diatomic gas ($k = 1.41$). Determine the required shaft power input to the compressor. ### Step 1 — Convert Mass Flow to Standard Conditions We first find the volumetric flow rate at inlet conditions to evaluate the compressor inlet volume flow, which is what the compressor physically "sees": $$ \dot{V}_1 = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_1} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot Z \cdot R_u \cdot T_1}{P_1 \cdot M_{H_2}} $$ Substituting values: $$ \dot{m} = 5{,}000 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{hr}} = \dfrac{5{,}000}{3{,}600} \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} = 1.389 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} $$ $$ \dot{V}_1 = \dfrac{1.389 \times 8.314 \times 298}{20 \times 10^{5} \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}} \quad \left[\text{assuming } Z \approx 1\right] $$ This gives an inlet volumetric flow of approximately **0.855 m³/s at 20 bar** — note how this is **vastly smaller than the equivalent natural gas flow** for the same energy delivery, illustrating why hydrogen pipelines must operate at much higher pressures to be economical. ### Step 2 — Determine Pressure Ratio $$ r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{70}{20} = 3.5 $$ ### Step 3 — Calculate the Polytropic Exponent Factor $$ \dfrac{k-1}{k} = \dfrac{1.41 - 1}{1.41} = 0.291 $$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Adiabatic Compressor Power Formula For an ideal gas undergoing adiabatic (isentropic) compression, the minimum theoretical specific work (per unit mass) is: $$ w_s = \dfrac{Z \cdot R_u \cdot T_1}{M_{H_2}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\eta_{isen}} \cdot \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}} - 1 \right] $$ Plugging in: $$ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{0.291} = 3.5^{0.291} $$ Computing the exponent numerically: $$ \ln(3.5^{0.291}) = 0.291 \cdot \ln(3.5) = 0.291 \cdot 1.2528 = 0.3646 $$ $$ 3.5^{0.291} = e^{0.3646} = 1.4401 $$ Therefore: $$ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{0.291} - 1 = 1.4401 - 1 = 0.4401 $$ Now: $$ w_s = \dfrac{1 \times 8.314 \times 298}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} \times \dfrac{1}{0.75} \times 0.4401 $$ $$ w_s = 1{,}228{,}955 \, \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{kg}} \times \dfrac{0.4401}{0.75} $$ $$ w_s = 1{,}228{,}955 \times 0.5868 = 720{,}840 \, \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{kg}} \approx 721 \, \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}} $$ > **Interpretation:** Each kilogram of hydrogen must receive **~721 kJ of mechanical energy** to be compressed from 20 bar to 70 bar. Since LHV of H₂ is 120,000 kJ/kg, the compression energy is **~0.6%** of the chemical energy carried — small but non-negligible at network scale. ### Step 5 — Compute Total Shaft Power $$ W_{shaft} = \dot{m} \times w_s = 1.389 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} \times 720{,}840 \, \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{kg}} $$ $$ W_{shaft} = 1{,}001{,}247 \, \text{W} \approx 1.00 \, \text{MW} $$ ### Final Result $$ \boxed{W_{shaft} \approx 1.00 \, \text{MW} \text{ for compressing } 5{,}000 \, \text{kg/hr of H}_2 \text{ from } 20 \text{ bar to } 70 \text{ bar at } \eta = 0.75} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frictional Pressure Drop Using Darcy–Weisbach **Problem Setup:** Hydrogen flows through a steel pipe of inner diameter $D = 0.4 \text{ m}$ (16-inch nominal), length $L = 50 \text{ km}$, at mass flow $\dot{m} = 1.389 \text{ kg/s}$ and average pressure $\bar{P} = 45 \text{ bar}$ with temperature $\bar{T} = 290 \text{ K}$. Pipe roughness $\varepsilon = 0.045 \text{ mm}$. Dynamic viscosity of H₂ at 290 K is $\mu \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$. Calculate the frictional pressure drop. ### Step 1 — Calculate Density at Average Conditions $$ \bar{\rho} = \dfrac{\bar{P} \cdot M_{H_2}}{R_u \cdot \bar{T}} = \dfrac{45 \times 10^{5} \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 290} $$ $$ \bar{\rho} = \dfrac{9{,}072}{2{,}411.06} = 3.76 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} $$ ### Step 2 — Calculate Velocity $$ \dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\bar{\rho}} = \dfrac{1.389}{3.76} = 0.3694 \, \dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} $$ $$ A_{pipe} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.4^2}{4} = 0.1257 \, \text{m}^2 $$ $$ v = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{A_{pipe}} = \dfrac{0.3694}{0.1257} = 2.94 \, \dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Reynolds Number $$ Re = \dfrac{\bar{\rho} \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu} = \dfrac{3.76 \times 2.94 \times 0.4}{1.05 \times 10^{-5}} $$ $$ Re = \dfrac{4.421}{1.05 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.21 \times 10^{5} $$ Since $Re > 4{,}000$, flow is **fully turbulent**. ### Step 4 — Compute Relative Roughness $$ \dfrac{\varepsilon}{D} = \dfrac{0.045 \times 10^{-3}}{0.4} = 1.125 \times 10^{-4} $$ ### Step 5 — Apply Colebrook Equation Iteratively $$ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log_{10}\!\left[ \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} \right] $$ Compute $Re^{0.9}$: $$ Re^{0.9} = (4.21 \times 10^5)^{0.9} $$ $$ \ln(Re^{0.9}) = 0.9 \cdot \ln(4.21 \times 10^5) = 0.9 \cdot 12.951 = 11.656 $$ $$ Re^{0.9} = e^{11.656} = 1.157 \times 10^5 $$ $$ \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} = \dfrac{5.74}{1.157 \times 10^5} = 4.96 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} = \dfrac{1.125 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} = 3.04 \times 10^{-5} $$ Sum inside logarithm: $$ 3.04 \times 10^{-5} + 4.96 \times 10^{-5} = 8.00 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log_{10}(8.00 \times 10^{-5}) = -2.0 \times (-4.097) = 8.194 $$ $$ \sqrt{f} = \dfrac{1}{8.194} = 0.1220 $$ $$ f = 0.0149 $$ ### Step 6 — Calculate Frictional Pressure Drop $$ \Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\bar{\rho} \cdot v^2}{2} $$ $$ \Delta P_f = 0.0149 \times \dfrac{50{,}000}{0.4} \times \dfrac{3.76 \times 2.94^2}{2} $$ $$ \Delta P_f = 0.0149 \times 125{,}000 \times \dfrac{3.76 \times 8.6436}{2} $$ $$ \Delta P_f = 1{,}862.5 \times 16.25 = 30{,}265 \, \text{Pa} \approx 0.303 \, \text{bar} $$ ### Step 7 — Calculate as Percentage of Average Pressure $$ \dfrac{\Delta P_f}{\bar{P}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.303}{45} \times 100\% \approx 0.67\% $$ ### Final Result $$ \boxed{\Delta P_f \approx 0.303 \text{ bar over 50 km pipe segment}} $$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** A pressure drop of ~0.3 bar per 50 km is **manageable**, meaning compressor spacing of **~150–200 km** is technically feasible for large-diameter H₂ trunk lines. This is the basis for planning compressor station distances. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Hydrogen vs Natural Gas Pipeline Operation | Parameter | Natural Gas (CH₄) | Hydrogen (H₂) | Consequence | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Molecular weight | 16.04 g/mol | 2.016 g/mol | H₂ is **8× lighter** → higher velocity for same mass flow | | Density at 70 bar, 15°C | ~50 kg/m³ | ~5.5 kg/m³ | H₂ needs **~9× larger volume** for same mass | | Sound velocity | ~430 m/s | ~1,200 m/s | **Higher** Mach-number risk for H₂ | | Flammability in air | 5–15 vol% | 4–75 vol% | **Wider** flammability → stricter leak control | | Heating value per Nm³ | ~35.9 MJ | ~10.8 MJ | H₂ delivers ~30% energy per Nm³ | | Energy per kg | 50 MJ/kg (HHV) | 142 MJ/kg (HHV) | H₂ has **2.8× gravimetric** energy density | | Hydrogen embrittlement | None | Severe | Mandates material selection & QA protocols | | Compressor energy per kg | Lower | Higher (more stages) | Increased OPEX | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hydrogen Pipeline System — Functional Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["PRODUCTION FACILITY
Electrolyzer or SMR"] --> nodeB["COMPRESSOR STATION 1
Boost 20 to 70 bar"] nodeB --> nodeC["TRUNK LINE SEGMENT 1
Diameter 24 to 36 inch"] nodeC --> nodeD["INTERMEDIATE VALVE STATION
ESDV plus PSV"] nodeD --> nodeE["COMPRESSOR STATION 2
Re-pressurize to 70 bar"] nodeE --> nodeF["TRUNK LINE SEGMENT 2
Continues to offtake"] nodeF --> nodeG["METERING SKID
Flow plus purity plus pressure"] nodeG --> nodeH{"OFFTAKE TYPE"} nodeH --> nodeI["REFINERY OR AMMONIA PLANT
Industrial use"] nodeH --> nodeJ["FUELING STATION
CGH2 cascade storage"] nodeH --> nodeK["POWER PLANT
Turbine or fuel cell"] nodeG -.-> nodeL["LEAK DETECTION SYSTEM
Acoustic plus fiber optic"] nodeG -.-> nodeM["SCADA CONTROL CENTER
Remote monitoring"] ``` ## 4.2 Hydrogen Pipeline Project — Stage Gate Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["STAGE 1 FEASIBILITY
Demand assessment plus route survey"] --> stepB["STAGE 2 DESIGN
Pipe sizing plus material selection"] stepB --> stepC["STAGE 3 PERMITTING
Environmental plus safety clearances"] stepC --> stepD["STAGE 4 CONSTRUCTION
Welding plus QA plus NDT"] stepD --> stepE["STAGE 5 COMMISSIONING
Pressure test plus purge with N2"] stepE --> stepF["STAGE 6 OPERATION
Continuous monitoring plus integrity"] stepF --> stepG["STAGE 7 DECOMMISSIONING
Purge plus inerting plus salvage"] ``` ## 4.3 Component-Level Detail of a Compressor Station ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INLET["INLET SIDE"] n1A["Suction Scrubber
Removes moisture and particulates"] --> n1B["Su-side ESDV
Emergency block valve"] end n1B --> n1C["RECIPROCATING COMPRESSOR
3 stage adiabatic 70 to 100 bar"] n1C --> n1D["Inter-stage Cooler 1
Reduces temperature to 40 C"] n1D --> n1E["Inter-stage Cooler 2"] n1E --> n1F["Inter-stage Cooler 3"] subgraph OUTLET["OUTLET SIDE"] n1F --> n1G["Discharge Scrubber
Condensate removal"] n1G --> n1H["Dis-side ESDV
Auto-actuated"] n1H --> n1I["Pressure Safety Valve
Set at 110 percent MAOP"] n1I --> n1J["FLOW METER
Coriolis plus ultrasonic"] n1J --> n1K["Delivery to Mainline"] end n1L["H2 LEL Detector Bank
4 sensors redundant"] -.monitors.-> n1C n1M["Flame Detector IR3
Triple path optical"] -.monitors.-> n1D ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip for Examiners:** When asked to draw or describe a hydrogen pipeline system in the KTU exam, always show at minimum: **(i) production source, (ii) compressor station, (iii) trunk line with ESDVs at section boundaries, (iv) offtake/metering, and (v) leak detection instrumentation.** Missing the safety instrumentation layer is a common cause of lost marks. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks = 6 marks) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]** *3 marks* **Differentiate between dedicated hydrogen pipelines and blended hydrogen (H₂–NG blend) pipelines. State two advantages and two disadvantages of each.** **Model Answer:** A **dedicated hydrogen pipeline** carries 100% pure H₂ as the transported medium, requiring new infrastructure or complete conversion of an existing natural gas line. Advantages: (1) full hydrogen purity for fuel-cell or ammonia applications, no quality compromise; (2) no methane–hydrogen cross-contamination concerns, simplifying end-use. Disadvantages: (1) very high capital expenditure for new pipelines; (2) hydrogen embrittlement risk on all components from day one. A **blended hydrogen pipeline** transports a mixture (typically 5–20 vol% H₂ in CH₄) through existing natural gas infrastructure. Advantages: (1) leverages existing CAPEX of natural gas grid; (2) low entry barrier for hydrogen market ramp-up. Disadvantages: (1) end-users receive lower-purity H₂ requiring separation; (2) blending accelerates leak rates and may impact downstream appliances. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** *3 marks* **Why is hydrogen transported at high pressures (typically 70–100 bar) in pipelines? Justify using two engineering reasons.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Low Volumetric Energy Density:** Hydrogen has an LHV of only **10.8 MJ/Nm³**, roughly one-third that of natural gas (~35.9 MJ/Nm³). Compressing H₂ to 70 bar raises its density to **~5.5 kg/m³**, increasing energy density by **~70×** relative to atmospheric conditions and making long-distance transport economically viable. 2. **Frictional Losses Mitigation:** Higher pressure allows smaller pipe diameters for the same energy throughput, reducing capital cost per km and lowering frictional pressure drop ($\Delta P_f \propto \rho v^2$), which in turn extends the permissible distance between compressor stations and reduces OPEX. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 marks each) ### Question A — Compressor Power & Pipeline Sizing **(*14 marks*)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A]** **A 200 km hydrogen pipeline is to be designed with a single intermediate compressor station located at the midpoint. The pipeline mass flow rate is 8,000 kg/hr at an average temperature of 290 K. Suction pressure at compressor inlet is 30 bar; discharge pressure is 80 bar. The compressor is three-stage reciprocating with overall isentropic efficiency $\eta = 0.78$. Assume $k = 1.41$, $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol, $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).** #### Part (a) — Compressor Power Calculation *(7 marks)* **Step 1 — Mass flow conversion:** $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{8{,}000}{3{,}600} = 2.222 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} $$ **Step 2 — Pressure ratio and exponent:** $$ r_p = \dfrac{80}{30} = 2.667 $$ $$ \dfrac{k-1}{k} = 0.291 $$ **Step 3 — Specific compression work:** $$ w_s = \dfrac{R_u \cdot T_1 \cdot Z}{M_{H_2} \cdot \eta} \left[r_p^{0.291} - 1\right] $$ $$ 2.667^{0.291}: \quad \ln = 0.291 \times \ln(2.667) = 0.291 \times 0.9808 = 0.2854, \quad e^{0.2854} = 1.3303 $$ $$ w_s = \dfrac{8.314 \times 290 \times 1}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.78} \times (1.3303 - 1) $$ $$ w_s = \dfrac{2{,}411.06}{1.5725 \times 10^{-3}} \times 0.3303 $$ $$ w_s = 1{,}533{,}440 \times 0.3303 = 506{,}514 \, \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{kg}} \approx 506.5 \, \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}} $$ **Step 4 — Total shaft power:** $$ W_{shaft} = \dot{m} \cdot w_s = 2.222 \times 506{,}514 = 1{,}125{,}475 \, \text{W} \approx 1.13 \, \text{MW} $$ > **Final Answer (a):** $W_{shaft} \approx 1.13 \text{ MW}$ #### Part (b) — Pipe Sizing & Pressure Drop *(7 marks)* Assume allowable fluid velocity $v \leq 12 \text{ m/s}$ (to keep Mach number $Ma < 0.3$ since H₂ sonic velocity ≈ 1,200 m/s) and design that the midpoint compressor exactly compensates the line's pressure drop. **Step 1 — Minimum diameter from velocity constraint:** $$ \dot{V}_{inlet} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot R_u \cdot T}{P_1 \cdot M_{H_2}} = \dfrac{2.222 \times 8.314 \times 290}{30 \times 10^5 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{5{,}359}{6{,}048} = 0.886 \, \dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} $$ $$ A_{min} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{inlet}}{v_{max}} = \dfrac{0.886}{12} = 0.0738 \, \text{m}^2 $$ $$ D_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_{min}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.0738}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.0940} = 0.307 \, \text{m} $$ Choose **standard DN 300 mm pipe** (12-inch nominal). **Step 2 — Actual velocity with chosen D:** $$ v = \dfrac{4 \dot{V}}{\pi D^2} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.886}{\pi \times 0.30^2} = \dfrac{3.544}{0.2827} = 12.53 \, \text{m/s} $$ (Acceptable; $Ma = 12.53/1200 = 0.0104 \ll 0.3$.) **Step 3 — Pressure drop over 100 km (one segment):** For $\Delta P$ over the half-line ($L = 100{,}000 \text{ m}$), use Darcy–Weisbach with assumed friction factor $f \approx 0.015$ (typical for turbulent H₂ flow in commercial steel). $$ \Delta P_f = 0.015 \times \dfrac{100{,}000}{0.30} \times \dfrac{3.76 \times 12.53^2}{2} $$ (Using density at average ~30 bar = 3.76 kg/m³ as derived in §3.2) $$ \Delta P_f = 0.015 \times 333{,}333 \times \dfrac{3.76 \times 157.0}{2} $$ $$ \Delta P_f = 5{,}000 \times 295.3 = 1{,}476{,}500 \, \text{Pa} \approx 14.77 \, \text{bar} $$ > **Final Answer (b):** Diameter $D = 300 \text{ mm}$, $\Delta P_f \approx 14.8 \text{ bar}$ per 100 km segment — well within the design range, confirming single intermediate compressor is feasible. --- ### Question B — Blended H₂–NG Pipelines & Embrittlement **(*14 marks*)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B]** **(a) Discuss the technical challenges of converting an existing natural gas pipeline to hydrogen service. Cover the topics of material compatibility, embrittlement, leak rates, compressor selection, and end-use impact. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** (i) **Material Compatibility & Embrittlement:** Existing natural gas pipelines are predominantly API 5L carbon steel. Direct exposure to high-pressure H₂ induces **hydrogen embrittlement (HE)** — atomic H diffuses into the steel lattice, drastically reducing fracture toughness and ductility. Mitigation options include internal polymer liners (HDPE), derating operating pressure by ~30%, or replacing critical fittings (valves, gaskets) with austenitic stainless steel. (ii) **Leak Rates:** The **permeability of H₂ through steel and gaskets is ~3–5× higher than methane** because H₂ molecules are smaller and more energetic. Existing flange designs, O-rings, and threaded joints may need upgrading to metal-to-metal seals. (iii) **Compressor Selection:** Natural gas turbines/compressors are unsuitable. Reciprocating compressors with H₂-compatible seals (e.g., PTFE labyrinth) and modified lubrication systems are required. Compression energy rises ~10–15% for H₂ at the same pressure ratio. (iv) **End-Use Impact:** Industrial burners calibrated for CH₄ will experience flame instability above ~20% H₂ blending due to higher flame speed (2.6 m/s for H₂ vs 0.4 m/s for CH₄). Domestic appliances (cooktops) tolerate up to ~10% H₂ without modification. (v) **Metering:** Thermal-mass flow meters (commonly used for natural gas) read inaccurately for H₂ blends; replacement with **Coriolis or ultrasonic meters** is recommended. **(b) A natural gas pipeline of length 80 km and diameter DN 600 mm (24-inch) currently operates at 40 bar, carrying methane at $7.5 \times 10^5 \text{ kg/day}$. If 10% by volume of the methane is replaced with hydrogen (assume no pressure change, isothermal at 290 K), calculate the new mass flow rate, the percentage change in energy delivered per day, and the percentage change in Reynolds number. Use $M_{CH_4} = 16.04$ g/mol, $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol, LHV(CH₄) = 50 MJ/kg, LHV(H₂) = 120 MJ/kg. (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Original methane mass flow:** $$ \dot{m}_{CH_4,orig} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 10^5}{86{,}400} = 8.681 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} $$ **Step 2 — Original volumetric flow rate at STP:** Using ideal gas: 1 kmol of CH₄ at STP = 22.414 m³. Molar flow CH₄: $$ \dot{n}_{CH_4} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{CH_4}}{M_{CH_4}} = \dfrac{8.681}{0.01604} = 541.2 \, \dfrac{\text{kmol}}{\text{s}} $$ Volumetric flow at STP: $$ \dot{V}_{STP,CH_4} = 541.2 \times 22.414 = 12{,}129 \, \dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} $$ **Step 3 — New conditions with 10 vol% H₂ replacement:** For every 100 mol of original gas, there are now 90 mol CH₄ + 10 mol H₂. The volume flow (which is bounded by the pipeline geometry and pressure drop) effectively **stays the same in kmol/s**, but composition shifts. Molar flow new CH₄: $$ \dot{n}_{CH_4,new} = 0.9 \times 541.2 = 487.1 \, \dfrac{\text{kmol}}{\text{s}} $$ Molar flow new H₂: $$ \dot{n}_{H_2,new} = 0.1 \times 541.2 = 54.12 \, \dfrac{\text{kmol}}{\text{s}} $$ **Step 4 — New mass flow rates and total:** $$ \dot{m}_{CH_4,new} = 487.1 \times 0.01604 = 7.813 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{H_2,new} = 54.12 \times 0.002016 = 0.1091 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{total,new} = 7.813 + 0.1091 = 7.922 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} $$ **Step 5 — Original energy delivery per day:** $$ E_{orig} = 7.5 \times 10^5 \times 50 = 3.75 \times 10^7 \, \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{day}} $$ **Step 6 — New energy delivery per day:** $$ \dot{m}_{CH_4,new,day} = 7.813 \times 86{,}400 = 6.75 \times 10^5 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{day}} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{H_2,new,day} = 0.1091 \times 86{,}400 = 9{,}426 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{day}} $$ $$ E_{new} = (6.75 \times 10^5 \times 50) + (9{,}426 \times 120) = 3.375 \times 10^7 + 1.131 \times 10^6 = 3.488 \times 10^7 \, \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{day}} $$ **Step 7 — Percentage change in energy delivered:** $$ \Delta E\% = \dfrac{E_{new} - E_{orig}}{E_{orig}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{3.488 - 3.75}{3.75} \times 100\% = -7.0\% $$ **Step 8 — Percentage change in Reynolds number:** Since pipe $D$, velocity $v$ (volume flow bounded), and viscosity $\mu$ change negligibly (viscosity of H₂ and CH₄ are similar at ~1.1 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s), but **density decreases** because H₂ is 8× lighter than CH₄. Average molecular weight of mix (90% CH₄, 10% H₂): $$ M_{mix} = 0.9 \times 16.04 + 0.1 \times 2.016 = 14.436 + 0.2016 = 14.638 \, \dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{mol}} $$ Original molecular weight = 16.04 g/mol. Density ratio: $$ \dfrac{\rho_{mix}}{\rho_{orig}} = \dfrac{14.638}{16.04} = 0.9126 $$ Therefore: $$ \Delta Re\% = (0.9126 - 1) \times 100\% = -8.7\% $$ > **Final Answers (b):** > - $\dot{m}_{total,new} = 7.922 \text{ kg/s}$ > - $\Delta E\% = -7.0\%$ (energy delivered **decreases**) > - $\Delta Re\% = -8.7\%$ (Reynolds number **decreases**) --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Hydrogen Pipeline Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the molar mass unit conversion** — $M_{H_2}$ must be in **kg/mol**, not g/mol, when using SI units consistently. This single error cascades into ~30% deviation in the final answer and is the most common valuation deduction. > 2. **Using $k = 1.4$ instead of $1.41$ for hydrogen** — Examiners check this. Always state $k = 1.41$ for diatomic gases. > 3. **Mixing up STP and NTP** — KTU convention is **STP at 273.15 K and 1.01325 bar**, but pipeline problems usually require you to compute density at *actual* P and T — do not blindly use STP density. > 4. **Ignoring compressor isentropic efficiency** — A common shortcut students take is to assume 100% efficiency. Examiners explicitly check $\eta$ in the formula; missing it loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Forgetting unit consistency for $R_u$** — Use $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) with molar mass in kg/mol, OR $R_u = 8{,}314$ J/(kmol·K) with molar mass in kg/kmol. Never mix. > 6. **In blended-pipeline questions, failing to state assumptions** (isothermal, ideal gas, no pressure change). Examiners deduct for unstated assumptions in 14-mark problems. > 7. **Skipping the safety/instrumentation layer** in any pipeline schematic — KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests **safety culture awareness** under NEP 2020; always annotate ESDV, LEL sensors, and PSV locations. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Hydrogen pipelines** are categorized into **low- (<1 MPa), medium- (1–10 MPa), and high-pressure (>10 MPa)** systems; trunk lines typically operate at **70–100 bar**. - Three transport modes exist for H₂: **pipelines, compressed gas trailers (CGH₂), and liquid H₂ (LH₂) tankers** — pipelines are most economical **above ~1,500 km and high annual tonnage**. - **Dedicated vs blended** pipelines are a key KTU 2024 distinction; blending leverages existing CH₄ infrastructure but limits end-use purity. - **Key formulas** (must be memorized): $w_s = \dfrac{R_u T_1}{M \eta}[r_p^{(k-1)/k} - 1]$, Darcy–Weisbach $\Delta P_f = f (L/D)(\rho v^2/2)$, Colebrook for friction factor. - **Hydrogen embrittlement** is the dominant material challenge; mitigated by austenitic stainless steel, polymer liners, or pressure derating. - **Compressor power** scales linearly with mass flow and roughly linearly with $\ln(P_2/P_1)$ — at 70 bar operation, compression energy is **~0.5–1%** of H₂'s LHV. - **Sound velocity of H₂ (~1,200 m/s)** demands velocity limits to avoid choking; **$Ma < 0.3$** is the design rule. - **Leak rate** through seals/gaskets is 3–5× higher than methane due to smaller molecular size. - **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg; HHV = 142 MJ/kg; density at STP = 0.0899 kg/m³** — these are exam constants. - **Real-world examples** to cite: Gulf Coast (USA), HyNetwork (Netherlands), HyPipe (Finland), India's National Hydrogen Mission pipelines. - Always include **safety layer** (ESDV, PSV, LEL sensors, SCADA) in any pipeline schematic for full KTU marks. - **Energy penalty** for blending 10% H₂ into CH₄: ~7% reduction in delivered energy at constant volume flow — a frequently tested numerical. - **Friction factor** for H₂ in commercial steel pipe typically lies in the range **0.012–0.018** at typical operating Reynolds numbers.

topics.title: Long Distance Hydrogen Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Long Distance Hydrogen Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long Distance Hydrogen Transmission** is the engineered transport of bulk hydrogen (typically over distances $\geq 100$ km) from centralized production sites (electrolysers, SMR + CCS, or reforming plants) to distributed end-use points such as industrial clusters, refuelling stations, power plants, and export terminals. The transmission is realized through three principal physical pathways: **(i) gaseous hydrogen (GH₂) pipelines**, **(ii) cryogenic liquid hydrogen (LH₂) tankers and ships**, and **(iii) chemical hydrogen carriers such as ammonia (NH₃), methanol (CH₃OH), and Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHCs).**

The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes the topic under **Transportation of Hydrogen** with explicit focus on energy losses, infrastructure compatibility, and thermodynamic limits.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of long-distance hydrogen transmission like **three different ways to ship drinking water from a mountain reservoir to a city far away**:

| Water Analogy | Hydrogen Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Pumping water through steel pipes under pressure | Compressed H₂ gas in pipelines |
| Refrigerating water into ice blocks and trucking them | Cryogenic liquid H₂ ($-252.87^{\circ}$C) in insulated tankers |
| Binding water chemically into a hydrate for shipping | Locking H₂ into NH₃ or LOHC molecules |

Each method trades off **energy spent during transport** against **convenience, cost, and end-use readiness** — exactly mirroring the engineering compromise in hydrogen logistics.

### 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield at KTU

> [!NOTE]
> Kerala's strategic push towards green hydrogen hubs (Vizhinjam port, Kochi LNG-adjacent facilities) makes transmission a Board-favourite topic. Examiners test **mode-selection logic**, **compression/liquefaction energy arithmetic**, and **pressure-drop calculations** — all directly drawn from this module.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Memorize)

| Property | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Molar mass, $M_{H_2}$ | **2.016 g/mol** | g/mol |
| Density at STP, $\rho_{H_2}$ | **0.0899 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| Boiling point at 1 atm | **20.27 K (−252.87 °C)** | K |
| Critical temperature, $T_c$ | **33.15 K** | K |
| Critical pressure, $P_c$ | **13.0 bar** | bar |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **33.33 kWh/kg** (≈ 120 MJ/kg) | kWh/kg |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) | **39.4 kWh/kg** (≈ 142 MJ/kg) | kWh/kg |
| Specific heat ratio, $\gamma$ | **1.405** | – |
| Volumetric energy density (STP) | **≈ 3.0 kWh/m³** (vs CH₄: 9.97) | kWh/m³ |

### 1.5 The Core Engineering Challenge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hydrogen's volumetric energy density at STP is roughly **3× lower than natural gas** and **~3,000× lower than gasoline**. Therefore, *transmitting hydrogen without compressing, liquefying, or chemically binding it is energetically absurd*. Every transmission mode exists to overcome this volumetric penalty — and each mode "pays" an energy tax.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Transmission Pathways — Structured Logic

#### A. Gaseous Hydrogen (GH₂) Pipeline Transmission
- Hydrogen is compressed (typically to **20–100 bar** for distribution, **70–100 bar** for transmission) and pushed through steel/composite pipelines.
- **Energy penalty**: Compression work scales with the log of pressure ratio.
- **Advantages**: Mature technology; lowest transport energy per kg when pipeline already exists.
- **Disadvantages**: Hydrogen embrittlement in steels; leakage rate of H₂ through seals is ~3× that of CH₄ due to smaller molecular diameter; high CAPEX for new pipelines.

#### B. Cryogenic Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂) Transport
- H₂ is liquefied at **20.27 K** using multi-stage Joule–Thomson expansion (Linde process) or Claude cycle.
- Transported in super-insulated cryogenic tankers (truck, rail, ship).
- **Energy penalty**: Liquefaction consumes **~30–40 % of LHV** (≈ 10–13 kWh per kg of H₂).
- **Boil-off losses**: 0.1–0.3 % per day depending on tank insulation quality.

#### C. Chemical Hydrogen Carriers
- **Ammonia (NH₃)**: H₂ reacts with N₂ via Haber–Bosch → NH₃. Easily liquefied at −33 °C / 9 bar. Cracked back to H₂ at the destination.
- **LOHC (e.g., toluene ⇌ methylcyclohexane)**: Reversible hydrogenation/dehydrogenation.
- **Methanol, formic acid**: Liquid at ambient conditions; reformed at the point of use.

### 2.2 Boundary Conditions and Design Constraints

| Parameter | Typical Range | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Pipeline pressure | 20–100 bar | Sets compressor duty |
| Pipeline diameter | 0.3–1.0 m | Sets volumetric throughput |
| Reynolds number, Re | $10^6$–$10^7$ | Turbulent regime; friction factor needed |
| Pipeline length | 100–3000 km | Pressure drop accumulates |
| Compression stages | 2–4 (with intercooling) | Each stage $P_{ratio} \approx 3$ |
| LH₂ boil-off rate | 0.1–0.3 %/day | Dictates voyage distance |
| NH₃ liquefaction | −33 °C, 9 bar | Reuses LPG/CO₂/LNG infrastructure |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Critical)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are directly testable in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P = f \,\dfrac{L}{D} \,\dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ | Darcy–Weisbach; $f$=friction factor, $L$=length, $D$=diameter, $\rho$=density, $v$=velocity | Pressure drop in pipeline |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number; $\mu$=dynamic viscosity | Flow regime check |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$ | Colebrook–White equation | Friction factor for turbulent H₂ flow |
| 4 | $W_{iso} = nRT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Isothermal compression work | Lower-bound energy for compression |
| 5 | $W_{adi} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\, nRT_1\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | Adiabatic (reversible) compression work | Realistic compression energy |
| 6 | $W_{polytropic} = \dfrac{n}{\eta}\cdot \dfrac{\gamma RT_1}{\gamma - 1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta}} - 1\right]$ | Polytropic with efficiency $\eta$ | Actual compressor work |
| 7 | $P_{stage} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/N}$ | $N$ = number of stages | Optimum stage pressure ratio |
| 8 | $W_{liq,min} = T_1\,(S_1 - S_2) - (H_1 - H_2)$ | Minimum theoretical liquefaction work | Linde/Carnot bound |
| 9 | $BOL = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{m\,\Delta h_{vap}}$ | Boil-off loss; $\dot{Q}$=heat ingress, $\Delta h_{vap}$=latent heat | LH₂ tanker storage |
| 10 | $H_2 \text{ density at } P,T:\ \rho = \dfrac{PM}{ZRT}$ | $Z$=compressibility factor | Real-gas density |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Repurposing natural gas pipelines** (e.g., the EU's "blending-ready" H₂-ready networks) requires re-rating for hydrogen's lower density, higher leak rate, and embrittlement susceptibility.
- **Export-oriented green ammonia chains** (Australia → Japan, Saudi Arabia → Europe) currently dominate international hydrogen trade.
- **On-shore wind-to-hydrogen-to-pipeline** projects (e.g., HyDeploy in the UK) inject 20 vol% H₂ directly into existing gas grids.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Isothermal Compression Energy for Hydrogen

**Statement**: Show that for an ideal gas compressed isothermally from $P_1$ to $P_2$, the minimum shaft work per mole is $W = nRT \ln(P_2/P_1)$.

**Step 1 — Start from the first law for a closed system in a reversible isothermal process:**

$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W$$

For an ideal gas, $dU = 0$ because temperature is constant (internal energy depends only on $T$ for ideal gas).

**Step 2 — Therefore:**

$$\delta Q = \delta W$$

**Step 3 — Substitute reversible work expression $\delta W_{rev} = P\,dV$:**

$$W_{rev} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV$$

**Step 4 — Replace $P$ using ideal gas law $PV = nRT$, hence $P = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$:**

$$W_{rev} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{nRT}{V}\,dV = nRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{dV}{V}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$W_{iso} = nRT\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Use Boyle's law (constant T): $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2 \Rightarrow V_2/V_1 = P_1/P_2$. Substituting:**

$$W_{iso} = nRT\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right) = -\,nRT\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

For compression $P_2 > P_1$, the magnitude of work **input** to the gas is:

$$\boxed{\vert W_{iso}\vert = nRT\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-favourite)**:
Compress 1 kg of H₂ from $P_1 = 1$ bar to $P_2 = 70$ bar at $T = 298$ K.

Number of moles: $n = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{1\,\text{kg}}{0.002016\,\text{kg/mol}} = 496.03$ mol.

$$W = 496.03 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \ln(70) = 496.03 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times 4.248$$

$$W \approx 5.225 \times 10^{6}\,\text{J} = 5.23\,\text{MJ} \approx 1.45\,\text{kWh per kg H}_2$$

This is ≈ **4.4 % of LHV** (33.33 kWh/kg) — confirming that **isothermal compression is the theoretical lower bound**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Adiabatic (Reversible) Compression Energy for Hydrogen

**Statement**: Derive the reversible adiabatic work for compressing an ideal gas from $(P_1, T_1)$ to $(P_2, T_2)$.

**Step 1 — Reversible work expressed in terms of P and V:**

$$W_{rev} = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} V\,dP$$

Because adiabatic reversible: $\delta Q = 0 \Rightarrow dU = -\delta W$. For ideal gas, $dU = nC_v\,dT$.

**Step 2 — Substituting $V = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$:**

$$W = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{nRT}{P}\,dP$$

But for an adiabatic process $TV^{\gamma-1} = $ const, which combined with $PV = nRT$ gives $T\,P^{(1-\gamma)/\gamma} = $ const, hence:

$$T = T_1\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the integral:**

$$W = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{nR}{P}\cdot T_1\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\,dP = nRT_1\!\left(\dfrac{1}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\!\int_{P_1}^{P_2} P^{-1/\gamma}\,dP$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral $\int P^{-1/\gamma} dP = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\, P^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$:**

$$W = nRT_1\cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}\cdot\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$$

**Step 5 — Final expression (work input to the gas):**

$$\boxed{W_{adi} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\, nRT_1\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]}$$

For hydrogen $\gamma = 1.405$, so $\gamma/(\gamma-1) = 3.41$.

**Numerical Example**:
Same conditions: $n = 496.03$ mol, $T_1 = 298$ K, $P_2/P_1 = 70$.

$$\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{0.405}{1.405}} = 70^{0.2883} = 4.066$$

$$W_{adi} = 3.41 \times 496.03 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times (4.066 - 1)$$

$$W_{adi} \approx 3.41 \times 1.229\times 10^{6} \times 3.066 \approx 12.86\,\text{MJ} \approx 3.57\,\text{kWh/kg H}_2$$

This is ≈ **10.7 % of LHV** — confirming that **real compressors (with intercooling, $\eta \approx 0.7$) approach the adiabatic limit, but still consume ~10 % of the energy content**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Drop in a Long-Distance Hydrogen Pipeline

**Statement**: Derive the pressure drop for turbulent compressible flow in a long pipeline (Weymouth-style formula).

**Step 1 — Darcy–Weisbach for incompressible flow:**

$$\Delta P = f\,\dfrac{L}{D}\,\dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Express velocity in terms of mass flow $\dot{m}$ and density $\rho$:**

$$v = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho A} = \dfrac{4\dot{m}}{\pi \rho D^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\rho = PM/(ZRT)$ (ideal/reduced gas law):**

$$v = \dfrac{4\dot{m}\,ZRT}{\pi D^{2}\,PM}$$

**Step 4 — Define mass flux $G = \dot{m}/A$ and combine with $\Delta P$:**

After substitution and integrating over a long pipeline where pressure varies, the **Weymouth equation** for gas pipelines emerges:

$$\boxed{Q = \dfrac{C\,T_0}{P_0}\!\left(\dfrac{(P_1^{2} - P_2^{2})\,D^{5}}{L\,Z\,T\,f\,G}\right)^{\!1/2}}$$

where $Q$ = volumetric flow rate, $C$ = Weymouth constant (≈ 3.74 for SI), and $G$ = gas specific gravity (= 0.0696 for H₂ w.r.t. air).

**Step 5 — Numerical Illustration**:
For $D = 0.5$ m, $L = 100$ km, $P_1 = 70$ bar, $P_2 = 65$ bar, $f = 0.02$, $T = 288$ K, $Z \approx 1.05$:

Mass flow $\dot{m}$ can be back-calculated, yielding flow rates of **~5–10 kg H₂/s** for large transmission lines. This represents **~180–360 tonnes/day**, equivalent to powering ~30,000–60,000 fuel-cell vehicles.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Minimum Theoretical Liquefaction Work

**Statement**: Show that the minimum work to liquefy a gas at ambient conditions equals the decrease in its Gibbs free energy.

**Step 1 — For a reversible isothermal process at the ambient temperature $T_0$, the minimum work equals the change in Gibbs free energy:**

$$W_{min} = T_0\,\Delta S - \Delta H = \Delta G$$

**Step 2 — For ideal gas → liquid transition at $T_0 = 298$ K, the entropy of the gas side is much higher than the liquid side:**

$$S_1 - S_2 = \int_{T_2}^{T_0} \dfrac{C_p\,dT}{T} + \Delta S_{vap}(T_2) + \int_{T_0}^{T_{vap}} \cdots$$

**Step 3 — Using tabulated values for H₂**:
- $S_{gas}^{\circ}(298\,\text{K}) = 130.68$ J/(mol·K)
- $S_{liq}^{\circ}(20.27\,\text{K}) = 10.5$ J/(mol·K) (after integration of $C_p$ and accounting for vaporization)
- $\Delta H$ from 298 K → liquid at 20.27 K ≈ −905 J/mol (net energy released)

**Step 4 — Compute minimum work:**

$$W_{min} = 298 \times (130.68 - 10.5) - (-905)$$

$$W_{min} = 298 \times 120.18 + 905 = 35,813 + 905 \approx 36,720\,\text{J/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to per-kg basis:**

$$W_{min} = \dfrac{36,720}{0.002016} \approx 18.21\,\text{MJ/kg} \approx 5.06\,\text{kWh/kg H}_2$$

This is **~15.2 % of LHV** — the absolute thermodynamic minimum. Real Linde/Claude processes consume **10–13 kWh/kg** (30–40 % of LHV), i.e., ~2–2.5× the theoretical minimum.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Transmission Modes

| Criterion | GH₂ Pipeline | LH₂ Cryogenic | NH₃ Carrier | LOHC Carrier |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy loss (% LHV) | 4–12 (compression) | 30–40 (liquefaction) | 25–35 (synthesis + cracking) | 30–40 (binding + release) |
| Capital cost | High (pipeline) | Medium (tankers) | High (Haber–Bosch) | Medium |
| Energy density | Low (gas) | 71 kg/m³ (liquid) | 121 kg H₂/m³ | ~50–60 kg H₂/m³ |
| Transport mode | Pipeline | Ship/Truck | Ship/Truck/Pipeline | Ship/Truck |
| Distance suitability | 100–3000 km | > 1000 km (sea) | > 1000 km | > 1000 km |
| Reboiler/Cracker at end | None | Regasification | NH₃ cracker | Dehydrogenation reactor |
| Ready for KTU board? | ✅ Common | ✅ Common | ✅ Increasing | ⚠ Advanced |

### 4.2 Pipeline Transmission Functional Flow (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Inlet Compressor Stage 1] --> P2[Intercooler 1]
    P2 --> P3[Compressor Stage 2]
    P3 --> P4[Intercooler 2]
    P4 --> P5[Compressor Stage N]
    P5 --> P6[Pipeline Inlet P1]
    P6 --> P7[Long Pipeline L kilometres]
    P7 --> P8[Pipeline Outlet P2]
    P8 --> P9[Pressure Regulator]
    P9 --> P10[Distribution Network]
    P10 --> P11[End User Customer]

    P1 -.consumes.-> EN1[Electrical Energy W comp]
    P3 -.consumes.-> EN1
    P5 -.consumes.-> EN1
    P7 -.loses.-> DP[Delta P friction loss]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each compressor stage dissipates heat that **must** be removed by intercoolers; otherwise the temperature rise degrades volumetric efficiency and can elevate pipeline material embrittlement risk. This is why all multi-stage H₂ compressors in KTU problems have **between 2 and 4 stages**.

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Transmission Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Distance more than 500 km?}
    Q1 -- No --> M1[Use compressed GH2 pipeline]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{End use requires pure H2?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Annual volume more than 100 kt yr?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> M2[New GH2 pipeline or LH2 shipping]
    Q3 -- No --> M3[LH2 cryogenic truck or rail]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Can carrier be reformed on site?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> M4[NH3 or methanol carrier]
    Q4 -- No --> M5[LOHC carrier]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT862]**
*List any three modes of long-distance hydrogen transmission and state the principal energy penalty associated with each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — one per mode):**

1. **Gaseous hydrogen (GH₂) pipeline transmission** → Energy penalty is the **compression work** required to overcome pipeline pressure drop; typically 4–12 % of LHV depending on pressure ratio and distance.
2. **Liquid hydrogen (LH₂) transport** → Energy penalty is the **liquefaction energy** consumed by the Linde/Claude cycle to cool H₂ to 20.27 K; practically 30–40 % of LHV (≈ 10–13 kWh/kg).
3. **Chemical hydrogen carriers (NH₃ / LOHC)** → Energy penalty is the **forward synthesis (e.g., Haber–Bosch) plus the reverse cracking/dehydrogenation** energy; typically 25–40 % of LHV.

*(Full credit: 1 mark each — naming the mode; 0.5 mark each — correctly stating the energy penalty.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECHT862]**
*Why is hydrogen's volumetric energy density so low at STP, and what is the engineering consequence for long-distance transmission?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

At STP, H₂ exists as a low-molecular-weight diatomic gas with density **ρ = 0.0899 kg/m³** and volumetric energy density ≈ **3.0 kWh/m³**, which is roughly **three times lower than methane (9.97 kWh/m³)** and **three thousand times lower than liquid gasoline (≈ 9,000 kWh/m³)**. The engineering consequence is that **raw gaseous H₂ is impractical to transmit at low pressure**; it must be compressed, liquefied, or chemically bound (NH₃, LOHC) before long-distance transport becomes energetically viable. (1 mark for quantitative statement of density; 1 mark for comparative claim; 1 mark for engineering consequence.)

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compressed GH₂ Pipeline Transmission**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PECHT862]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block schematic, describe the **functional stages** of a long-distance gaseous hydrogen pipeline system. Explain why multi-stage compression with intercooling is preferred over single-stage compression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A long-distance hydrogen pipeline has the following data:
- Diameter, $D = 0.5$ m; Length, $L = 200$ km; Inlet pressure, $P_1 = 70$ bar
- Outlet pressure, $P_2 = 50$ bar; Temperature, $T = 288$ K
- Mass flow rate, $\dot{m} = 2$ kg/s; Friction factor, $f = 0.018$
- $\gamma = 1.405$; $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol

Calculate the **(i)** velocity at pipeline inlet, **(ii)** the energy lost to friction per kg of H₂ transmitted. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Block schematic — refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes for the functional flow diagram. The principal stages are:

1. **Inlet compressor train (multi-stage, with intercoolers)** — to raise H₂ from atmospheric storage pressure (~20 bar) to transmission pressure (70–100 bar). Multi-stage compression is preferred because:
   - Each stage handles a moderate pressure ratio (typically ≤ 4), avoiding excessive discharge temperature that would damage compressor blades.
   - Intercooling between stages returns gas close to inlet temperature, **reducing the volumetric work input** compared to a single adiabatic compression.
   - The optimum stage pressure ratio for minimum work is $r = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$, where $N$ is the number of stages.

2. **Long transmission pipeline** — designed for turbulent flow, with smooth bore or composite liner to mitigate hydrogen embrittlement.

3. **Pressure regulator and let-down station** — reduces pressure to distribution level.

4. **Distribution network** — delivers to end users.

**(b) Solution:**

**(i) Velocity at pipeline inlet:**
Pipeline area:
$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.5)^{2}}{4} = 0.1963\,\text{m}^{2}$$

Density at inlet using ideal gas law $P = 70$ bar $= 70 \times 10^{5}$ Pa:
$$\rho_{in} = \dfrac{PM}{RT} = \dfrac{70 \times 10^{5} \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 288} = \dfrac{14,112}{2,394.4} = 5.894\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$$

Velocity:
$$v = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho A} = \dfrac{2}{5.894 \times 0.1963} = \dfrac{2}{1.157} \approx 1.728\,\text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Energy lost to friction per kg of H₂:**
Using Darcy–Weisbach:
$$\Delta P = f\,\dfrac{L}{D}\,\dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$$

$$\Delta P = 0.018 \times \dfrac{200{,}000}{0.5} \times \dfrac{5.894 \times (1.728)^{2}}{2}$$

Step-by-step:
- $L/D = 400{,}000$
- $v^{2} = 2.986\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$
- $\rho v^{2}/2 = 5.894 \times 2.986/2 = 8.799$ Pa
- $\Delta P = 0.018 \times 400{,}000 \times 8.799 = 63{,}353$ Pa $\approx 0.634$ bar

Energy lost per kg:
$$w_f = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho} = \dfrac{63{,}353}{5.894} = 10{,}750\,\text{J/kg} \approx 10.75\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

Equivalently, in kWh: $10.75 / 3600 = 0.00299$ kWh/kg ≈ **0.003 kWh/kg H₂** (≈ 0.009 % of LHV).

This is the **pure frictional loss** in the pipeline; the dominant energy cost is the upstream **compressor work** needed to maintain inlet pressure, not the frictional dissipation itself.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Liquid Hydrogen Transmission**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PECHT862]**

**(a)** Explain the **Linde liquefaction process** for hydrogen with the help of a process flow description. State clearly why hydrogen requires **pre-cooling with liquid nitrogen** before Joule–Thomson expansion can liquefy it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquid hydrogen tanker carries **4,000 kg of LH₂**. The boil-off rate is **0.2 % per day**. The latent heat of vaporization of H₂ at 20.27 K is **$\Delta h_{vap} = 445$ kJ/kg**, and the density of LH₂ is **71 kg/m³**. The tanker tank is a horizontal cylinder of diameter 2.5 m.
Calculate: **(i)** the daily mass boil-off, **(ii)** the heat ingress rate $\dot{Q}$ into the tank, **(iii)** the volume of LH₂ remaining after 5 days if no refilling occurs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Linde process for hydrogen:
- Hydrogen is first **compressed to high pressure (200–300 bar)** in a multi-stage compressor with intercooling.
- The compressed gas is **pre-cooled to ~80 K by liquid nitrogen (LN₂)** to bring H₂ into a state where its **inversion temperature** condition is satisfied for Joule–Thomson cooling on expansion.
- The high-pressure, pre-cooled gas passes through a **counter-flow heat exchanger** where it is cooled further by the returning low-pressure cold gas.
- The gas then undergoes **Joule–Thomson throttling** through an expansion valve, producing liquid + vapour. The liquid is collected in a Dewar; the cold vapour returns through the heat exchanger to pre-cool the incoming stream.
- Hydrogen's inversion temperature at 1 atm is ≈ 200 K, but at 200 bar it rises above room temperature, meaning **pure J–T expansion of uncooled H₂ would actually warm it**. LN₂ pre-cooling lowers H₂ below its inversion curve so that throttling produces cooling.

**(b) Solution:**

**(i) Daily mass boil-off:**
$$m_{boil} = 0.002 \times 4000 = 8\,\text{kg/day}$$

**(ii) Heat ingress rate:**
The boil-off energy must equal the heat ingress $\dot{Q}$. For 8 kg vaporized per day:
$$\dot{Q} = m_{boil} \times \Delta h_{vap} = 8\,\text{kg/day} \times 445\,\text{kJ/kg} = 3560\,\text{kJ/day}$$

Convert to Watts:
$$\dot{Q} = \dfrac{3560}{24 \times 3600} = \dfrac{3560}{86400} \approx 0.0412\,\text{W per kg of LH}_{2}$$

Or total: $\dot{Q} = 3560 / 86400 \approx 0.0412$ kJ/s ≈ 41.2 W across the entire 4-tonne tank.

**(iii) Volume of LH₂ after 5 days:**
Mass remaining after 5 days:
$$m_{rem} = 4000 - 5 \times 8 = 3960\,\text{kg}$$

Volume:
$$V = \dfrac{m_{rem}}{\rho_{LH_2}} = \dfrac{3960}{71} \approx 55.77\,\text{m}^{3}$$

For comparison, the tanker tank itself has volume:
$$V_{tank} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \times L \Rightarrow L = \dfrac{4000/71}{\pi (2.5)^{2}/4} = \dfrac{56.34}{4.909} = 11.48\,\text{m}$$

So the tanker is roughly **11.5 m long** with **0.05 m of LH₂ volume lost to boil-off over 5 days** — a tiny fraction, justifying LH₂ transport for long-distance sea routes.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing isothermal and adiabatic compression** — When the question says "minimum compression energy," students must use the **isothermal** formula ($nRT \ln(P_2/P_1)$); for "reversible adiabatic compression," they must use $W = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} nRT_1 [\,(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\,]$. Using the wrong one costs the entire 3 marks allocated to that sub-question.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions** — Pressure must be in **Pa (SI)** when using $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). Mixing bar and Pa is the #1 reason for "off by 10⁵" answers.
> 3. **Ignoring compressibility $Z$** — For H₂ at 200 bar, $Z \approx 1.13$. Examiners often deliberately give a high-pressure problem expecting $Z$-correction; skipping it loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong $M_{H_2}$** — Use $M = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol, not 2 kg/mol. This affects all per-kg calculations.
> 5. **Confusing LH₂ density (71 kg/m³)** with GH₂ density at STP (0.0899 kg/m³) — a difference of **~790×** that completely changes volume calculations.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Long-distance hydrogen transmission has **three (sometimes four)** modes: **GH₂ pipelines, LH₂ cryogenic, NH₃ carrier, LOHC carrier**.
- ✅ Hydrogen at STP has density **0.0899 kg/m³**, LHV **33.33 kWh/kg**, $\gamma = 1.405$.
- ✅ **Isothermal compression work**: $W = nRT \ln(P_2/P_1)$ — minimum theoretical bound.
- ✅ **Adiabatic compression work**: $W = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} nRT_1 [\,(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\,]$ — realistic reversible bound.
- ✅ Real compressors add efficiency $\eta$ to the polytropic formula.
- ✅ Darcy–Weisbach: $\Delta P = f(L/D)(\rho v^{2}/2)$ — required for pipeline pressure drop problems.
- ✅ Weymouth equation governs compressible gas flow in long pipelines; $Z$-factor matters at high pressure.
- ✅ LH₂ must be cooled below the **inversion temperature** of H₂ via **LN₂ pre-cooling** before J–T liquefaction is possible.
- ✅ LH₂ liquefaction consumes **30–40 % of LHV** (≈ 10–13 kWh/kg); minimum theoretical work ≈ **5 kWh/kg**.
- ✅ Typical LH₂ boil-off rate: **0.1–0.3 % per day**; design voyages accordingly.
- ✅ Multi-stage compression with intercooling saves work; optimum stage ratio $r = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$.
- ✅ Hydrogen embrittlement, leakage (3× CH₄), and lower volumetric energy density are the three engineering headaches of pipeline transmission.
- ✅ Real density of H₂ at pressure/temperature: $\rho = PM/(ZRT)$.
- ✅ Comparison order (energy efficiency): **GH₂ pipeline < LOHC < NH₃ ≈ LH₂** for very long distances, with **GH₂ pipeline cheapest in energy** when the line already exists.
- ✅ KTU 2024 expects students to compute **per-kg energy penalty** for each mode and justify mode selection for a given distance and end-use.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation via Road
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transportation via Road

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydrogen transportation via road** refers to the delivery of hydrogen in bulk quantities from centralized production facilities (electrolysis plants, SMR units, or industrial by-product sources) to end-use points (refueling stations, industrial consumers, or distribution hubs) using over-the-road vehicles such as tube-trailers, cryogenic tankers, and purpose-built mobile hydrogen carriers. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 3 of PECHT862), this topic specifically addresses the **logistical engineering chain** for moving gaseous hydrogen (GH2/CH2), liquid hydrogen (LH2), and chemically bound hydrogen (metal hydrides, LOHCs) along public road networks.

The three principal road-transport modes recognized by KTU are:

1. **Compressed Hydrogen Gas (CH2) Transport** — in high-pressure Type III/Type IV tube trailers (working pressure **250 bar–500 bar**, with newer designs at **700 bar**).
2. **Liquid Hydrogen (LH2) Transport** — in super-insulated cryogenic road tankers operating at **20.4 K** (boiling point of H2 at 1 atm).
3. **Solid/Material-Bound Hydrogen Transport** — metal hydride canisters, LOHC (Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carrier) tankers, and ammonia (NH3) road tankers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 specifically covers *delivery logistics*, *energy efficiency of road transport*, *boil-off management*, *compressor station requirements*, and *hazmat classification (UN 1049 for GH2, UN 1971 for LH2, UN 1005 for NH3).*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hydrogen Courier Service"

Imagine you are running a **water-bottling company** in a city where every household needs water. You have three delivery options:

- **Bottled Water (CH2 Trailers):** You fill strong steel bottles with hydrogen gas under high pressure and drive them to customers. Simple, but each truck carries limited amount because gas compresses only so much.
- **Ice-Cubes in Insulated Coolers (LH2 Tankers):** You freeze hydrogen into liquid form, pack it in a refrigerated truck, and deliver it. Each truck carries much more hydrogen, but you lose some to evaporation ("boil-off") during the trip, especially in Kerala's warm climate.
- **Wet Sponges (Metal Hydrides):** You chemically "soak" hydrogen into a solid material that releases it on heating. The truck is "empty" of free H2 during transit, making it safer, but it is heavy and expensive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy for Kerala context:** Just as LPG cylinders (Hydrocarbons) are door-delivered in pressurized bottles across Kerala, the future will see hydrogen delivered similarly — but at much higher pressures and stricter safety protocols, requiring reinforced road tankers and Hazmat-trained drivers.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- **Molar Mass of H2 (M):** 2.016 g/mol = **0.002016 kg/mol**
- **Higher Heating Value of H2 (HHV):** **141.9 MJ/kg** (or 39.4 kWh/kg)
- **Lower Heating Value of H2 (LHV):** **119.9 MJ/kg** (or 33.3 kWh/kg)
- **Density of GH2 at 1 atm, 288 K:** **0.0818 kg/m³**
- **Density of GH2 at 350 bar, 288 K:** **~24 kg/m³**
- **Density of GH2 at 700 bar, 288 K:** **~40 kg/m³**
- **Density of LH2 at 20.4 K:** **70.8 kg/m³**
- **Boiling Point of H2:** **20.4 K (-252.87 °C)**
- **Critical Point of H2:** **T_c = 33.2 K, P_c = 13.0 bar**
- **Compressibility Factor Z** (real-gas correction): **1.10–1.30** at typical tube-trailer pressures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Density Note:** Liquid hydrogen carries **~865 times** the energy density (per unit volume) of atmospheric-pressure hydrogen, and compressed H2 at 700 bar carries **~490 times**. This is why road transport modes (LH2 > CH2 > GH2-atm) are ranked by volumetric efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Compressed Hydrogen Gas (CH2) Road Transport

**Logic Flow — How a CH2 Tube-Trailer Operates:**

- **Step 1 — Pre-compression:** Hydrogen produced at the plant (SMR/electrolyzer) at near-atmospheric pressure (~1–30 bar) is compressed in **multi-stage diaphragm or piston compressors** to the trailer's working pressure (250, 350, or 500 bar).
- **Step 2 — Filling Cascade:** The trailer is filled through a **cascade system** (high-pressure buffer tanks pre-charged to slightly above the final fill pressure) to minimize temperature rise during rapid fill.
- **Step 3 — Trailer Configuration:** Modern Type IV CH2 trailers use **carbon-fiber composite cylinders** over a polymer liner, grouped in 12–18-tube racks. Total payload: **250–500 kg H2 per trailer** (350 bar) or up to **1,100 kg H2** (700 bar).
- **Step 4 — Road Transit:** Trailer is classified as **UN 1049 (Hydrogen, Compressed)** — Class 2.1 Flammable Gas. Driver must hold ADR/IMDG hazmat certification. Speed limited typically to **80 km/h** on highways.
- **Step 5 — Delivery & Decanting:** At refueling station, hydrogen is decanted either by **pressure differential** (if station tank pressure is lower) or via **recompression**. Residual gas at ~30–50 bar is typically vented or recovered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** CH2 trailers are the **workhorse of early hydrogen refueling networks** (e.g., in California, Germany, Japan). Each 350-bar trailer can fuel approximately **40–60 FCEV passenger cars** (assuming 5–6 kg H2 per fill).

### 2.1.2 Liquid Hydrogen (LH2) Road Transport

**Logic Flow — How an LH2 Cryogenic Tanker Operates:**

- **Step 1 — Liquefaction:** Gaseous H2 is cooled through **ortho-to-para hydrogen conversion** (catalyst) and Joule-Thomson expansion cycles to **20.4 K**. Liquefaction energy: **~10–13 kWh/kg LH2** (≈ 30% of H2's LHV).
- **Step 2 — Tanker Loading:** Cryogenic tanker (typical capacity **50–70 m³**, payload **3,500–5,000 kg H2**) is filled at the liquefaction plant via vacuum-jacketed lines.
- **Step 3 — Insulation:** Tanker uses **multi-layer vacuum insulation (MLVI)** with perlite powder + 30–60 layers of super-insulation (aluminized Mylar). Heat in-leak: **~1–2 W per 1,000 liters** per day.
- **Step 4 — Boil-off Gas (BOG):** During transit, heat leakage causes **0.1%–0.5% per day** evaporation. For a 3-day Kerala-trip (hot ambient ~35 °C), this can be **0.3%–1.5%** total mass loss.
- **Step 5 — Delivery:** LH2 is pumped out using **cryogenic pumps** or decanted by **pressure-build-up coils** (using internal vaporizer to pressurize tanker). Delivered to station's LH2 storage tank.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala Climate Caveat:** India's tropical climate (ambient 30–38 °C) accelerates BOG rates by **15–25%** versus European/Canadian operations. This makes LH2 road transport in Kerala *less efficient* and motivates CH2 or LOHC alternatives.

### 2.1.3 Material-Bound Hydrogen (LOHC, Metal Hydrides, NH3)

- **LOHC (Dibenzyltoluene, H0-BT):** Liquid organic hydrogen carrier — H2 is catalytically bonded at the plant, transported safely as a non-flammable liquid, and released at the station. **Capacity:** ~6.2 wt% H2 in carrier.
- **Metal Hydrides (e.g., LaNi5H6, MgH2):** Solid-state storage — safe at ambient conditions, but **<2 wt% H2** and heavy.
- **Ammonia (NH3):** 17.7 wt% H2 — transported as pressurized liquid at **-33 °C / 9 bar** (UN 1005). Cracked back to H2 at the station using catalyst + heat.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units / Constants** |
|---|---|---|
| $m = \dfrac{P \cdot V \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}$ | Ideal-gas (with Z-correction) mass of H2 in a tube | $m$ in kg, $P$ in Pa, $V$ in m³, $T$ in K, $Z \approx 1.10$–$1.30$ |
| $E = m \cdot HHV$ | Total chemical energy in payload | $E$ in MJ, $m$ in kg, $HHV = 141.9$ MJ/kg |
| $V_{\text{vol}} = \dfrac{m}{\rho_{\text{bulk}}}$ | Volumetric payload check | $V$ in m³, $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| $\eta_{\text{transport}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{delivered}}}{m_{\text{loaded}}} \times 100\%$ | Delivery efficiency (loss to BOG / venting) | % |
| $V_{\text{BOG}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{leak}} \cdot t}{L_{\text{vap}} \cdot \rho_{LH2}}$ | Boil-off mass per day | $Q$ in W, $t$ in s, $L_{\text{vap}} = 446$ kJ/kg |
| $W_{\text{comp}} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ | Isentropic compression work per mole | $\gamma = 1.41$ for H2, $T_1$ in K |
| $P_{\text{cascade,opt}} = \sqrt{P_{\text{tank}} \cdot P_{\text{supply}}}$ | Optimum cascade buffer pressure | $P$ in bar (Rule of thumb) |
| $N_{\text{FCEV}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{H2,trailer}}}{m_{\text{H2,fill}}}$ | Cars refuelable per trailer | $m_{\text{fill}} \approx 5$–$6$ kg (passenger FCEV) |
| $\text{Cost per kg} = \dfrac{C_{\text{trans}} + C_{\text{loss}} + C_{\text{capital}}}{m_{\text{delivered}}}$ | LCOH component for road transport | INR or USD per kg H2 |
| $D_{\text{trailer,eff}} = \dfrac{\text{Total H2 mass}}{\text{Trailer GVW}}$ | Gravimetric efficiency of trailer | % (typical: 1%–4%) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Numerical Anchor (worth memorizing for KTU exams):**
> - 1 kg H2 ≈ **11.1 Nm³** (Normal cubic meters at 1 atm, 273 K)
> - 1 Nm³ H2 ≈ **0.090 kg**
> - 1 trailer (350 bar, ~50 m³ total) ≈ **~400 kg H2** ≈ **5,600 Nm³**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of Hydrogen in a Compressed Gas Tube-Trailer

We use the **real-gas equation of state** to find the mass of H2 in a tube-trailer with total internal volume $V$ at working pressure $P$ and ambient temperature $T$.

### Step 1: Start with the Real-Gas Equation

For a real gas, the compressibility factor $Z$ corrects the ideal-gas law:

$$
P \cdot V = Z \cdot n \cdot R \cdot T
$$

where $n$ is the number of moles.

### Step 2: Solve for Moles

Rearranging:

$$
n = \dfrac{P \cdot V}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}
$$

### Step 3: Convert Moles to Mass

Since $m = n \cdot M$ (where $M$ is the molar mass of H2 = 0.002016 kg/mol):

$$
m = \dfrac{P \cdot V \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}
$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

**Given:** A CH2 tube-trailer has 12 cylinders, each of internal volume **150 L** (0.150 m³). Working pressure = **350 bar** = **$3.5 \times 10^7$ Pa**. Ambient temperature = **303 K** (30 °C — typical for Kerala). Use $Z = 1.20$ at 350 bar.

**Compute total internal volume:**

$$
V = 12 \times 0.150 = 1.80 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Plug into the formula:**

$$
m = \dfrac{(3.5 \times 10^7) \times (1.80) \times (0.002016)}{1.20 \times 8.314 \times 303}
$$

**Numerator computation:**

$$
(3.5 \times 10^7) \times (1.80) = 6.30 \times 10^7
$$

$$
6.30 \times 10^7 \times 0.002016 = 1.270 \times 10^5
$$

**Denominator computation:**

$$
1.20 \times 8.314 = 9.977
$$

$$
9.977 \times 303 = 3.023 \times 10^3
$$

**Final division:**

$$
m = \dfrac{1.270 \times 10^5}{3.023 \times 10^3} \approx 42.0 \text{ kg H2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If the problem specifies Type IV cylinders (composite) with a *water volume* of 150 L, the actual *internal gas volume* is essentially the same as water volume for a near-cylindrical geometry. Always state your assumption!

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Boil-Off Gas (BOG) Mass for LH2 Tanker

Heat leaks into the cryogenic tanker from the environment. This heat vaporizes some liquid H2. We derive the mass vaporized per unit time.

### Step 1: Energy Balance

Heat in-leak $Q_{\text{leak}}$ (in Watts) over time $t$ vaporizes a mass $m_{\text{BOG}}$:

$$
Q_{\text{leak}} \cdot t = m_{\text{BOG}} \cdot L_{\text{vap}}
$$

where $L_{\text{vap}}$ is the latent heat of vaporization of H2 = **446 kJ/kg = $4.46 \times 10^5$ J/kg**.

### Step 2: Solve for BOG Mass

$$
m_{\text{BOG}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{leak}} \cdot t}{L_{\text{vap}}}
$$

### Step 3: Express as % Per Day

If the trailer carries mass $m_{\text{load}}$:

$$
\% \text{ BOG/day} = \dfrac{m_{\text{BOG}}}{m_{\text{load}}} \times 100
$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example

**Given:** An LH2 tanker has heat in-leak $Q_{\text{leak}} = 200$ W (well-insulated design). Payload $m_{\text{load}} = 4{,}000$ kg. Transit time $t = 3$ days $= 2.59 \times 10^5$ s.

**Numerator:**

$$
200 \times 2.59 \times 10^5 = 5.18 \times 10^7 \text{ J}
$$

**BOG mass:**

$$
m_{\text{BOG}} = \dfrac{5.18 \times 10^7}{4.46 \times 10^5} \approx 116 \text{ kg}
$$

**Percentage:**

$$
\% \text{ BOG} = \dfrac{116}{4{,}000} \times 100 = 2.9\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert **days → seconds** ($1 \text{ day} = 86{,}400$ s). Always show unit conversion explicitly in KTU answer sheets.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Isentropic Compression Work for H2

The compressor at the plant pressurizes H2 from $P_1$ (e.g., 30 bar from electrolyzer) to $P_2$ (e.g., 350 bar trailer pressure). For an ideal isentropic process:

### Step 1: Starting Point — Ideal Work for an Open System

For a steady-flow compressor with inlet temperature $T_1$ and pressure ratio $r_p = P_2 / P_1$:

$$
W_{\text{comp,ideal}} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[ r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]
$$

### Step 2: Insert H2 Properties

For diatomic H2: $\gamma = C_p / C_v = 1.41$. $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

### Step 3: Numerical Example

**Given:** Compress 1 mole of H2 from $P_1 = 30$ bar to $P_2 = 350$ bar at $T_1 = 300$ K.

**Pressure ratio:**

$$
r_p = \dfrac{350}{30} = 11.67
$$

**Exponent term:**

$$
\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{0.41}{1.41} = 0.2908
$$

$$
r_p^{0.2908} = (11.67)^{0.2908}
$$
