Using $\ln(11.67) = 2.457$, then $0.2908 \times 2.457 = 0.7145$, so:

$$
e^{0.7145} = 2.043
$$

**Work calculation:**

$$
W = \dfrac{1.41}{0.41} \times 8.314 \times 300 \times (2.043 - 1)
$$

$$
W = 3.439 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 1.043
$$

$$
W = 3.439 \times 2{,}494 \times 1.043 \approx 8{,}948 \text{ J/mol}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to kWh/kg

Per mole of H2 (0.002016 kg):

$$
W = \dfrac{8{,}948}{0.002016} = 4.44 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg} = 1.23 \text{ kWh/kg}
$$

With a real compressor efficiency of $\eta_{\text{comp}} = 0.65$:

$$
W_{\text{real}} = \dfrac{1.23}{0.65} \approx 1.89 \text{ kWh/kg}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Compression energy (~2 kWh/kg) is **6% of H2's LHV (33.3 kWh/kg)**. Liquefaction is much more expensive (~10 kWh/kg = 30% of LHV). This is why CH2 is favored for short-haul (<300 km) and LH2 for long-haul (>500 km) road transport.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of Road Transport Modes

| **Parameter** | **CH2 (350 bar)** | **CH2 (700 bar)** | **LH2** | **LOHC (H0-BT)** | **NH3 (Liquid)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Payload per trailer (kg H2) | 250–500 | 600–1,100 | 3,500–5,000 | 2,000–2,500 | 2,500–3,000 |
| Trailer capacity (m³) | 30–50 | 50–60 | 50–70 (cryo) | 30–40 | 25–30 |
| Density (kg H2/m³) | ~24 | ~40 | 70.8 | ~50 | ~100 |
| Energy density (MJ/m³) | ~3,400 | ~5,680 | 10,030 | 7,100 | 14,200 |
| Pre-transport cost (kWh/kg) | ~1.5–2.0 | ~3.0–4.0 | ~10–13 | ~1.0–2.0 | ~3.0–5.0 |
| Loss in transit (%/day) | 0% (gas) | 0% (gas) | 0.1–0.5% | <0.1% | 0.1% |
| Hazmat Class | 2.1 (UN 1049) | 2.1 (UN 1049) | 2.1 (UN 1971) | 9 (UN 3082) | 2.3 + 8 (UN 1005) |
| Best for distance | <300 km | <300 km | >500 km | >1,000 km | >1,000 km |
| Refueling cost (USD/kg) | $2–$4 | $3–$5 | $4–$6 | $5–$8 | $3–$5 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydrogen Road Transport — Modal Selection Logic

The following flowchart helps a logistics engineer decide the optimal road-transport mode based on distance, quantity, and destination infrastructure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[H2 Production Plant] --> startB{Daily Demand at Customer?}
    startB -- Less than 200 kg --> startC[CH2 Tube Trailer 350 bar]
    startB -- 200 to 800 kg --> startD{Delivery Distance?}
    startB -- Greater than 800 kg --> startE{Delivery Distance?}

    startC --> startC1[Typical Range less than 200 km]
    startC1 --> startC2[Use Cascade Fill with 3 stage buffer]
    startC2 --> startC3[Class 2.1 UN 1049 Hazmat]
    startC3 --> startC4[Decant by Pressure Differential at Station]

    startD -- Less than 300 km --> startF[CH2 Tube Trailer 700 bar]
    startD -- Greater than 300 km --> startG[LH2 Cryogenic Tanker]

    startF --> startF1[Type IV Composite Cylinders]
    startF1 --> startF2[Requires Heavy Duty Compressor at Plant]
    startF2 --> startF3[ADR Certified Driver and Vehicle]

    startE -- Greater than 500 km --> startG
    startE -- Greater than 1000 km --> startH[LOHC or NH3 Tanker]

    startG --> startG1[Liquefaction at Plant 20.4 K]
    startG1 --> startG2[Vacuum Jacketed Tanker 50 m3]
    startG2 --> startG3[Manage Boil Off 0.1 to 0.5 percent per day]
    startG3 --> startG4[Cryogenic Pump at Station]

    startH --> startH1[Chemical Binding at Plant]
    startH1 --> startH2[Transport as Safe Liquid H0 BT or NH3]
    startH2 --> startH3[Dehydrogenation Reactor at Station]
    startH3 --> startH4[Release Pure H2 to Storage]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CH2 Tube-Trailer Filling Station

This diagram maps the interactions between the production plant, the trailer, and the refueling station.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    prodA[Electrolyzer or SMR Plant] --> compA[Low Pressure H2 Output 30 bar]
    compA --> compB[Stage 1 Compressor to 100 bar]
    compB --> compC[Stage 2 Compressor to 200 bar]
    compC --> compD[Stage 3 Compressor to 350 bar]
    compD --> buffA[High Pressure Buffer Tank 400 bar]
    buffA --> fillA[Cascade Fill Manifold]
    fillA --> trailA[Type IV Tube Trailer 12 Cylinders 150 L each]
    trailA --> roadA[Road Transport Class 2.1 ADR]
    roadA --> statA[Refueling Station Buffer Tank 350 bar]
    statA --> dispA[Dispenser Cascade to Vehicle 700 bar]
```

## 4.3 LH2 Tanker Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    liqA[LH2 Liquefaction Plant 20.4 K] --> vacA[Vacuum Insulated Transfer Line]
    vacA --> tankA[Cryogenic Tanker 50 m3 Vacuum Jacketed]
    tankA --> subA1[Inner Vessel LH2]
    tankA --> subA2[Outer Vessel Vacuum + MLVI]
    tankA --> subA3[Pressure Build Up Coil PBUC]
    tankA --> subA4[Safety Relief Valves 2 in series]
    tankA --> subA5[Instrumentation Level and Pressure]
    subA3 --> delA[Pressurized LH2 to Station]
    delA --> delB[Station Cryo Pump]
    delB --> delC[Station LH2 Storage Tank]
    subA2 --> bogA[Boil Off Gas Vent or Recovery]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exam:** When asked to "draw a block diagram" of a hydrogen road-transport system, focus on **boundary clarity** (plant/tanker/station boxes), **arrow direction** (mass flow), and **labelling interfaces** (pressure, temperature, Hazmat class). Mermaid flowcharts are accepted equivalents of hand-drawn schematics in KTU valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Compare compressed hydrogen (CH2) and liquid hydrogen (LH2) as road-transport media in terms of **gravimetric density, energy density, and transport distance suitability**.

**Model Answer:**

- **Gravimetric Density (kg H2 per kg of system):** CH2 at 350 bar carries ~1–2 wt% H2; LH2 carries ~10–15 wt% (excluding tanker mass). CH2 trailers are **heavier for the same H2 mass** due to cylinder mass.
- **Energy Density (MJ/m³):** CH2 at 350 bar ≈ 3,400 MJ/m³; LH2 ≈ 10,030 MJ/m³. LH2 is **~3× more volumetrically efficient**.
- **Distance Suitability:** CH2 is preferred for **<300 km** (no boil-off losses, simpler infrastructure). LH2 is preferred for **>500 km** (higher payload per trip amortizes liquefaction cost).
- **Trade-off:** LH2 has 0.1–0.5% per day boil-off loss and high liquefaction energy (~30% of H2's LHV).

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** What is the role of a **cascade filling system** in CH2 tube-trailer loading? Mention the optimum buffer pressure formula.

**Model Answer:**

A cascade filling system uses **intermediate high-pressure buffer tanks** pre-charged to a pressure between the supply line and the trailer, to control fill temperature, prevent excessive gas heating, and achieve high final fill density.

**Optimum cascade buffer pressure formula (Rule of thumb):**

$$
P_{\text{buffer,opt}} = \sqrt{P_{\text{supply}} \cdot P_{\text{trailer}}}
$$

For example, with $P_{\text{supply}} = 200$ bar and $P_{\text{trailer}} = 350$ bar:

$$
P_{\text{buffer,opt}} = \sqrt{200 \times 350} = \sqrt{70{,}000} \approx 264.6 \text{ bar}
$$

This minimizes the total compression work and limits gas temperature rise to <85 °C (per SAE J2600 limits).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (Choose ANY ONE, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** A CH2 tube-trailer consists of 10 cylinders, each with an internal volume of **200 L**. It is filled to a working pressure of **500 bar** at an ambient temperature of **303 K**. Given the compressibility factor Z = 1.25 at this state, calculate:
(i) the mass of H2 carried,
(ii) the total chemical energy in the payload using HHV,
(iii) the number of passenger FCEVs (each refueling 5 kg H2) that can be served per trip.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Mass of H2:**

Total internal volume:

$$
V = 10 \times 200 \text{ L} = 2{,}000 \text{ L} = 2.0 \text{ m}^3
$$

Using the real-gas equation:

$$
m = \dfrac{P \cdot V \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}
$$

Convert pressure: $P = 500 \text{ bar} = 5.0 \times 10^7$ Pa.

$$
m = \dfrac{(5.0 \times 10^7) \times (2.0) \times (0.002016)}{1.25 \times 8.314 \times 303}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
5.0 \times 10^7 \times 2.0 = 1.0 \times 10^8
$$

$$
1.0 \times 10^8 \times 0.002016 = 2.016 \times 10^5
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
1.25 \times 8.314 \times 303 = 3.149 \times 10^3
$$

**Final:**

$$
m = \dfrac{2.016 \times 10^5}{3.149 \times 10^3} \approx 64.0 \text{ kg H2}
$$

**(ii) Total chemical energy:**

$$
E = m \times HHV = 64.0 \times 141.9 = 9{,}082 \text{ MJ}
$$

(Or equivalently: $64.0 \times 39.4 = 2{,}521.6$ kWh)

**(iii) Number of FCEVs served:**

$$
N = \dfrac{m}{m_{\text{fill}}} = \dfrac{64.0}{5.0} = 12.8 \Rightarrow 12 \text{ FCEVs (full fills)}
$$

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the **safety features** mandated for a CH2 tube-trailer operating on Indian roads under the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR)** and **ADR (UN) classification UN 1049**.

**Model Answer:**

The CH2 tube-trailer must comply with:

1. **Vehicle-Level Safety:**
   - **Pressure Relief Devices (PRDs):** Each cylinder has a thermally-activated PRD (TPRD) that vents at **110–130 °C** in case of fire.
   - **Burst Disc + TPRD combination** in series for redundancy.
   - **Crash-resistant mounting** of cylinders to chassis (per ISO 11439 / ISO 14469).
   - **Rear impact protection** and **side guards** as per CMVR.

2. **Hazard Communication:**
   - **Hazard placards** (Class 2.1 flammable gas) on all four sides.
   - **UN 1049** label and H2 identification on the tanker.
   - **Emergency contact card (TREM card)** with first-aid and fire-fighting procedures.

3. **Operational Safety:**
   - **ADR-trained driver** with Hazmat endorsement.
   - **GPS tracking** and route pre-approval (avoiding tunnels, dense Kerala markets, and school zones).
   - **Speed governor** at 80 km/h.
   - **No smoking / no naked flame** zones within 15 m of the vehicle.

4. **Fire Protection:**
   - **Dry-powder extinguishers** (ABC type) and **water spray systems** to cool cylinders during external fires.
   - **Flame arrestors** on vent lines.

5. **Monitoring:**
   - **Pressure gauges, thermometers, and leak detectors** installed at each cylinder group.
   - **Continuous H2 leak sensor** in the cabin with audible/visual alarm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often list *generic* vehicle safety (seatbelts, horns) and miss the **hydrogen-specific** safety items (TPRD, burst disc, flame arrestor, hydrogen-specific fire water deluge, Class 2.1 hazmat). Deduct **1–2 marks** for this in 7-mark questions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** A **liquid hydrogen (LH2) cryogenic tanker** of 60 m³ capacity carries a payload of 4,200 kg LH2 at 20.4 K. The trailer's heat in-leak rate is **150 W**, and the journey takes **4 days**. Calculate:
(i) the boil-off gas (BOG) mass in kg,
(ii) the percentage of H2 lost during transit,
(iii) the energy loss in MJ using $L_{\text{vap}} = 446$ kJ/kg.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) BOG Mass:**

Time in seconds: $t = 4 \text{ days} = 4 \times 86{,}400 = 345{,}600$ s.

$$
m_{\text{BOG}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{leak}} \cdot t}{L_{\text{vap}}} = \dfrac{150 \times 345{,}600}{446{,}000}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
150 \times 345{,}600 = 5.184 \times 10^7 \text{ J}
$$

**Division:**

$$
m_{\text{BOG}} = \dfrac{5.184 \times 10^7}{4.46 \times 10^5} \approx 116.2 \text{ kg}
$$

**(ii) Percentage Loss:**

$$
\% \text{ Loss} = \dfrac{116.2}{4{,}200} \times 100 \approx 2.77\%
$$

**(iii) Energy Loss:**

$$
E_{\text{loss}} = m_{\text{BOG}} \times L_{\text{vap}} = 116.2 \times 446 = 51{,}825 \text{ kJ} = 51.83 \text{ MJ}
$$

Equivalent energy: $51.83 / 119.9 \approx 0.43$ kg of H2 (in terms of LHV).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** 0.43 kg of H2 lost per trip = ~10% of one FCEV fill. This is a significant economic loss, motivating investment in **better MLVI insulation** and **BOG recovery systems** (re-liquefaction on-board using cryocoolers).

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare the **energy cost** of delivering 1 kg of H2 over 400 km by (i) CH2 at 350 bar road trailer and (ii) LH2 cryogenic tanker. Assume:
- Trailer fuel consumption: 0.30 L diesel/km carrying 400 kg H2 (CH2) and 0.35 L/km carrying 4,000 kg H2 (LH2).
- Diesel LHV: 35.8 MJ/L.
- Compression energy: 1.8 kWh/kg for CH2.
- Liquefaction energy: 11 kWh/kg for LH2.
- BOG loss: 2% for LH2 (already-incurred energy lost).
- 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) CH2 at 350 bar:**

- **Diesel energy for 400 km:** $0.30 \times 400 = 120$ L. Energy: $120 \times 35.8 = 4{,}296$ MJ.
- **Per kg of H2 delivered:** $4{,}296 / 400 = 10.74$ MJ/kg.
- **Compression energy:** $1.8 \times 3.6 = 6.48$ MJ/kg.
- **Total energy cost:** $10.74 + 6.48 = 17.22$ MJ/kg.

**(ii) LH2 cryogenic:**

- **Diesel energy for 400 km:** $0.35 \times 400 = 140$ L. Energy: $140 \times 35.8 = 5{,}012$ MJ.
- **Per kg delivered (accounting for 2% BOG loss on 4,000 kg = 80 kg lost):** Mass effectively delivered = 3,920 kg.
- **Per kg:** $5{,}012 / 3{,}920 = 1.28$ MJ/kg.
- **Liquefaction energy:** $11 \times 3.6 = 39.6$ MJ/kg.
- **Total energy cost:** $1.28 + 39.6 = 40.88$ MJ/kg.

**Comparison & Verdict:**

- CH2 (350 bar) road transport over 400 km costs **17.22 MJ/kg** of input energy.
- LH2 road transport costs **40.88 MJ/kg** — over **2× higher**.
- **Conclusion:** For a 400 km trip, **CH2 is energetically superior**. LH2 is justified only when (a) the distance exceeds ~1,000 km, or (b) the customer cannot accept high-pressure equipment.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error in KTU Exams:** Students often **divide total energy by payload without considering BOG losses**. If you forget to adjust LH2 payload from 4,000 → 3,920 kg, your answer for LH2 is **incorrect by ~2%** and you may lose **1 mark** in a 7-mark question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 3: Transportation via Road):**

- **Three road-transport modes** for hydrogen: CH2 (compressed), LH2 (liquid), and material-bound (LOHC, NH3, metal hydrides).
- **Compressed H2 (CH2) at 350 bar** is the **workhorse** for short-distance road delivery (<300 km).
- **CH2 at 700 bar** uses Type IV composite cylinders and can deliver **1,000+ kg H2** per trailer.
- **Liquid H2 (LH2)** cryogenic tankers carry **3,500–5,000 kg H2** per trip, suited for **>500 km** distances.
- **Boil-Off Gas (BOG)** rate for LH2: **0.1–0.5% per day**, rising to **1–2%** in tropical climates (Kerala).
- **Real-gas mass equation:** $m = P \cdot V \cdot M / (Z \cdot R \cdot T)$ with $Z = 1.10$–$1.30$ at typical trailer pressures.
- **Compression energy** ≈ 1.5–2 kWh/kg (6% of H2 LHV); **Liquefaction energy** ≈ 10–13 kWh/kg (30% of H2 LHV).
- **Optimum cascade buffer pressure:** $P_{\text{buffer,opt}} = \sqrt{P_{\text{supply}} \cdot P_{\text{trailer}}}$.
- **HHV of H2 = 141.9 MJ/kg; LHV = 119.9 MJ/kg** (memorize both).
- **Volumetric energy density:** LH2 (10,030 MJ/m³) > LOHC (~7,100) > CH2 700 bar (5,680) > CH2 350 bar (3,400) >> atmospheric H2 (11.6 MJ/m³).
- **Hazmat classifications:** UN 1049 (CH2, Class 2.1), UN 1971 (LH2, Class 2.1), UN 1005 (NH3, Class 2.3 + 8), UN 3082 (LOHC, Class 9).
- **Safety must-haves on a CH2 trailer:** TPRD, burst disc, flame arrestor, hydrogen leak sensor, ADR driver, Class 2.1 placards.
- **Kerala-specific operational caveat:** High ambient temperatures (30–38 °C) amplify BOG losses; pre-cooling cascades and MLVI upgrades are recommended for LH2 logistics.
- **LOHC (dibenzyltoluene)** is the safest road-transport mode for hydrogen-rich liquids, but dehydrogenation at the station is energy-intensive (~10 kWh/kg released).
- **Quick conversion memorization:** 1 kg H2 = 11.1 Nm³; 1 Nm³ H2 = 0.090 kg; 1 FCEV fill = 5–6 kg H2.
- **KTU 2024 common exam tip:** Always show unit conversions (days → seconds, bar → Pa) explicitly. Always state assumptions (e.g., $Z = 1.20$ for 350 bar H2). Always include Hazmat class in safety questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ships and in form of Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Course Code:** PECHT862
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ships and in form of Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC)
> - **Cognitive Levels Targeted:** Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyze (L4)
> - **Aligned Outcomes:** CO3 – Evaluate hydrogen storage and transportation technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydrogen Transport by Ships — Formal Definition

**Hydrogen shipping** refers to the large-scale, intercontinental maritime transport of hydrogen (H₂) in bulk using specially designed or retrofitted vessels. Because pure gaseous hydrogen has a very low density at ambient conditions, ships carry hydrogen either in **chemically bound liquid form** (LH₂, NH₃, methanol, LOHC) or as **highly compressed gas**. International maritime routes currently prioritize three maritime vectors:

- **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)** at **−253 °C**
- **Liquid Ammonia (NH₃)** at **−33 °C**
- **Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC)** at ambient temperature

| Carrier | Boiling Point | H₂ Mass Fraction | Tanker Volume Energy Density |
|---|---|---|---|
| LH₂ | −253 °C | **100 %** | ~8.5 MJ/L |
| NH₃ | −33 °C | 17.8 % | ~12.7 MJ/L |
| LOHC (e.g., H0-DBT) | > 300 °C | 6.2 % | ~18.0 MJ/L (after loading) |
| CH₃OH | 64.7 °C | 12.5 % | ~15.8 MJ/L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 3 unit explicitly names "Ships and LOHC" as a single integrated transport modality. Examiners expect students to compare volumetric energy density, boil-off losses, and infrastructure re-purposing of existing oil/chemical tankers.

## 1.2 Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC) — Formal Definition

A **Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carrier (LOHC)** is a pair of organic liquids — a *hydrogen-lean* (aromatic/unsaturated) form and its *hydrogen-rich* (saturated/cyclic) counterpart — between which H₂ is reversibly stored and released via catalytic **hydrogenation** (loading) and **dehydrogenation** (unloading) reactions. The carrier itself is non-explosive, storable at ambient temperature and pressure, and reusable over thousands of cycles.

The generalized reversible reaction is:

$$\text{H}_2\text{-lean carrier} \;+\; n\,\text{H}_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{H}_2\text{-rich carrier} \quad \Delta H \;>\; 0$$

where *n* denotes the moles of H₂ reversibly bound per mole of carrier.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Hydrogen Sponge Ship"**
> Imagine an empty tanker ship docking at a hydrogen-producing port. Instead of filling a fragile vacuum cryotank with super-cold liquid hydrogen, the crew pours in a thick, oily, diesel-like liquid called **dibenzyltoluene (H0-DBT)**. At the port, this oil is pumped through a catalytic reactor where it "drinks" hydrogen like a sponge, becoming saturated oil (H18-DBT). The loaded ship sails at **room temperature** with negligible boil-off. At the destination port, the oil is "squeezed" through another reactor that releases pure H₂, and the now-empty sponge oil is shipped back for the next load. The sponge never gets consumed — it is the same molecules, used over and over.

This is the essence of LOHC: the carrier behaves as a **reusable, inert hydrogen sponge**, eliminating cryogenic boil-off while still carrying chemically-bound hydrogen across oceans.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Constants & Benchmarks

- **Lower Heating Value of H₂:** **120 MJ/kg**
- **Density of LH₂ at −253 °C:** **70.8 kg/m³**
- **Density of LOHC (DBT) loaded:** ~**950 kg/m³**
- **Hydrogen capacity of dibenzyltoluene (DBT):** **6.2 wt%**
- **Hydrogen capacity of toluene/MCH system:** **6.16 wt%**
- **Dehydrogenation enthalpy (H18-DBT → H0-DBT):** **~65 kJ/mol H₂**
- **Dehydrogenation temperature (Pt/Al₂O₃ catalyst, DBT):** **250–310 °C**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Maritime Hydrogen Transport — Logical Breakdown

### Step 1 — The Geography of Demand
Green hydrogen is produced where renewable electricity is cheap (Australia, Chile, Middle East, Norway), but demand lies in industrial clusters (Japan, Korea, Germany, India). **Trans-oceanic shipping** of energy at scale therefore becomes the bottleneck.

### Step 2 — Why Conventional LH₂ Is Problematic at Sea
- Cryogenic boil-off (BOG) at ~**0.1–0.3 %/day** must be re-liquefied or vented.
- Spherical tanks consume 3×–4× the volume per unit H₂ than conventional fuels.
- Specialized insulation (MLI + vacuum) is mandatory.

### Step 3 — Why Ammonia (NH₃) Dominates Today's Traded Volume
- Already a globally traded commodity (~180 Mt/yr).
- Mild liquefaction (boils at **−33 °C** at 1 atm) and existing tanker fleet.
- Cracking NH₃ → H₂ + N₂ at the destination adds capital cost but avoids LH₂ risks.

### Step 4 — Why LOHC Is the "Ambient-Temperature Alternative"
- No boil-off, no high pressure, no cryogenic equipment.
- Compatible with existing **chemical/paraffin tanker fleet** (double-hull, IMO Type 2).
- Round-trip efficiency **35–40 %** when dehydrogenation heat is not valorised.

## 2.2 The LOHC Cycle — Catalytic Hydrogenation & Dehydrogenation

### Loading (Hydrogenation, Exothermic)
$$\text{Toluene} \;+\; 3\,\text{H}_2 \;\xrightarrow{\text{Ni or Pt, 50–200 °C, 10–50 bar}}\; \text{Methylcyclohexane (MCH)}$$
$$\Delta H_{\text{hyd}} = -205 \;\text{kJ/mol}_{\text{MCH}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -68.3\;\text{kJ/mol}_{\text{H}_2}$$

### Unloading (Dehydrogenation, Endothermic)
$$\text{Methylcyclohexane} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Pt/Al}_2\text{O}_3,\; 300–400\;°\text{C}}\; \text{Toluene} \;+\; 3\,\text{H}_2$$
$$\Delta H_{\text{dehyd}} = +205 \;\text{kJ/mol}_{\text{MCH}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; +68.3\;\text{kJ/mol}_{\text{H}_2}$$

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Hydrogenation (Loading Port):** Gaseous H₂ reacts with the unsaturated aromatic carrier in a fixed-bed reactor; heat must be **removed** to drive equilibrium forward.
2. **Storage & Transport:** The hydrogen-saturated liquid is filled into standard IMO chemical tankers at ambient conditions.
3. **Dehydrogenation (Discharge Port):** The saturated liquid is heated above 250 °C; heat must be **supplied** to break C–H bonds and release H₂.
4. **Carrier Return:** The now-hydrogen-lean aromatic liquid is shipped back to the loading port to repeat the cycle (typically thousands of times).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gravimetric H₂ capacity | $w_H = \dfrac{n \cdot M_{H_2}}{M_{\text{carrier}} + n \cdot M_{H_2}}$ | wt% | n = mol H₂ bound per mol carrier |
| 2 | Volumetric H₂ capacity | $\rho_H = w_H \cdot \rho_{\text{LOHC}}$ | kg H₂ / m³ | Use loaded LOHC density |
| 3 | Energy density of carrier | $E_v = \rho_H \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2}$ | MJ/m³ | LHV(H₂) = 120 MJ/kg |
| 4 | Round-trip efficiency (electrical) | $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{\text{electrolyser}} \cdot \eta_{\text{hyd}} \cdot \eta_{\text{trans}} \cdot \eta_{\text{dehyd}} \cdot \eta_{\text{fuel cell}}$ | % | Typical 22–35 % |
| 5 | Thermal energy for dehydrogenation | $Q_{\text{dehyd}} = n \cdot \Delta H_{\text{dehyd}}$ | kJ | Divides into per-mol-H₂ basis |
| 6 | Tank-to-tank loss | $\text{Loss} = \dfrac{V_{\text{H}_2,\text{input}} - V_{\text{H}_2,\text{output}}}{V_{\text{H}_2,\text{input}}} \times 100$ | % | < 0.1 % for LOHC (vs. 5–10 % LH₂) |
| 7 | Equil. conversion (dehyd.) | $K_p = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}\right)$ | bar | Le Chatelier drives with T↑, P↓ |
| 8 | Boil-off rate (LH₂) | $\text{BOG} \approx 0.1$–$0.3$ | %/day | Insignificant for LOHC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember for KTU exams:** Marks are awarded for *correct units* and for showing the substitution step. Always state **"per mole of H₂ released"** when dividing enthalpies by *n*.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Refinery integration:** LOHC can be dehydrogenated using waste heat from FCC units.
- **Industrial gas logistics:** Replacing tube-trailer H₂ with LOHC trucks reduces pressure-vessel costs.
- **Maritime bunker fuel:** NH₃ and LOHC are the leading candidates to decarbonize the shipping sector itself.
- **Seasonal energy storage:** LOHC is a "long-duration" storage class (>100 hours) with no self-discharge.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gravimetric and Volumetric Hydrogen Capacity of an LOHC

**Statement:** A candidate LOHC is **perhydro-dibenzyltoluene (H18-DBT, C₂₁H₃₈)**, molar mass **M_carrier = 278.5 g/mol**, and the hydrogen-lean form is **H0-DBT (C₂₁H₂₀)**, M_carrier = 272.4 g/mol. Each molecule of H0-DBT binds **n = 9 mol of H₂** reversibly (i.e., 18 H atoms).

### Step 1 — Write the chemical balance

$$\text{C}_{21}\text{H}_{20} \;+\; 9\,\text{H}_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{C}_{21}\text{H}_{38}$$

### Step 2 — Identify molar mass contributions

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{H}_2} &= 2.016 \;\text{g/mol} \\
n \cdot M_{\text{H}_2} &= 9 \times 2.016 = 18.144 \;\text{g/mol} \\
M_{\text{loaded LOHC}} &= M_{\text{H0-DBT}} + n \cdot M_{\text{H}_2} = 272.4 + 18.144 = 290.544 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Gravimetric capacity definition

The gravimetric hydrogen capacity is defined as the mass of stored H₂ divided by the total mass of the loaded LOHC:

$$w_H = \frac{\text{mass of H}_2}{\text{mass of loaded carrier}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the molar values

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_H &= \frac{n \cdot M_{\text{H}_2}}{M_{\text{H0-DBT}} + n \cdot M_{\text{H}_2}} \\
&= \frac{9 \times 2.016}{272.4 + 9 \times 2.016} \\
&= \frac{18.144}{290.544} \\
&= 0.06245
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to percentage

$$w_H \;\approx\; \mathbf{6.25 \; wt\%} \;\;\text{(matches the published 6.2 % value for DBT)}$$

### Step 6 — Volumetric capacity

Given the density of loaded H18-DBT ≈ **0.90 g/cm³** = **900 kg/m³**, the volumetric hydrogen capacity is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_H &= w_H \cdot \rho_{\text{LOHC}} \\
&= 0.06245 \times 900 \\
&= 56.2 \;\text{kg H}_2 / \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Energy density

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_v &= \rho_H \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2} \\
&= 56.2 \;\text{kg/m}^3 \times 120 \;\text{MJ/kg} \\
&= 6{,}744 \;\text{MJ/m}^3 \\
&\approx \mathbf{1{,}873 \;\text{kWh/m}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Insight:** Compressed H₂ at 700 bar reaches only ~**40 kg/m³** (≈ 1,330 kWh/m³), while LH₂ reaches ~**70 kg/m³**. DBT-based LOHC sits between compressed H₂ and LH₂ — a strong compromise for tanker shipping where **mass and volume budgets are both binding**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Per-Mole-H₂ Dehydrogenation Energy of Toluene/MCH

**Given:** The MCH → Toluene + 3 H₂ reaction enthalpy is **ΔH_rxn = +205 kJ/mol_MCH** at 298 K.

### Step 1 — Identify H₂ released per mole of MCH

From stoichiometry, 1 mol of MCH releases **3 mol of H₂**. Hence, the per-mole-H₂ dehydrogenation energy is:

$$\Delta H_{\text{per mol H}_2} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}}{n_{\text{H}_2}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H_{\text{per mol H}_2} &= \frac{+205 \;\text{kJ/mol}_{\text{MCH}}}{3 \;\text{mol}_{\text{H}_2}/\text{mol}_{\text{MCH}}} \\
&= +68.33 \;\text{kJ/mol}_{\text{H}_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to per-kg-H₂ basis (for unit consistency in tankers)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H_{\text{per kg H}_2} &= \frac{68.33 \;\text{kJ/mol}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{kg/mol}} \\
&= 33{,}896 \;\text{kJ/kg} \\
&\approx \mathbf{33.9 \;\text{MJ/kg}_{\text{H}_2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Sanity check via LHV

The lower heating value of H₂ is **120 MJ/kg**, so the dehydrogenation heat is **~28 % of the LHV** of the released H₂. This explains why **waste-heat integration** is critical to making LOHC economical.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tank-to-Tank Energy Comparison, LOHC vs LH₂

**Hypothesis:** A 100,000 m³ tanker (≈ Suezmax size) carries hydrogen either as LH₂ or as H18-DBT. Estimate the mass of H₂ delivered and the energy equivalent.

### Step 1 — LH₂ case

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{tank}} &= 1.0 \times 10^5 \;\text{m}^3 \\
\rho_{\text{LH}_2} &= 70.8 \;\text{kg/m}^3 \\
m_{\text{H}_2}^{LH_2} &= V_{\text{tank}} \times \rho_{\text{LH}_2} = 7.08 \times 10^6 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Accounting for **5 % boil-off** in a 30-day voyage:
$$m_{\text{H}_2,\text{delivered}}^{LH_2} = 0.95 \times 7.08 \times 10^6 = 6.73 \times 10^6 \;\text{kg}$$

### Step 2 — LOHC case (assume 90 % fill of nominal capacity, density 900 kg/m³)

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{LOHC}} &= 0.90 \times 1.0 \times 10^5 \times 900 = 8.1 \times 10^7 \;\text{kg} \\
m_{\text{H}_2}^{LOHC} &= m_{\text{LOHC}} \times w_H = 8.1 \times 10^7 \times 0.0625 = 5.06 \times 10^6 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Energy comparison

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{LH}_2} &= 6.73 \times 10^6 \times 120 = 8.07 \times 10^8 \;\text{MJ} \\
E_{\text{LOHC}} &= 5.06 \times 10^6 \times 120 = 6.07 \times 10^8 \;\text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Ratio

$$\frac{E_{\text{LOHC}}}{E_{\text{LH}_2}} = \frac{6.07 \times 10^8}{8.07 \times 10^8} \approx 0.75 \;\;(75\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A standard LOHC tanker delivers about **75 %** of the hydrogen energy that a purpose-built LH₂ tanker delivers, but uses **conventional chemical-tanker engineering** and has **near-zero boil-off**, drastically reducing the total cost of logistics.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — LOHC Material Selection

| Carrier | wt% H₂ | T_dehyd (°C) | ΔH_dehyd (kJ/mol H₂) | Toxicity | Ref. Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Toluene / MCH | 6.16 | 300–400 | 68.3 | Carcinogen (toluene) | **HySTRA** (Japan) |
| Dibenzyltoluene (DBT) | 6.23 | 250–310 | 65.0 | Low | **Hydrogenious LOHC** (Germany) |
| N-ethylcarbazole (NEC) | 5.79 | 200–250 | 50.4 | Moderate | **Air Products / H2-Logistics** (EU) |
| Bicyclohexyl / Dicyclohexyl | 7.20 | 300 | 67.0 | Low | Research stage |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydrogen Maritime Transport — Decision Flow

The flowchart below shows how the choice of carrier is made based on voyage distance, existing infrastructure, and destination H₂ purity requirements.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrogen Export Required] --> B{Voyage Distance}
    B -->|Short < 1500 km| C[Pipeline or Tube Trailer]
    B -->|Medium 1500-5000 km| D{Existing Tanker Available?}
    B -->|Long > 5000 km| E{Energy Density Priority?}
    D -->|Yes| F[NH3 Tanker]
    D -->|No| G[LOHC Chemical Tanker]
    E -->|Mass & Volume Critical| H[LH2 Membrane Tanker]
    E -->|Ambient Operation| I[LOHC Carrier]
    F --> J[Cracking at Destination]
    G --> K[Dehydrogenation at Destination]
    H --> L[Re-liquefaction On-board]
    I --> M[Pump to Storage Tank]
    J --> N[Pure H2 to User]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 LOHC Functional Architecture — Round-Trip Block Diagram

The diagram below maps the **hydrogenation → shipping → dehydrogenation → return** cycle as functional blocks interacting at port and ship boundaries.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph loadingPort[LOADING PORT]
        EL[Electrolyser] --> HU[Hydrogenation Reactor]
        HU --> HOT[Heat Exchanger Removes Hyd Heat]
        HOT --> TANK1[Storage Tank H18 Carrier]
    end
    TANK1 --> SHIP[Chemical Tanker Ship Ambient Temperature 0.1 percent per day loss]
    SHIP --> TANK2[Storage Tank H18 Carrier at Destination]
    subgraph dischargePort[DISCHARGE PORT]
        TANK2 --> DH[Dehydrogenation Reactor 280 to 310 degree C]
        DH --> HEX2[Heat Exchanger Supplies Dehyd Heat]
        HEX2 --> PSA[Purity H2 to User]
        HEX2 --> RTN[H0 Carrier Return Tank]
    end
    RTN --> SHIP
    HEX2 --> DH
    subgraph wasteHeat[WASTE HEAT SOURCE]
        IND[Industrial Furnace or Solar Thermal] --> HEX2
    end
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — LH₂ vs NH₃ vs LOHC at the Ship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LH2[LH2 TANKER]
        L1[Cryostat -253 C] --> L2[Vacuum MLI Insulation]
        L2 --> L3[Boil-off Recovery Compressor]
    end
    subgraph NH3[NH3 TANKER]
        N1[Pressurized Tank -33 C at 1 atm] --> N2[Refrigeration Loop]
        N2 --> N3[Toxic Gas Sensors]
    end
    subgraph LOHC[LOHC TANKER]
        R1[Ambient Double Hull Tank] --> R2[No Insulation Required]
        R2 --> R3[Standard IMO Type 2]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The LOHC block has the *fewest* active subsystems — no cryogenic compressor, no refrigeration loop, no toxic-gas monitoring. This explains the **lower capex** and **higher safety margin** cited in 2024 KTU module notes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define LOHC. List any two LOHC material pairs and mention one advantage of LOHC over LH₂ for maritime transport.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carrier (LOHC)** is an organic liquid that reversibly binds and releases hydrogen via catalytic hydrogenation and dehydrogenation reactions, allowing hydrogen to be stored and transported at ambient conditions.

- Toluene / Methylcyclohexane (MCH) — used in the Japanese HySTRA project.
- Dibenzyltoluene (DBT) — used by Hydrogenious LOHC Technologies, Germany.

**One advantage over LH₂:** LOHC systems operate at ambient temperature and pressure, eliminating boil-off losses and the need for cryogenic insulation. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for pair, 1 mark for advantage)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]
**Why is liquid hydrogen (LH₂) difficult to transport by ship? Mention two practical reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. LH₂ must be maintained at **−253 °C**, requiring cryogenic double-walled tanks, vacuum-jacketed multi-layer insulation, and on-board re-liquefaction systems. (1.5 marks)
2. Continuous **boil-off gas (BOG) loss** of ~0.1–0.3 %/day occurs, reducing delivered H₂ mass; venting methane-equivalent losses to the atmosphere poses economic and environmental concerns. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — LOHC Deep Dive

> **"Explain the principle of Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC) for hydrogen storage and transportation. With a neat sketch, describe the hydrogenation–dehydrogenation cycle using the toluene / methylcyclohexane (MCH) system. Estimate the dehydrogenation energy per kg of H₂ released and comment on its engineering significance."** [10 + 4 marks; 14 marks total]

#### (a) Principle and Cycle Description — 7 marks

The LOHC concept is based on the **reversible catalytic binding of H₂** to an unsaturated organic liquid. In the toluene / methylcyclohexane (MCH) pair:

- **Loading (Hydrogenation):** Toluene (C₇H₈) reacts with 3 H₂ over a Ni or Pt catalyst at 50–200 °C and 10–50 bar to form **methylcyclohexane (C₇H₁₄)**.
- **Storage and Transport:** MCH is a stable, non-corrosive, ambient-condition liquid, easily shipped in conventional chemical tankers.
- **Unloading (Dehydrogenation):** MCH is heated to 300–400 °C in the presence of Pt/Al₂O₃ catalyst, releasing 3 H₂ per molecule and regenerating toluene.
- **Carrier return:** The hydrogen-lean toluene is shipped back to the loading site for reuse over thousands of cycles.

> Sketch: A two-port block diagram showing the hydrogenation reactor at Port A, the chemical tanker in transit, and the dehydrogenation reactor at Port B, with the toluene return loop.

**Mark distribution:** 2 marks definition, 2 marks hydrogenation, 2 marks dehydrogenation, 1 mark for sketch.

#### (b) Numerical Estimate of Dehydration Energy — 7 marks

**Given:** ΔH_rxn (MCH → Toluene + 3 H₂) = +205 kJ/mol_MCH

**Step 1:** Per mole of H₂
$$\Delta H_{\text{per mol H}_2} = \frac{205}{3} = 68.33 \;\text{kJ/mol H}_2$$

**Step 2:** Per kilogram of H₂
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H_{\text{per kg H}_2} &= \frac{68.33 \times 10^3 \;\text{J/mol}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{kg/mol}} \\
&= 33.9 \times 10^6 \;\text{J/kg H}_2 \\
&= 33.9 \;\text{MJ/kg H}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Engineering significance
- The dehydrogenation heat is **~28 % of the LHV of H₂ (120 MJ/kg)**.
- Heat must be supplied at 300–400 °C; using **industrial waste heat or solar thermal** is essential for economic operation.
- If this heat is **not valorised**, the round-trip efficiency drops below 35 %; with heat integration, it can reach 45–50 %.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Many students write "+205 kJ/mol H₂" instead of per mole of H₂. The reaction enthalpy is **per mole of MCH**, not per mole of H₂. Always divide by *n*. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Sketch requirement:** A diagram with the four blocks (loading → ship → unloading → return) earns 1 full mark. Skipping it costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Catalyst omission:** Naming Pt/Al₂O₃ for dehydrogenation and Ni or Pt for hydrogenation shows depth. Generic "with a catalyst" loses a mark.
> 4. **Heat integration comment:** Without commenting on waste-heat recovery, the "engineering significance" sub-part remains incomplete. (Lose 1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Maritime Hydrogen Economics

> **"Discuss the different forms in which hydrogen is currently transported by ships. Compare LH₂, NH₃, and LOHC in terms of energy density, transport losses, infrastructure compatibility, and round-trip efficiency. Recommend the most suitable carrier for a 20-day, 10 000 km shipping route between Australia and Japan."**

#### (a) Comparative Discussion — 7 marks

| Parameter | LH₂ | NH₃ | LOHC (DBT) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Storage T (°C) | −253 | −33 | Ambient (~20–40) |
| Storage P (bar) | 1–3 | 1–10 | 1 |
| H₂ mass fraction | 100 % | 17.8 % | 6.2 % |
| Volumetric energy density (MJ/L) | ~8.5 | ~12.7 | ~18.0 |
| Boil-off / loss | 0.1–0.3 %/day | ~negligible | < 0.01 %/day |
| Existing fleet | None (purpose-built) | Large (180 Mt/yr) | Chemical tankers (re-usable) |
| On-board equipment | Cryocooler | Refrigeration | Pumps only |
| Round-trip η (electrical) | 30–38 % | 25–35 % | 22–35 % |
| Toxicity | None | High (NH₃) | Low (DBT), Medium (toluene) |
| Destination cracking | None | NH₃ → H₂ + N₂ | DeH₂ at 280–310 °C |

**Comparative discussion points (mark allocation):**

- **LH₂:** Highest purity at destination; cryogenic complexity penalises economics. (1.5 marks)
- **NH₃:** Mature trade routes and re-uses existing fleet; cracking cost is the bottleneck. (2 marks)
- **LOHC:** Best ambient operation, lowest infrastructure cost, but lowest round-trip efficiency due to dehydrogenation energy. (2 marks)
- **Infrastructural reusability** of chemical tankers makes LOHC attractive for newly developed green-trade lanes. (1.5 marks)

#### (b) Recommendation and Justification — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Define the route constraints:** 20-day voyage, 10,000 km Australia–Japan corridor, with Japanese industrial demand for green ammonia and H₂.

**Step 2 — Loss accounting over 20 days:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LH}_2 \text{ loss} &\approx 0.2\%/\text{day} \times 20 = 4\% \text{ (with active reliquefaction, can be cut to 1–2 %)} \\
\text{NH}_3 \text{ loss} &\approx < 0.1\% \text{ total} \\
\text{LOHC loss} &\approx < 0.01\% \text{ total}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Capital expenditure ranking** (per tonne of H₂ delivered, indicative, 2024 LCOH studies):
$$\text{LOHC (lowest)} \;<\; \text{NH}_3 \;<\; \text{LH}_2 \text{ (highest)}$$

**Step 4 — Recommendation:**
For the **Australia–Japan green H₂ corridor**, **NH₃ is currently the most practical** for large-scale roll-out, given:
1. Mature shipping infrastructure and trained crews.
2. Japan already plans to burn NH₃ directly in coal-fired power plants (e.g., JERA's 20 % NH₃ co-firing programme).
3. Lower capex per tonne of H₂ delivered.

However, **LOHC is the most attractive long-term option** (2026+ deployment) because:
- Zero boil-off and no toxic emissions during transit.
- Japanese steelmaker **Nippon Steel** has trialled LOHC for blast-furnace H₂ injection.
- Heat for dehydrogenation can be sourced from the destination steel mill's waste heat.

**Final answer:** **NH₃ for the 2024–2027 transition; LOHC (DBT) for the 2028+ scale-up.**

**Mark distribution:** 2 marks loss calculation, 2 marks capex reasoning, 3 marks justified recommendation with reference to real projects.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks Lost on This Question:**
> 1. **Quantitative loss accounting missing** — many students discuss qualitatively but forget to multiply BOG by voyage days. (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Round-trip efficiency column ignored** — the question specifically asks for it. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Real project reference missing** — citing HySTRA (Japan), JERA, or Hydrogenious LOHC boosts marks. (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **Recommendation must be justified**, not just stated. "LOHC is best" without cost/loss rationale loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LOHC = Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carrier** — a reversible, catalytic H₂-binding organic liquid pair.
- **Two key reactions:** Hydrogenation (exothermic, loading) and Dehydrogenation (endothermic, unloading).
- **Common LOHC systems:** Toluene ↔ MCH (6.16 wt%); Dibenzyltoluene ↔ H18-DBT (6.23 wt%).
- **Operating envelope:** Ambient storage; dehydrogenation at **250–400 °C**; hydrogenation at **50–200 °C, 10–50 bar**.
- **Per-mole-H₂ dehydrogenation energy** for toluene/MCH = **68.3 kJ/mol H₂** = **33.9 MJ/kg H₂** (~28 % of H₂'s LHV).
- **Gravimetric capacity formula:** $w_H = \dfrac{n \cdot M_{H_2}}{M_{\text{carrier}} + n \cdot M_{H_2}}$
- **Maritime comparison:** LH₂ (best purity, worst BOG); NH₃ (most mature fleet, toxic); LOHC (best ambient ops, lowest efficiency).
- **Real-world projects to remember:** HySTRA (Japan, MCH), Hydrogenious LOHC (Germany, DBT), JERA (Japan, NH₃ co-firing), Suiso Frontier (Japan, LH₂ carrier).
- **Boil-off rate:** LH₂ ≈ 0.1–0.3 %/day; LOHC ≈ negligible.
- **Energy density of LOHC (DBT):** ~6,700 MJ/m³; ~1,870 kWh/m³.
- **Round-trip electrical efficiency:** 22–35 % for LOHC (improves to 45 %+ with waste-heat integration).
- **Safety advantage:** LOHC is non-explosive, non-cryogenic, and storable at room temperature — the safest bulk H₂ transport modality currently demonstrated.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Transported as Ammonia
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Transported as Ammonia

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Hydrogen Transported as Ammonia

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Transported as Ammonia (NH₃ Carrier Pathway):** A chemical hydrogen-carrier strategy in which gaseous hydrogen (H₂) is first chemically bound with nitrogen (N₂) to form ammonia (NH₃) via the Haber–Bosch synthesis reaction, then transported in liquid form (boiling point **−33 °C at 1 atm**), and finally "cracked" back into high-purity hydrogen at the point of use through catalytic decomposition of ammonia.

The full hydrogen lifecycle when transported as ammonia is:
$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{N}_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; \tfrac{1}{3}\text{NH}_3$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of ammonia as a "hydrogen lunchbox."**
> Pure hydrogen gas is like a fragile, low-density foam — bulky, hard to pack, and impossible to ship cheaply over long distances. Ammonia is like a sturdy, dense lunchbox that **locks the hydrogen inside a stable molecule**. You can stack thousands of these lunchboxes into a refrigerated truck, sail them across an ocean, and only when you reach your destination do you "open the lunchbox" (catalytic cracking) to release the original hydrogen fuel.

**Why ammonia instead of liquid H₂ directly?**

| Carrier | Boiling Point | Energy to Liquefy |
|---|---|---|
| Liquid H₂ | −253 °C | ~30–40 % of LHV lost |
| Liquid NH₃ | −33 °C | ~10–15 % of stored energy lost |

Because NH₃ condenses at a much higher (and cheaper) temperature, the existing **LPG / propane / butane logistics chain** (tankers, bullets, storage spheres, port terminals) can be largely reused.

### 1.3 Salient Physical Constants

- **Molar mass of NH₃ = 17.031 g mol⁻¹**
- **Density of liquid NH₃ at −33 °C = 681 kg m⁻³**
- **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ kg⁻¹**
- **Hydrogen content (mass fraction) in NH₃ ≈ 17.65 wt %**
- **Volumetric H₂ density in liquid NH₃ ≈ 120 kg H₂ m⁻³** (vs 70.8 kg m⁻³ in liquid H₂)

> [!TIP]
> Ammonia carries **more hydrogen per cubic metre than even liquid hydrogen itself**, because NH₃ molecules pack tightly in the liquid phase.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Ammonia? The Thermodynamic Logic Chain

1. **High hydrogen mass loading.** Three H atoms are packed into every 17 g of NH₃ — far denser than compressed-gas cylinders.
2. **Mild cryogenic requirement.** Condensation at **−33 °C** (vs −253 °C for H₂) drastically cuts liquefaction CAPEX and boil-off losses.
3. **Existing global ammonia infrastructure.** ~180 Mt of NH₃ is already shipped annually for fertilizer use (Haber-Bosch plants in every continent, ~120 ports handling NH₃).
4. **Carbon-free carrier.** Ammonia carries no carbon, so the cracking step produces **only N₂ + H₂** — no CO₂ emissions.
5. **Long-duration storage possible.** Liquid NH₃ can be stored for months in atmospheric tanks with acceptable boil-off.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Reaction Logic

### Step A — Ammonia Synthesis (Forward Direction)
$$\text{N}_2(g) + 3\,\text{H}_2(g) \;\rightleftharpoons\; 2\,\text{NH}_3(g) \qquad \Delta H_{298\text{ K}} = -92.4\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

- Exothermic — favoured by **low temperature** and **high pressure**.
- Industrially operated at **400–500 °C** and **150–300 bar** over an iron-based catalyst.
- Conversion per pass is only 15–20 %, so the gas is recycled.

### Step B — Ammonia Decomposition / Cracking (Reverse Direction)
$$2\,\text{NH}_3(g) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{N}_2(g) + 3\,\text{H}_2(g) \qquad \Delta H_{298\text{ K}} = +46.2\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

- Endothermic — needs continuous heat input (often waste heat or catalytic burners).
- Operated at **600–900 °C** over Ru, Ni, or Co catalysts.
- A small residual NH₃ slip (typically **< 200 ppm**) must be removed before the H₂ enters a fuel cell.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory equations for solving KTU numerical questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Symbolic Form | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrogen mass fraction in NH₃ | $w_{H_2} = \dfrac{3 \cdot M_{H}}{M_{NH_3}} = \dfrac{3 \times 1.008}{17.031}$ | **17.76 wt %** (often quoted as 17.65 %) |
| 2 | Density of liquid NH₃ | $\rho_{NH_3,\ell}$ | **681 kg m⁻³** at −33 °C |
| 3 | LHV of H₂ | $LHV_{H_2}$ | **120 MJ kg⁻¹** |
| 4 | H₂ energy per kg NH₃ | $E_{NH_3 \to H_2} = w_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2}$ | ≈ **21.3 MJ kg⁻¹ NH₃** |
| 5 | Volumetric H₂ energy in liquid NH₃ | $E_V = \rho_{NH_3,\ell} \cdot w_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2}$ | ≈ **14 425 MJ m⁻³** |
| 6 | Liquefaction energy (NH₃) | $W_{liq,NH_3}$ | **~0.37 MJ kg⁻¹** (≈ 1.7 % of LHV of carried H₂) |
| 7 | Cracking conversion efficiency | $\eta_{crack} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2,\text{out}}/3}{\dot{n}_{NH_3,\text{in}}/2}$ | **90 – 99 %** |
| 8 | Residual NH₃ slip for fuel-cell grade | $x_{NH_3,slip}$ | **< 0.2 ppm** (PEMFC); < 200 ppm (industrial) |
| 9 | Boiling point of NH₃ at 1 atm | $T_{bp,NH_3}$ | **−33.34 °C** |
| 10 | Critical point of NH₃ | $T_c, P_c$ | **132.4 °C, 113.3 bar** |

> **Engineering utility:** These equations let the designer size the **mass of NH₃ required for a target H₂ delivery**, the **storage tank volume**, and the **cracker reactor duty** (heat input required).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Japan's "Hydrogen Society" roadmap:** imports green H₂ from Australia as NH₃, cracks it at receiving ports.
- **Saudi Arabia → Europe shipping trials (2022 onwards):** SABIC and Aramco testing NH₃ carriers on VLGC-class vessels.
- **Marine fuel:** Ammonia itself is being adopted as a **carbon-free bunker fuel** (dual-fuel engines from MAN Energy Solutions, Wärtsilä).
- **Fertilizer ↔ Energy bridge:** Existing 200 Mt NH₃/y infrastructure is the foundation of a global hydrogen economy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive derivation policy:** Every algebraic transition is shown in full. No shortcuts, no skipped lines.

## 3.1 Derivation — Hydrogen Mass Fraction in Ammonia

**Given:** Molar masses — $M_H = 1.008\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$, $M_N = 14.007\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$.

**Step 1 — Write the molecular formula and total molar mass of NH₃.**

$$M_{NH_3} = M_N + 3 \cdot M_H$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{NH_3} &= 14.007 + 3 \times 1.008 \\[4pt]
         &= 14.007 + 3.024 \\[4pt]
         &= 17.031\ \text{g mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of hydrogen in one mole of NH₃.**

$$m_H = 3 \times M_H = 3 \times 1.008 = 3.024\ \text{g}$$

**Step 4 — Mass fraction of hydrogen.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{H_2}^{NH_3} &= \dfrac{m_H}{M_{NH_3}} \\[6pt]
               &= \dfrac{3.024}{17.031} \\[6pt]
               &= 0.1776 \\[4pt]
               &\approx 17.76\ \text{wt \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **single most cited number in KTU viva questions** on ammonia-as-carrier.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Volumetric Hydrogen Energy Density of Liquid Ammonia

**Goal:** Compute the usable H₂ energy stored in **1 m³ of liquid NH₃**.

**Step 1 — Mass of 1 m³ of liquid NH₃.**

$$m_{NH_3} = \rho_{NH_3,\ell} \times V = 681\ \text{kg m}^{-3} \times 1\ \text{m}^{3} = 681\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of H₂ that can be released after complete cracking.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= m_{NH_3} \times w_{H_2}^{NH_3} \\[4pt]
        &= 681 \times 0.1776 \\[4pt]
        &= 120.93\ \text{kg H}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy content at LHV of H₂.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_V &= m_{H_2} \times LHV_{H_2} \\[4pt]
    &= 120.93 \times 120\ \text{MJ kg}^{-1} \\[4pt]
    &= 14\,512\ \text{MJ m}^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with pure liquid H₂.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{V,\ell H_2} &= \rho_{\ell H_2} \times LHV_{H_2} \\
               &= 70.8 \times 120 \\
               &= 8\,496\ \text{MJ m}^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Ratio (ammonia advantage).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{E_{V,NH_3}}{E_{V,\ell H_2}} &= \dfrac{14\,512}{8\,496} \\
                                    &\approx 1.71
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Liquid NH₃ stores ~1.71× the energy per unit volume compared to liquid H₂.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Cracking Reactor Heat Duty

The decomposition reaction:

$$2\,\text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow \text{N}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2 \qquad \Delta H = +92.4\ \text{kJ per 2 mol NH}_3$$

**Per kg NH₃ fed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{crack} &= \dfrac{\Delta H / 2}{M_{NH_3}} \\[4pt]
          &= \dfrac{92.4 / 2}{17.031 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg}} \\[4pt]
          &= \dfrac{46.2}{0.017031} \\[4pt]
          &= 2\,712.9\ \text{kJ kg}^{-1}\ \text{NH}_3 \\[4pt]
          &\approx 2.71\ \text{MJ kg}^{-1}\ \text{NH}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

> Roughly **2.71 MJ of heat** must be supplied to crack **every kilogram of NH₃**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Score-Card of H₂ Carriers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this matrix** — it is a frequent KTU Part B (14-mark) question.

| Property | Compressed H₂ (700 bar) | Liquid H₂ (LH₂) | Ammonia (NH₃) | LOHC (e.g. Dibenzyltoluene) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier mass density (kg H₂ per 100 kg system) | ~3–4 | ~8–10 | **17.6** | 6–7 |
| Boiling / operating temperature | 25 °C | −253 °C | **−33 °C** | 25–300 °C |
| Liquefaction / charging energy | low | **30–40 % of LHV** | **~1.7 % of LHV** | moderate |
| Volumetric H₂ density (kg H₂ m⁻³ system) | ~40 | 70.8 | **120** | ~55 |
| Toxicity | none | none | **HIGH** | low |
| Existing infra reuse | partial | new build | **YES (LPG-like)** | partial |
| Cracking / release step required | no | no | **YES (~2.7 MJ/kg)** | YES (~moderate) |
| CO₂ in release products | none | none | **none** | none |
| Suitability for PEMFC | direct | direct | needs polishing | direct after release |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A fuel-cell bus fleet needs **50 kg of H₂ per day**. The hydrogen is to be delivered as liquid NH₃. Find:
> (a) the mass of NH₃ to be shipped,
> (b) the volume of the transport tank,
> (c) the heat required at the cracking station.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mass of NH₃.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{NH_3} &= \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{w_{H_2}^{NH_3}} \\[4pt]
         &= \dfrac{50}{0.1776} \\[4pt]
         &= 281.5\ \text{kg NH}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Tank volume.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{NH_3} &= \dfrac{m_{NH_3}}{\rho_{NH_3,\ell}} \\[4pt]
         &= \dfrac{281.5}{681} \\[4pt]
         &= 0.413\ \text{m}^{3} \;\;(\approx\; 413\ \text{L})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Cracking heat duty.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= m_{NH_3} \times 2.71\ \text{MJ kg}^{-1} \\[4pt]
  &= 281.5 \times 2.71 \\[4pt]
  &= 762.9\ \text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This heat is often supplied by **burning a slip-stream of the produced H₂** (~3–5 %), or by **industrial waste heat**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydrogen-as-Ammonia End-to-End Supply Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Green H2 from Electrolyser] --> B[Haber-Bosch Synthesis Reactor\n400-500 C, 150-300 bar]
    N[Air Separation Unit / N2 Supply] --> B
    B --> C[NH3 Liquefaction\n-33 C, 1 atm]
    C --> D[Ship / Rail / Pipeline Transport\nLPG-class bulk carriers]
    D --> E[Atmospheric Storage Sphere\nat Import Terminal]
    E --> F[NH3 Cracker Reactor\n600-900 C, Ru/Ni Catalyst]
    F --> G[Purification Unit\nNH3 slip removal]
    G --> H[PEMFC / Gas Turbine / Industrial Use]
    H --> I[H2O + N2 byproduct\nno CO2]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture of a Cracking Station

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph input [Input Side]
        P1[Liquid NH3 Pump]
        P2[Vaporizer]
        P3[Superheater]
    end

    subgraph reactor [Cracking Core]
        R1[Pre-heater\n600 C]
        R2[Catalytic Reactor\nRu on Al2O3]
        R3[Heat Exchanger\nrecovers waste heat]
    end

    subgraph output [Purification and Delivery]
        Q1[PSA / Membrane H2 Separator]
        Q2[NH3 Slip Adsorber]
        Q3[Compressed H2 Storage Buffer]
    end

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3
    R3 -.waste heat.-> R1
```

## 4.3 Mass-Flow Logic Per Tonne of Liquid Ammonia

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[1000 kg Liquid NH3] --> M2[H atoms locked: 177.6 kg]
    M1 --> M3[N atoms: 822.4 kg]
    M2 --> M4[After Cracking: 177.6 kg H2]
    M2 --> M5[Released: 822.4 kg N2]
    M4 --> M6[Energy = 177.6 x 120 = 21 312 MJ]
    M5 --> M7[Vent or capture for re-synthesis]
```

> **Reading aid:** Every **1 tonne** of liquid NH₃ releases **~21.3 GJ** of hydrogen energy and **~822 kg** of nitrogen (which can be recycled back to the Haber-Bosch front-end, closing the loop).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain in 5 lines why ammonia is considered a promising hydrogen carrier.**

**Model Answer:**
> Ammonia (NH₃) contains **17.65 wt % hydrogen**, has a low boiling point of **−33 °C** that allows cheap liquefaction, exhibits **higher volumetric H₂ density (≈ 120 kg/m³) than liquid H₂ itself**, can be transported using existing LPG-class bulk infrastructure, and releases only **N₂ and H₂** on catalytic cracking — making it a carbon-free, dense, and reusable hydrogen carrier.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Write the balanced equations for ammonia synthesis and decomposition.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Synthesis (Haber–Bosch):} \quad \text{N}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2 &\rightleftharpoons 2\,\text{NH}_3 \quad \Delta H = -92.4\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1} \\[4pt]
\text{Decomposition (Cracking):} \quad 2\,\text{NH}_3 &\rightleftharpoons \text{N}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +46.2\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme).** Students must answer **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With neat flow diagrams, explain the **production of green ammonia** from renewable electricity, covering electrolysis, air separation, and Haber–Bosch synthesis. List the operating conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **catalytic cracking of ammonia** to recover high-purity hydrogen. Discuss catalyst choices, operating temperature range, and the residual NH₃ slip issue for PEMFC application. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Renewable electricity** (solar PV / wind) feeds a **PEM or alkaline electrolyser** to produce green H₂ at 30–35 bar.
2. A **cryogenic / PSA air separation unit (ASU)** produces high-purity N₂.
3. The H₂ + N₂ mixture (1 : 3 N₂ : H₂ stoichiometry, but recycled to 1 : 3) is compressed to **150–300 bar**, mixed with recycle gas, and sent to a **Haber–Bosch loop** containing:
   - **Feed–effluent heat exchanger**
   - **Reformer / converter** with **iron-promoted catalyst** at **400–500 °C**
   - **Chiller / condenser** to liquefy NH₃
   - **Recycle compressor**
4. Liquid NH₃ is drawn off and stored at **−33 °C**.
5. Single-pass conversion ≈ **15–20 %**; overall loop conversion > **95 %**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. **Catalyst choices:** Ru on Al₂O₃ (highest activity, expensive), Ni on Al₂O₃ (cheaper, industrial standard), Co or Mo₂N for niche work.
2. **Operating temperature:** **600–900 °C**; higher T → higher conversion but more energy penalty.
3. **Reactor types:** fixed-bed, monolith, or membrane reactor (Pd-based) for in-situ H₂ separation.
4. **Residual NH₃ slip:** Un-cracked NH₃ poisons the Pt catalyst of a PEMFC. Acceptable level: **< 0.2 ppm** for fuel cells.
5. **Purification methods:** **PSA (Pressure Swing Adsorption)**, **membrane separation**, or **catalytic afterburner** to decompose residual NH₃.
6. **Heat integration:** Cracking endotherm (~2.7 MJ kg⁻¹ NH₃) often supplied by burning 3–5 % slipstream of produced H₂.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the **mass fraction of hydrogen in ammonia** and compute the **energy stored per cubic metre** of liquid NH₃ in MJ. Compare with liquid hydrogen. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **safety, storage, and transportation challenges** of liquid ammonia as a hydrogen carrier and compare its infrastructure readiness with liquid hydrogen. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Derivation (full steps as in §3.1 and §3.2 above):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{H_2}^{NH_3} &= \dfrac{3 \times 1.008}{17.031} = 0.1776 \approx 17.76\ \text{wt \%} \\[6pt]
m_{NH_3}\ \text{per m}^3 &= 681\ \text{kg} \\[6pt]
m_{H_2}\ \text{per m}^3 &= 681 \times 0.1776 = 120.9\ \text{kg} \\[6pt]
E_V &= 120.9 \times 120 = 14\,512\ \text{MJ m}^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** Liquid H₂ gives only 8 496 MJ m⁻³ → liquid NH₃ is **1.71× better** in volumetric energy density.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Aspect | Liquid NH₃ | Liquid H₂ |
|---|---|---|
| Toxicity | **Toxic (IDLH = 300 ppm)** — main hazard | Non-toxic, but asphyxiant |
| Storage temp | −33 °C → mild cryogenic, low boil-off | −253 °C → severe cryogenic, 0.1 %/day boil-off |
| Tank material | Carbon steel (NH₃ is dry) | Multi-layer vacuum super-insulation |
| Infrastructure readiness | **Reuses LPG chain** (tankers, bullets, ports) | Requires purpose-built cryogenic vessels |
| Public acceptance | Low (odour threshold 5 ppm is detectable, but toxic above 25 ppm) | Higher (only flammability risk) |
| Energy lost in liquefaction | ~1.7 % of carried H₂ energy | ~30 % of carried H₂ energy |
| Overall safety cost | High (must monitor leaks, scrubbers needed) | Moderate |

**Conclusion:** NH₃ is **infrastructure-ready but safety-demanding**; LH₂ is **safety-friendly but energy-expensive**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Using 17.65 % vs 17.76 %** interchangeably — KTU examiners mark exact value mismatches. Use **17.76 wt %** for full credit (from atomic masses 14.007 + 3×1.008 = 17.031).
> 2. **Forgetting the cracking heat duty** — quoting energy density without accounting for the **+46.2 kJ mol⁻¹** endothermic penalty.
> 3. **Confusing boiling point and operating temperature** of storage. NH₃ storage is **at or below −33 °C**, not at ambient.
> 4. **Ignoring NH₃ slip** in PEMFC applications — examiners expect a statement on purification requirements.
> 5. **Forgetting to balance recycle stoichiometry** — the Haber–Bosch feed is 3 H₂ : 1 N₂, but plant operation uses a recycle loop; mis-stating this loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision checklist for the KTU exam hall:**

- ☐ NH₃ = 17.031 g mol⁻¹; hydrogen mass fraction ≈ **17.76 wt %**.
- ☐ Boiling point of NH₃ at 1 atm = **−33.34 °C**.
- ☐ Density of liquid NH₃ = **681 kg m⁻³**.
- ☐ LHV of H₂ = **120 MJ kg⁻¹**; energy per kg NH₃ after cracking ≈ **21.3 MJ**.
- ☐ **Volumetric H₂ energy in liquid NH₃ ≈ 14 500 MJ m⁻³**, beating LH₂ (~8 500 MJ m⁻³).
- ☐ **Synthesis:** N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃, **ΔH = −92.4 kJ mol⁻¹**, 400–500 °C, 150–300 bar, Fe catalyst.
- ☐ **Cracking:** 2NH₃ → N₂ + 3H₂, **ΔH = +46.2 kJ mol⁻¹**, 600–900 °C, Ru/Ni catalyst.
- ☐ **Cracking heat duty ≈ 2.71 MJ per kg NH₃**.
- ☐ **NH₃ slip** to PEMFC must be **< 0.2 ppm** → PSA / membrane purification needed.
- ☐ Main hazards: **toxicity (IDLH 300 ppm)**, corrosivity to copper alloys, water pollution.
- ☐ Existing **180 Mt/yr NH₃ logistics** is the strongest argument for the ammonia pathway.
- ☐ Ammonia is also emerging as a **direct marine fuel** (zero-carbon shipping).
- ☐ For 1 tonne NH₃: yields ≈ **177.6 kg H₂** (energy = 21.3 GJ) + **822.4 kg N₂** (recyclable).
- ☐ Compare and contrast with **LOHC** and **liquid H₂** — examiners love comparison tables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquefied Hydrogen (LH2) Transportation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Liquefied Hydrogen (LH2) Transportation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: LIQUEFIED HYDROGEN (LH2) TRANSPORTATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liquefied Hydrogen (LH2) Transportation** is defined as the cryogenic bulk transport of molecular hydrogen (H₂) in its liquid state at a temperature of **−253 °C (20.28 K)** and near-atmospheric pressure, using specially engineered double-walled, super-insulated cryogenic vessels that conform to standards such as **ISO 13985**, **ASME BPVC Section VIII**, and the **IGC Code (International Code for the Construction and Equipment of Ships Carrying Liquefied Gases in Bulk)** for marine transport.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, LH2 transport is treated as the **high-energy-density, long-distance, large-volume delivery pathway** of the hydrogen value chain, sitting between the gaseous distribution (tube trailers, pipelines) and emerging solid/chemical carriers (LOHCs, NH₃, metal hydrides).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Thermos Flask on Wheels":**
> Think of LH2 transportation exactly like carrying a giant **Dewar flask (thermos bottle)** mounted on a truck or a ship. Inside this flask, hydrogen has been cooled to such an extreme low temperature that it condenses into a liquid — much like water vapour condenses into water droplets on a cold windowpane. The challenge is that the *outside world* is *273 °C warmer* than the liquid inside, so the flask is constructed like a Russian doll — an inner vessel, a vacuum gap (to stop heat conduction by gas), and layers of super-insulation (to stop radiation), all wrapped in an outer vessel. Even with all this engineering, a tiny bit of hydrogen continuously evaporates — called **"Boil-Off Gas" (BOG)** — and must be safely vented, reliquefied, or burned off.

### 1.3 Why LH2 Transport Matters for the Hydrogen Economy

- **Energy density advantage:** Liquid H₂ has a volumetric energy density of **~8.5 MJ/L**, about **2.6× higher** than 700 bar compressed gaseous H₂ (~2.7 MJ/L).
- **Long-haul economics:** For distances exceeding **~1,500 km** and volumes above **~50 tonnes/day**, LH2 shipping becomes more cost-effective than gaseous or pipeline transport.
- **Current global trade flow:** Liquid H₂ is already shipped commercially in trailers (5,000–40,000 L) and bulk ISO containers.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Physical Constants (KTU High-Yield)

| Constant | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Boiling point of H₂ at 1 atm | **20.28 K (−252.87 °C)** | K |
| Critical temperature (T_c) | **33.19 K** | K |
| Critical pressure (P_c) | **1.297 MPa (12.97 bar)** | MPa |
| Liquid density at NBP | **70.8 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| Gas density at STP | **0.0899 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| Volumetric expansion ratio (liquid → gas, 1 atm, 20 °C) | **1 : 845** | — |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **120 MJ/kg** | MJ/kg |
| Specific heat of vaporization | **445.6 kJ/kg** | kJ/kg |
| Para-ortho conversion enthalpy release | **527 kJ/kg** | kJ/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Always remember the **20.28 K** boiling point and the **845× expansion ratio** — these two numbers alone can fetch 2 marks in a Part A question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Cryogenic Chain

The end-to-end LH2 transport process is a **four-stage cryogenic chain**:

1. **On-site Liquefaction:** Hydrogen is cooled in multi-stage Claude or Brayton cycles (using He/N₂ as the secondary refrigerant) from ambient to 20 K, and condensed at ~1–2 bar.
2. **Storage at Loading Terminal:** LH2 is held in large cryogenic bulk tanks (typically 1,000–100,000 m³) at the production/port facility.
3. **Loading & Transport:** LH2 is gravity or pump-fed into road tankers, rail wagons, ISO tank containers, or marine bunker vessels, with a typical loading mass of **4,000 kg** (road) to **>10,000 tonnes** (ship).
4. **Receiving, Regasification & Delivery:** At the destination, LH2 is either vaporised (for direct pipeline/gaseous use) or transferred to end-user storage.

### 2.2 The Two Core Thermodynamic Challenges

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Challenge 1 — Heat In-leak (Q̇_in):**
> Even with multi-layer insulation (MLI) and vacuum jackets, a finite heat flux **q (W/m²)** penetrates the inner vessel wall. This heat continuously vaporises the LH2 — the rate is called the **Boil-Off Rate (BOR)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Challenge 2 — Para-ortho Conversion:**
> Normal hydrogen is 75% *ortho* and 25% *para* at room temperature, but the **equilibrium concentration at 20 K is ~99.8% para-hydrogen**. Conversion from ortho → para is **exothermic**, releasing **527 kJ/kg** of heat — this is internally generated and accelerates boil-off during the first 2–3 days after liquefaction if not catalytically converted beforehand.

### 2.3 Design of the LH2 Transport Vessel

A modern LH2 transport vessel is a **multi-layered cryogenic sandwich**:

| Layer (from inside → outside) | Function | Typical Material / Spec |
|---|---|---|
| **Inner vessel (primary)** | Holds liquid H₂ at 20 K | Austenitic stainless steel **304L / 316L** (low magnetic permeability) |
| **Inner support system** | Holds inner vessel concentric | Pultruded glass-fibre/epoxy struts (low thermal conductivity ~0.3 W/m·K) |
| **Vacuum jacket** | Eliminates convective gas conduction | Vacuum at **< 10⁻³ Pa (10⁻⁵ mbar)** |
| **Multi-Layer Insulation (MLI)** | Suppresses radiation heat transfer | 30–60 layers of aluminised Mylar® interleaved with nylon netting, **effective emissivity ε ≈ 0.01–0.05** |
| **Outer vessel (secondary)** | Holds vacuum + structural load | Carbon steel (typically SA-516) |
| **Pressure relief / BOG piping** | Safely vents evaporated H₂ | Burst disc + dual PRV set at 1.05 × MAWP |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6 essential equations for LH2 transport problems:**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{BOG} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{in}}{h_{fg}}$ | Boil-off mass flow from heat leak | kg/s |
| 2 | $\dot{Q}_{in} = (U \cdot A) \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat in-leak by overall heat transfer | W |
| 3 | $U \approx \dfrac{1}{\frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{\delta}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{1}{h_{rad}}}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient (vacuum + MLI) | W/m²·K |
| 4 | $h_{rad} = \sigma \cdot \dfrac{(T_h^4 - T_c^4)}{\Delta T} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_i} + \frac{A_i}{A_o}\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_o} - 1\right)}$ | Radiative heat transfer between concentric cylinders | W/m²·K |
| 5 | $BOR(\%/day) = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{BOG}}{M_{LH2}} \times \dfrac{86400}{1} \times 100$ | Daily boil-off rate as % of stored mass | %/day |
| 6 | $\eta_{exergy} = 1 - \dfrac{T_0}{T_{LH2}} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{T_0}{T_{LH2}}\right) - \dfrac{s_{LH2} - s_0}{R}$ | Exergy (available work) of LH2 vs. ambient | — |

> **Critical substitution rule (KTU Pitfall!):** When $\Delta T$ is large (e.g., 293 K), the linearised $U A \Delta T$ slightly over-estimates $\dot{Q}_{in}$. The exam-grade accurate form is $q_{rad} = \sigma (T_o^4 - T_i^4) \cdot \text{view factor}$, but for Part A, $q = U A \Delta T$ is acceptable.

### 2.5 Transportation Modes — Comparative Logic

| Mode | Typical Capacity | Use Case | Capital Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Road cryogenic tanker** | 4–5 tonnes LH₂ | Urban/regional, last-mile | Moderate |
| **Rail tank car** | 50–100 tonnes LH₂ | Continental long-distance | High (track access) |
| **ISO Tank Container (40 ft)** | ~2.8 tonnes LH₂ | Intermodal (road + sea + road) | Moderate |
| **Marine LH2 carrier** | 10,000–100,000+ m³ | Trans-oceanic, intercontinental | Very high |
| **Pipeline (cryogenic)** | Continuous | Fixed-route (rare for H₂) | Very high |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Kawasaki Heavy Industries (Suiso Frontier, 2022):** World's first liquid H₂ carrier ship — 1,250 m³ capacity, Australia → Japan.
- **NASA & ISRO cryogenic stage technology:** Direct spillover of MLI design, vacuum-jacketed dewars, and ortho-para conversion catalysts from rocket technology into commercial LH2 logistics.
- **Airbus ZEROe roadmap:** LH2 as the reference cryofuel for hydrogen aircraft — drives the need for airport LH2 bunkering infrastructure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Boil-Off Rate (BOR) of an LH2 Road Tanker

> **Problem statement (KTU textbook-style):**
> An LH2 road tanker carries **M = 4,000 kg** of liquid hydrogen. The cryogenic vessel has an **effective heat-leak area A = 60 m²** and an **overall heat transfer coefficient U = 0.5 W/m²·K** (with MLI + vacuum). The ambient temperature is **T₀ = 298 K** and the LH2 temperature is **T_LH2 = 20 K**. Take h_fg = 446 kJ/kg. Calculate:
> (a) The steady-state heat in-leak in Watts.
> (b) The boil-off mass flow rate in kg/hr.
> (c) The % BOR per day.
> (d) The fraction of stored H₂ lost if transit takes 5 days with no reliquefaction.

**Step (a) — Heat in-leak**

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = (0.5\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}) \times (60\ \text{m}^2) \times (298 - 20)\ \text{K}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = 0.5 \times 60 \times 278
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = 8{,}340\ \text{W} = 8.34\ \text{kW}
$$

> The dominant thermal load is the **conduction through support struts and pipe penetrations**, not the MLI itself (which limits radiation to <2 W/m²).

**Step (b) — Boil-off mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{m}_{BOG} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{in}}{h_{fg}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{BOG} = \frac{8{,}340\ \text{W}}{446{,}000\ \text{J/kg}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{BOG} = 0.01870\ \text{kg/s}
$$

Converting to per hour:

$$
\dot{m}_{BOG} = 0.01870 \times 3{,}600
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{BOG} = 67.32\ \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Step (c) — % BOR per day**

$$
BOR = \frac{\dot{m}_{BOG} \times 24}{M_{LH2}} \times 100
$$

$$
BOR = \frac{67.32 \times 24}{4{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
BOR = \frac{1{,}615.68}{4{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
BOR \approx 40.4\ \%/\text{day}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's trap:** 40%/day is unrealistically high for a *modern* well-designed vessel. Real-world figures are **0.1–0.3 %/day**. The problem above intentionally uses a poor U-value. In exam answers, **always state the realistic 0.1–0.3 %/day figure** and discuss *why* MLI + vacuum is essential to achieve it.

**Step (d) — 5-day transit loss**

$$
M_{lost} = M_{LH2} \times \left(1 - e^{-\frac{BOR}{100} \times t}\right) \quad \text{(continuous-decay model)}
$$

Or simply linearly approximated for short transits:

$$
M_{lost} \approx M_{LH2} \times \frac{BOR}{100} \times t
$$

$$
M_{lost} \approx 4{,}000 \times 0.404 \times 5
$$

$$
M_{lost} \approx 8{,}080\ \text{kg (i.e., more than the entire load!)}
$$

This immediately shows why a low U-value is non-negotiable. With the realistic U = 0.02 W/m²·K, the same calculation gives:

$$
\dot{Q}_{in}^{realistic} = 0.02 \times 60 \times 278 = 333.6\ \text{W}
$$

$$
BOR^{realistic} = \frac{333.6 / 446{,}000 \times 86400}{4{,}000} \times 100 \approx 1.6\ \%/\text{day}
$$

> *Note:* The very best modern LH2 tankers (e.g., NASA-grade or Hydrogenious LOHC vessels) reach **0.05–0.1 %/day**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Exergy (Available Work) of LH2

> **Why this matters:** For the KTU Part B question, you may be asked to justify why LH2 is thermodynamically "richer" than ambient H₂.

The **specific exergy of a cryogenic liquid** at temperature $T_{LH2}$ relative to ambient $T_0$ is:

$$
e_{cryo} = (h_0 - h_{LH2}) - T_0 (s_0 - s_{LH2})
$$

For LH2 at 20 K vs. ambient H₂ at 298 K, the term $(h_0 - h_{LH2})$ is large (cooling demand ≈ 4,000 kJ/kg), so the **exergy penalty of liquefaction** is on the order of **10.5 MJ/kg** of LH2.

> **Implication for transport:** Roughly **8–12 kWh/kg** of energy is "embedded" in the cryogenic state of LH2 — *this is energy that must be re-supplied at the destination* if the H₂ is regasified and used as a gas. This is a hidden cost driver in the hydrogen economy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — LH2 Transport Vessel (Concentric Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[LH2 Cargo Space at 20 K] --> B[Inner Vessel 304L SS]
```
