## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** | **Q1. Define Liquefied Hydrogen (LH2). Why is it stored and transported at 20 K? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Liquefied Hydrogen (LH2) is molecular hydrogen in its condensed liquid phase, existing at a temperature of **20.28 K (−252.87 °C)** and near-atmospheric pressure (~1 bar). It is stored and transported at this temperature because:
1. **Boiling point:** 20.28 K is the saturation temperature of H₂ at 1 atm, the lowest practical temperature achievable with industrial He/N₂ cascade refrigeration.
2. **Volumetric density:** At 70.8 kg/m³, LH2 has ~800× higher density than gaseous H₂, making bulk transport economically feasible.
3. **Avoidance of compression losses:** Liquefaction avoids the very high pressures (350–700 bar) needed for gaseous transport, eliminating compressor work and gas compression hazards.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** | **Q2. What is Boil-Off Gas (BOG) in LH2 transport? State two methods to manage it. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Boil-Off Gas (BOG) is the hydrogen vapour generated continuously inside an LH2 transport vessel due to unavoidable heat in-leak from the surroundings through the vessel wall, pipe penetrations, and support struts, and additionally due to the exothermic ortho → para hydrogen conversion.

**Two methods to manage BOG:**
1. **Vent / flare:** Release the BOG safely through a vent stack and burn it — simplest method, but loses hydrogen.
2. **On-board reliquefaction:** Use a small cryocooler (Gifford-McMahon/Joule-Thomson) to re-condense BOG back to liquid — energy-intensive but enables zero-loss long transit.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a double-walled vacuum-insulated LH2 road tanker. Discuss the role of Multi-Layer Insulation (MLI) and the ortho-para conversion catalyst. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A double-walled vacuum-insulated LH2 road tanker is a cryogenic dewar optimised for mobile transport of liquid hydrogen. Its construction includes the following components:

1. **Inner vessel (primary container):** Made of austenitic stainless steel (304L/316L) which retains good toughness at 20 K. It directly holds the LH2 at 1–2 bar gauge. Internal design uses hemispherical or elliptical heads to minimise stress concentrations.

2. **Outer vessel (secondary container):** Made of carbon steel (SA-516). It contains the vacuum and the MLI, and bears the structural and road-load stresses.

3. **Vacuum annulus:** The space between the two vessels is evacuated to <10⁻³ Pa. Vacuum eliminates gas conduction (the dominant heat transfer mode at cryogenic temperatures) and convective currents.

4. **Multi-Layer Insulation (MLI):** 30–60 alternate layers of aluminised Mylar® film and low-conductivity spacer netting (nylon/dacron) are wrapped around the inner vessel. Each layer reflects ~98% of incident thermal radiation. Effective emissivity $\varepsilon_{eff} \approx 0.01$–$0.05$ reduces radiative heat transfer by 50–100×.

5. **Support struts:** Pultruded glass-fibre reinforced epoxy rods suspend the inner vessel from the outer. They have very low thermal conductivity (~0.3 W/m·K) and minimise conductive heat leak.

6. **Ortho-para conversion catalyst (typically Fe(OH)₃ or rare-earth hydrides):** Located inside the inner vessel, it catalyses the conversion of ortho-H₂ → para-H₂. Without it, the **527 kJ/kg** heat release during spontaneous conversion would double or triple the BOR during the first 72 hours post-loading.

7. **Pressure relief & BOG management:** Twin spring-loaded PRVs set at 1.05 × MAWP, plus a burst disc — they release BOG if pressure exceeds safe limits. Some tankers also have a small reliquefaction unit.

8. **Instrumentation:** Level sensors (differential pressure, capacitance), temperature probes (Pt-100, silicon diodes), pressure transmitters, and gas detectors in the annulus.

**Working principle:** LH2 is loaded through the top fill valve; boil-off vapour is vented or reliquefied; the MLI+vacuum combination keeps the daily boil-off rate as low as **0.1–0.3 %/day**, allowing multi-day transits with minimal loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (1-mark loss typical):** Students often forget to mention that the **vacuum also serves as the primary safety containment** — if the inner vessel leaks, the outer vessel still contains the hydrogen, preventing release to atmosphere.

---

**(b) An LH2 spherical storage tank of diameter 6 m holds 50,000 kg of liquid hydrogen. The vessel is double-walled with MLI (effective U = 0.04 W/m²·K). Ambient T = 300 K, LH2 T = 20 K, h_fg = 446 kJ/kg. Calculate the daily BOG and the %BOR. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Surface area of sphere**
$$
A = \pi D^2 = \pi \times 6^2 = 36\pi = 113.1\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Heat in-leak**
$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = U A \Delta T = 0.04 \times 113.1 \times (300 - 20)
$$
$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = 0.04 \times 113.1 \times 280
$$
$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = 1{,}266.7\ \text{W} = 1.267\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — BOG mass rate**
$$
\dot{m}_{BOG} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{in}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{1{,}266.7}{446{,}000}
$$
$$
\dot{m}_{BOG} = 2.84 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily BOG**
$$
M_{BOG,daily} = \dot{m}_{BOG} \times 86{,}400
$$
$$
M_{BOG,daily} = 2.84 \times 10^{-3} \times 86{,}400
$$
$$
M_{BOG,daily} = 245.4\ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 5 — % BOR per day**
$$
BOR = \frac{245.4}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 0.491\ \%/\text{day}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 0.49 %/day BOR is excellent for a 6 m sphere. Real-world large LH2 storage spheres achieve 0.1–0.3 %/day; the slightly higher value here is because spherical geometry, while minimising surface-to-volume ratio, has a relatively simple MLI wrapping.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**
> **[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]**

**(a) Compare LH2 transportation with compressed gaseous H2 (CGH2) and pipeline transport. Discuss the criteria for selecting LH2 as the preferred mode. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | LH2 Tanker | CGH2 (700 bar) Tube Trailer | H₂ Pipeline |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volumetric energy density** | 8.5 MJ/L (high) | 2.7 MJ/L (low) | 2.7 MJ/L (gas) |
| **Mass per delivery unit** | 4,000 kg (road) | 250–500 kg (road) | Continuous |
| **Effective range** | 1,000–10,000 km | < 300 km | Fixed route, any distance |
| **Capital cost per unit mass transported** | Moderate | Low (short range) | Very high (CAPEX intensive) |
| **Energy required for transport (incl. liquefaction)** | 10–12 kWh/kg | 1.5–2.5 kWh/kg (compression) | 0.5–1 kWh/kg (compression at source) |
| **Boil-off / leakage loss** | 0.1–0.3 %/day | ~negligible | 0.5–2 %/100 km |
| **CAPEX for new infrastructure** | Moderate | Low | Very high |
| **Lead time to deploy** | 1–2 years | < 1 year | 5–10 years |

**Criteria favouring LH2 as the preferred mode:**
1. **Long-distance, intercontinental shipping** (e.g., Australia → Japan, Middle East → Europe) where pipelines are impossible.
2. **Large annual volumes** (>5,000 tonnes/year) where the high CAPEX of dedicated LH2 carriers is amortised.
3. **No fixed route** — LH2 ships can call any port with receiving infrastructure (unlike pipelines).
4. **Decoupling of supply and demand** — LH2 storage buffers seasonal and demand variability better than gaseous systems.
5. **End-use requiring liquid H₂** — rocket propellant (NASA/ISRO), hydrogen aircraft (Airbus ZEROe), scientific cryogenics.

LH2 is **not preferred** when: distance is short (<300 km), volume is low, energy efficiency is paramount, or CAPEX is constrained.

---

**(b) Discuss the safety hazards in LH2 transportation and the design measures used to mitigate them. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Major Safety Hazards:**

1. **Cryogenic burns and embrittlement:** Spill on skin or structural material causes instant frostbite and brittle fracture of carbon steel (loss of ductility below 233 K). Design measure: All exposed parts use austenitic SS, cryogenic gloves, and PPE for operators; outer vessel shields personnel.

2. **Flammability and explosion:** H₂ has a wide flammability range (4–75 % in air) and very low minimum ignition energy (0.02 mJ). A small leak from any valve, flange, or instrument can create an explosive cloud. Design measure: Hydrogen detectors (catalytic bead, electrochemical) at all flanges, leak-checked double-seal fittings, flame arrestors, electrical area classification to ATEX/IECEx Zone 1.

3. **Pressure rise (rollover):** Continuous BOG accumulation raises inner vessel pressure. If unrelieved, it can rupture. Design measure: Dual pressure relief valves (set at 1.05 × MAWP and 1.10 × MAWP), burst disc, vent stacks with flame arrestors, and adequate ullage.

4. **Vapour cloud ignition (delayed deflagration):** H₂ released as warm gas rises rapidly (buoyancy) and can ignite at distant ignition sources, leading to flash-back. Design measure: Venting stack of height ≥ 7.5 m, no ignition sources within exclusion zone, gas dispersion modelling.

5. **Asphyxiation:** Cold H₂ vapour displaces O₂ in confined spaces. Design measure: Oxygen monitors in enclosed handling bays, forced ventilation.

6. **Ortho-para conversion runaway:** If catalyst is missing and large volume is filled, the heat release can spike BOR to dangerous levels. Design measure: Pre-conversion during liquefaction (95%+ para) and in-tank catalyst as backup.

7. **Vessel impact / road accident:** Road tankers face crash risk. Design measure: Rear bumpers, rollover protection, double-walled construction provides secondary containment.

8. **Material compatibility:** Hydrogen embrittlement of high-strength steels at cryogenic temperature. Design measure: Use 304L/316L austenitic stainless steels, avoid martensitic steels.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (1-mark loss):** Students often list "hydrogen explosion" as a single bullet. KTU examiners expect you to distinguish between **jet fire**, **vapour cloud deflagration**, and **detonation (DDT)** — each requires a different mitigation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — LH2 Transportation**

- **LH2 temperature:** 20.28 K (−252.87 °C) at 1 atm — remember the **845×** expansion ratio liquid → gas.
- **Critical point:** T_c = 33.19 K, P_c = 1.297 MPa — LH2 must always be stored *below* both.
- **Density:** LH2 = **70.8 kg/m³**; GH2 at STP = **0.0899 kg/m³** — the 800× compression advantage is what enables transport.
- **LHV:** **120 MJ/kg** (lower than gasoline but very high by mass).
- **Boil-off sources:** (1) Heat in-leak through wall, (2) ortho → para conversion (**527 kJ/kg**).
- **Vessel design:** Inner (304L SS) → support struts (GF epoxy) → vacuum (10⁻³ Pa) → MLI (30–60 layers) → outer (SA-516 CS).
- **MLI mechanism:** Reflects thermal radiation; reduces emissivity from ~0.05 (polished metal) to **0.01**.
- **BOG equation:** $\dot{m}_{BOG} = \dot{Q}_{in} / h_{fg}$ and $\dot{Q}_{in} = U A \Delta T$.
- **Realistic BOR targets:** Road tanker 0.1–0.3 %/day; storage sphere 0.05–0.2 %/day; modern ship 0.1 %/day.
- **Transport modes:** Road (4–5 t), Rail (50–100 t), ISO container (2.8 t), Marine (10,000+ t), Cryogenic pipeline.
- **Transport selection rule:** LH2 is preferred for **distance > 1,500 km** and **volume > 50 t/day**.
- **Safety hierarchy:** Cryogenic → Pressure → Flammability → Embrittlement → Asphyxiation.
- **Liquefaction energy penalty:** ~**10–12 kWh/kg** of LH2 — this is the largest thermodynamic cost.
- **Codes & standards:** ISO 13985, ASME BPVC Sec VIII, IGC Code (marine), ADR (road), EN 13458 (cryogenic vessels).
- **Indian context:** India's National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023) targets 5 MMT/year by 2030, with LH2 shipping being one of three priority transport pathways.
- **Exam arithmetic tip:** Always convert $\Delta T$ in K (not °C) — for LH2 problems, $T_0 - T_{LH2} \approx 280$ K is the standard temperature difference.
- **One-line mantra:** *"Cold, cold, colder — 20 K; boil-off is the enemy; MLI + vacuum are the knights."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Refuelling Stations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hydrogen Refuelling Stations ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Hydrogen Refuelling Station (HRS)** is a specialized fuelling infrastructure facility that delivers compressed or cryo-compressed hydrogen gas to Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs) at prescribed pressure, temperature, flow rate, and purity levels conforming to international fueling protocols such as **SAE J2601** and **ISO 17268**. It integrates the functions of hydrogen reception/storage, mechanical compression, pre-cooling, dispensing, metering, and safety interlocking within a single engineered boundary. The two universally accepted dispensing pressures mandated by global FCEV manufacturers are: - **H35 Protocol:** 35 MPa (≈ 350 bar) for light-duty passenger vehicles - **H70 Protocol:** 70 MPa (≈ 700 bar) for light-duty passenger vehicles (most common global standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must know that an HRS is not merely a "gas cylinder refill station." It is a *cryo-compression system* because hydrogen must be cooled below **–40 °C** (typically to **–40 °C to –33 °C**) at the nozzle during a 700-bar fill to stay within the vehicle tank's thermoplastic polymer liner temperature limit. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Plain-English Explanation:** Imagine a **PET bottle filling plant** in a Coca-Cola factory — but with three critical twists: 1. The "drink" is hydrogen gas at **700 times atmospheric pressure** (≈ 7000 m of water column equivalent!). 2. The bottle (FCEV tank) is made of **carbon-fiber wound polymer** that **softens above 85 °C** — so you cannot just slam gas in; you must *pre-chill* it like refrigerating a drink before carbonating it. 3. The customer (car) arrives unpredictably, so the station must store gas in a **buffer cascade of multiple cylinders at different pressures** — much like a multi-tier water tank system that refills the upper tank from below. This is why an HRS has **three high-pressure vessels** stacked at different pressures (≈ 250, 500, 880 bar) — the **cascade system** — to deliver a fast fill (3–5 minutes for 5 kg of H₂) without choking the compressor. --- ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics at a Glance | Metric | Typical Value | Standard / Source | |---|---|---| | Dispensing Pressure | **70 MPa (H70)** | SAE J2601 | | Pre-cool Temperature | **–40 °C** | SAE J2601-2 | | Fill Duration (5 kg) | **3–5 minutes** | ISO 17268 | | H₂ Purity Grade | **ISO 14687:2019 Grade D** | ISO 14687 | | Hydrogen Index | ≥ 99.97 % | ISO 14687 | | Maximum Vehicle Tank Temp | **85 °C** | SAE J2799 | > [!NOTE] > The **Round-Trip Efficiency** of an HRS (from grid electricity to dispensed H₂) is typically **60–70 %** because compression alone consumes ≈ 10–13 % of H₂'s lower heating value. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Functional Sub-Systems of an HRS An HRS is engineered as the integration of **seven sub-systems**. KTU examiners frequently test block-level understanding: 1. **H₂ Reception Module** — receives H₂ either from a delivery trailer (gaseous tube-trailer or liquid H₂ tanker) or from on-site production (electrolyzer / SMR / methane reformer). 2. **Compression Module** — multi-stage diaphragm or piston compressors boosting from 20–45 bar inlet to 450–900 bar discharge. 3. **Cascade Storage Module** — three pressure vessels (Low / Medium / High) acting as a hydraulic buffer. 4. **Pre-cooling Module** — a **Joule-Thomson (JT) cryogenic chiller** using a refrigerant loop (typically R-744 / CO₂) cooling H₂ to **–40 °C**. 5. **Dispensing Module** — mass-flow meter, breakaway coupling, hose, nozzle, and vehicle identification via **ISO 17268** infrared handshake. 6. **Control & Safety Module** — PLC / SCADA interlocks, leak detectors, flame detectors, ESD (Emergency Shutdown) system, thermal imaging. 7. **Vent & Flare Stack** — for safe controlled depressurization. --- ### 2.2 Why Pre-cooling is Mandatory (Joule-Thomson Effect) When hydrogen expands rapidly from 900 bar storage to a partially-empty 700-bar vehicle tank, the **inversion temperature of H₂ is ≈ 220 K (–53 °C)**. Below this temperature, expansion produces **cooling** instead of heating — the *Joule-Thomson effect*. The vehicle tank's **carbon-fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP) liner** (typically high-density polyethylene or polyamide) can fail above **85 °C** and embrittle below **–40 °C**. The Joule-Thomson effect causes ΔT that would otherwise push temperatures **above +90 °C** during a fast fill, hence mandatory pre-cooling. $$\Delta T_{JT} \approx \mu_{JT} \cdot \Delta P$$ where $\mu_{JT}$ is the Joule-Thomson coefficient (≈ −0.025 K/bar for H₂ at 300 K, 70 MPa). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Reality:** Without pre-cooling, a 5-kg fill in 3 minutes raises tank temperature by ~110 °C — exceeding the 85 °C limit. **Pre-cooling is not optional — it is a safety-critical function.** --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning | Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---| | $W_{comp} = nRT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | $n$ = moles, $T$ = inlet K, $P$ = bar | Ideal isothermal compression work | Valid only when $T$ ≈ constant | | $W_{real} = \dfrac{nRT Z}{\eta_{isen}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | $Z$ = compressibility, $\eta_{isen}$ = efficiency | Real (polytropic) compressor work | $\eta_{isen} \approx 0.70$ for H₂ | | $P_c = \dfrac{P_L V_L + P_M V_M + P_H V_H}{V_L + V_M + V_H}$ | Subscripts L/M/H = Low/Med/High cascade vessels | Average cascade pressure | Used to estimate buffer availability | | $\Delta T_{fill} = T_{ambient} - T_{precool} + \Delta T_{JT}$ | Temperatures in °C or K | End-of-fill tank temperature | Must be $\le 85\,^\circ$C | | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\rho A v}{1}$ | $\rho$ = H₂ density, $A$ = nozzle area, $v$ = velocity | Mass flow rate at dispenser | SAE J2601 limits $\dot{m} \le 60$ g/s | | $E_{stored} = m \cdot LHV_{H_2}$ | $LHV = 120\,MJ/kg$ | Chemical energy stored in vehicle | Used for fueling cost calc | | $\eta_{HRS} = \dfrac{E_{dispensed}}{E_{input}}$ | Dimensionless | Round-trip station efficiency | $\eta_{HRS} \approx 0.60 - 0.70$ | | $\dot{Q}_{cool} = \dot{m}\,c_p\,(T_{amb} - T_{target})$ | $c_p \approx 14.3\,kJ/(kg\cdot K)$ | Pre-cooler heat-load | At 5 kg in 3 min, ≈ 15–18 kW | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder:** The symbols $\mid x \mid$ (absolute value) must be rendered as $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX. Inside markdown tables, vertical pipes must NEVER appear raw — use `\lvert` or `\mid`. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Logistics:** HRS enables the *last-mile* of the H₂ value chain — without it, FCEV adoption is impossible. - **Urban Air Quality:** Replaces diesel refuelling with zero-tailpipe-emission mobility. - **Industrial Clusters:** Acts as buffer storage for green-H₂ produced by remote solar/wind electrolyzers. - **Backup Power Linkage:** The same cascade storage can feed stationary PEM fuel cells for telecom towers or data centers. - **Energy Security:** Strategic asset for nations aiming for *Net-Zero 2070* (India's target). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Theoretical Compression Work **Objective:** Derive the work required to compress hydrogen isothermally from delivery pressure $P_1$ to storage pressure $P_2$. **Step 1 — Start from the First Law for an open system (steady flow):** For a quasi-static isothermal process on an ideal gas: $$dW = -P\,dV$$ **Step 2 — Express $V$ in terms of $P$ using the ideal gas law:** $$V = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$$ $$dV = -\dfrac{nRT}{P^2}\,dP$$ **Step 3 — Substitute back into the work expression:** $$dW = -P \cdot \left(-\dfrac{nRT}{P^2}\right) dP = \dfrac{nRT}{P}\,dP$$ **Step 4 — Integrate between $P_1$ and $P_2$:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{comp} &= \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{nRT}{P}\,dP \\[4pt] &= nRT \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{dP}{P} \\[4pt] &= nRT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Introduce the compressibility factor and isentropic efficiency for real compressors:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{real} &= \dfrac{nRT Z}{\eta_{isen}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Numerical Example (typical HRS):** - Delivery pressure: $P_1 = 20$ bar - Storage pressure: $P_2 = 900$ bar - Mass to compress: 5 kg = 2500 mol (since $M_{H_2}=2$ g/mol) - Inlet temperature: $T = 298$ K - $Z \approx 1.45$ at 900 bar - $\eta_{isen} = 0.70$ $$ \begin{aligned} W_{real} &= \dfrac{2500 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times 1.45}{0.70} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{900}{20}\right) \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{8.97 \times 10^{6}}{0.70} \times \ln(45) \\[4pt] &= 1.281 \times 10^{7} \times 3.807 \\[4pt] &= 4.876 \times 10^{7}\ \text{J} \\[4pt] &= 48.76\ \text{MJ per 5 kg fill} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Convert to useful engineering unit (kWh):** $$W_{real} = \dfrac{48.76\ \text{MJ}}{3.6\ \text{MJ/kWh}} \approx 13.5\ \text{kWh per 5 kg fill}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This matches field data: a 70-MPa HRS consumes **≈ 2.5–3.0 kWh per kg of H₂ compressed**. For a 5-kg fill, expect **12.5–15 kWh**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Cascade Storage Pressure Balancing A cascade has three vessels of equal volume $V$ initially at $P_L$, $P_M$, $P_H$. After equilibration (valve opened), common pressure $P_c$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} P_c &= \dfrac{P_L V + P_M V + P_H V}{V + V + V} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{P_L + P_M + P_H}{3} \end{aligned} $$ **With unequal volumes $V_L \neq V_M \neq V_H$:** $$ P_c = \dfrac{P_L V_L + P_M V_M + P_H V_H}{V_L + V_M + V_H} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Pre-cooler Heat Load $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{Q}_{cool} &= \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,H_2} \cdot \left( T_{amb} - T_{target} \right) \\[4pt] &= \left(\dfrac{5\ \text{kg}}{180\ \text{s}}\right) \times 14.3\ \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}\cdot\text{K}} \times (30 - (-40))\ \text{K} \\[4pt] &= 0.0278 \times 14.3 \times 70 \\[4pt] &= 27.8\ \text{kW (peak cooling demand)} \end{aligned} $$ This is why a **30-kW cryo-chiller** (R-744 / CO₂ transcritical) is mandatory for 70-MPa HRS. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 70-MPa HRS ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Delivery Trailer or On-site Electrolyzer
20 to 45 bar Gaseous H2]:::inlet B[Inlet Buffer
Low Pressure Receiver Tank]:::buffer C[Multi-stage Diaphragm Compressor
Stage1: 45 to 200 bar
Stage2: 200 to 450 bar
Stage3: 450 to 900 bar]:::compressor D[Cascade Storage Bank
Low Vessel 250 bar
Medium Vessel 500 bar
High Vessel 900 bar]:::storage E[Joule-Thomson Pre-cooler
R744 Transcritical Chiller
Target minus 40 degree C]:::cooler F[Dispenser Unit
Mass Flow Meter
Breakaway Coupling
Hose and Nozzle]:::dispenser G[Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle
Vehicle Tank 70 MPa
Carbon Fiber Composite]:::vehicle H[Safety and Control Layer
PLC and SCADA
Leak Detectors
Flame and Thermal Sensors
Emergency Shutdown ESD]:::safety I[Vent and Flare Stack
Controlled Depressurization]:::vent A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G H -. monitors .- A H -. monitors .- B H -. monitors .- C H -. monitors .- D H -. monitors .- E H -. monitors .- F D -. safe relief .- I C -. safe relief .- I classDef inlet fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B26B00,color:#000 classDef buffer fill:#FFE9A8,stroke:#A67900,color:#000 classDef compressor fill:#A8D8FF,stroke:#003E7E,color:#000 classDef storage fill:#C7E9C0,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 classDef cooler fill:#B8E0F5,stroke:#01579B,color:#000 classDef dispenser fill:#F5C2E0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000 classDef vehicle fill:#D7CCC8,stroke:#3E2723,color:#000 classDef safety fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000 classDef vent fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#424242,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Solid arrows = mass flow path (H₂ pipeline). - Dotted arrows from the Safety layer = monitoring/control signal path (NOT H₂ flow). - The compressor has **three stages** because compressing hydrogen in a single stage to 900 bar is thermodynamically prohibitive — ΔT would exceed 600 °C. - The cascade bank is the **hydraulic buffer** ensuring fast fills even when the compressor cannot keep up. - The **Vent/Flare** never operates during a normal fill; it only vents in over-pressure or thermal runaway. --- ### 4.2 Process Flow: Why Three Cascade Vessels? ```mermaid flowchart LR V1[Low Pressure Vessel
200 to 300 bar]:::low V2[Medium Pressure Vessel
450 to 550 bar]:::mid V3[High Pressure Vessel
820 to 900 bar]:::hi F[Fill Vehicle to 700 bar]:::fill V3 -- initial top-up --> F V2 -- mid stage top-up --> F V1 -- bottom stage top-up --> F classDef low fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 classDef mid fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000 classDef hi fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#870000,color:#fff classDef fill fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 ``` The vehicle receives gas in **three stages**, each at a lower pressure than the previous, preventing reverse flow and excessive compressor cycling. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *List any three international standards that govern hydrogen refuelling station design and dispensing. State the dispensing pressure and pre-cool temperature specified by SAE J2601.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **SAE J2601** — Fueling Protocol for Light-Duty Gaseous Hydrogen Vehicles (defines H35 and H70 protocols). 2. **ISO 17268** — Gaseous hydrogen land vehicle refuelling connection devices. 3. **ISO 14687:2019** — Hydrogen fuel quality (Grade D for PEM FCEVs). 4. **NFPA 2** — Hydrogen Technologies Code (US fire safety). 5. **EN 17127** — European HRS standard. **Dispensing:** 70 MPa (H70); **Pre-cool:** –40 °C. **(1 mark)**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *Why is pre-cooling of hydrogen mandatory at a 70-MPa refuelling station? Mention the physical principle involved.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** During fast (3–5 min) high-pressure fills, hydrogen undergoes rapid pressurization inside the vehicle tank. Without cooling, the temperature rise exceeds the **85 °C** thermal limit of the polymer liner, causing **creep rupture** of the carbon-fiber composite overwrap. The principle is the **Joule-Thomson effect**: hydrogen below its inversion temperature (~220 K) cools upon expansion, so depressurizing from 900 bar to 700 bar tends to heat the gas (positive ΔT in this pressure regime) if not pre-cooled. A **Joule-Thomson cryo-chiller** (typically R-744 transcritical) cools H₂ to **–40 °C** at the dispenser inlet, ensuring end-of-fill temperature stays ≤ 85 °C. **(3 marks total)** --- ### PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** *(Choose ONE of A or B)* **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the major sub-systems of a 70-MPa hydrogen refuelling station. Describe the function of each block. **(b) [7 marks]** A 70-MPa HRS delivers 5 kg of hydrogen in 4 minutes from a delivery pressure of 25 bar to a vehicle tank at 700 bar. Calculate: - (i) The theoretical isothermal compression work. - (ii) The real compression work assuming $Z = 1.45$ and isentropic efficiency $\eta_{isen} = 0.70$. - (iii) The specific compression energy in **kWh/kg**. - (iv) Comment on whether this makes sense for a real HRS. **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram Explanation [7 marks]:** | Block | Function | |---|---| | Delivery Module | Receives H₂ from trailer or on-site electrolyzer at 20–45 bar. | | Low-Pressure Buffer | Smooths pressure fluctuations from delivery. | | Multi-stage Compressor | Boosts to 900 bar in 3 stages. | | Cascade Storage (L/M/H) | Hydraulic buffer: 250/500/900 bar. | | Pre-cooler (JT) | Cools H₂ to –40 °C before dispensing. | | Dispenser | Mass flow meter, hose, nozzle, IR handshake. | | Safety & Control | PLC, leak detection, ESD-1, ESD-0. | | Vent/Flare | Controlled depressurization path. | *Student should also draw a labelled flowchart as shown in Section 4.* **(Full 7 marks with diagram.)** **(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:** Given: $m = 5$ kg; $M_{H_2} = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol; $T = 298$ K; $P_1 = 25$ bar; $P_2 = 900$ bar. **Step 1 — Moles of H₂:** $$ n = \dfrac{5}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 2500\ \text{mol} $$ **Step 2 — Theoretical isothermal work:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{ideal} &= nRT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \\[4pt] &= 2500 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \ln(36) \\[4pt] &= 6.19 \times 10^{6} \times 3.584 \\[4pt] &= 2.22 \times 10^{7}\ \text{J} = 22.2\ \text{MJ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Real work with $Z$ and $\eta$:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{real} &= \dfrac{W_{ideal} \cdot Z}{\eta_{isen}} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{22.2 \times 1.45}{0.70} \\[4pt] &= 45.97\ \text{MJ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Specific energy:** $$ e_{comp} = \dfrac{45.97}{5} = 9.19\ \text{MJ/kg} = \dfrac{9.19}{3.6} = 2.55\ \text{kWh/kg} $$ **Step 5 — Comment:** Industry data shows **2.5–3.0 kWh/kg** for 70-MPa HRS. Our calculation (2.55 kWh/kg) lies within range and validates the model. **(7 marks total.)** --- #### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)* **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the cascade storage system used in hydrogen refuelling stations. Why are three vessels of different pressures used instead of a single high-pressure vessel? **(b) [7 marks]** Compute the pre-cooler heat load (in kW) for a station refueling 6 kg of H₂ in 3 minutes from an ambient temperature of 35 °C to a target pre-cool temperature of –40 °C. Take $c_p = 14.3$ kJ/(kg·K). **Model Solution:** **(a) Cascade Storage Explanation [7 marks]:** A cascade storage system is a **multi-vessel buffer** that holds hydrogen at three discrete pressure tiers, enabling fast vehicle fills **without overworking the compressor**. - **Low-pressure vessel (~250 bar):** Receives gas from the compressor's first stage. - **Medium-pressure vessel (~500 bar):** Receives gas from the compressor's second stage. - **High-pressure vessel (~900 bar):** Receives gas from the compressor's third stage and supplies the dispenser. **Why three vessels?** 1. A single 900-bar vessel would require continuous compressor operation between fills. 2. During a 3-minute fast fill, a single vessel would experience a pressure drop that violates the **SAE J2601 fueling protocol** (which mandates a pressure-vs-time ramp curve). 3. Cascade logic maintains **acceptable pressure delta** at the nozzle, prevents backflow, and reduces compressor cycling fatigue. 4. Allows simultaneous refuelling of multiple vehicles. 5. Provides redundancy — if one vessel is offline, others continue to operate. *(Student should sketch the 3-vessel schematic with arrows as shown in Section 4.2.)* **(7 marks.)** **(b) Pre-cooler Heat Load [7 marks]:** Given: $m = 6$ kg; $t = 3\ \text{min} = 180\ \text{s}$; $T_{amb} = 35\,^\circ\text{C}$; $T_{target} = -40\,^\circ\text{C}$; $c_p = 14.3$ kJ/(kg·K). **Step 1 — Mass flow rate:** $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{6}{180} = 0.0333\ \text{kg/s} $$ **Step 2 — Temperature difference:** $$ \Delta T = 35 - (-40) = 75\ \text{K} $$ **Step 3 — Heat load:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{Q}_{cool} &= \dot{m}\,c_p\,\Delta T \\[4pt] &= 0.0333 \times 14.3 \times 75 \\[4pt] &= 35.75\ \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Verification:** This is consistent with industry chiller ratings of **30–40 kW** for H70 stations. **(7 marks total.)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't confuse "bar" with "MPa" in the final answer.** 1 MPa = 10 bar. Marks are deducted when students write 700 bar instead of 70 MPa. > 2. **Always include the compressibility factor $Z$** in compressor calculations. Real H₂ deviates significantly from ideal-gas behavior above 200 bar. > 3. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in $T$ in $\ln(P_2/P_1)$ formula loses 1 mark. The ratio is unitless, but $T$ MUST be in Kelvin. > 4. **Memorizing "–40 °C" without explaining *why*** loses CO-3 marks. Always state the polymer-liner thermal limit (85 °C) and the Joule-Thomson principle. > 5. **Writing "cascade = three tanks"** without explaining WHY a single tank is inadequate loses CO-2 (understanding) marks. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** HRS = cryo-compression infrastructure dispensing 70 MPa (or 35 MPa) H₂ per SAE J2601. - **Two Protocols:** H35 (35 MPa, less common) and **H70 (70 MPa, global standard)**. - **Pre-cooling:** Mandatory to **–40 °C** to keep vehicle tank ≤ 85 °C (Joule-Thomson + fast-fill heating). - **Cascade Storage:** Three vessels at 250 / 500 / 900 bar for hydraulic buffering. - **Compression Energy:** ≈ **2.5–3.0 kWh/kg** at 70 MPa. - **Round-Trip Station Efficiency:** 60–70 %. - **Key Standards:** SAE J2601, ISO 17268, ISO 14687 (Grade D), NFPA 2, EN 17127. - **Purity:** ≥ 99.97 % H₂ (PEM-FCEV grade). - **Fill Time:** 3–5 minutes for 5 kg H₂. - **Pre-cooler:** R-744 transcritical chiller, 30–40 kW rating. - **Compressor Type:** Multi-stage (3-stage) diaphragm or piston. - **Mass Flow Limit:** ≤ 60 g/s per SAE J2601. - **Safety:** ESD-1 (component), ESD-0 (station-wide), leak detectors, flame detectors, thermal imaging, vent/flare stack. - **Inversion Temperature of H₂:** ≈ 220 K (–53 °C) — drives the JT cooling physics.

topics.title: Overview of Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview of Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Cell (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> A **fuel cell** is a static, galvanic (electrochemical) energy conversion device that directly converts the **chemical energy** of a continuously supplied fuel (most commonly hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically oxygen from air) into **electrical energy (DC)** and **heat**, with **water** as the primary by-product. The conversion proceeds *without* combustion and is governed by the principles of reversible electrochemistry.

Unlike a battery, a fuel cell does not store energy internally; it is an **energy converter** that generates electricity as long as fuel and oxidant are supplied — earning it the academic label of an *open thermodynamic system* for the chemical reactants.

The universally quoted net cell reaction for the **Hydrogen–Oxygen (H₂/O₂) fuel cell** is:

$$\mathrm{H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \longrightarrow H_2O(l) + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}}$$

This single line reaction produces a **standard theoretical open-circuit voltage of 1.229 V at 25 °C (298 K) and 1 atm pressure.**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Fuel Cell vs. Battery vs. Engine**
> - Think of a **conventional battery** as a *sealed water bottle* — it has a fixed amount of water (chemical energy) inside. Drink it (use it), and it's empty; you must throw it away or plug it in to recharge.
> - A **fuel cell** is like a *water cooler connected to a municipal pipeline*. As long as the pipeline (hydrogen supply) keeps flowing, you never run out of water (electricity). When demand is high, you simply open the tap wider.
> - A **combustion engine** is like a *match* — it burns fuel in one big, noisy, hot step, losing a lot of energy as uncontrolled heat and noise.
> - A **fuel cell** is like a *quiet, well-controlled candle* — the same chemistry happens (oxidation of hydrogen), but in a *cool, silent, electrochemical* manner that captures electrons as a useful stream of current.

**Why is this important to KTU examiners?**
They *love* testing whether you can distinguish a fuel cell from a battery. The rule of thumb is:

| Device | Energy Source | Refillable? | Stores Energy? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Battery** | Stored chemicals inside | No (or re-charge) | ✅ Yes |
| **Fuel Cell** | External fuel feed | ✅ Yes (refuel) | ❌ No |
| **Engine** | External fuel + combustion | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |

---

## 1.3 Historical Context (Often Asked in 2–3 Mark Questions)

> [!NOTE]
> - **1839** — Sir **William Robert Grove** (Welsh physicist) demonstrated the first working fuel cell, the "Grove cell," using dilute sulfuric acid as electrolyte and platinum electrodes. He called it a *gas battery*.
> - **1889** — **Charles Langer** and **Ludwig Mond** coined the term **"fuel cell"** while attempting to produce electricity from coal-derived fuels.
> - **1950s–60s** — **NASA** adopted alkaline fuel cells (AFCs) for the **Apollo, Gemini, and Space Shuttle** missions — providing both electricity and drinking water for astronauts.
> - **1990s–Present** — Commercialization of **PEMFC** (Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell) and **SOFC** (Solid Oxide Fuel Cell) for automotive, stationary, and portable power.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Theoretical reversible voltage (E°):** **1.229 V** per cell at 25 °C (product = liquid H₂O)
> - **Theoretical reversible voltage (E°'):** **1.185 V** per cell if product = water vapour
> - **Practical operating voltage:** **0.6 – 0.75 V** per cell (due to irreversibilities)
> - **Operating temperature range:** **25 °C (PEMFC) – 1000 °C (SOFC)**
> - **Theoretical electrical efficiency (ΔG/ΔH, HHV):** **83 %**
> - **Theoretical electrical efficiency (ΔG/ΔH, LHV):** **98 %**
> - **Practical system efficiency (PEMFC):** **40 – 60 %**
> - **Combined heat & power (CHP) efficiency:** **up to 85 – 90 %**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — How a Fuel Cell Works

A fuel cell's operation can be described in **four logical stages**:

1. **Fuel Delivery & Adsorption:**
   - Hydrogen gas is fed to the **anode** (negative electrode / fuel electrode).
   - Oxygen (from air) is fed to the **cathode** (positive electrode / oxidant electrode).
   - A **catalyst** (typically platinum nanoparticles in PEMFC) adsorbs H₂ onto its surface.

2. **Electrochemical Oxidation (At the Anode — Negative Terminal):**
   - The catalyst splits H₂ into protons (H⁺) and electrons (e⁻).
   - **Half-reaction (acidic PEMFC):**
     $$\mathrm{H_2 \longrightarrow 2H^{+} + 2e^{-}}$$
   - Protons migrate through the **electrolyte**; electrons are forced through the **external circuit** (doing useful work).

3. **Electrochemical Reduction (At the Cathode — Positive Terminal):**
   - Electrons return via the external circuit and react with O₂ and H⁺ at the cathode.
   - **Half-reaction (acidic PEMFC):**
     $$\mathrm{\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow H_2O}$$
   - **Product water** is formed here and removed (as liquid or vapour).

4. **Charge & Mass Balance:**
   - The electrolyte must conduct **ions only** (H⁺ in PEMFC, O²⁻ in SOFC) and **block electrons** — this forces electrons through the external load.
   - Continuous flow of fuel + oxidant gives a **steady-state** current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no combustion?**
> The Gibbs free energy of the reaction is released *directly* as electrical work — there is no flame, no NOₓ, no soot, and no intermediate thermal step. The cell is **silent**, modular, and scalable.

---

## 2.2 Core Components of a Generic Fuel Cell

| Component | Function | Common Materials |
|---|---|---|
| **Anode** | Oxidises fuel; releases electrons | Pt/C, Ni-YSZ cermet (SOFC) |
| **Cathode** | Reduces oxidant; receives electrons | Pt/C, LSM (SOFC) |
| **Electrolyte** | Conducts ions, blocks electrons & gas crossover | Nafion™ (PEMFC), YSZ (SOFC), KOH (AFC) |
| **Catalyst Layer** | Speeds up sluggish ORR (Oxygen Reduction Reaction) | Pt nanoparticles (PEMFC) |
| **Bipolar Plates / Current Collectors** | Distribute gases, conduct current, remove water | Graphite, stainless steel, coated metals |
| **Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL)** | Even gas distribution & water management | Carbon paper/cloth with PTFE |
| **Sealants & Interconnects** | Prevent gas leakage; stack cells | Mica, ceramic-glass composites |

---

## 2.3 Classification of Fuel Cells (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Classify fuel cells and state their operating temperatures and applications."* Memorize this table.

| Fuel Cell Type | Electrolyte | Mobile Ion | Operating Temp. | Electrical Efficiency | Major Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AFC** (Alkaline Fuel Cell) | KOH (aqueous) | OH⁻ | 50 – 200 °C | 50 – 60 % | Space (Apollo) |
| **PEMFC** (Proton Exchange Membrane FC) | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | H⁺ | 50 – 100 °C | 40 – 60 % | Automobiles (Toyota Mirai), portable |
| **DMFC** (Direct Methanol FC) | Polymer membrane | H⁺ | 60 – 130 °C | 30 – 40 % | Portable electronics |
| **PAFC** (Phosphoric Acid FC) | H₃PO₄ (liquid) | H⁺ | 150 – 220 °C | 40 – 50 % | Stationary CHP (200 kW) |
| **MCFC** (Molten Carbonate FC) | Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ (molten) | CO₃²⁻ | 600 – 700 °C | 50 – 60 % | Large stationary power plants |
| **SOFC** (Solid Oxide FC) | YSZ (solid ceramic) | O²⁻ | 600 – 1000 °C | 50 – 65 % | Industrial, large-scale CHP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Memory Trick:** Temperature increases as you go **PEM < AFC < DMFC < PAFC < MCFC < SOFC**. The hotter the cell, the more tolerant it is to fuel impurities, but the more exotic the materials required.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is **gold for the 14-mark derivations**. Do not skip this section.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Value (H₂/O₂) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | Gibbs free energy ↔ cell voltage | $\Delta G^\circ = -237.2\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$ |
| 2 | $E = -\dfrac{\Delta G}{nF}$ | Reversible cell EMF | $E^\circ = 1.229\ \mathrm{V}$ |
| 3 | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | **Nernst Equation** | E drops as T↑ |
| 4 | $E_T = E^\circ + \dfrac{\Delta S}{nF}(T - 298)$ | Temperature correction to EMF | $\Delta S \approx -163\ \mathrm{J/(mol\cdot K)}$ |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ | Theoretical electrical efficiency | HHV: 83 % ; LHV: 98 % |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{volt}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}}}{1.229}$ | Voltage efficiency | ~0.5 (typical) |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{faradaic}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{actual}}}{I_{\text{theoretical}}}$ | Faradaic / current efficiency | ~0.95 |
| 8 | $V_{\text{cell}} = E - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$ | Actual operating voltage | 0.6 – 0.75 V |
| 9 | $P_{\text{stack}} = N \times V_{\text{cell}} \times I$ | Stack power (N = no. of cells) | — |
| 10 | $E_{\text{thermo-neutral}} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{nF} = 1.481\ \mathrm{V}$ | Voltage if *all* heat converted | Used in Carnot limit |

**Symbols to memorize:** $n$ = number of electrons transferred (= 2 for H₂), $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**, $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stationary Power:** Combined Heat & Power (CHP) units in hospitals, data centres (e.g., Bloom Energy's SOFC servers in eBay/Apple).
- **Transport:** Zero-emission vehicles (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo, Mercedes-Benz GLC F-CELL) — refuel in 5 minutes, range ~650 km.
- **Space & Defence:** NASA Space Shuttle ran 3 kW AFC stacks providing 7 days of crew drinking water.
- **Portable:** Backup power for telecom towers, soldier-wearable power, laptop chargers (DMFC).
- **Industrial:** Hydrogen forklifts (Walmart, Amazon warehouses) — safer indoors than diesel.

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU examiner's favourite application question: *"Why is PEMFC preferred for automotive applications despite its lower efficiency compared to SOFC?"*
> **Model Answer:** PEMFC operates at low temperature (≈ 80 °C), starts up in seconds, has high power density, and tolerates vibration — making it ideal for cars. SOFC's 800 °C start-up time (~hours) is unsuitable for frequent on/off cycling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thermodynamic Origin of the Cell Voltage (E°)

**Goal:** Show that the open-circuit voltage of a fuel cell arises from the Gibbs free energy of the overall reaction.

**Starting Point (First Law of Thermodynamics for a Reversible Electrochemical Cell):**

For any reversible electrochemical reaction at constant temperature and pressure, the **maximum non-PV (electrical) work** obtainable equals the **decrease in Gibbs free energy**:

$$W_{\text{elec, max}} = -\Delta G$$

The electrical work done by a cell transferring $n$ moles of electrons per mole of reaction through a potential $E$ is:

$$W_{\text{elec}} = nFE$$

(where $F$ = Faraday constant = 96,485 C/mol, $E$ = cell EMF in volts).

**Equating the two:**

$$nFE = -\Delta G$$

**Solving for E:**

$$E = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}$$

> **Key Insight:** The cell voltage is *not* a magical property of the electrode material — it is a direct thermodynamic consequence of the chemistry.

**Step-by-step numerical evaluation for the H₂/O₂ cell at standard conditions:**

The standard Gibbs free energy of formation of liquid water at 25 °C is:
$\Delta G_f^\circ(\mathrm{H_2O,\,l}) = -237.2\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$
$\Delta G_f^\circ(\mathrm{H_2,\,g}) = 0$ (reference)
$\Delta G_f^\circ(\mathrm{O_2,\,g}) = 0$ (reference)

Therefore:

$$\Delta G^\circ = \Delta G_f^\circ(\mathrm{H_2O}) - \Delta G_f^\circ(\mathrm{H_2}) - \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta G_f^\circ(\mathrm{O_2})$$

$$\Delta G^\circ = -237.2 - 0 - 0 = -237.2\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$$

Substituting into $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ / (nF)$ with $n = 2$:

$$E^\circ = -\frac{(-237{,}200\ \mathrm{J/mol})}{2 \times 96{,}485\ \mathrm{C/mol}}$$

$$E^\circ = \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{E^\circ = 1.229\ \mathrm{V}}$$

This is the universally quoted **standard theoretical OCV** of a hydrogen fuel cell.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nernst Equation for a Fuel Cell

**Goal:** Show how cell voltage depends on partial pressures of reactants and temperature.

**Starting from the general Gibbs free energy change at non-standard conditions:**

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient**. For the H₂/O₂ reaction:

$$\mathrm{H_2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H_2O}$$

$$Q = \frac{a_{\mathrm{H_2O}}}{a_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot a_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}} = \frac{p_{\mathrm{H_2O}}}{p_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot p_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}}$$

(using fugacity/activity ≈ partial pressure for ideal gases, in atm).

Dividing the entire expression by $-nF$:

$$-\frac{\Delta G}{nF} = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

Recognising $E = -\Delta G / nF$ and $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ / nF$:

$$\boxed{E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{\mathrm{H_2O}}}{p_{\mathrm{H_2}}\,p_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}}\right)}$$

**Numerical Example:**

A PEMFC operating at $T = 353\ \mathrm{K}$ (80 °C) with $p_{\mathrm{H_2}} = 1\ \mathrm{atm}$, $p_{\mathrm{O_2}} = 0.21\ \mathrm{atm}$ (air), and $p_{\mathrm{H_2O}} = 0.5\ \mathrm{atm}$:

$$E = 1.229 - \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485}\ln\!\left(\frac{0.5}{1 \times 0.21^{0.5}}\right)$$

$$E = 1.229 - \frac{2935}{192{,}970}\ln\!\left(\frac{0.5}{0.4583}\right)$$

$$E = 1.229 - (0.01521)\ln(1.0910)$$

$$E = 1.229 - (0.01521)(0.0872)$$

$$E = 1.229 - 0.00133$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 1.228\ \mathrm{V}}$$

**Interpretation:** The voltage drops only marginally from standard conditions when pure H₂ and ambient air are used — but it falls sharply under load due to *irreversible* losses, not Nernst effects.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Temperature Dependence of Cell Voltage

**Goal:** Show how OCV changes with operating temperature.

Differentiating $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\Delta S$$

But also from $E = -\Delta G / nF$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\frac{1}{nF}\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{\Delta S}{nF}$$

Integrating between 298 K and $T$:

$$E(T) = E^\circ + \frac{\Delta S}{nF}(T - 298)$$

**For the H₂/O₂ cell:** $\Delta S^\circ = -163.2\ \mathrm{J/(mol\cdot K)}$ (system loses entropy as 1.5 mol gas → 0 mol gas in liquid product).

$$E(T) = 1.229 + \frac{-163.2}{2 \times 96{,}485}(T - 298)$$

$$E(T) = 1.229 - 8.46 \times 10^{-4}\,(T - 298)\ \mathrm{V}$$

**At $T = 353$ K (80 °C):**

$$E = 1.229 - 8.46 \times 10^{-4} \times 55 = 1.229 - 0.0465 = 1.183\ \mathrm{V}$$

**At $T = 1073$ K (800 °C, SOFC temperature):**

$$E = 1.229 - 8.46 \times 10^{-4} \times 775 = 1.229 - 0.656 \approx 0.573\ \mathrm{V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Higher operating temperature *reduces* the theoretical OCV. This is why SOFC, despite running at 800 °C, starts with a lower reversible voltage than PEMFC. However, hot cells recover this loss through **faster kinetics** and **lower activation overpotentials**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Theoretical (Thermodynamic) Efficiency

**Goal:** Derive the upper bound of fuel cell efficiency.

**Definition:** The theoretical (reversible) efficiency is the ratio of useful electrical work to the total chemical energy released (heating value):

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{W_{\text{elec}}}{Q_{\text{released}}} = \frac{-\Delta G}{-\Delta H}$$

**Using Higher Heating Value (HHV)** — product is liquid water:
$\Delta H_{\mathrm{HHV}} = -285.8\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$

$$\eta_{\text{HHV}} = \frac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.830 = \boxed{83.0\%}$$

**Using Lower Heating Value (LHV)** — product is water vapour:
$\Delta H_{\mathrm{LHV}} = -241.8\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$

$$\eta_{\text{LHV}} = \frac{237.2}{241.8} = 0.981 = \boxed{98.1\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this surprising?**
> A Carnot heat engine between the same temperature reservoirs is limited to ~70 % at best. A fuel cell *bypasses* the heat step entirely, sidestepping Carnot's theorem — this is the fundamental reason fuel cells can theoretically beat internal combustion engines in efficiency.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Actual Operating Voltage (Polarisation Curve)

**Goal:** Quantify the three major irreversibilities.

A real fuel cell operates below $E^\circ$ due to three voltage losses:

$$V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$$

**1. Activation Overpotential (η_act):** Energy barrier for electron-transfer reactions (esp. sluggish ORR at cathode). Modelled by the **Butler–Volmer** equation:

$$\eta_{\text{act}} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

where $i$ = current density, $i_0$ = exchange current density, $\alpha$ = transfer coefficient (≈ 0.5).

**2. Ohmic Overpotential (η_ohm):** Ionic & electronic resistance of cell components. Linear with current:

$$\eta_{\text{ohm}} = iR_{\text{internal}}$$

**3. Concentration Overpotential (η_conc):** Mass-transport limitation at high current:

$$\eta_{\text{conc}} = \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{i}{i_L}\right)$$

where $i_L$ = limiting current density.

**Typical Polarisation Values for a PEMFC at 1 A/cm²:**

| Loss | Magnitude |
|---|---|
| Activation (cathode dominates) | ~0.3 – 0.4 V |
| Ohmic | ~0.05 – 0.1 V |
| Concentration | ~0.05 – 0.1 V |
| **Total loss** | **~0.4 – 0.6 V** |
| **Resulting V_cell** | **0.6 – 0.7 V** |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fuel Cell vs Battery vs Engine

| Parameter | Fuel Cell (PEMFC) | Li-ion Battery | Internal Combustion Engine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Conversion | Electrochemical | Electrochemical | Thermomechanical |
| Theoretical Efficiency | 83 % (HHV) | ~100 % (charge) | 30 – 40 % (Carnot) |
| Practical Efficiency | 40 – 60 % | 80 – 95 % | 20 – 35 % |
| Emissions (tail) | H₂O only | None (use phase) | CO₂, NOₓ, CO, PM |
| Refuel/Recharge | Minutes (H₂ refill) | Hours (plug-in) | Minutes (petrol) |
| Operating Temp. | 50 – 100 °C | 25 – 45 °C | 600 – 1200 °C |
| Noise | Silent | Silent | Loud |
| Power Density (W/kg) | 300 – 1000 | 250 – 2500 | 500 – 1000 |
| Energy Density (Wh/kg system) | 800 – 1000 (with H₂ tank) | 150 – 250 | 1200 – 2000 |
| Moving Parts | **None** | None | Hundreds |
| Cold-start | Challenging (< 0 °C) | Excellent | Excellent |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Single PEM Fuel Cell Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2TANK[Hydrogen Fuel Tank] --> ANODEIN
    AIRBL[Air Blower] --> CATHODEIN
    ANODEIN --> ANODE[/Anode Side GDL/]
    CATHODEIN --> CATHODE[/Cathode Side GDL/]
    ANODE --> CATLAY[/Catalyst Layer Pt-C/]
    CATHODE --> CATLAY2[/Catalyst Layer Pt-C/]
    CATLAY --> MEM[(Proton Exchange Membrane Nafion)]
    CATLAY2 --> MEM
    MEM --> H2SPLIT{H2 Molecule Split}
    H2SPLIT -->|2H+| PROTON{Migrates via Electrolyte}
    H2SPLIT -->|2e-| EXTCIRCUIT[/External Circuit - Does Work/]
    EXTCIRCUIT --> CATLAY2
    CATLAY2 --> ORR{O2 + 4H+ + 4e- -> 2H2O}
    PROTON --> ORR
    ORR --> WATER[/Water and Heat Removed/]
    ANODE -.Heat.-> HEATMGMT[Thermal Management]
    CATHODE -.Heat.-> HEATMGMT
    EXTCIRCUIT -.Current.-> LOAD[DC Load or Inverter]
    LOAD --> ACGRID[AC Output or Vehicle Motor]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Major Components and Their Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FUEL_DELIVERY[Fuel Delivery Subsystem]
        F1[Hydrogen Storage Cylinder 350 or 700 bar]
        F2[Pressure Regulator]
        F3[Humidifier]
    end
    subgraph OXIDANT_DELIVERY[Oxidant Delivery Subsystem]
        O1[Air Filter]
        O2[Compressor / Blower]
        O3[Humidifier]
    end
    subgraph STACK[Electrochemical Stack]
        S1[Single Cell Anode GDL]
        S2[Anode Catalyst Pt]
        S3[Membrane Electrode Assembly MEA]
        S4[Cathode Catalyst Pt]
        S5[Cathode GDL]
        S6[Bipolar Plates]
    end
    subgraph POWER_COND[Power Conditioning]
        P1[DC-DC Converter]
        P2[DC-AC Inverter]
    end
    subgraph THERMAL[Thermal Management Subsystem]
        T1[Coolant Pump]
        T2[Radiator or Heat Exchanger]
    end
    F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> S1
    O1 --> O2 --> O3 --> S5
    S2 --> S3 --> S4
    S3 -.Water.-> S5
    S6 --> P1 --> P2
    S1 -.Heat.-> T1 --> T2
    S5 -.Heat.-> T1
```

## 4.3 Classification Flow — Major Fuel Cell Families

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FC[Fuel Cells]
    FC --> LOWT[Low Temperature 25 to 250 C]
    FC --> HIGHT[High Temperature 600 to 1000 C]
    LOWT --> AFC[AFC Alkaline]
    LOWT --> PEMFC[PEMFC Proton Exchange]
    LOWT --> DMFC[DMFC Direct Methanol]
    LOWT --> PAFC[PAFC Phosphoric Acid]
    HIGHT --> MCFC[MCFC Molten Carbonate]
    HIGHT --> SOFC[SOFC Solid Oxide]
    PEMFC --> APP1[Automobiles, Portable]
    AFC --> APP2[Space, Military]
    PAFC --> APP3[Stationary CHP 200 kW]
    MCFC --> APP4[Large Power Plants MW]
    SOFC --> APP5[Industrial CHP, Data Centres]
    DMFC --> APP6[Consumer Electronics]
```

## 4.4 Polarisation Curve — Voltage vs Current Density

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POL[Typical PEMFC V-i Curve]
        A[Start at 1.23 V at i = 0] --> B[Rapid Drop Activation Losses 0 to 0.1 A per cm2]
        B --> C[Linear Drop Ohmic Region 0.1 to 1.0 A per cm2]
        C --> D[Steep Drop Concentration Losses above 1.0 A per cm2]
        D --> E[End at Limiting Current Density iL around 1.5 A per cm2]
    end
    POL -.Voltage.-> YAX[Y axis V_cell 1.23 to 0 V]
    POL -.Current.-> XAX[X axis i A per cm2 0 to 1.5]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the polarisation curve in your exam:**
> - **Region 1 (low current, steep drop):** Activation losses dominate. Solution: better catalyst, higher temperature.
> - **Region 2 (linear drop):** Ohmic losses dominate. Solution: thinner membrane, better bipolar plate conductivity.
> - **Region 3 (high current, sharp drop):** Concentration losses. Solution: improved GDL, higher reactant flow.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"Define a fuel cell. How is it fundamentally different from a primary battery?"**

**Model Answer (Structured for 3 marks):**
1. **Definition (2 marks):** A fuel cell is an electrochemical device that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (e.g., H₂) and an oxidant (e.g., O₂) into electricity and heat, with water as the by-product, *without* combustion. The overall reaction is:
   $$\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}}$$
2. **Difference (1 mark):** A primary battery has a *finite* amount of active material sealed inside — once consumed, it is discarded. A fuel cell is an *open system* with externally supplied reactants; it can generate electricity continuously as long as fuel is fed, and it does not need recharging, only refuelling.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**"State the half-cell reactions occurring at the anode and cathode of a PEMFC. What is the role of the catalyst?"**

**Model Answer:**
- **Anode reaction (1 mark):** $\mathrm{H_2 \rightarrow 2H^{+} + 2e^{-}}$ (oxidation of hydrogen).
- **Cathode reaction (1 mark):** $\mathrm{\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_2O}$ (oxygen reduction reaction, ORR).
- **Role of catalyst (1 mark):** The platinum catalyst lowers the activation energy barrier for the otherwise kinetically slow reactions (especially ORR at the cathode), enabling practical current densities at low overpotentials.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (Choose ONE 14-mark question)

### **Question B – Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

#### (a) **Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC). State the overall and half-cell reactions. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (4 marks):** A PEMFC consists of a proton-conducting polymer membrane (Nafion™) sandwiched between two platinum-catalysed porous carbon electrodes (anode and cathode). Hydrogen is fed to the anode side, oxygen (or air) to the cathode. At the anode catalyst, H₂ splits into protons and electrons. The protons migrate through the hydrated membrane to the cathode side; the electrons, blocked by the membrane, are forced through the external circuit, providing useful DC electrical work. At the cathode catalyst, electrons, protons, and oxygen combine to form water and release heat. A schematic block diagram is as shown in **Section 4.1** above.

**Reactions (3 marks):**

- **Anode (oxidation):** $\mathrm{H_2 \rightarrow 2H^{+} + 2e^{-}} \quad E^\circ = 0\ \mathrm{V}$ (SHE reference)
- **Cathode (reduction):** $\mathrm{\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_2O} \quad E^\circ = 1.229\ \mathrm{V}$ (SHE)
- **Overall:** $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O} \quad \Delta E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = 1.229\ \mathrm{V}$

The membrane must be hydrated (≈ 50 % relative humidity) to maintain proton conductivity; water management is critical to prevent flooding (cathode) and drying (anode).

---

#### (b) **Derive the Nernst equation for a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell and estimate the open-circuit voltage at 80 °C if the partial pressures are $p_{\mathrm{H_2}} = 1\ \mathrm{atm}$, $p_{\mathrm{O_2}} = 0.21\ \mathrm{atm}$ and $p_{\mathrm{H_2O}} = 0.5\ \mathrm{atm}$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** Starting from the Gibbs free energy at non-standard conditions:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

Dividing by $-nF$ and substituting $E = -\Delta G/nF$, $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ/nF$:

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

For the reaction $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O}$:

$$Q = \frac{p_{\mathrm{H_2O}}}{p_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot p_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}}$$

Therefore:

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{\mathrm{H_2O}}}{p_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot p_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}}\right)$$

**Numerical Evaluation (3 marks):** With $T = 353\ \mathrm{K}$, $R = 8.314\ \mathrm{J/(mol\cdot K)}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485\ \mathrm{C/mol}$, $E^\circ = 1.229\ \mathrm{V}$:

$$E = 1.229 - \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485}\ln\!\left(\frac{0.5}{1 \times \sqrt{0.21}}\right)$$

$$E = 1.229 - (0.01521)\ln\!\left(\frac{0.5}{0.4583}\right) = 1.229 - (0.01521)(0.0872)$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 1.228\ \mathrm{V}}$$

---

### **Question B – Option B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### (a) **Classify the major types of fuel cells. Compare their electrolyte, mobile ion, operating temperature, and application in a tabular form. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Full table, 7 marks):**

| Type | Electrolyte | Mobile Ion | Operating T (°C) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AFC** | KOH (aq) | OH⁻ | 50 – 200 | Space (Apollo) |
| **PEMFC** | Polymer (Nafion) | H⁺ | 50 – 100 | Cars (Mirai), backup power |
| **DMFC** | Polymer (Nafion) | H⁺ | 60 – 130 | Portable electronics |
| **PAFC** | H₃PO₄ (liq) | H⁺ | 150 – 220 | Stationary CHP (200 kW) |
| **MCFC** | Molten Li/K carbonate | CO₃²⁻ | 600 – 700 | Large-scale power (MW) |
| **SOFC** | YSZ (solid) | O²⁻ | 600 – 1000 | Industrial CHP, data centres |

**Key observations (must be mentioned for full marks):**
- Low-T cells (AFC, PEMFC) need **pure H₂** (CO poisons Pt catalyst).
- High-T cells (MCFC, SOFC) tolerate **CO, CH₄, hydrocarbons** — internal reforming possible.
- Higher temperature → **faster kinetics** and **higher tolerance to impurities**, but **longer start-up** and **sealing difficulties**.

---

#### (b) **Starting from Gibbs free energy, derive the relationship between cell EMF and the change in Gibbs free energy. Calculate the standard EMF of a H₂/O₂ fuel cell at 25 °C. Given: $\Delta G_f^\circ(\mathrm{H_2O,\,l}) = -237.2\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$, $F = 96{,}485\ \mathrm{C/mol}$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** For a reversible electrochemical cell at constant T and P, the maximum electrical work equals the decrease in Gibbs free energy:

$$W_{\text{elec, max}} = -\Delta G$$

For $n$ moles of electrons transferred per mole of reaction, the electrical work through potential $E$ is:

$$W_{\text{elec}} = nFE$$

Equating:

$$nFE = -\Delta G \quad\Longrightarrow\quad E = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}$$

**Calculation (3 marks):** For the H₂/O₂ cell, $n = 2$ and $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta G_f^\circ(\mathrm{H_2O,\,l}) - 0 - 0 = -237.2\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$.

$$E^\circ = -\frac{(-237{,}200\ \mathrm{J/mol})}{2 \times 96{,}485\ \mathrm{C/mol}} = \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{E^\circ = 1.229\ \mathrm{V}}$$

This is the maximum theoretical OCV at 25 °C and 1 atm — the practical cell voltage is always lower due to activation, ohmic, and concentration losses.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of ΔG:** ΔG_f°(H₂O) is *negative*; if you write $-E = \Delta G/(nF)$ you will get $-1.229$ V, which is *meaningless*. Always write $E = -\Delta G/nF$.
> 2. **Confusing HHV and LHV:** LHV efficiency is ~98 %, HHV is ~83 %. Examiners will deduct a full mark if you don't specify which you used.
> 3. **Wrong Nernst sign:** $\ln Q$ is **subtracted** from $E^\circ$, not added. Memorize: $E = E^\circ - (RT/nF)\ln Q$.
> 4. **Mixing up mobile ions:** PEMFC → **H⁺**, AFC → **OH⁻**, MCFC → **CO₃²⁻**, SOFC → **O²⁻**. Examiners love this.
> 5. **Sketches without labels:** A diagram *without* labelled anode, cathode, electrolyte, electron flow, and ion flow = **0 marks for the figure**. Always label both arrows.
> 6. **Ignoring overpotentials in operating voltage:** Saying "operating voltage = 1.229 V" in a real cell. The correct practical answer is **0.6 – 0.75 V/cell**.
> 7. **Not specifying units:** $F$ is C/mol, $R$ is J/(mol·K), $\Delta G$ must be in **Joules**, not kJ.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density, last-minute revision points for KTU exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Fuel cell = direct electrochemical converter of fuel + oxidant → electricity + heat + water. **No combustion**.
- ✅ **Net reaction (acid electrolyte):** $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O}$, $E^\circ = \mathbf{1.229\ V}$ at 25 °C.
- ✅ **Theoretical efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{HHV}} = \mathbf{83\%}$, $\eta_{\text{LHV}} = \mathbf{98\%}$.
- ✅ **Key equation:** $\boxed{E = -\dfrac{\Delta G}{nF} = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q}$
- ✅ **Faraday's constant:** $F = \mathbf{96{,}485\ C/mol}$; $R = \mathbf{8.314\ J/(mol\cdot K)}$.
- ✅ **Three voltage losses:** Activation (catalyst), Ohmic (resistance), Concentration (mass transport).
- ✅ **Practical operating voltage:** $\mathbf{0.6 - 0.75\ V/cell}$ (not 1.229 V).
- ✅ **Fuel cell ≠ battery:** Fuel cell is an *open* system, battery is *closed* / sealed.
- ✅ **Mobile ions (must memorise):** PEMFC = $\mathrm{H^+}$; AFC = $\mathrm{OH^-}$; PAFC = $\mathrm{H^+}$; MCFC = $\mathrm{CO_3^{2-}}$; SOFC = $\mathrm{O^{2-}}$.
- ✅ **Temperature order (ascending):** PEMFC < AFC < DMFC < PAFC < MCFC < SOFC.
- ✅ **High-T cells (MCFC, SOFC):** tolerant to CO and hydrocarbons; can do *internal reforming* of methane.
- ✅ **Low-T cells (PEMFC, AFC):** require *pure* H₂; CO poisons the Pt catalyst.
- ✅ **Inventor:** Sir William Grove (1839) — the "gas battery."
- ✅ **Space applications:** NASA Apollo & Space Shuttle used AFCs (provided power + drinking water).
- ✅ **Polarisation curve:** E drops with current density in three distinct regions (activation → ohmic → concentration).
- ✅ **CHP efficiency** (with heat recovery) can reach **85 – 90 %**.
- ✅ **Vocabulary:** OCV = Open-Circuit Voltage, ORR = Oxygen Reduction Reaction, HOR = Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction, MEA = Membrane Electrode Assembly, GDL = Gas Diffusion Layer, CHP = Combined Heat & Power.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
