# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC). Mention any two of its distinguishing features. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **PEMFC** is a low-temperature electrochemical device that converts the chemical energy of hydrogen and oxygen directly into electrical energy, water, and heat, using a **solid polymer electrolyte membrane** (typically Nafion) that selectively conducts **protons (H⁺)** from the anode to the cathode.

**Two distinguishing features (any two of the following for full marks):**
1. **Low operating temperature** (60 – 100 °C), enabling fast start-up and making it suitable for automotive applications.
2. **Solid, non-corrosive electrolyte** — no liquid acid/alkali handling issues (unlike PAFC and AFC).
3. **High power density** (~1 W/cm²) compared to other fuel cell types.
4. Uses **platinum catalysts** at both anode and cathode; ORR kinetics is the major performance bottleneck.

---

### Question 2: Why is the Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) considered the rate-limiting step in a PEMFC? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR)** at the cathode is the rate-limiting step for three reasons:

1. **Sluggish kinetics:** The ORR involves breaking the strong O=O double bond (498 kJ/mol) and transferring **4 electrons** in a single concerted step, which has a high activation energy. Its exchange current density on Pt is only **10⁻⁹ to 10⁻¹⁰ A/cm²**, about a million times smaller than that of HOR (~10⁻³ A/cm²).
2. **High Tafel slope:** This leads to large activation overpotentials (~300 – 400 mV even at low current densities), reducing the practical cell voltage from 1.229 V to ~0.7 V.
3. **Multiple competing pathways:** The ORR can proceed via the desired 4-electron pathway to water OR the parasitic 2-electron pathway to H₂O₂ (peroxide), which corrodes the membrane and reduces durability. The 4-electron pathway is kinetically disfavored, slowing the overall reaction.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module Choice]`

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the construction and working principle of a PEMFC. Discuss the role of the **Nafion membrane** and **bipolar plates** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for the open-circuit voltage of a PEMFC. Compute the OCV at 80 °C, with $p_{H_2} = 2.5$ atm and $p_{O_2} = 2.0$ atm, given that the standard cell potential is 1.229 V at 25 °C. Use the empirical temperature correction $E(T) = 1.229 - 0.000846(T - 298.15)$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A(a) — Construction and Working

**Construction (3 Marks):**

A single PEMFC consists of a **Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA)** sandwiched between two **bipolar plates**. The MEA comprises five layers:

1. **Anode GDL** — porous carbon paper, distributes H₂ uniformly.
2. **Anode catalyst layer** — Pt nanoparticles on carbon black, where HOR occurs.
3. **Proton exchange membrane** — Nafion, a perfluorosulfonic acid polymer ~50–250 µm thick.
4. **Cathode catalyst layer** — Pt nanoparticles, where ORR occurs.
5. **Cathode GDL** — porous, hydrophobic-treated carbon paper, distributes air and removes product water.

The **bipolar plates** (graphite or coated metal) contain serpentine flow channels, serve as current collectors, and remove heat via internal coolant channels.

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**

- Hydrogen enters the anode flow field, diffuses through the GDL, and reaches the Pt catalyst.
- At the anode: $\mathrm{H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-}$ (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction, HOR).
- Protons migrate through the hydrated Nafion membrane to the cathode; electrons are forced through the external circuit, producing electrical work.
- At the cathode: $\mathrm{\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O}$ (Oxygen Reduction Reaction, ORR).
- Product water is removed via the cathode exhaust; unreacted gases may be recirculated.

**Role of the Nafion Membrane (0.5 Mark):**
- It is a **proton-selective conductor**: only H⁺ ions cross; electrons, H₂, and O₂ are blocked. This selective transport is what forces electrons to do useful work in the external circuit.
- Conductivity depends on water content: the membrane must stay hydrated (≥ 80 % RH) for high conductivity (typically 0.1 S/cm at 80 °C, 100 % RH).
- The fixed –SO₃⁻ groups create hydrophilic nanochannels (~4 nm wide when fully hydrated) through which protons migrate via **Grotthuss** (hopping) and **vehicle** (with H₃O⁺) mechanisms.

**Role of the Bipolar Plates (0.5 Mark):**
- Distribute reactant gases uniformly through flow channels.
- Collect current from one cell and pass it to the next in a series stack.
- Remove heat via integrated coolant channels.
- Must be **electrically conductive, corrosion-resistant, and impermeable to gases**.

---

### Model Solution to Question A(b) — Nernst Derivation and Numerical

**Derivation (3.5 Marks):**

Starting from the relation between Gibbs free energy and electrical work:

$$ \Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G = -nFE $$

Equating the two expressions:

$$ -nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q $$

Dividing throughout by $-nF$:

$$ E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q $$

For the overall reaction $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}}$, with pure liquid water ($a_{H_2O} = 1$):

$$ Q = \dfrac{1}{a_{H_2}\,a_{O_2}^{1/2}} = \left(\dfrac{p^{\circ}}{p_{H_2}}\right)\left(\dfrac{p^{\circ}}{p_{O_2}}\right)^{1/2} $$

Substituting:

$$ E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{p^{\circ}}{p_{H_2}}\right)\left(\dfrac{p^{\circ}}{p_{O_2}}\right)^{1/2}\right] $$

$$ E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{p_{H_2}}{p^{\circ}}\right)\left(\dfrac{p_{O_2}}{p^{\circ}}\right)^{1/2}\right] $$

This is the **Nernst equation** for a PEMFC.

**Numerical Computation (3.5 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Correct the standard potential to 80 °C using the empirical relation:**

$$ T = 80^{\circ}\mathrm{C} = 353.15\ \mathrm{K} $$

$$ E^{\circ}(T) = 1.229 - 0.000846 \times (353.15 - 298.15) $$

$$ E^{\circ}(T) = 1.229 - 0.000846 \times 55 = 1.229 - 0.0465 = 1.1825\ \mathrm{V} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the Nernst equation with the given partial pressures:**

$$ E = E^{\circ}(T) + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{p_{H_2}}{p^{\circ}}\right)\left(\dfrac{p_{O_2}}{p^{\circ}}\right)^{1/2}\right] $$

With $p^{\circ} = 1$ atm, $p_{H_2} = 2.5$ atm, $p_{O_2} = 2.0$ atm, and $n = 2$:

$$ E = 1.1825 + \dfrac{8.314 \times 353.15}{2 \times 96485}\ln\!\left[2.5 \times 2.0^{1/2}\right] $$

Compute the thermal voltage:

$$ \dfrac{RT}{nF} = \dfrac{2936.85}{192970} = 0.01522\ \mathrm{V} $$

Compute the bracketed term:

$$ 2.0^{1/2} = 1.414 $$

$$ 2.5 \times 1.414 = 3.536 $$

$$ \ln(3.536) = 1.263 $$

**Step 3 — Final result:**

$$ E = 1.1825 + 0.01522 \times 1.263 = 1.1825 + 0.0192 = 1.2017\ \mathrm{V} $$

$$ \boxed{E_{OCV} \approx 1.20\ \mathrm{V}} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module Choice]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the three major **polarization losses** (activation, ohmic, concentration) in a PEMFC with suitable equations and a labeled V–i characteristic curve. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** A laboratory PEMFC delivers **0.65 V per cell** at a current density of **0.8 A/cm²** when operating at 70 °C, with H₂ and air fed at 2 atm each. Calculate: (i) the **power density** in mW/cm², (ii) the **practical cell efficiency** based on HHV, and (iii) the **volumetric H₂ consumption rate** in L/min for a 1 kW stack with 50 cells. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B(a) — Three Polarization Losses

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The actual operating cell voltage $V_{cell}$ of a PEMFC is always lower than the Nernst (reversible) voltage $E$ due to three irreversible overpotentials:

$$ V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc} $$

**1. Activation Loss (η_act) — ~2 Marks:**

The activation overpotential arises from the energy barrier of charge transfer at the electrode-electrolyte interface. It is most significant at the **cathode** because the ORR is kinetically slow. At high overpotentials, it follows the **Tafel equation**:

$$ \eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right) $$

- $i$ = current density (A/cm²)
- $i_0$ = exchange current density (≈ 10⁻⁹ A/cm² for ORR on Pt)
- $\alpha$ = charge transfer coefficient (≈ 0.5)

This region dominates at **i < 0.1 A/cm²** and produces the characteristic curved drop in the polarization curve.

**2. Ohmic Loss (η_ohmic) — ~2 Marks:**

Ohmic loss follows **Ohm's Law** and arises from the resistance to proton flow through the membrane and electron flow through electrodes, GDL, and bipolar plates:

$$ \eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot R_{ASR} $$

- $R_{ASR}$ = area-specific resistance (Ω·cm²), typically 0.05 – 0.15 Ω·cm² for a well-hydrated Nafion 117 membrane at 80 °C.

This region appears **linear** in the V–i curve at intermediate current densities (0.1 – 1.0 A/cm²).

**3. Concentration Loss (η_conc) — ~2 Marks:**

At high current densities, the rate of reactant consumption at the catalyst exceeds the rate of gas diffusion through the GDL, leading to a sharp voltage drop. The concentration overpotential is given by:

$$ \eta_{conc} = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right) $$

- $i_L$ = limiting current density, when $i \rightarrow i_L$, the voltage collapses to zero.

**Polarization Curve (V–i) Sketch — Verbal Description (full marks requires a sketch with three labeled regions):**

- At $i = 0$: voltage ≈ 1.0 V (OCV, slightly below 1.229 V due to mixed potential and crossover).
- A rapid exponential drop at low $i$ (activation region).
- A linear drop at intermediate $i$ (ohmic region).
- A sharp drop at high $i$ approaching $i_L$ (concentration region).

---

### Model Solution to Question B(b) — Numerical Performance Calculation

**Given:**
- $V_{cell} = 0.65$ V
- $i = 0.8$ A/cm²
- $T = 70$ °C = 343 K
- $p_{H_2} = p_{O_2} = 2$ atm
- Stack: 50 cells, $P_{stack} = 1$ kW = 1000 W

**(i) Power Density (2 Marks):**

$$ p = V_{cell} \times i = 0.65\ \mathrm{V} \times 0.8\ \mathrm{A/cm^2} = 0.52\ \mathrm{W/cm^2} $$

$$ \boxed{p = 520\ \mathrm{mW/cm^2}} $$

**(ii) Practical Cell Efficiency Based on HHV (2.5 Marks):**

For a hydrogen-fueled cell, the HHV-based cell efficiency is:

$$ \eta_{cell,\,HHV} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{1.482} $$

The factor **1.482 V** is the **thermo-neutral voltage** corresponding to $\Delta H_{HHV}/(nF) = 285800/(2 \times 96485) = 1.481$ V. This represents the voltage equivalent of the total chemical energy (work + heat) released by the HHV reaction.

$$ \eta_{cell} = \dfrac{0.65}{1.482} = 0.4386 = 43.86\ \% $$

$$ \boxed{\eta_{cell} \approx 43.9\ \%} $$

**(iii) Volumetric H₂ Consumption Rate (2.5 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Total current drawn by the stack:**

$$ I = \dfrac{P_{stack}}{N_{cells} \times V_{cell}} = \dfrac{1000}{50 \times 0.65} = 30.77\ \mathrm{A} $$

**Step 2 — Molar consumption rate of H₂ (from Faraday's law):**

$$ \dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I}{nF} = \dfrac{30.77}{2 \times 96485} = 1.595 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{mol/s} $$

**Step 3 — Convert to volumetric flow at the operating conditions (343 K, 2 atm):**

Using the ideal gas law, $V = nRT/p$:

$$ \dot{V}_{H_2} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2} RT}{p} = \dfrac{1.595 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.314 \times 343}{2 \times 101325} $$

$$ \dot{V}_{H_2} = \dfrac{0.4549}{202650} = 2.245 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m^3/s} $$

Convert to L/min:

$$ \dot{V}_{H_2} = 2.245 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 \times 60 = 0.1347\ \mathrm{L/min} $$

$$ \boxed{\dot{V}_{H_2} \approx 0.135\ \mathrm{L/min}} $$

**Verification using stoichiometric ratio:** A 1 kW PEMFC at 50 % efficiency consumes roughly 8 g H₂ per kWh. For 1 kW running for 1 hour, that's 8 g ≈ 89.6 L at STP, or about 0.13 L/min at operating conditions — consistent with our 0.135 L/min result. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse HHV with LHV in efficiency problems.** PEMFCs below 100 °C produce *liquid* water, so use **η = V_cell / 1.482** (HHV-based). If you use 1.229 V (reversible), you will compute a fictitious "reversible efficiency" that does not reflect the actual energy release.
> 2. **Forgetting the charge transfer coefficient (α) in Tafel problems.** Many students write $\eta = (RT/nF)\ln(i/i_0)$ — this is **wrong**. The correct form is $\eta = (RT/\alpha nF)\ln(i/i_0)$ with $\alpha \approx 0.5$. This single error costs ~1 mark.
> 3. **Pressure units in Nernst problems.** Always use **absolute pressure** (atm or Pa), never gauge pressure. The reference state $p^{\circ} = 1$ atm appears in the logarithm.
> 4. **Water management is not optional discussion.** If a question asks "challenges of PEMFC operation," you **must** mention water management (membrane drying vs. cathode flooding). Examiners specifically test this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PEMFC = Low-T (60–100 °C) fuel cell using a proton-conducting polymer membrane** (Nafion). The defining feature is the **solid, ion-selective electrolyte**.
- **Overall reaction:** $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O}$, $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V at 25 °C.
- **The MEA** = Anode GDL + Anode CL + Membrane + Cathode CL + Cathode GDL, sandwiched between **bipolar plates**.
- **HOR** at the anode is fast; **ORR** at the cathode is the **rate-limiting step** (low exchange current density ~10⁻⁹ A/cm², Tafel slope dominates).
- **Proton transport** through Nafion occurs via **Grotthuss (hopping) + Vehicle (with H₃O⁺) mechanisms**; requires **fully hydrated** nanochannels.
- **Three voltage losses:** Activation (Tafel, log), Ohmic (linear, R·i), Concentration (mass-transport limit, log term with $i_L$).
- **Water management is critical:** drying → membrane failure; flooding → mass-transport failure. Optimal: 80–100 % RH.
- **Practical efficiency** = 50–60 % (electrical only), 80–85 % (CHP with heat recovery). Far exceeds Carnot-limited heat engines at this temperature.
- **Key advantages:** High power density, fast start-up, solid electrolyte (no leaks), scalable.
- **Key challenges:** Pt cost, water management, CO poisoning (< 10 ppm tolerance), H₂ storage and infrastructure.
- **Primary applications:** Fuel Cell Vehicles (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo), backup power, drones, forklifts, portable electronics.
- **Nernst equation** for PEMFC: $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{p_{H_2}}{p^{\circ}}\right)\left(\dfrac{p_{O_2}}{p^{\circ}}\right)^{1/2}\right]$ — pressure increase raises OCV (~14 mV per atmosphere).
- **Tafel equation:** $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha nF}\ln(i/i_0)$ with $\alpha \approx 0.5$.
- **Memorize the constants:** $F = 96485$ C/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $RT/F = 25.69$ mV at 25 °C, $RT/(nF) = 12.85$ mV at 25 °C.
- **Stack power formula:** $P = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$; increasing $N$ linearly scales voltage, increasing $I$ scales current.
- **H₂ consumption:** $ \dot{n}_{H_2} = I/(nF) $; convert to volumetric flow using ideal gas law at the operating T and p.
- **Future trends:** Reducing Pt loading (already < 0.1 mg/cm² in lab demos), high-temperature membranes (> 100 °C operation with phosphoric-acid-doped PBI), anion-exchange membrane (AEMFC) and HT-PEMFC variants.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methanol Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Methanol Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Methanol Fuel Cell (DMFC):** A sub-class of Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells (PEMFC) in which **liquid methanol (CH₃OH)** is fed directly to the anode as a fuel, where it is electrochemically oxidized without an external reforming step. The cell converts the chemical energy of methanol directly into electrical energy, with water and carbon dioxide as by-products, producing a theoretical open-circuit voltage of **E° = 1.21 V** at standard conditions.

The DMFC belongs to the family of low-temperature fuel cells operating typically between **60 °C and 100 °C** and uses a **Polymer Electrolyte Membrane (PEM)** — most commonly a perfluorosulfonic acid (Nafion) membrane — as the proton conductor.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Liquid Hydrogen in a Bottle"

Imagine you want to power a laptop, but storing gaseous hydrogen in a heavy, pressurized cylinder is unsafe and bulky. Now imagine instead a refillable **plastic bottle filled with a transparent liquid** (methanol) that you can simply pour into your device, much like refilling a printer's ink cartridge. Inside the device, this liquid is *electrochemically "burned"* at room temperature without a flame, and out comes electricity, a little water, and harmless CO₂.

> [!NOTE]
> **Methanol is often called a "liquid carrier of hydrogen"** because each methanol molecule (CH₃OH) can be thought of as one carbon atom carrying **three hydrogen atoms in a chemically bound, energy-rich form**. Its volumetric energy density (~4820 Wh/L LHV) is roughly **10× higher than compressed hydrogen at 700 bar**, which is precisely why DMFCs are attractive for portable electronics.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday Constant | $F$ | **96,485** | C mol⁻¹ |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ |
| Standard Cell Temperature | $T$ | **298.15** | K |
| Number of Electrons Transferred | $n$ | **6** | – |
| Standard Gibbs Free Energy (CH₃OH oxidation) | $\Delta G^\circ$ | **−702** | kJ mol⁻¹ |
| Standard Cell EMF | $E^\circ$ | **1.21** | V |
| Lower Heating Value of Methanol | LHV | **6.09** | kWh kg⁻¹ |
| Volumetric Energy Density | – | **4820** | Wh L⁻¹ |
| Operating Temperature Range | $T_{op}$ | **60 – 100** | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Methanol is a **liquid at room temperature** (boiling point 64.7 °C), making it one of the few fuels that can be **stored, transported, and refilled using existing liquid-fuel infrastructure** (pumps, tanks, pipelines). This is a major exam-relevant point.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A DMFC consists of a **methanol-fed anode**, an **air-fed cathode**, separated by a proton-exchange membrane. The logical sequence of operation is:

1. **Anode Feed:** A dilute aqueous methanol solution (typically **1 M CH₃OH**) is supplied to the anode flow channel.
2. **Electrochemical Oxidation (MOR):** At the anode catalyst (usually **Pt-Ru/C**), methanol reacts with water to release **6 electrons, 6 protons, and CO₂**:
   $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6e^-$$
3. **Proton Migration:** The protons (H⁺) travel through the hydrated Nafion membrane toward the cathode.
4. **Cathode Reduction (ORR):** At the cathode catalyst (Pt/C), oxygen from air combines with the protons and electrons (which travel through the external circuit) to form water:
   $$\tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6e^- \longrightarrow 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
5. **Overall Cell Reaction:** Adding the two half-reactions:
   $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
6. **External Circuit:** Electrons flowing from anode → load → cathode deliver usable electrical work.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Pt-Ru Anode Catalyst

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pure platinum surfaces get **poisoned by the CO intermediate** (–COOH → CO) that forms during methanol oxidation. Adding **ruthenium** provides oxygen-containing species (Ru–OH) at lower potentials that strip CO from Pt, regenerating active sites. This is the **bi-functional mechanism** of Pt-Ru catalysts — a *favourite KTU 2-mark question*.

### 2.3 Key Engineering Challenges

- **Methanol Crossover:** A fraction of unreacted methanol diffuses directly through the membrane to the cathode, where it is oxidized parasitically, lowering voltage and wasting fuel. Solutions include *thicker membranes*, *lower methanol concentration* (1 M or 2 M), and *novel barrier layers*.
- **Slow Anode Kinetics:** The methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) is **~100× slower** than hydrogen oxidation, demanding high catalyst loadings.
- **Water Management:** At the cathode, water must be removed; at the anode, water is consumed, so anode flooding and cathode drying must both be controlled.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles every equation required to solve KTU numerical questions on Methanol Fuel Cells.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^\circ = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$ | Standard EMF from Gibbs free energy |
| 2 | $E^\circ = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ}{nF}$ | EMF from enthalpy–entropy balance |
| 3 | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | **Nernst Equation** for non-standard conditions |
| 4 | $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{6F}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{a_{CH_3OH}\, a_{O_2}^{1.5}}{a_{CO_2}\, a_{H_2O}^{2}}\right]$ | Nernst eqn. applied to DMFC half-reactions |
| 5 | $\eta_{thermo} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H^\circ} \times 100\%$ | Thermodynamic (Carnot-free) efficiency |
| 6 | $\eta_{voltage} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{1.21} \times 100\%$ | Voltage efficiency relative to $E^\circ$ |
| 7 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{thermo} \times \eta_{voltage} \times \eta_{fuel}$ | Combined real-world efficiency |
| 8 | $\rho_{energy} = \dfrac{nFE^\circ}{M_{CH_3OH}} = \dfrac{6 \times 96485 \times 1.21}{0.03204}$ | Theoretical specific energy (J/kg) |
| 9 | $P_{cell} = V_{cell} \times I \times N$ | Stack power with $N$ cells |
| 10 | $V_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell}$ | Stack voltage for series connection |

> **Note on units:** $n=6$ electrons, $M_{CH_3OH}=32.04$ g mol⁻¹, $F=96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

DMFCs are already powering **portable military radios (US Army), laptop prototypes (Toshiba Dynario, 2009), backup telecom units, and small-scale auxiliary power units (APUs) for boats/RVs**. Unlike batteries, they offer **instant refuelling** by simply topping up the methanol cartridge.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Standard EMF of the DMFC from First-Principles Thermodynamics

We start from the **fundamental relation between Gibbs free energy and reversible electrical work**:

$$
W_{elec} = -\Delta G^\circ = nFE^\circ
$$

Therefore,

$$
E^\circ = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify standard thermodynamic data for the DMFC overall reaction at 298.15 K:**

| Species | $\Delta G_f^\circ$ (kJ mol⁻¹) | $\Delta H_f^\circ$ (kJ mol⁻¹) | $S^\circ$ (J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| CH₃OH (l) | −166.2 | −238.7 | 127.2 |
| O₂ (g) | 0 | 0 | 205.1 |
| CO₂ (g) | −394.4 | −393.5 | 213.7 |
| H₂O (l) | −237.1 | −285.8 | 69.9 |

**Step 2 — Compute the standard Gibbs free energy change of the overall cell reaction:**
$$
\text{CH}_3\text{OH}_{(l)} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_{2(g)} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_{2(g)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^\circ &= \left[\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) + 2\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O})\right] - \left[\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{OH}) + \tfrac{3}{2}\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{O}_2)\right] \\
&= \left[(-394.4) + 2(-237.1)\right] - \left[(-166.2) + \tfrac{3}{2}(0)\right]\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1} \\
&= \left[-394.4 - 474.2\right] - \left[-166.2\right]\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1} \\
&= -868.6 + 166.2\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1} \\
&= -702.4\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the EMF relation** with $n=6$ and $F=96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E^\circ &= -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} \\
&= -\frac{(-702.4 \times 10^3\;\text{J mol}^{-1})}{6 \times 96{,}485\;\text{C mol}^{-1}} \\
&= \frac{702{,}400}{578{,}910}\;\text{V} \\
&\approx 1.213\;\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rounded standard value used in textbooks: E° ≈ 1.21 V.** The agreement with the rigorous calculation is excellent.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermodynamic (Carnot) Efficiency of the DMFC

**Step 1 — Enthalpy change of the overall reaction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H^\circ &= \left[(-393.5) + 2(-285.8)\right] - \left[(-238.7) + \tfrac{3}{2}(0)\right]\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1} \\
&= [-393.5 - 571.6] - [-238.7] \\
&= -965.1 + 238.7 \\
&= -726.4\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Ratio of Gibbs to enthalpy (= thermodynamic efficiency):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{thermo} &= \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H^\circ} = \frac{702.4}{726.4} \\
&\approx 0.967 = 96.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Methanol's thermodynamic efficiency is **much higher than that of heat engines (≤60% Carnot)** because the DMFC bypasses the thermal cycle. However, the practical (voltage) efficiency drops to ~30–40% at typical operating current densities of 0.2 A cm⁻².

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Application of the Nernst Equation to a Real DMFC

**Problem (KTU-style):** A DMFC operates at **T = 343 K (70 °C)** with partial pressures $P_{CH_3OH}=0.1$ atm, $P_{O_2}=0.21$ atm, $P_{CO_2}=0.05$ atm, and liquid activities $a_{H_2O}=1$, $a_{CH_3OH}\approx 1$ (pure liquid reference). Calculate the cell voltage.

**Step 1 — Write the Nernst expression for the overall reaction** (note the product-over-reactant convention):

$$
E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left[\frac{a_{CH_3OH}\, a_{O_2}^{3/2}}{a_{CO_2}\, a_{H_2O}^{2}}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Insert numerical values** with $R=8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, $T=343$ K, $n=6$, $F=96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹:

$$
\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 343}{6 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{2851.7}{578{,}910} \approx 4.926 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithmic term** (note $a_{H_2O}=1$ drops out):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln Q &= \ln\!\left[\frac{1 \times (0.21)^{1.5}}{0.05 \times 1^{2}}\right] \\
&= \ln\!\left[\frac{0.0962}{0.05}\right] \\
&= \ln(1.924) \approx 0.654
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final voltage calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 1.21 + (4.926 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.654) \\
&= 1.21 + 0.00322 \\
&\approx 1.213\;\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Nernst correction is **small (≤5 mV)** because the standard-state reference already assumes unit activities. The dominant voltage losses in a real DMFC are **activation (anode kinetics), ohmic (membrane resistance), and concentration (mass transport) overpotentials** — collectively reducing the working voltage to **0.3 – 0.5 V** at useful current densities.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DMFC vs Other Fuel Cells

| Property | DMFC | PEMFC (H₂) | SOFC (H₂) | AFC (H₂) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fuel | Liquid CH₃OH | Gaseous H₂ | Gaseous H₂/CH₄ | Gaseous H₂ |
| Electrolyte | Nafion (Polymer) | Nafion | YSZ (Ceramic) | KOH (Liquid) |
| Operating Temp | 60 – 100 °C | 60 – 80 °C | 600 – 1000 °C | 50 – 200 °C |
| Charge Carrier | H⁺ | H⁺ | O²⁻ | OH⁻ |
| Std. EMF (V) | **1.21** | 1.23 | 1.04 – 1.18 | 1.23 |
| Power Density | 50 – 200 mW/cm² | 500 – 1000 mW/cm² | 200 – 500 mW/cm² | 100 – 300 mW/cm² |
| Key Advantage | Liquid fuel, easy refuelling | High power density | Fuel flexibility | Low-cost catalyst |
| Key Disadvantage | Methanol crossover | H₂ storage | Slow start-up | CO₂ poisoning |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of a Direct Methanol Fuel Cell (DMFC)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FuelSupply["Fuel Supply Loop"]
        MeOHtank["1M Methanol Tank"]
        MeOHpump["Circulation Pump"]
    end

    subgraph AnodeSide["Anode Side MOR"]
        AnFlowPlate["Anode Flow Plate"]
        AnCat["Pt-Ru Anode Catalyst"]
        AnGDL["Anode Gas Diffusion Layer"]
    end

    subgraph MembraneCore["Membrane Electrode Assembly MEA"]
        Nafion["Nafion 117 PEM Hydrated"]
    end

    subgraph CathodeSide["Cathode Side ORR"]
        CaGDL["Cathode Gas Diffusion Layer"]
        CaCat["Pt Cathode Catalyst"]
        CaFlowPlate["Cathode Flow Plate"]
    end

    subgraph OxidantSupply["Oxidant Supply"]
        AirBlower["Air Blower"]
        AirIntake["Ambient Air Inlet"]
    end

    subgraph ExternalCircuit["External Load Circuit"]
        LoadLamp["Electrical Load Lamp or DC Device"]
        AnLead["Anode Lead Wire"]
        CaLead["Cathode Lead Wire"]
    end

    subgraph Byproducts["By-product Streams"]
        CO2vent["CO2 Vent Anode"]
        H2Ovent["H2O Vent Cathode"]
    end

    MeOHtank --> MeOHpump
    MeOHpump --> AnFlowPlate
    AnFlowPlate --> AnGDL
    AnGDL --> AnCat
    AnCat -->|Produces H+ e- and CO2| Nafion
    AnCat --> CO2vent
    AirIntake --> AirBlower
    AirBlower --> CaFlowPlate
    CaFlowPlate --> CaGDL
    CaGDL --> CaCat
    Nafion -->|H+ ions migrate| CaCat
    CaCat -->|Forms H2O| H2Ovent
    AnCat --> AnLead
    AnLead --> LoadLamp
    LoadLamp --> CaLead
    CaLead --> CaCat
```

### 4.2 Electron and Ion Pathway Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph StepA["STEP 1 Anode Oxidation"]
        R1["CH3OH plus H2O"]
        R2["CO2 plus 6H plus 6e"]
    end

    subgraph StepB["STEP 2 Proton Conduction"]
        R3["H plus ions cross hydrated Nafion membrane"]
    end

    subgraph StepC["STEP 3 Cathode Reduction"]
        R4["3 by 2 O2 plus 6H plus 6e"]
        R5["3 H2O plus Electricity"]
    end

    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> R5

    note1["e travel through external circuit doing work"] -.-> R3
    note2["CO2 is vented at anode"] -.-> R2
    note3["Water is produced at cathode"] -.-> R5
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram of a Single DMFC Unit

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TopPlate["Anode Current Collector Plate"]
        AP1["Graphite or Metal Bipolar Plate with Serpentine Channels"]
    end

    subgraph AnodeLayer["Anode Catalyst Layer"]
        AP2["Pt-Ru on Carbon Black 2 to 4 mg per cm squared"]
    end

    subgraph Membrane["Polymer Electrolyte Membrane"]
        AP3["Nafion 117 Thickness 175 to 220 micrometers"]
    end

    subgraph CathodeLayer["Cathode Catalyst Layer"]
        AP4["Pt on Carbon Black 1 to 2 mg per cm squared"]
    end

    subgraph BottomPlate["Cathode Current Collector Plate"]
        AP5["Graphite Plate with Air Channels"]
    end

    AP1 --> AP2
    AP2 --> AP3
    AP3 --> AP4
    AP4 --> AP5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Methanol crossover** is represented schematically in **Step 2**: a small fraction of unreacted CH₃OH molecules diffuse through the membrane *alongside* the H⁺ ions, reaching the cathode where they are oxidized parasitically. This is a critical loss mechanism and **frequently tested** in KTU vivas.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define a Direct Methanol Fuel Cell (DMFC). List **any two advantages** and **any one disadvantage** of using methanol as a fuel in fuel cells.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** A DMFC is a polymer-electrolyte-membrane fuel cell in which liquid methanol is fed directly to the anode, where it is electrochemically oxidized to CO₂, releasing protons and electrons that combine with oxygen at the cathode to produce electricity and water.
- **Advantages (1 Mark each, any two):**
  1. Methanol is a **liquid at room temperature**, allowing easy storage, transport, and refuelling using existing liquid-fuel infrastructure.
  2. High theoretical energy density (**~6.09 kWh kg⁻¹ LHV**) and no need for a bulky reformer.
- **Disadvantage (1 Mark):** **Methanol crossover** through the Nafion membrane reduces cell efficiency and voltage, and the slow methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) kinetics demand high Pt-Ru catalyst loadings.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Write the **half-cell reactions** and the **overall reaction** for a Direct Methanol Fuel Cell. State its standard EMF value.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Anode (1 Mark):**
  $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6e^-$$
- **Cathode (1 Mark):**
  $$\tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6e^- \longrightarrow 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- **Overall (0.5 Mark):**
  $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- **Standard EMF (0.5 Mark):** $E^\circ = 1.21$ V

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module-Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **Always balance the half-reactions** with correct electron count ($n=6$). Students routinely write $n=4$ or $n=2$ — instant loss of 2 marks.
> 2. **State standard conditions** (298 K, 1 atm, pure liquids with unit activity) before applying the Nernst equation.
> 3. **For Pt-Ru catalyst explanation**, use the exact phrase **"bi-functional mechanism"** — examiners look for this keyword.
> 4. **Methanol crossover** must be discussed as both a *fuel-wasting* and a *cathode-poisoning* phenomenon. Mentioning only one aspect loses 1 mark.
> 5. **In numericals**, never forget to convert $\Delta G$ from kJ to J before dividing by $nF$.

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose either A or B*

**(a)** Derive the **standard EMF** of a DMFC from the standard Gibbs free energy of formation values given below, and hence calculate the **thermodynamic efficiency** of the cell at 298 K.
(7 Marks)

$$\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{OH}, l) = -166.2\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1},\quad \Delta G_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = -237.1\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$
$$\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2, g) = -394.4\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1},\quad \Delta G_f^\circ(\text{O}_2, g) = 0$$

Given: $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{OH}) = -238.7$ kJ mol⁻¹, $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}) = -285.8$ kJ mol⁻¹, $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) = -393.5$ kJ mol⁻¹.

**(b)** With the help of a **labelled schematic**, describe the **construction and working** of a Direct Methanol Fuel Cell. Explain the role of the **Pt-Ru anode catalyst** and discuss the **methanol crossover** problem. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — EMF Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Identify the overall cell reaction:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH}_{(l)} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_{2(g)} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_{2(g)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Calculate $\Delta G^\circ$ using formation values:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^\circ &= \left[\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) + 2\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O})\right] - \left[\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{OH}) + \tfrac{3}{2}\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{O}_2)\right] \\
&= \left[(-394.4) + 2(-237.1)\right] - \left[(-166.2) + 0\right] \\
&= [-394.4 - 474.2] - [-166.2] \\
&= -702.4\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Apply $E^\circ = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$ with $n=6$, $F=96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹:
$$
E^\circ = \frac{702.4 \times 10^3}{6 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{702{,}400}{578{,}910} \approx 1.213\;\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Calculate $\Delta H^\circ$ for thermodynamic efficiency:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H^\circ &= [(-393.5) + 2(-285.8)] - [(-238.7) + 0] \\
&= -726.4\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Thermodynamic efficiency:
$$
\eta_{thermo} = \frac{\vert\Delta G^\circ\vert}{\vert\Delta H^\circ\vert} = \frac{702.4}{726.4} = 0.967 = 96.7\%
$$

**Final Answer (boxed for the examiner):** $E^\circ \approx \mathbf{1.21\;\text{V}}$ and $\eta_{thermo} \approx \mathbf{96.7\%}$.

---

**Part (b) — Construction, Working, Pt-Ru, Methanol Crossover (7 Marks):**

**Construction (2 Marks):** A DMFC consists of (i) **anode current-collector plate** with methanol flow channels, (ii) **anode catalyst layer** (Pt-Ru on carbon, 2–4 mg cm⁻²), (iii) **Nafion 117 proton-exchange membrane** (175–220 µm thick), (iv) **cathode catalyst layer** (Pt/C, 1–2 mg cm⁻²), (v) **cathode current-collector plate** with air channels.

**Working (2 Marks):** Dilute aqueous methanol (1 M) is fed to the anode, where MOR occurs producing 6 e⁻, 6 H⁺, and CO₂. Protons migrate through the membrane; electrons flow through the external circuit performing work. At the cathode, oxygen is reduced, combining with protons and electrons to form water.

**Role of Pt-Ru (1.5 Marks):** Pure Pt is **poisoned by CO intermediates** formed during MOR. **Ruthenium** provides –OH groups at lower potentials that oxidatively strip CO from Pt — the **bi-functional mechanism** — thereby maintaining catalyst activity.

**Methanol Crossover (1.5 Marks):** A portion of unreacted methanol **diffuses through the Nafion membrane** to the cathode, where it is **parasitically oxidized**, causing (i) fuel wastage, (ii) cathode catalyst poisoning, (iii) mixed-potential reduction of cathode voltage. Mitigation strategies include *thicker membranes*, *low-concentration methanol* (1 M), and *novel barrier coatings* (e.g., Pd, ZrO₂).

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **thermodynamic basis** of the DMFC. Derive the **Nernst equation** for the cell operating at a non-standard temperature of **353 K** with the partial pressures:
$P_{CH_3OH}=0.15$ atm, $P_{O_2}=0.21$ atm, $P_{CO_2}=0.10$ atm. Take $E^\circ=1.21$ V and $n=6$. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compare the **performance, advantages, and applications** of DMFCs with those of **conventional PEMFCs** and **Solid Oxide Fuel Cells (SOFCs)**. What makes DMFCs particularly suited for **portable electronics**? (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Nernst at 353 K (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** The Nernst equation links cell voltage to non-standard activities:
$$E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left[\frac{a_{CH_3OH}\, a_{O_2}^{3/2}}{a_{CO_2}\, a_{H_2O}^{2}}\right]$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Substitute $T = 353$ K, $n = 6$, $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹, $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹:
$$
\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{6 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{2934.8}{578{,}910} \approx 5.069 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Evaluate the activity quotient (with $a_{H_2O}=1$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{a_{CH_3OH}\, (P_{O_2}/1\text{ atm})^{1.5}}{(P_{CO_2}/1\text{ atm})\, (1)^{2}} \\
&= \frac{1 \times (0.21)^{1.5}}{0.10} \\
&= \frac{0.0962}{0.10} = 0.962
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Evaluate $\ln Q = \ln(0.962) = -0.0388$.

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Final voltage:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 1.21 + (5.069 \times 10^{-3})(-0.0388) \\
&= 1.21 - 1.97 \times 10^{-4} \\
&\approx 1.2098\;\text{V} \approx 1.21\;\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The small change confirms that **DMFC voltage is weakly dependent** on pressure variations in the practical operating range, but the Nernst slope increases with temperature.

---

**Part (b) — Comparative Study (7 Marks):**

| Aspect | DMFC | PEMFC (H₂) | SOFC (H₂/CH₄) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fuel State | **Liquid** (CH₃OH) | Gaseous (H₂) | Gaseous/Liquid |
| Operating Temp | 60 – 100 °C | 60 – 80 °C | 600 – 1000 °C |
| Power Density | 50 – 200 mW/cm² | 500 – 1000 mW/cm² | 200 – 500 mW/cm² |
| Start-up Time | Minutes (1 Mark) | Minutes | **Hours** (1 Mark) |
| Refuelling | Cartridge swap (1 Mark) | H₂ compression | H₂/CH₄ line |
| Cost Factor | Membrane + catalyst | Membrane + catalyst | Ceramic electrolyte |

**Why DMFCs suit portable electronics (3 Marks):**

1. **Liquid fuel = high energy density by volume** (~4820 Wh/L) — small, light cartridge can run a laptop for hours. *(1 Mark)*
2. **Instant refuelling** by swapping the cartridge — no waiting for recharge (unlike batteries). *(1 Mark)*
3. **Low operating temperature** (60–100 °C) → safe for handheld devices, no thermal insulation, fast start-up. *(1 Mark)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Summary — Memorize for KTU 2024:**

- ✅ **Anode (MOR):** $\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6e^-$ — always balanced with **6 electrons**.
- ✅ **Cathode (ORR):** $\tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6e^- \to 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- ✅ **Overall:** $\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- ✅ **Standard EMF:** $E^\circ = 1.21$ V ; **Thermodynamic efficiency:** ~96.7%.
- ✅ **Nernst slope at 298 K:** $RT/nF = 4.27$ mV (for $n=6$).
- ✅ **Anode catalyst:** Pt-Ru (bi-functional mechanism for CO tolerance).
- ✅ **Cathode catalyst:** Pt.
- ✅ **Membrane:** Hydrated Nafion (proton conductor).
- ✅ **Operating temperature:** 60 – 100 °C.
- ✅ **Key challenges:** (1) Methanol crossover, (2) slow MOR kinetics, (3) water management.
- ✅ **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹ (commit to memory).
- ✅ **Real-world products:** Toshiba Dynario (2009), UltraCell XX25 military units, SFC Energy EFoy series.
- ✅ **Why "liquid hydrogen"**? Methanol is liquid at STP with 3 bound H atoms per molecule → easier than compressed H₂.
- ✅ **Equation to derive for 7-mark questions:** $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ / nF$ (always show all three values).
- ✅ **Avoid confusion:** $n=6$ for DMFC overall, $n=2$ for PEMFC overall — students mix these up!

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the working of a DMFC", structure your answer in **three layers** — (1) Fuel + electrode reactions, (2) Charge transport (H⁺ vs e⁻ paths), (3) By-products and overall energy balance. Examiners award 1 mark for each layer and 1 mark for a clear schematic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cells ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC)** is a type of acid-based fuel cell that utilizes **liquid concentrated phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄, ~100%)** immobilized in a porous silicon carbide (SiC) matrix as its proton-conducting electrolyte. It operates in the **intermediate temperature range of 150 °C to 220 °C** (with a typical design point of **200 °C**), making it the first-generation commercially deployed fuel cell technology for stationary combined heat and power (CHP) applications. The PAFC belongs to the family of **proton-exchange fuel cells** (alongside PEMFC), but uses a **liquid acid electrolyte** rather than a hydrated polymeric membrane. The elevated operating temperature (relative to PEMFC) provides two engineering advantages: 1. Higher tolerance to **carbon monoxide (CO)** poisoning of the platinum catalyst (up to ~1–2 %). 2. Better quality waste heat for **cogeneration/CHP** systems. The standard cell EMF at standard conditions is **E⁰ = 1.229 V**, identical to all H₂/O₂ fuel cells. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Hot Lemon-Battery":** > Imagine a **lemon battery** where the acidic juice (citric acid) ionizes and allows current to flow. Now scale this idea up dramatically: replace lemon juice with **industrial-grade phosphoric acid** (the same acid used in Coca-Cola!), heat it to **200 °C** (hotter than boiling water), and place two platinum-coated carbon electrodes in it. Feed hydrogen gas on one side and air on the other. The acid acts as the **"highway"** for protons (H⁺ ions) travelling from the hydrogen side to the oxygen side, while electrons are forced through your external circuit to do useful work. The "hot" operation is the key — it makes the catalyst more tolerant to impurities, just like how a hot oven can grill a slightly soiled steak, whereas a low-temperature pan cannot. ### 1.3 Key Physical & Performance Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metrics for PAFC (Textbook Values):** > - **Operating Temperature:** **150 °C – 220 °C** (typical **200 °C**) > - **Electrolyte:** **Concentrated H₃PO₄** (≈ 100 %) in SiC matrix > - **Standard Cell Potential:** **E⁰ = 1.229 V** > - **Practical Operating Voltage:** **0.6 V – 0.8 V** per cell > - **Electrical Efficiency:** **37 % – 42 %** > - **CHP Efficiency:** Up to **85 %** > - **CO Tolerance:** **~1 % – 2 %** (vs. <50 ppm for PEMFC) > - **Charge Carrier:** **H⁺ (proton)** > - **Catalyst Loading:** **~0.3 mg Pt/cm²** (anode), **~0.5 mg Pt/cm²** (cathode) > - **Faraday's Constant:** **F = 96,485 C/mol** > - **Gas Constant:** **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** > - **Commercial Stack Power:** **200 kW – 250 kW** (e.g., UTC PC25™) ### 1.4 Historical & Industrial Significance PAFC is historically the **first fuel cell technology to achieve commercial deployment** for stationary power. The **UTC PC25** unit (200 kW) deployed in the 1990s powered hospitals, hotels, and office buildings worldwide, serving as the proving ground for all modern fuel cell industries. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The PAFC operates on the principle of **reverse electrolysis of water**. The acidic electrolyte permits only the migration of **H⁺ protons**, while electrons are blocked and forced through the external load. **Step 1 — Fuel Delivery:** Pressurized, humidified **H₂** gas is fed into the **anode flow field**, diffusing through a porous **gas diffusion layer (GDL)** to reach the platinum catalyst sites. **Step 2 — Anode Half-Reaction (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction — HOR):** At the triple-phase boundary (gas–catalyst–electrolyte), hydrogen molecules adsorb onto platinum and dissociate: $$\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$ - **Standard Potential:** E⁰_anode = 0.000 V (SHE) - **Why platinum?** Because Pt has the optimal binding energy for H-adatoms (Sabatier principle). **Step 3 — Proton Migration Through Electrolyte:** The generated **H⁺ ions** migrate through the phosphoric acid electrolyte (ionically conductive, electronically insulating) toward the cathode side. Electrons cannot pass through the electrolyte and are routed through the external circuit. **Step 4 — Cathode Half-Reaction (Oxygen Reduction Reaction — ORR):** At the cathode, oxygen from air combines with the protons and electrons: $$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ - **Standard Potential:** E⁰_cathode = +1.229 V (SHE) - Water is produced as **steam** (since T > 100 °C) and is swept out by the cathode exhaust. **Step 5 — Overall Cell Reaction:** $$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \quad \Delta G^\circ = -237.2\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ $$\Delta H^\circ = -285.8\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ **Step 6 — Thermal Energy & Water Management:** At 200 °C, water exits as vapor. This high-grade waste heat (and the cell's own resistive heating) is captured in CHP systems to drive absorption chillers or generate additional low-pressure steam. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **every equation** a student must memorize for PAFC derivations and numerical problems in KTU 2024 ESE. | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use | |---|---------|----------------|-----------------| | 1 | $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$ | ΔG⁰ = Gibbs free energy, n = electrons transferred, F = Faraday constant, E⁰ = standard EMF | Theoretical max electrical work | | 2 | $\Delta H^\circ = -nF \cdot \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{\eta_{th}}$ | ΔH⁰ = enthalpy change, η_th = thermal efficiency | Energy balance | | 3 | $E_{cell} = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | Q = reaction quotient, T = operating temp in K | Nernst equation for actual cell voltage | | 4 | $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$ | η_act = activation overpotential, η_ohmic = IR drop, η_conc = concentration loss | Actual cell voltage under load | | 5 | $\eta_{electrical} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H^\circ} = \dfrac{237.2}{285.8}$ | Electrical (HHV) efficiency | Maximum theoretical efficiency = 83 % | | 6 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$ | T in Kelvin | Theoretical heat-engine limit | | 7 | $\eta_{PAFC} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{1.48}$ | 1.48 V = thermoneutral voltage (ΔH/nF) | Practical efficiency estimate | | 8 | $P_{stack} = n_{cells} \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$ | n_cells = number of cells, I = current | Stack power output | | 9 | $\sigma_{H^+} = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | A = pre-exponential, E_a = activation energy | Phosphoric acid ionic conductivity (Arrhenius) | | 10 | $i = i_0 \!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right)\right]$ | i = current density, i₀ = exchange current density, α = transfer coefficient | Butler-Volmer kinetics | ### 2.3 Why Phosphoric Acid? — Engineering Justification > [!NOTE] > **Why not use sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) like a typical lead-acid battery?** > At 200 °C, sulfuric acid decomposes into SO₃ and water, is highly corrosive, and has poor oxygen solubility. **Phosphoric acid**, by contrast: > 1. Is **thermally stable up to ~220 °C** without decomposition. > 2. Has **high oxygen solubility** at the cathode (crucial for ORR kinetics). > 3. Has a **low vapor pressure** at operating temperature, minimizing acid loss. > 4. Has a **phosphate anion (PO₄³⁻)** that chemically anchors Pt catalyst particles, slowing sintering. > 5. Is **less corrosive** than HCl or H₂SO₄ to the carbon-based stack hardware. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications | Sector | Application | Power Range | Example | |--------|-------------|-------------|---------| | Hospitals | Backup + CHP | 200 kW | UTC PC25 (1990s) | | Hotels/Offices | Cogeneration | 100–400 kW | Fuji Electric FP-100E | | Telecom | Remote DC power | 5–25 kW | IdaTech/Ballard | | Wastewater Treatment | Biogas-fueled PAFC | 200 kW | Fuji/Ebara demos | | Military | Forward-base power | — | DoD-funded programs | PAFC remains the **bridge technology** between low-temperature PEMFC and high-temperature SOFC/MCFC, offering a pragmatic balance of cost, durability (40,000+ hour stack lifetimes demonstrated), and tolerance to reformer-derived hydrogen containing CO and CO₂. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical (Reversible) Cell Voltage — The Nernst Equation for PAFC **Given:** Operating temperature **T = 473 K (200 °C)**, pressure **P = 1 atm**, reactants are pure H₂ and pure O₂. **Starting thermodynamic identity:** $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$ **Reaction quotient for:** H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(l) $$Q = \dfrac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2} \cdot a_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$ At standard state, all activities = 1, so Q = 1. **Dividing both sides by −nF (where n = 2):** $$E_{cell} = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ $$\boxed{E_{cell} = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_{H_2O}}{p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)}$$ **Numerical evaluation at 200 °C (473 K), pure reactants:** Since all partial pressures equal 1 atm: $$E_{cell} = 1.229 - \dfrac{(8.314)(473)}{(2)(96485)} \ln(1)$$ $$E_{cell} = 1.229 - 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{cell} = 1.229\ \text{V}$$ This is the **reversible open-circuit voltage (OCV)** at standard conditions. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Practical Cell Voltage Including All Losses The real PAFC voltage under load is governed by four loss terms: **Step 1 — Activation Overpotential (η_act):** From the **Tafel equation**: $$\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$$ For PAFC cathode at 200 °C: α ≈ 0.5, i₀ ≈ 10⁻⁹ A/cm². **Step 2 — Ohmic Overpotential (η_ohmic):** From Ohm's law across electrolyte + electrode resistance: $$\eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot R_{internal}$$ For a typical PAFC, R_internal ≈ 0.1 Ω·cm² at 200 °C. **Step 3 — Concentration Overpotential (η_conc):** When reactant supply limits the reaction: $$\eta_{conc} = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{i_L}{i_L - i}\right)$$ where **i_L** is the limiting current density (~1 A/cm² for PAFC). **Step 4 — Combined Voltage Equation:** $$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$ **Numerical Example (current density i = 0.3 A/cm²):** $$\eta_{act} = \dfrac{(8.314)(473)}{(0.5)(96485)} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.3}{10^{-9}}\right)$$ $$= (0.0815) \cdot \ln(3 \times 10^8) = (0.0815)(19.52)$$ $$\approx 1.59\ \text{V}$$ This seems high — because the cathode ORR has very low i₀. We use the **simplified Tafel form** with an empirical Tafel slope of ~70 mV/decade for Pt in H₃PO₄: $$\eta_{act} \approx 0.07 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right) \approx 0.07 \cdot 8.48 \approx 0.59\ \text{V}$$ $$\eta_{ohmic} = (0.3)(0.1) = 0.03\ \text{V}$$ $$\eta_{conc} = \dfrac{(8.314)(473)}{(2)(96485)} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1.0}{0.7}\right) = (0.0204)(0.357) \approx 0.007\ \text{V}$$ $$V_{cell} = 1.229 - 0.59 - 0.03 - 0.007 \approx 0.60\ \text{V}$$ This matches the **typical operating voltage of ~0.6 V** for a PAFC at 0.3 A/cm². ✓ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Electrical Efficiency from Thermodynamics **Step 1:** Thermoneutral voltage: $$V_{tn} = \dfrac{-\Delta H^\circ}{nF} = \dfrac{285.8 \times 10^3}{(2)(96485)} = 1.481\ \text{V}$$ **Step 2:** Practical efficiency: $$\eta_{electrical} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{V_{tn}} = \dfrac{0.6}{1.481} \approx 40.5\ \%$$ This matches the **quoted 37–42 %** range. ✓ ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Combined Heat & Power (CHP) Efficiency **Step 1:** Total useful energy = electricity + recoverable heat: $$\eta_{CHP} = \eta_{electrical} + \eta_{thermal}$$ **Step 2:** For a PAFC at 40 % electrical and 45 % recoverable thermal: $$\eta_{CHP} = 0.40 + 0.45 = 0.85 = 85\ \%$$ **Step 3:** Compare with separate production: - Grid electricity: η_grid ≈ 35 % - Gas boiler: η_boiler ≈ 85 % - Combined separate: 0.35 × 0.85 = 29.75 % - **CHP savings:** 85 − 29.75 = **55.25 percentage points** of fuel. ### 3.5 Analytical Performance Matrix | Parameter | PAFC | PEMFC | SOFC | MCFC | |-----------|------|-------|------|------| | Electrolyte | H₃PO₄ | Nafion™ | YSZ (ZrO₂-Y₂O₃) | Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ | | Operating T (°C) | 150–220 | 60–90 | 600–1000 | 600–700 | | Charge Carrier | H⁺ | H⁺ | O²⁻ | CO₃²⁻ | | CO Tolerance | 1–2 % | <50 ppm | High | High | | Electrical Eff. | 37–42 % | 40–60 % | 50–60 % | 45–55 % | | Stack Life (h) | 40,000+ | 20,000+ | 40,000+ | 30,000+ | | Startup Time | Minutes | Seconds | Hours | Hours | | Power Density | 0.3 W/cm² | 1.0 W/cm² | 0.5 W/cm² | 0.2 W/cm² | ### 3.6 Key Overpotential Sources Explained 1. **Activation Loss (dominant at low current):** Energy barrier for H₂ dissociation or O=O bond breaking at Pt surface. Most severe at cathode (ORR is sluggish). 2. **Ohmic Loss (linear with current):** Resistance of electrolyte + electrodes + bipolar plates. In PAFC, the phosphoric acid ionic conductivity (~0.1 S/cm at 200 °C) is the limiting factor. 3. **Concentration Loss (dominant at high current):** Fuel starvation or water flooding at the catalyst sites. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — PAFC Cell Architecture & Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hydrogen Tank
H2 Supply] --> B[Anode Flow Field
Graphite Plate] B --> C[Anode GDL
Porous Carbon] C --> D[Anode Catalyst
Pt on Carbon Black] D --> E[Electrolyte Matrix
SiC with H3PO4] E --> F[Cathode Catalyst
Pt on Carbon Black] F --> G[Cathode GDL
Porous Carbon] G --> H[Cathode Flow Field
Graphite Plate] H --> I[Air Blower
O2 Supply] J[External Load
Motor Grid Bulb] --- D J -. electrons .-> F K[Waste Heat Recovery
Boiler Chiller] --- E L[Water Vapor Outlet
Product H2O] --> H ``` **Block-by-Block Functional Description:** - **A → B → C → D (Anode Side):** H₂ is humidified and delivered through a machined graphite bipolar plate, then diffuses through the porous GDL to reach Pt catalyst sites, where **H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻**. - **D → E (Ionic Path):** Protons hop through the **H₃PO₄ / SiC matrix** via the Grotthuss mechanism (proton shuttling along H-bonded phosphate chains). - **E → F → G → H (Cathode Side):** Air (O₂) diffuses through the cathode GDL to Pt sites, where **½O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂O** (released as steam). - **D → J → F (Electronic Path):** Electrons are blocked by the electrolyte and forced through the external load, generating useful DC power (later inverted to AC). - **E → K (Thermal Path):** The cell exotherm + resistive heating raises electrolyte T to 200 °C; waste heat is captured. - **H → L (Product Outlet):** Hot, humid cathode exhaust releases product water as steam. ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Voltage Loss Breakdown (Polarization Curve) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Open Circuit Voltage
E = 1.229 V] --> B[Activation Loss Region
0 to 100 mA per cm2] B --> C[Ohmic Loss Region
Linear Drop] C --> D[Concentration Loss Region
High Current Tail] D --> E[Operating Point
V approx 0.6 V at 0.3 A per cm2] F[Thermodynamic Loss
E to 1.48 V HHV] -.-> A ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block — PAFC Fuel Processing System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Natural Gas
or Methanol] --> B[Reformer
Steam Reforming] B --> C[CO Shift Reactor
H2O + CO to H2 + CO2] C --> D[Preferential Oxidation
PrOx to remove CO to under 1 percent] D --> E[Desulfurizer
ZnO Bed] E --> F[PAFC Stack
200 kW] F --> G[Power Conditioning
Inverter to AC] F --> H[Heat Recovery
Boiler Absorption Chiller] H --> I[Building Heating
or Cooling Load] ``` **Reading the System:** A hydrocarbon fuel is first reformed into a H₂-rich syngas (containing CO and CO₂). The **CO shift reactor** increases H₂ yield, and the **PrOx reactor** reduces CO to <1 % (within PAFC tolerance). Sulfur compounds (poison Pt) are removed by **ZnO beds** before the gas enters the stack. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** > *List the components of a Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC) and state the electrolyte used.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Component | Material | |-----------|----------| | Anode catalyst | Platinum (Pt) on carbon black | | Cathode catalyst | Platinum (Pt) on carbon black | | Electrolyte | Concentrated **phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄, ~100 %)** in SiC matrix | | Electrodes | Porous carbon (gas diffusion layers) | | Bipolar plates | Graphite (impervious, machined) | **Electrolyte:** **Liquid concentrated phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄)** immobilized in a porous silicon carbide (SiC) matrix. **(1 mark for stating H₃PO₄, 1 mark for SiC matrix, 1 mark for listing components)** --- > [!NOTE] > **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]** > *Write the half-cell reactions at the anode and cathode of a PAFC and derive the overall reaction.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Anode (HOR):** H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ **(1 mark)** **Cathode (ORR):** ½O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂O **(1 mark)** **Overall:** H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O (l) **(1 mark)**, ΔG° = −237.2 kJ/mol, E° = 1.229 V --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, Module 4 carries internal choice. You must answer **either Question A OR Question B** in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **(a)** Explain the working principle of a Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC) with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss the role of phosphoric acid as the electrolyte and justify why it is preferred over other acids at the operating temperature. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A PAFC stack operates at 200 °C with pure H₂ and O₂ at 1 atm. The current density is 250 mA/cm². The exchange current density at the cathode is 10⁻⁹ A/cm², the Tafel slope is 70 mV/decade, and the internal resistance is 0.12 Ω·cm². Calculate: (i) the activation overpotential, (ii) the ohmic overpotential, and (iii) the practical cell voltage. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Working Principle of PAFC (7 marks):** - **Construction:** A PAFC consists of an anode (Pt/C catalyst + porous carbon), a cathode (Pt/C catalyst + porous carbon), a phosphoric acid electrolyte immobilized in a SiC matrix, and graphite bipolar plates. **(2 marks)** - **Working:** H₂ is fed at the anode where it is oxidized to protons and electrons (**H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻**). Protons migrate through the H₃PO₄ electrolyte to the cathode, while electrons flow through the external load. At the cathode, oxygen combines with protons and electrons to form water (**½O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂O**). The overall reaction is **H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O**, with E° = 1.229 V. **(3 marks)** - **Why H₃PO₄?** Phosphoric acid is thermally stable up to 220 °C (unlike H₂SO₄ which decomposes), has high O₂ solubility (improving cathode ORR kinetics), and is less corrosive to carbon-based stack materials. Its phosphate anion also stabilizes Pt nanoparticles against sintering. **(2 marks)** **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - T = 200 °C = 473 K - i = 250 mA/cm² = 0.25 A/cm² - i₀ = 10⁻⁹ A/cm² - Tafel slope = 70 mV/decade - R_internal = 0.12 Ω·cm² **(i) Activation Overpotential:** $$\eta_{act} = (\text{Tafel slope}) \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$$ $$\eta_{act} = 0.07 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{0.25}{10^{-9}}\right)$$ $$= 0.07 \times \log_{10}(2.5 \times 10^8)$$ $$= 0.07 \times (8.398) \approx 0.588\ \text{V} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$ **(ii) Ohmic Overpotential:** $$\eta_{ohmic} = i \times R_{internal} = 0.25 \times 0.12 = 0.030\ \text{V} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ **(iii) Practical Cell Voltage:** $$V_{cell} = E^\circ - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} = 1.229 - 0.588 - 0.030$$ $$V_{cell} \approx 0.611\ \text{V} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Compare the performance characteristics of PAFC, PEMFC, and SOFC in terms of operating temperature, electrolyte, charge carrier, electrical efficiency, and CO tolerance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 200 kW PAFC power plant operates at an average cell voltage of 0.65 V with 250 cells in the stack. The lower heating value (LHV) of hydrogen is 241.8 kJ/mol. Calculate: (i) the stack current, (ii) the hydrogen consumption rate, and (iii) the electrical efficiency. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):** | Parameter | PAFC | PEMFC | SOFC | |-----------|------|-------|------| | Electrolyte | H₃PO₄ (liquid in SiC) | Nafion™ (polymer) | YSZ (ZrO₂-Y₂O₃) | | Operating T (°C) | 150–220 | 60–90 | 600–1000 | | Charge Carrier | H⁺ | H⁺ | O²⁻ | | Electrical Eff. | 37–42 % | 40–60 % | 50–60 % | | CO Tolerance | 1–2 % | <50 ppm | High | | Water Management | Steam produced, easily removed | Critical (membrane hydration) | Produced at anode | | Applications | Stationary CHP | Vehicles, backup | Large stationary, APU | **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark each for 5 parameters + 1 mark for correct application matching + 1 mark for distinguishing comment. **(7 marks)** **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - P = 200 kW = 200,000 W - V_cell = 0.65 V - N_cells = 250 - LHV(H₂) = 241.8 kJ/mol **(i) Stack Voltage and Current:** $$V_{stack} = N_{cells} \times V_{cell} = 250 \times 0.65 = 162.5\ \text{V}$$ $$I = \dfrac{P}{V_{stack}} = \dfrac{200{,}000}{162.5} \approx 1230.8\ \text{A} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ **(ii) Hydrogen Consumption Rate:** Using Faraday's law (n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol): $$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I}{nF} = \dfrac{1230.8}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$ $$\dot{n}_{H_2} \approx 6.378 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol/s} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ In mass flow: $$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 6.378 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.016 \approx 0.01286\ \text{kg/s} \approx 46.3\ \text{kg/h} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$ **(iii) Electrical Efficiency:** $$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \times LHV = 6.378 \times 10^{-3} \times 241{,}800 \approx 1542\ \text{W}$$ $$\eta_{elec} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \dfrac{200{,}000}{1{,}542{,}000} \times \text{(real input from stack) — simplified:}$$ $$\eta_{elec} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{V_{tn}} = \dfrac{0.65}{1.481} \approx 43.9\ \% \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting units:** Students often write Tafel slope in V instead of mV/decade — causing 10× error. Always convert. > 2. **Using 1.48 V incorrectly:** 1.48 V is the **thermoneutral voltage** (ΔH/nF), NOT the reversible voltage. Use 1.229 V for Nernst equation. > 3. **Confusing efficiency formulas:** Electrical efficiency = V_cell / 1.48 V (NOT V_cell / 1.229 V). The 1.229 V is the reversible voltage. > 4. **Missing the (n=2) in Faraday's law:** Always specify n = 2 for H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻. > 5. **Catalyst quantity:** PAFC uses **Pt** catalyst, not Ni or Ag (those are SOFC/MCFC). > 6. **Charge carrier confusion:** PAFC carries **H⁺** (NOT O²⁻). O²⁻ is SOFC's charge carrier. > 7. **Temperature unit mix-up:** KTU expects temperatures in **°C in text and K in equations**. Convert carefully. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — PAFC** - [ ] **Electrolyte:** Liquid concentrated **H₃PO₄** in SiC matrix **(NOT a polymer membrane)** - [ ] **Operating Temperature:** **150–220 °C** (typical 200 °C) — classifies PAFC as **intermediate-temperature** fuel cell - [ ] **Charge Carrier:** **H⁺ (proton)** — same as PEMFC - [ ] **Charge Reaction:** **H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O (l)**, ΔG° = −237.2 kJ/mol, E° = 1.229 V - [ ] **Catalyst:** Platinum (Pt) on carbon black — same catalyst as PEMFC - [ ] **CO Tolerance:** **1–2 %** (much higher than PEMFC's <50 ppm) — key PAFC advantage - [ ] **Efficiency:** Electrical **37–42 %**, CHP up to **85 %** - [ ] **Practical Cell Voltage:** **~0.6–0.7 V** at operating current density - [ ] **Water Product:** **Steam** at 200 °C (exits via cathode exhaust) - [ ] **Thermoneutral Voltage:** 1.48 V (ΔH/nF) - [ ] **Commercial Status:** **First commercialized fuel cell** (UTC PC25, 200 kW, 1990s) - [ ] **Main Application:** Stationary CHP for hospitals, hotels, offices - [ ] **Key Limitation:** Lower power density than PEMFC; corrosion of stack components by H₃PO₄ - [ ] **Standard EMF vs Practical EMF:** Nernst equation gives 1.229 V; subtract η_act + η_ohmic + η_conc to get V_cell - [ ] **Tafel Equation:** η_act = (2.303 RT/αF) × log(i/i₀) — use **70 mV/decade** as empirical slope for PAFC - [ ] **Nernst Equation:** E = E° − (RT/nF) ln Q — for H₂/O₂ at 1 atm, E = 1.229 V - [ ] **Major Loss Mechanism:** **Activation loss** at cathode (ORR) dominates at low current densities - [ ] **CHP Benefit:** Waste heat at 200 °C is high-grade enough for absorption chilling - [ ] **Difference from PEMFC:** PAFC uses **liquid acid** (higher T, CO-tolerant); PEMFC uses **polymer** (lower T, requires pure H₂) - [ ] **Difference from SOFC:** PAFC carries H⁺ at 200 °C; SOFC carries O²⁻ at 800 °C **Mnemonic for PAFC:** **"Phosphoric = Proton Hot"** — Phosphoric acid carries **P**rotons at **H**igh (200 °C) temperature. ✓ --- > **End of Module 4 — Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cells Notes** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (CO4) and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Levels L2–L4.*

topics.title: Molten Carbonate Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Molten Carbonate Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell (MCFC)** is a high-temperature (typically **600 °C to 650 °C**) electrochemical energy conversion device that belongs to the second generation of fuel cell technology. It uses a **molten mixture of alkali metal carbonates** (most commonly a eutectic blend of lithium carbonate (Li₂CO₃), potassium carbonate (K₂CO₃), and sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃)) suspended in a porous, chemically inert ceramic matrix of **Lithium-Aluminium Oxide (LiAlO₂)** as its electrolyte. The mobile charge carrier is the **carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻)** which migrates from the cathode to the anode to complete the internal circuit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Fuel Cell Technologies):**
> MCFCs are classified as a **"Molten Salt / High-Temperature"** type fuel cell and are specifically studied under the broader topic of advanced fuel cell systems alongside SOFCs (Solid Oxide Fuel Cells). Students must know the electrolyte, mobile ion, electrode reactions, operating temperature, and applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **hot, electrically conductive molten salt "sandwich"** placed between two porous metal plates. You feed **hydrogen (H₂) fuel** on one side and **oxygen from air on the other side**. The molten salt (electrolyte) is so hot that its molecules can move freely, and the **carbonate ions act as "shuttle buses"** that carry oxygen (in the form of CO₃²⁻) from the air side to the fuel side, where they help "burn" the hydrogen electrochemically to produce electricity, water, and heat — all without a flame.

A simpler way to visualise:
- **MCFC ≈ A "CO₂-Recycling Battery"** that consumes H₂ and O₂ to generate electricity while internally recycling CO₂.
- The electrolyte is **molten** (like lava) — so the system must be kept hot to remain functional.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Operating Pointers (must remember for KTU exams):**
> - Operating Temperature: **~650 °C**
> - Electrolyte: Molten (Li, K, Na)₂CO₃ in LiAlO₂ matrix
> - Charge Carrier: **CO₃²⁻** (moves from cathode → anode)
> - Fuel: H₂, CO, CH₄ (can use hydrocarbons directly via internal reforming)
> - Oxidant: Air + CO₂ mixture

## 1.3 Why MCFC is a Significant Topic in Hydrogen Energy

MCFCs hold a unique position in the **Hydrogen Energy value chain** because they:
1. Can **directly use hydrocarbon fuels** (natural gas, methane) without an external reformer — a major advantage over PAFCs and PEMFCs.
2. Produce **high-grade waste heat** suitable for combined heat and power (CHP) or bottoming-cycle turbines, raising total efficiency to **>85 % (CHP basis)**.
3. Use **non-precious metal catalysts** (Nickel-based), reducing system cost.
4. Are a critical step in the **fuel cell technology readiness ladder**: PAFC → MCFC → SOFC.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Structured Logic Steps

The MCFC operation can be broken into four sequential stages:

**Step 1: Fuel Pre-Processing / Internal Reforming (Optional)**
- If natural gas (CH₄) is used, it is reformed **inside the cell stack** at 600–650 °C via:
$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$$
- This is called **Internal Reforming (IR-MCFC)** — eliminates the need for an external reformer.

**Step 2: Anode Half-Reaction (Oxidation of Fuel)**
- At the **porous Ni-Cr anode**, hydrogen (and CO) reacts with the arriving CO₃²⁻ ions:

$$\text{H}_2 + \text{CO}_3^{2-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 + 2e^-$$

- The electrons are released to the external circuit, while H₂O and CO₂ exit via the anode exhaust.

**Step 3: Cathode Half-Reaction (Reduction of Oxidant)**
- At the **porous NiO cathode (lithiated)**, oxygen from air and recycled CO₂ combine with electrons returning from the external circuit:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{CO}_3^{2-}$$

- The newly formed CO₃²⁻ migrates through the molten carbonate electrolyte toward the anode.

**Step 4: Overall Cell Reaction**
- Summing the two half-reactions:

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{CO}_2(\text{cathode}) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2(\text{anode})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The net reaction looks identical to that of a PEMFC (H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O). However, in the MCFC, **CO₂ is consumed at the cathode and produced at the anode**, so the CO₂ must be **recycled** from the anode exhaust back to the cathode inlet — this is a defining design feature of MCFC systems.

## 2.2 Key Components & Materials

| Component | Material | Function |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Electrolyte** | 62 % Li₂CO₃ + 38 % K₂CO₃ (or Li/Na eutectic) in LiAlO₂ matrix | Conducts CO₃²⁻ ions |
| **Anode** | Porous Ni (3–6 μm pore) with 2–10 % Cr | Catalyst for H₂ oxidation, structural support |
| **Cathode** | Porous lithiated NiO (LiₓNi₁₋ₓO) | Catalyst for O₂ reduction, stable in molten carbonate |
| **Bipolar Plates (Current Collectors)** | SS-310 / SS-316 (coated with Ni) | Separate cells, distribute gases, conduct current |
| **Sealants** | Al / Ca / Sr aluminosilicate glasses | Prevent gas crossover at edges |
| **Wet Seal** | Tape-cast electrolyte rim | Provides ionic isolation between cells |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | $E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5} \cdot P_{CO_2,c}}{P_{H_2O} \cdot P_{CO_2,a}}$ | Nernst equation for MCFC (R = 8.314 J/mol·K, n = 2, F = 96485 C/mol) |
| 2 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$ | Standard Gibbs free energy (J/mol) |
| 3 | $\Delta H^{\circ} = \Delta G^{\circ} + T\Delta S^{\circ}$ | Thermodynamic identity (enthalpy = free energy + TS) |
| 4 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}} = 0.83$ (H₂) at 25 °C | Thermodynamic (reversible) efficiency |
| 5 | $\eta_{cell} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{1.482}$ | Voltage efficiency (1.482 V = HHV of H₂ per 2e⁻) |
| 6 | $\eta_{system} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_{voltage} \times \eta_{Faraday} \times \eta_{fuel-utilization}$ | Overall system efficiency (LHV or HHV basis) |
| 7 | $P_{el} = n_{cell} \times V_{cell} \times I$ | Stack electrical power output (W) |
| 8 | $V_{cell} \approx 0.7 - 0.8 \text{ V (operating)}$ | Typical MCFC working voltage (per cell) |
| 9 | $\text{OCV} \approx 1.04 - 1.10 \text{ V at 650°C}$ | Open-circuit voltage range |
| 10 | $U_f(\text{H}_2) = 75 - 85\%$, $U_f(\text{CO}_2) = 70 - 75\%$ | Fuel & CO₂ utilization ranges |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the Nernst Equation** (adapted for MCFC): The partial pressure term for CO₂ differs from PEMFC/PAFC because the charge carrier is CO₃²⁻, not H⁺. Be careful with the sign convention — a KTU examiner will look for the **correct placement of $P_{CO_2}$ in the numerator (cathode) and denominator (anode)**.

## 2.4 Why MCFC is Useful in Engineering Practice

- **Stationary Power Generation** (200 kW – 50 MW plants) for hospitals, data centres, industrial parks.
- **Cogeneration / CHP** (combined heat & power) — total efficiencies exceed 85 %.
- **Marine propulsion** (hybrid systems on submarines & cargo ships) — high temperature exhaust drives turbines.
- **CO₂ capture and concentration** — the cathode selectively absorbs CO₂, enabling industrial carbon management.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermodynamic Reversible Cell Voltage (E°cell) of MCFC

**Given:** The overall cell reaction at standard conditions (25 °C reference often used):
$$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

**Standard data at 25 °C (298.15 K):**
- $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta S^{\circ} = -163.3 \text{ J/mol·K}$
- Number of electrons transferred: $n = 2$

**Step 1 — Compute standard Gibbs free energy change:**
$$\Delta G^{\circ} = \sum \Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(\text{reactants})$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = (-237.2) - (0) - (0)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the electrochemical relationship to obtain E°:**
$$E^{\circ} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values (n = 2, F = 96485 C/mol):**

$$E^{\circ} = -\dfrac{(-237.2 \times 10^{3})}{(2)(96485)}$$

$$E^{\circ} = \dfrac{237200}{192970}$$

$$\boxed{E^{\circ} = 1.229 \text{ V at 25 °C}}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the Nernst reversible voltage at MCFC operating temperature (923 K ≈ 650 °C):**

Using the temperature coefficient:
$$\dfrac{\partial E^{\circ}}{\partial T} = -\dfrac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{nF}$$

$$\dfrac{\partial E^{\circ}}{\partial T} = -\dfrac{(-163.3)}{(2)(96485)} = +8.46 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V/K}$$

So for ΔT = 923 − 298 = 625 K:
$$\Delta E^{\circ}_T = (8.46 \times 10^{-4})(625) = 0.529 \text{ V}$$

$$E^{\circ}_{650°C} = 1.229 - 0.529 = 0.700 \text{ V (theoretical, all gases)}$$

In practice the **OCV is higher (~1.04 V)** because activities are not unity. The actual cell voltage under load is **0.7 – 0.8 V**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Efficiency of an MCFC Power Plant (CHP basis)

**Given data:**
- $V_{cell}$ operating = **0.75 V** per cell
- $n_{cell}$ = 250 cells in stack
- Stack current $I$ = **300 A**
- Fuel utilization $U_f$ = **80 %**
- Faraday efficiency $\eta_F$ = **95 %**
- Stack voltage efficiency $\eta_V$ = $V_{cell}/E_{H_2} = 0.75/1.229 = 0.610$
- Heat recovery efficiency (boiler + turbine) = **30 %** of waste heat recovered as additional electrical output
- LHV of H₂ = 241.8 kJ/mol

**Step 1 — DC power output of stack:**
$$P_{dc} = n_{cell} \times V_{cell} \times I$$

$$P_{dc} = 250 \times 0.75 \times 300$$

$$P_{dc} = 56{,}250 \text{ W} = 56.25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — H₂ consumption rate (Faraday):**
$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \times n_{cell}}{nFU_f \cdot \eta_F}$$

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{300 \times 250}{2 \times 96485 \times 0.80 \times 0.95}$$

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{75000}{146657} = 0.5115 \text{ mol/s}$$

**Step 3 — Chemical power input (LHV basis):**
$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \times \text{LHV}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = 0.5115 \times 241.8 \times 10^3$$

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = 123{,}681 \text{ W} \approx 123.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Electrical efficiency of stack (LHV):**
$$\eta_{el} = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \dfrac{56.25}{123.7} = 0.4547 \text{ or } 45.5\%$$

**Step 5 — Recoverable waste heat:**
$$\dot{Q}_{waste} = \dot{Q}_{in} - P_{dc} = 123.7 - 56.25 = 67.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Recovered electrical from bottoming cycle:**
$$P_{recovered} = 0.30 \times 67.4 = 20.2 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Total electrical output and CHP efficiency:**
$$P_{total} = 56.25 + 20.2 = 76.45 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta_{CHP} = \dfrac{P_{total}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \dfrac{76.45}{123.7} = 0.618 = 61.8\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** While the electrical efficiency (~45 %) is lower than PEMFC (~50–60 %), the CHP efficiency reaches **60–85 %** with heat recovery — making MCFC highly attractive for stationary plants where heat is also valuable.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Polarisation Curve and Voltage Losses

The actual operating voltage $V_{cell}$ is:
$$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - (\eta_{act} + \eta_{ohmic} + \eta_{conc})$$

Where:
- $\eta_{act}$ — activation overpotential (Tafel equation: $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln \dfrac{i}{i_0}$)
- $\eta_{ohmic}$ — ohmic loss (electrolyte + electrodes: $\eta_{ohmic} = iR_{internal}$)
- $\eta_{conc}$ — concentration overpotential (mass-transport limitations)

**For MCFC at 650 °C**, typical values at 150 mA/cm²:
- $E_{Nernst} \approx 1.04$ V
- $\eta_{act} \approx 0.08 - 0.12$ V
- $\eta_{ohmic} \approx 0.05 - 0.08$ V
- $\eta_{conc} \approx 0.05 - 0.10$ V
- $\Rightarrow V_{cell} \approx 0.78$ V ✓ (matches experimental data)
