# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an MCFC System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Natural Gas CH4 Supply] --> nodeB[Internal Reformer]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Anode Inlet - H2 / CO]
    nodeD[Air Blower] --> nodeE[CO2 Recycled from Anode Exhaust]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Cathode Inlet - Air and CO2]
    nodeC --> nodeG[MCFC Stack]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Anode Exhaust - H2O and CO2]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Cathode Exhaust - O2 depleted]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[CO2 Separator and Recycle Loop]
    nodeJ --> nodeE
    nodeG --> nodeK[Power Conditioning Unit - DC to AC Inverter]
    nodeK --> nodeL[AC Load - Grid or Local Consumers]
    nodeG --> nodeM[Waste Heat Recovery Boiler]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Bottoming Cycle Turbine]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Additional Electrical Output]
    nodeM --> nodeP[Thermal Load - CHP Network]
```

## 4.2 Electrochemical Reaction Flow inside the MCFC Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[H2 Fuel Inlet] --> nodeB[Porous Ni Anode]
    nodeB -- electrons e minus --> nodeK[External Circuit - Load]
    nodeK -- electrons e minus --> nodeC[Porous NiO Cathode]
    nodeD[Air and CO2 Inlet] --> nodeC
    nodeC -- CO3 2 minus ions --> nodeE[CO3 2 minus migration through molten carbonate]
    nodeE --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeF[Anode Exhaust - H2O and CO2]
    nodeB -- H2O and CO2 produced --> nodeF
    nodeF -- CO2 recycle --> nodeD
    nodeC -- O2 depleted exhaust --> nodeG[Cathode Exhaust]
    nodeB -- Heat Q --> nodeH[Temperature 650 C maintained]
    nodeC -- Heat Q --> nodeH
```

## 4.3 MCFC Stack Cross-Section (Conceptual Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MCFC_STACK[MCFC Single Cell Cross Section]
        nodeA[Bipolar Plate - Cathode Side] --> nodeB[Current Collector - Ni coated]
        nodeB --> nodeC[Porous NiO Cathode]
        nodeC --> nodeD[Electrolyte Matrix - LiAlO2 plus Molten Carbonate]
        nodeD --> nodeE[Porous Ni Anode]
        nodeE --> nodeF[Current Collector - Ni]
        nodeF --> nodeG[Bipolar Plate - Anode Side]
    end
    nodeC -. Cathode Reaction O2 plus CO2 plus 2e minus forms CO3 2 minus .-> nodeD
    nodeD -. CO3 2 minus migrates to anode .-> nodeE
    nodeE -. Anode Reaction H2 plus CO3 2 minus forms H2O plus CO2 plus 2e minus .-> nodeF
```

## 4.4 Performance / Polarisation Curve (Qualitative Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Open Circuit Voltage - approx 1.04 V] --> nodeB[Activation Loss Region - rapid drop at low current]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Ohmic Loss Region - linear decline]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Concentration Loss Region - sharp drop at high current]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Limiting Current Density - approx 150 to 200 mA per cm2]
    nodeF[Current Density Axis - mA per cm2] --> nodeE
    nodeG[Cell Voltage Axis - V] --> nodeA
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]
**List the electrolyte, mobile charge carrier, and operating temperature of a Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell. State any two advantages of MCFC over PAFC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Electrolyte: Molten mixture of alkali carbonates (Li₂CO₃ + K₂CO₃) held in a LiAlO₂ matrix. **(1 mark)**
- Mobile charge carrier: Carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻). **(1 mark)**
- Operating temperature: 600 – 650 °C. **(1 mark)**
- *(Bonus 0 marks, optional)* Two advantages: (i) Internal reforming eliminates external reformer; (ii) higher waste heat enables CHP with >85 % efficiency.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]
**Write the anode, cathode, and overall cell reactions of an MCFC operating on hydrogen fuel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Anode: $\text{H}_2 + \text{CO}_3^{2-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 + 2e^-$ **(1 mark)**
- Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{CO}_3^{2-}$ **(1 mark)**
- Overall: $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ **(1 mark)**
- *(Bonus mention)* CO₂ is recycled from anode exhaust to cathode inlet.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### ➤ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a **Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell (MCFC)**. Discuss the materials used for anode, cathode, electrolyte, and current collectors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Nernst equation for MCFC** and discuss the role of CO₂ partial pressure on cell voltage. A MCFC operates at 650 °C with $P_{H_2} = 0.8$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 0.2$ atm, $P_{CO_2,c} = 0.3$ atm, $P_{H_2O} = 0.15$ atm, $P_{CO_2,a} = 0.25$ atm. Calculate the Nernst voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**
- (1 mark) Cell components: Anode, Cathode, Electrolyte matrix, Bipolar plates, Current collectors, Seals.
- (1 mark) Anode: Porous Ni (3–6 μm) with 2–10 % Cr for creep resistance.
- (1 mark) Cathode: Porous lithiated NiO (LiₓNi₁₋ₓO).
- (1 mark) Electrolyte: 62 % Li₂CO₃ + 38 % K₂CO₃ eutectic in LiAlO₂ matrix, thickness ~0.5–1 mm.
- (1 mark) Current collectors: SS-310 / SS-316 bipolar plates coated with Ni on the wet-seal side.
- (1 mark) Working: H₂ supplied to anode, air + recycled CO₂ supplied to cathode; CO₃²⁻ migrates from cathode → anode; water and CO₂ exit at anode; electrons flow through external circuit.
- (1 mark) Internal reforming allows direct use of CH₄: CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ inside the cell.

**(b) Nernst Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):**

The Nernst equation from $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$ and $\Delta G = -nFE$:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5} \cdot P_{CO_2,c}}{P_{H_2O} \cdot P_{CO_2,a}}$$

- (1 mark) Derivation: Standard derivation as shown in Section 3.1, with R = 8.314 J/mol·K, n = 2, F = 96485 C/mol.
- (1 mark) At 650 °C, T = 923 K, $E^{\circ} \approx 0.7$ V (theoretical). For practical MCFC, OCV is ~1.04 V; the form above is the **practical Nernst** (with $E^{\circ}$ replaced by the value that matches OCV at the operating point — KTU expects use of $E^{\circ} = 0.7$ V OR 1.04 V depending on which convention is taught; both are accepted).
- (1 mark) Role of CO₂: Higher $P_{CO_2,c}$ → higher voltage; lower $P_{CO_2,a}$ → higher voltage. This is why CO₂ recycling is essential.
- (1 mark) Substitution into Nernst formula:

$$E = 0.7 + \dfrac{(8.314)(923)}{(2)(96485)} \ln \dfrac{(0.8)(0.2)^{0.5}(0.3)}{(0.15)(0.25)}$$

- (1 mark) Inner term:
$$Q = \dfrac{(0.8)(0.4472)(0.3)}{(0.15)(0.25)} = \dfrac{0.1073}{0.0375} = 2.862$$
- (1 mark) $\ln Q = \ln(2.862) = 1.052$
- (1 mark) $\dfrac{RT}{nF} = \dfrac{(8.314)(923)}{(2)(96485)} = 0.03977$ V
$$E = 0.7 + (0.03977)(1.052) = 0.7 + 0.0418 \approx 0.742 \text{ V}$$
- **Final answer: $E_{Nernst} \approx 0.74$ V** (consistent with operating range of 0.7–0.8 V).

---

### ➤ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Compare **MCFC and PAFC** with respect to electrolyte, charge carrier, operating temperature, catalyst, and efficiency. List any four advantages and three disadvantages of MCFC systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An MCFC stack has **300 cells**, operating at **0.78 V per cell** with a current of **350 A**. Fuel (pure H₂) utilization is **82 %** and the Faraday efficiency is **96 %**. Determine: (i) DC power output, (ii) H₂ consumption in mol/s, (iii) Electrical efficiency on LHV basis (LHV of H₂ = 241.8 kJ/mol). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | PAFC | MCFC |
|-----------|------|------|
| Electrolyte | Conc. H₃PO₄ (liquid) | Molten (Li/K)₂CO₃ in LiAlO₂ |
| Charge carrier | H⁺ | CO₃²⁻ |
| Operating T | 180–220 °C | 600–650 °C |
| Catalyst | Pt (anode & cathode) | Ni (anode), NiO (cathode) — non-precious |
| Electrical efficiency | 40–45 % | 50–55 % (alone), 60–85 % (CHP) |
| Internal reforming | No | Yes (direct CH₄ use) |
| CO₂ management | Not required | CO₂ recycle mandatory |

- (1 mark) **Four advantages:** (i) no noble metal catalyst, (ii) internal reforming, (iii) high-grade waste heat for CHP, (iv) higher electrical efficiency than PAFC, (v) fuel flexibility.
- (1 mark) **Three disadvantages:** (i) slow startup due to high T, (ii) corrosive molten salt → material degradation, (iii) CO₂ recycling adds balance-of-plant complexity, (iv) limited cycle life (~40,000 h).
- (5 marks) Comparison content.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) DC power output:** **(2 marks)**
$$P_{dc} = n_{cell} \times V_{cell} \times I = 300 \times 0.78 \times 350$$
$$P_{dc} = 81{,}900 \text{ W} = 81.9 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) H₂ consumption rate:** **(2 marks)**
$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{n_{cell} \times I}{nFU_f \cdot \eta_F} = \dfrac{300 \times 350}{2 \times 96485 \times 0.82 \times 0.96}$$
$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{105000}{151864} = 0.6914 \text{ mol/s}$$

**(iii) Chemical input power:** **(1 mark)**
$$\dot{Q}_{in} = 0.6914 \times 241.8 \times 10^3 = 167{,}180 \text{ W} = 167.18 \text{ kW}$$

**(iv) Electrical efficiency:** **(2 marks)**
$$\eta_{el} = \dfrac{81.9}{167.18} = 0.490 = 49.0\%$$

- **Final answers: $P_{dc}$ = 81.9 kW; $\dot{n}_{H_2}$ = 0.691 mol/s; $\eta_{el}$ = 49 % (LHV)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the CO₂ term in the Nernst equation** — many students write the PEMFC equation. Always include $P_{CO_2,c}$ in numerator and $P_{CO_2,a}$ in denominator. **−2 marks penalty.**
> 2. **Mixing up LHV (241.8 kJ/mol) and HHV (285.8 kJ/mol) of H₂** — state which you are using. KTU expects LHV for fuel cell efficiency unless explicitly told HHV.
> 3. **Confusing anode and cathode reactions** — CO₃²⁻ is FORMED at the cathode and CONSUMED at the anode. Reversing them costs **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 5. **Calculating fuel utilization wrongly** — it is based on H₂ actually reacted ÷ H₂ supplied; it must appear in the denominator of the consumption formula.
> 6. **Writing OCV as 1.229 V at 25 °C and using it at 650 °C without correction** — always state the temperature at which the Nernst equation is evaluated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Electrolyte:** Molten (Li, K, Na)₂CO₃ eutectic in a **LiAlO₂** matrix — never call it a "solid" electrolyte.
- **Mobile ion:** **CO₃²⁻** (carbonate) — migrates from **cathode → anode**.
- **Operating temperature:** **600 – 650 °C** (high-temp fuel cell category).
- **Anode material:** Porous **Ni + Cr**; Cathode: **Lithiated NiO**.
- **Catalysts:** Non-precious metals (Ni-based) — cost advantage over PAFC and PEMFC.
- **Anode reaction:** $\text{H}_2 + \text{CO}_3^{2-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 + 2e^-$
- **Cathode reaction:** $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{CO}_3^{2-}$
- **Overall reaction:** $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (CO₂ cancels out internally).
- **CO₂ recycling** is mandatory — from anode exhaust back to cathode inlet.
- **Internal reforming** of CH₄ (and other hydrocarbons) is a unique MCFC advantage.
- **Typical cell voltage (operating):** 0.7 – 0.8 V; **OCV:** 1.04 – 1.10 V at 650 °C.
- **Electrical efficiency (alone):** 45 – 55 %; **CHP efficiency:** 60 – 85 %.
- **Stack life:** ~40,000 hours (commercial target).
- **Main applications:** Stationary power (200 kW – 50 MW), CHP, marine, industrial parks.
- **Main challenges:** Corrosion of cathode, NiO dissolution, slow startup, electrolyte loss, sealing.
- **MCFC vs PAFC:** MCFC is hotter, has higher efficiency, no noble metal catalyst, supports internal reforming, and requires CO₂ recycling.
- **MCFC vs SOFC:** Both high-temperature, but MCFC uses liquid electrolyte (easier sealing in some aspects), while SOFC uses solid ceramic (longer life, no CO₂ recycle needed).
- **Charge carrier check:** PAFC → H⁺; PEMFC → H⁺; MCFC → CO₃²⁻; SOFC → O²⁻; AFC → OH⁻.
- **Nernst convention for MCFC:** $P_{CO_2,c}$ in **numerator** (reactant at cathode), $P_{CO_2,a}$ in **denominator** (product at anode).
- **KTU hot keywords for theory answers:** "Molten Salt Electrolyte," "Internal Reforming," "CO₂ Recycle Loop," "Non-precious Catalyst," "Combined Heat and Power," "High-Temperature Fuel Cell."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid Oxide Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solid Oxide Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — SOLID OXIDE FUEL CELLS (SOFC)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard Wording):**
> A **Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC)** is a high-temperature electrochemical energy conversion device that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically **H₂, CH₄, CO, or hydrocarbons**) into electrical energy through an oxidation reaction mediated by a **solid ceramic electrolyte** that conducts **oxygen ions (O²⁻)** from the cathode to the anode at operating temperatures typically in the range of **600 °C – 1000 °C**.

SOFCs belong to the family of **high-temperature fuel cells** along with **Molten Carbonate Fuel Cells (MCFCs)**. Unlike Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells (PEMFCs), which use a hydrated polymer membrane that conducts H⁺ ions, an SOFC uses a **rigid, non-porous ceramic solid** (most commonly **Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia, YSZ**) as its electrolyte. Because the electrolyte is solid, there are no liquid-phase corrosion, flooding, or electrolyte-management problems, which is the principal reason SOFCs have achieved some of the highest demonstrated electrical efficiencies of any fuel-cell class — typically **50 % – 60 %** in standalone operation and **up to 85 %** in CHP (combined heat and power) mode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Board Fact:** SOFCs are the **only commercially deployed fuel-cell technology** for **stationary megawatt-scale power generation** (e.g., Bloom Energy Servers). They are also the natural partner for **hydrogen production by water electrolysis** when operated in **reversible (solid-oxide electrolysis cell, SOEC)** mode.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot Ceramic Sandwich"

Imagine a **hot ceramic sandwich**:

- The **top slice (cathode)** breathes air and pulls oxygen out of it.
- The **filling (solid ceramic electrolyte)** lets only the **negatively charged oxygen atoms (O²⁻)** squeeze through it, but rejects everything else — even electrons and nitrogen.
- The **bottom slice (anode)** receives fuel (H₂) on the other side. The O²⁻ ions meet the H₂, react violently, release water, and give up the electrons they were carrying.

Those electrons **cannot pass through the ceramic**, so they are forced to take the **long way around** through an external circuit — and *that* flow of electrons is what we call **electricity**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember the SOFC ion:** "**SO**lid = **O**xygen-ion conductor." The "SO" in SOFC literally stands for **Solid Oxide**, and the ion it conducts is **O²⁻**, the opposite polarity to a PEMFC (which conducts H⁺).

### Standard Physical Constants & Metrics for SOFCs

| Parameter | Typical Value (KTU Board Standard) |
|---|---|
| Operating temperature | **800 °C – 1000 °C** (high-temp) / **500 °C – 700 °C** (intermediate-temp, IT-SOFC) |
| Electrolyte | **Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ)**, 8 mol % Y₂O₃ in ZrO₂ |
| Anode | **Ni-YSZ cermet** (nickel + YSZ ceramic composite) |
| Cathode | **Lanthanum Strontium Manganite (La₁₋ₓSrₓMnO₃, LSM)** or LSCF |
| Charge carrier | **O²⁻ ion** |
| Fuel | H₂, CH₄, CO, syngas, light hydrocarbons |
| Oxidant | Air (O₂) |
| Open-circuit voltage (theoretical) | ~**1.0 V – 1.2 V** per cell |
| Practical operating voltage | **0.6 V – 0.8 V** per cell |
| Electrical efficiency (LHV) | **45 % – 60 %** |
| CHP efficiency | up to **85 %** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Electrochemistry of an SOFC

An SOFC is a **concentration cell of oxygen ions**. The driving force for current flow is the **difference in oxygen chemical potential (partial pressure of O₂)** between the cathode side (high pO₂, air) and the anode side (low pO₂, fuel side).

The complete thermodynamic picture is captured by the **Nernst equation**, which gives the reversible open-circuit cell voltage $E_{Nernst}$ as a function of temperature and the partial pressures of the reactants and products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Concept:** A student should be able to derive the half-cell reactions, the overall reaction, and the corresponding Nernst voltage equation for any fuel (H₂, CH₄, CO) in an SOFC. This is a **favourite 7-mark question**.

### 2.2 Half-Cell and Overall Reactions

**For hydrogen fuel (H₂):**

At the **cathode** (air side), oxygen molecules are reduced and pick up electrons from the external circuit to form oxygen ions:

$$\frac{1}{2} O_2 + 2 e^{-} \longrightarrow O^{2-}$$

This is the **oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)**.

The O²⁻ ion is then driven through the dense YSZ electrolyte by the chemical-potential gradient to the **anode** (fuel side), where it reacts with H₂:

$$H_2 + O^{2-} \longrightarrow H_2O_{(g)} + 2 e^{-}$$

The electrons released travel through the external circuit, doing useful electrical work, and return to the cathode.

The **overall cell reaction** is therefore:

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O_{(l)} \quad \Delta H^\circ = -285.8 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$

**For methane (CH₄) — internal reforming:**

$$CH_4 + 4 O^{2-} \longrightarrow 2 H_2O + CO_2 + 8 e^{-}$$

**For carbon monoxide (CO):**

$$CO + O^{2-} \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2 e^{-}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SOFCs are uniquely suited to hydrocarbon fuels:** Because they operate at 800–1000 °C, SOFCs can internally reform CH₄ to H₂ and CO via a catalytic or steam-reforming reaction without the need for an external reformer — a major system simplification compared to PEMFCs.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^0 = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{n F}$ | Standard reversible cell voltage from Gibbs free energy | V |
| 2 | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_{H_2}\, p_{O_2}^{1/2}}{p_{H_2O}}\right)$ | Nernst equation for H₂/O₂ SOFC (n = 2) | V |
| 3 | $\eta_{thermo} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H^\circ}$ | Thermodynamic (ideal) efficiency | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{voltage} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E^0}$ | Voltage efficiency (accounts for irreversibilities) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta_{fuel} = \dfrac{\text{Fuel utilisation factor}}{1}$ | Fraction of input fuel actually electrochemically oxidised | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_{electrical} = \eta_{thermo} \cdot \eta_{voltage} \cdot \eta_{fuel}$ | Overall electrical efficiency of the cell | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\sigma_{ion} = \sigma_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{kT}\right)$ | Ionic conductivity of YSZ (Arrhenius form) | S/cm |
| 8 | $V_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell}$ | Total stack voltage (N cells in series) | V |
| 9 | $P = V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot N$ | Stack power output | W |
| 10 | $\Delta S = -\dfrac{\partial (\Delta G)}{\partial T}$ | Entropy change of reaction — sets sign of temp coefficient of $E$ | J/(mol·K) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-symbol rule for LaTeX:** In KTU answer sheets, when you need the magnitude of an absolute value or determinant, write $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert \Delta G \rvert$ — **never** use the raw keyboard pipe symbol `|` inside a markdown table cell.

### 2.4 Why Each Component Must Be a Specific Material

**1. Electrolyte — Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ, (ZrO₂)₀.₉₂(Y₂O₃)₀.₀₈)**
- Pure ZrO₂ is a poor ionic conductor.
- Doping with **8 mol % Y₂O₃** substitutes Y³⁺ on Zr⁴⁺ sites, generating **oxygen vacancies** in the lattice.
- These vacancies allow O²⁻ to hop from site to site, giving an ionic conductivity of $\sim 0.1 \; \text{S/cm}$ at 1000 °C — comparable to a liquid electrolyte.
- **Key requirements:** gas-tight (dense), chemically stable in both oxidising and reducing atmospheres, electronically insulating (only ions move, not electrons, to avoid short-circuiting).

**2. Anode — Nickel-YSZ Cermet**
- Ni provides the **catalytic activity** for H₂ oxidation and excellent **electronic conductivity**.
- YSZ in the cermet matches the **thermal expansion coefficient (TEC)** of the electrolyte, preventing delamination during thermal cycling.
- The porous structure (≈ 30–40 % porosity) allows fuel gas to diffuse to the triple-phase boundary (TPB) — the locus where **gas, electrolyte, and electronic conductor meet**.

**3. Cathode — Lanthanum Strontium Manganite (La₁₋ₓSrₓMnO₃, LSM)**
- A **perovskite** (ABO₃) structure with mixed electronic–ionic conductivity.
- Sr-doping on the A-site creates oxygen vacancies and increases electronic conductivity.
- Operates in a strongly oxidising atmosphere and must be chemically compatible with YSZ.

**4. Interconnects — Lanthanum Chromite (LaCrO₃)** or stainless steel
- Connect individual cells electrically in series to form a **stack**.
- Must be gas-tight, electronically conductive, and chemically stable on **both** the air and fuel sides simultaneously — an extreme materials requirement.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stationary power**: SOFCs are deployed in data centres, hospitals, and microgrids where their high efficiency (50–60 %) and CHP capability (85 %) make them economically attractive.
- **Reversible operation (SOEC)**: The same cell stack, fed with electricity and steam, produces green H₂ with very high efficiency (~80 % LHV).
- **Industrial co-generation**: Waste heat at 800–1000 °C can drive absorption chillers, gas turbines (SOFC-GT hybrid), or be used for internal fuel reforming.
- **Syngas and biogas tolerance**: Unlike PEMFCs, SOFCs tolerate CO (it is a fuel!) and trace H₂S, making them ideal for **coal-derived syngas** and **biogas** applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reversible Cell Voltage $E^0$

The **first-law energy balance** for a fuel cell relates the Gibbs free-energy change to the maximum reversible electrical work that can be extracted:

$$W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G^\circ$$

For a reaction involving $n$ moles of electrons transferred, this work is also equal to the electrical charge times the reversible cell voltage:

$$W_{elec,max} = n F E^0$$

where $F$ is the **Faraday constant = 96 485 C/mol** and $n$ is the number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction (for the H₂/O₂ reaction, $n = 2$).

Equating the two expressions and solving for $E^0$:

$$\boxed{E^0 = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{n F}}$$

**Numerical evaluation for the H₂/O₂ reaction at standard conditions (298 K, 1 atm):**

$$\Delta G^\circ = -237.2 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$E^0 = -\frac{(-237.2 \times 10^3 \; \text{J/mol})}{2 \times 96\,485 \; \text{C/mol}}$$

$$E^0 = \frac{237\,200}{192\,970}$$

$$E^0 \approx 1.229 \; \text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** $E^0 = 1.229 \; \text{V}$ is the **standard theoretical voltage of the H₂/O₂ cell at 25 °C**. At SOFC operating temperatures (800–1000 °C), this value **drops** to about **0.9–1.0 V** because $\Delta G^\circ$ becomes less negative with increasing temperature (entropy contribution). Memorise both numbers.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Nernst Equation for an SOFC

Starting from the general Nernst equation applied to the half-cell reaction

$$\frac{1}{2} O_2 + 2 e^- \longrightarrow O^{2-}$$

and the overall cell reaction

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O$$

we have, for the **reaction quotient** $Q$:

$$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2} \cdot a_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$

For gas-phase species at low pressure, activities are replaced by partial pressures in atm:

$$Q = \frac{p_{H_2O}}{p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$

The Nernst equation for the cell is:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

Substituting $n = 2$ and the expression for $Q$, and rewriting the log for clarity:

$$\boxed{E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}}{p_{H_2O}}\right)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Trick:** Some textbooks define $E^0$ at SOFC operating temperatures (not 298 K). When substituting numbers, always check whether the supplied $E^0$ is at 25 °C or at the operating temperature, and **explicitly state both $E^0_{298}$ and $E^0_{op}$** in your answer to avoid losing the "consistency of units" mark.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** An SOFC operates at **1000 °C** with the following gas partial pressures:
- $p_{H_2} = 0.8 \; \text{atm}$
- $p_{O_2} = 0.21 \; \text{atm}$ (air at cathode)
- $p_{H_2O} = 0.2 \; \text{atm}$

Take $E^0_{1000°C} \approx 0.95 \; \text{V}$, $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$, $F = 96\,485 \; \text{C/mol}$.

**Step 1 — Temperature conversion:**

$$T = 1000 + 273 = 1273 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the argument of the logarithm:**

$$\frac{p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}}{p_{H_2O}} = \frac{0.8 \cdot (0.21)^{1/2}}{0.2}$$

$$= \frac{0.8 \cdot 0.4583}{0.2} = \frac{0.3666}{0.2} = 1.833$$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature-dependent prefactor:**

$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{8.314 \times 1273}{2 \times 96\,485} = \frac{10\,584}{192\,970} = 0.05486 \; \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Nernst equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 0.95 + 0.05486 \cdot \ln(1.833) \\
  &= 0.95 + 0.05486 \cdot 0.6061 \\
  &= 0.95 + 0.0333 \\
  &= 0.983 \; \text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret:**

$$E_{Nernst} \approx 0.983 \; \text{V}$$

This is the **theoretical open-circuit voltage**. In practice, activation, ohmic, and concentration losses will reduce the operating cell voltage to about **0.6–0.8 V** at useful current densities (typically 200–500 mA/cm²).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — SOFC vs Other Fuel Cells (High-Yield KTU Comparison)

| Property | **SOFC** | **PEMFC** | **MCFC** | **AFC** | **PAFC** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | YSZ (solid ceramic) | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | Molten Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ | KOH solution | H₃PO₄ |
| Charge carrier | **O²⁻** | **H⁺** | **CO₃²⁻** | **OH⁻** | **H⁺** |
| Operating T | **600–1000 °C** | 60–100 °C | 600–700 °C | 60–250 °C | 150–220 °C |
| Catalyst | Ni / perovskite (no Pt needed) | Pt | Ni | Pt / Ag | Pt |
| Fuel | H₂, CH₄, CO, syngas | Pure H₂ | H₂, CH₄, CO | Pure H₂ | H₂ |
| CO tolerance | **Excellent** (CO is fuel) | Poisoning (>10 ppm) | Good | Poor | Poor |
| Efficiency (LHV) | **50–60 %** | 40–60 % | 45–55 % | 50–60 % | 40–50 % |
| Power range | 1 kW – multi-MW | 1 W – 250 kW | 100 kW – multi-MW | 1–100 kW | 50 kW – 11 MW |
| Start-up time | **Hours** (thermal inertia) | Seconds–minutes | Hours | Minutes | Hours |
| Application | Stationary, CHP | Transport, portable | Utility power | Space, military | Stationary, buses |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic Cross-Section of a Single Planar SOFC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    airIn["AIR IN O2 0.21 atm"]:::gasFlow
    cathode["CATHODE LSM La1-xSrxMnO3 Perovskite"]:::cathodeNode
    orr["OXYGEN REDUCTION REACTION 1/2 O2 plus 2e gives O2-"]:::reactionNode
    electrolyte["ELECTROLYTE YSZ 8 mol percent Y2O3 ZrO2 O2- ion conductor"]:::electrolyteNode
    o2ion["OXYGEN ION O2- MIGRATION"]:::ionFlow
    anode["ANODE Ni-YSZ cermet Porous"]:::anodeNode
    h2ox["HYDROGEN OXIDATION H2 plus O2- gives H2O plus 2e"]:::reactionNode
    fuelIn["FUEL IN H2 or CH4"]:::gasFlow
    h2oOut["H2O OUT product"]:::gasFlow
    extern["EXTERNAL CIRCUIT electrons flow e-"]:::circuitNode
    elecOut["ELECTRICAL OUTPUT DC"]:::outputNode

    airIn --> cathode
    cathode --> orr
    orr --> electrolyte
    electrolyte --> o2ion
    o2ion --> anode
    fuelIn --> anode
    anode --> h2ox
    h2ox --> h2oOut
    orr --> extern
    h2ox --> extern
    extern --> elecOut

    classDef gasFlow fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#0C4A6E

    classDef circuitNode fill:#E9D5FF,stroke:#7E22CE,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#581C87
    classDef outputNode fill:#A7F3D0,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#064E3B
```

### 4.2 SOFC Stack Functional Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph fuelSupply["FUEL SUPPLY SUBSYSTEM"]
        f1["Hydrocarbon or H2 Inlet"]:::inNode
        f2["Desulfuriser H2S Removal"]:::procNode
        f3["Pre-heater to 800 C"]:::procNode
    end

    subgraph airSupply["AIR SUPPLY SUBSYSTEM"]
        a1["Air Blower or Compressor"]:::procNode
        a2["Pre-heater to 800 C"]:::procNode
    end

    subgraph sofcStack["SOFC STACK ASSEMBLY"]
        c1["Cell 1 Cathode Anode Electrolyte"]:::cellNode
        c2["Cell 2 Cathode Anode Electrolyte"]:::cellNode
        c3["Cell N Cathode Anode Electrolyte"]:::cellNode
        ic1["Interconnect Bipolar Plate"]:::interconnect
        ic2["Interconnect Bipolar Plate"]:::interconnect
    end

    subgraph boP["BALANCE OF PLANT BOP"]
        wr["Waste Heat Recovery Boiler"]:::procNode
        chp["CHP Steam or Chilled Water"]:::outNode
        inv["DC to AC Inverter"]:::procNode
    end

    subgraph powerOut["POWER OUTPUT"]
        grid["AC Grid or Local Load"]:::outNode
    end

    f1 --> f2 --> f3 --> c1
    a1 --> a2 --> c1
    c1 --- ic1 --- c2 --- ic2 --- c3
    c1 --> wr
    c2 --> wr
    c3 --> wr
    wr --> chp
    c1 --> inv
    c2 --> inv
    c3 --> inv
    inv --> grid

    classDef inNode fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#1E3A8A

    classDef cellNode fill:#FECACA,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F1D1D
    classDef interconnect fill:#E9D5FF,stroke:#7E22CE,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#581C87
    classDef outNode fill:#BBF7D0,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#064E3B
```

### 4.3 SOFC Polarisation (V–I) Curve — Loss Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axis["Current Density J mA per cm2 increasing"]:::axisNode
    e0["E0 Nernst Open Circuit 0.95 to 1.0 V"]:::ideal
    actLoss["ACTIVATION LOSS eta act Slow ORR kinetics at cathode"]:::loss
    ohmLoss["OHMIC LOSS eta ohm Linear with J Ionic plus electronic resistance"]:::loss
    concLoss["CONCENTRATION LOSS eta conc Mass transport limit at high J"]:::loss
    vcell["Operating Cell Voltage Vcell 0.6 to 0.8 V"]:::actual

    axis --> e0
    axis --> actLoss
    axis --> ohmLoss
    axis --> concLoss
    axis --> vcell

    e0 -. voltage drops .-> actLoss
    actLoss -. voltage drops .-> ohmLoss
    ohmLoss -. voltage drops .-> concLoss
    concLoss -. operating point .-> vcell

    classDef axisNode fill:#F3F4F6,stroke:#374151,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#111827

    classDef loss fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#7F1D1D
```
