Note the explicit placement of **LPM mixing** upstream of the combustor can — this is the canonical engineering response to the high-flame-speed challenge of hydrogen.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** *Explain why hydrogen requires a modified combustor design in gas turbines compared with natural gas.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Hydrogen has (i) a laminar flame speed 8× higher than methane, (ii) a flammability range of 4–75 % in air versus 5–15 % for CH4, and (iii) a minimum ignition energy of 0.02 mJ. These properties cause flashback, auto-ignition inside the premix zone, and combustion dynamics. Hence **Lean Premixed (LPM)** or **Micromix** combustors are used with $\phi \le 0.5$ to keep the adiabatic flame temperature below 1,800 K, suppressing thermal-NOx and stabilising the flame. (3 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]** *List three properties of hydrogen that are advantageous in turbine applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Zero carbon content** → no CO2 or unburned hydrocarbons in exhaust.
2. **High LHV (120 MJ/kg)** → 2.6× the mass energy of methane, allowing compact fuel storage.
3. **Wide flammability limits (4–75 %)** → flexible operation across a broad equivalence-ratio envelope, enabling lean-burn NOx control.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a hydrogen-fuelled open-cycle gas turbine. Explain the modifications required in the combustor and materials. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A hydrogen-fuelled open-cycle gas turbine ingests atmospheric air, compresses it in an axial compressor (1), mixes it with H2 in a lean-premixed combustor (2), expands the high-temperature products through the turbine (3), and exhausts them to the atmosphere.

**Modifications needed (key points, 1 mark each):**
- **Combustor:** Replace conventional diffusion flame with **LPM / Micromix can-annular combustors** at $\phi \le 0.5$ to prevent flashback and reduce thermal-NOx. Multi-port fuel injectors and axial swirler vanes ensure premixed homogeneous air–fuel mixture.
- **Materials:** Use **Ni-based superalloys with Pt-Al or MCrAlY bond coats** on first-stage blades. Hydrogen-resistant alloys (e.g., Inconel 718, Hastelloy) minimise hydrogen embrittlement. Internal blade cooling passages remain but are de-coupled from H2 exposure.
- **Sealing:** Use **palm oil-based or dry-gas face seals** because conventional labyrinth seals leak H2 too easily due to low molecular weight.
- **Fuel system:** Use **double-block-and-bleed valves** and **hydrogen-specific sensors (catalytic bead)** because H2 has 8× diffusivity of CH4.
- **Ignition:** High-energy **surface-discharge igniters** with continuous pilot because min. ignition energy of H2 is only 0.02 mJ.
- **Emissions:** Post-combustion **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)** is optional; LPM design usually keeps NOx below 25 ppm dry.
- **Conclusion:** These modifications enable 100 % H2 firing without flame instability.

**(b)** A hydrogen gas turbine plant delivers 80 MW of electrical output at an overall efficiency of 50 %. Calculate (i) the hourly hydrogen consumption in kg/h and (ii) the equivalent volume at NTP. LHV of H2 = 120 MJ/kg; density of H2 at NTP = 0.0838 kg/Nm³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i)** Hourly H2 mass consumption:

$$
\dot m_{H_2} = \dfrac{P_{elec}}{\eta_{overall} \cdot LHV_{H_2}} = \dfrac{80 \times 10^{6}}{0.50 \times 120 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\dot m_{H_2} = \dfrac{80}{60} = 1.333 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

Per hour:

$$
\dot m_{H_2} = 1.333 \times 3600 = \mathbf{4{,}800 \, kg/h}
$$

**(ii)** Volumetric flow at NTP:

$$
\dot V_{H_2} = \dfrac{4{,}800}{0.0838} = \mathbf{57{,}280 \, Nm^3/h}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A(b):** Students commonly forget to convert **seconds → hours** (loses 1 mark) or use density at STP (1.25 kg/m³) instead of NTP (loses 1 mark). Always state the reference condition explicitly: **"NTP = 0 °C, 1 atm"**.

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, May 2024 — Alternative]**

**(a)** Discuss the combustion challenges of using hydrogen as a gas turbine fuel and explain how lean-premixed combustion addresses them. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Hydrogen combustion challenges (each point 1 mark):
1. **Flashback** — High flame speed (2.5–3 m/s) propagates back into the premix ducts.
2. **Auto-ignition** — Wide flammability range causes unintended ignition in the mixing zone.
3. **Thermal NOx** — High adiabatic flame temperature (2,400 K) drives the Zeldovich mechanism.
4. **Flame lift-off / blow-off** — Low density of H2 reduces flame anchoring at high velocities.
5. **Combustion dynamics** — Coupled acoustic–heat-release oscillations can damage combustor liners.

**How LPM addresses them (each 1 mark):**
- **Lean operation (φ ≤ 0.5):** Reduces adiabatic flame temperature to ≈ 1,700–1,800 K, suppressing thermal-NOx to <25 ppm.
- **Premixing:** Ensures fuel and oxidiser are mixed at molecular level → no diffusion flame → no hot spots.
- **Swirl stabilisers:** Generate central recirculation zones that anchor the flame against high inlet velocities.
- **Micromix burners:** Use many small premix tubes to shorten the mixing length and prevent flashback.
- **Pilot stage diffusion flame:** Provides flame anchoring during transient loads without dominating emissions.
- **Acoustic damping:** Helmholtz resonators and chevron liners suppress thermoacoustic instabilities.

**(b)** Compare hydrogen, methane and ammonia as gas turbine fuels on the basis of LHV, flame speed, flammability range, NOx emission and retrofit feasibility. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (1 mark per row, plus 2 marks for comparative conclusion):**

| Parameter | H2 | CH4 | NH3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 120 | 50 | 18.6 |
| Flame speed (m/s) | 2.5–3.0 | 0.4 | 0.07 |
| Flammability range (%) | 4–75 | 5–15 | 15–28 |
| NOx emission | Low (LPM) | Moderate | **High** (fuel-bound) |
| Retrofit feasibility | Moderate (combustor mods) | Baseline | Very Difficult |

**Conclusion:** Hydrogen offers the highest LHV and zero CO2 but needs the most extensive combustor modification; ammonia has low flame speed (instability) and high fuel-NOx; methane remains the operational baseline but cannot deliver carbon-neutral operation. **(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B(a):** A common mistake is to confuse **thermal-NOx** (Zeldovich) with **fuel-bound NOx** (relevant only for ammonia). Examiners deduct 1 mark if the student writes "H2 produces fuel-bound NOx" — hydrogen does NOT contain nitrogen. Only ammonia (NH3) produces fuel-bound NOx.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Hydrogen Turbines (KTU PECHT862, Module 4):**

- ✅ Hydrogen turbine = **modified Brayton cycle gas turbine** burning H2 instead of CH4.
- ✅ Core advantages: **zero CO2, high LHV (120 MJ/kg), wide flammability range, very low ignition energy**.
- ✅ Core challenges: **flashback, thermal-NOx, flame lift-off, hydrogen embrittlement**.
- ✅ Countermeasure technology: **Lean Premixed (LPM) / Micromix combustors at φ ≤ 0.5**.
- ✅ Material upgrade: **Ni-superalloys (Inconel, Hastelloy) with Pt-Al / MCrAlY coatings**.
- ✅ Industry leaders: **Siemens Silyzer, MHI H-25, GE 7HA.03, Rolls-Royce Pearl 100**.
- ✅ Key efficiency formula: $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{(T_3 - T_{4,actual}) - (T_{2,actual} - T_1)}{T_3 - T_{2,actual}}$.
- ✅ Hydrogen mass-flow equation: $\dot m_{H_2} = P_{elec} / (\eta_{overall} \cdot LHV_{H_2})$.
- ✅ Standard densities: H2 = **0.0838 kg/Nm³ (NTP)**; LH2 = **71 kg/m³ at −253 °C**.
- ✅ Conversion memory aid: **1 kg H2 ≈ 11.93 Nm³**.
- ✅ 100 MWe at η = 0.55 → ≈ **5.45 t/h** of H2 fuel.
- ✅ NOx control: drop TIT below ~1,800 K OR install SCR aftertreatment.
- ✅ Closed-cycle H2-He turbines couple nuclear / solar-thermal heat sources to grid electricity.
- ✅ Hydrogen **does NOT** produce fuel-NOx (only ammonia does — a common examiner trap).
- ✅ Auxiliary systems needing redesign: **fuel skid, sealing, ignition, sensors, blade cooling**.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Hydrogen Turbines. Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PECHT862, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework. Next topic candidates: Hydrogen Safety Codes & Standards / Hydrogen Sensors & Detection.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 Hydrogen Gas Burner — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hydrogen Gas Burner** is an engineered combustion device designed to **mix, ignite, and combust hydrogen fuel with an oxidizer (typically air or pure oxygen)** in a controlled manner to release thermal energy, produce a stable flame, or generate high-temperature gases for industrial, domestic, or propulsion applications. In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, a hydrogen burner is treated as the terminal energy-conversion apparatus of a hydrogen economy, where the chemical energy bound within the H–H bond (**bond dissociation energy = 436 kJ/mol**) is released as sensible heat and radiation.

Mathematically, the combustion of pure hydrogen with oxygen follows the global reaction:

$$\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \Delta H_c$$

where the **lower heating value (LHV) of hydrogen ≈ 119.96 MJ/kg** and the **higher heating value (HHV) ≈ 141.86 MJ/kg** at standard reference conditions (25 °C, 101.325 kPa).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A hydrogen burner is *not* merely a flame holder; it is a **chemical reactor** that simultaneously performs (i) fluid mixing, (ii) heat transfer, (iii) flame stabilization, and (iv) emissions control (NOx minimization).

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Real World" Analogy

Imagine a **precision gas stove**, but instead of the calm, slow-burning LPG flame, you have a fuel whose flame travels **eight times faster**, ignites from a **static-electricity spark a thousand times weaker**, and burns **invisibly in daylight**. That is a hydrogen gas burner.

Think of it like this:
- A candle flame walks slowly across a pool of wax — a **laminar diffusion flame**.
- An LPG stove has a moderate, visible, blue-yellow flame — a **partially premixed flame**.
- A hydrogen burner is like a **race car engine crossed with a magician's flash paper**: it is incredibly fast, extremely eager to ignite, and the flame is so hot and so clean that it can be nearly invisible in sunlight.

In a domestic scenario, a **hydrogen-ready boiler** replaces natural gas with H₂. The challenge: the burner geometry designed for CH₄ flame speed (~0.4 m/s) cannot contain hydrogen flame speed (~2.5–3.0 m/s). This is why burner **retrofit** and **design adaptation** form a major part of KTU Module 4.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Module 4 emphasizes that hydrogen's *unique combustion properties* (high flame speed, wide flammability limits, low minimum ignition energy) make burner design a **safety-critical engineering discipline** — not a simple "swap the fuel" exercise.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Methane (CH₄) | Significance |
|----------|---------------|---------------|--------------|
| LHV (MJ/kg) | **119.96** | 50.02 | Hydrogen carries ~2.4× more energy per kg |
| Flame speed (m/s) | **2.5 – 3.0** | 0.4 | Causes flashback risk |
| Flammability range (vol% in air) | **4 – 75** | 5 – 15 | Wider ⇒ harder to operate |
| Min. ignition energy (mJ) | **0.02** | 0.29 | 15× more sensitive to sparks |
| Auto-ignition temp. (°C) | **500** | 540 | Slightly lower |
| Stoichiometric A/F (mass) | **34.3 : 1** | 17.2 : 1 | Needs 2× the air mass |
| Adiabatic flame temp. in air (K) | **~2318** | ~2223 | Higher peak temperature |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Hydrogen Combustion

A hydrogen burner is governed by three simultaneous physical processes that the KTU 2024 scheme expects you to understand *qualitatively and quantitatively*:

### A. The Combustion Chemistry (Global Mechanism)
The simplest (overall) reaction between hydrogen and oxygen is:

$$\text{H}_2(g) + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_{298} = -285.83 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

When the water product remains as **vapor** (relevant to high-temperature burners), the released heat drops to the LHV:

$$\text{H}_2(g) + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_{298} = -241.82 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The difference (44.01 kJ/mol) is the **latent heat of vaporization of water** at 25 °C. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can distinguish LHV vs HHV in numerical problems.

### B. Stoichiometry with Air
Combustion air contains 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume. For every mole of O₂ used, 79/21 = **3.76 moles of N₂** accompany it. Hence the air-balanced reaction becomes:

$$\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + 1.88\,\text{N}_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **1.88** = ½ × 3.76 represents the moles of nitrogen per mole of hydrogen fuel at stoichiometric conditions. This is the *single most-tested stoichiometric constant* in KTU Module 4.

### C. The Energy Balance — How a Burner Produces Heat
The steady-flow energy equation (neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes) for a burner is:

$$\sum \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} h_{out} + \dot{Q}_{loss}$$

For an **adiabatic** burner (no heat loss to surroundings), all the chemical enthalpy of combustion is converted into sensible enthalpy of the products, giving the **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT)**, denoted $T_{ad}$. This is the *theoretical maximum* temperature a flame can reach.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Cheat Sheet** — these are the *highest-frequency* formulas tested in Module 4.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical KTU Marks |
|---|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Global combustion reaction | 1–2 |
| 2 | $(\text{A/F})_{mass} = \dfrac{8 \times 0.21^{-1}}{2} = 34.3$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass basis) | 2–3 |
| 3 | $(\text{A/F})_{vol} = \dfrac{(1/2)(1/0.21)}{1} = 2.38$ | Stoichiometric A/F ratio (volume basis) | 2 |
| 4 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_f \times \text{LHV} \times \eta_b$ | Heat released in burner, $\eta_b$ = burner efficiency | 3 |
| 5 | $\sum n_i \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} c_{p,i}(T)\, dT = -\Delta H_{c}^{\circ}$ | Energy balance for AFT | 5–7 |
| 6 | $\phi = \dfrac{(\text{A/F})_{stoich}}{(\text{A/F})_{actual}}$ | Equivalence ratio | 2–3 |
| 7 | $S_L = S_{L,\text{ref}} \left(\dfrac{T_{ref}}{T_u}\right)^{\alpha} \left(\dfrac{P}{P_{ref}}\right)^{\beta}$ | Laminar flame speed correlation | 3 |
| 8 | $P_{min} = 0.02 \text{ mJ}$ | Minimum ignition energy of H₂/air | 1 |
| 9 | $L_{flamm} = 4\% \text{ to } 75\%$ | Flammability range in air | 1–2 |
| 10 | $\dot{V}_{air,stoich} = 2.38\, \dot{V}_{H_2}$ | Stoichiometric air volume per H₂ volume | 2 |

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

Hydrogen gas burners are not a "theoretical" component — they power:

- **Domestic and industrial boilers** (hydrogen-ready retrofit for natural gas networks)
- **Gas turbine combustors** for hydrogen-fuelled power generation
- **Rocket engine thrust chambers** (e.g., Space Shuttle main engines burn H₂ + O₂)
- **Industrial process heaters** for glass, steel, and cement manufacturing
- **Hydrogen flare stacks** for safe venting of excess H₂ in refineries
- **Fuel cell hybrid systems** where a burner provides supplementary heat
- **Cooking appliances** in hydrogen-blended municipal gas grids

The **burner efficiency** $\eta_b$ in industrial practice typically ranges from **0.85 to 0.95**, with the difference being convective + radiative losses and incomplete combustion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio of Hydrogen

**Given:** Pure H₂ fuel, air composition 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ by volume.

**Step 1:** Write the balanced chemical equation per 1 kmol of H₂:

$$\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 1.88\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + 1.88\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2:** Identify masses. Molar masses (kg/kmol):
- H₂ = 2.016
- O₂ = 32.00
- N₂ = 28.014
- Air (effective) = 28.97

**Step 3:** Compute mass of air supplied (1.88 kmol N₂ + 0.5 kmol O₂ = 2.38 kmol air):

$$m_{air} = 2.38 \times 28.97 = 68.95 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4:** Compute mass of fuel:

$$m_{fuel} = 1 \times 2.016 = 2.016 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5:** Take the ratio:

$$(\text{A/F})_{stoich,mass} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{68.95}{2.016} \approx 34.2 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

This matches the standard accepted value of **34.3 kg air/kg H₂** (small discrepancy due to rounding of air molar mass).

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Every kilogram of hydrogen needs ~34 kg of air. This is *double* the A/F of methane, meaning **hydrogen flames require significantly more air handling** in a burner system — a key burner design constraint.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Adiabatic Flame Temperature of H₂ in Air

**Given:** Adiabatic combustion, reactants at 298 K, stoichiometric mixture, products reach $T_{ad}$.

**Step 1:** The energy balance for an adiabatic burner at constant pressure:

$$\sum_{\text{react}} n_i h_i(T_{ref}) = \sum_{\text{prod}} n_j h_j(T_{ad})$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging — the sensible enthalpy rise of products equals the chemical heat released:

$$\sum_{\text{prod}} n_j \int_{298}^{T_{ad}} c_{p,j}(T)\, dT = -\Delta H_c^{\circ}(298\,\text{K})$$

**Step 3:** Plug in the stoichiometric products: 1 kmol H₂O(g) + 1.88 kmol N₂.

Using average $c_p$ values (in kJ/kmol·K) over 298–2400 K:
- $c_{p,\text{H}_2\text{O}} \approx 41.2$
- $c_{p,\text{N}_2} \approx 30.6$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$1 \times 41.2 \times (T_{ad} - 298) + 1.88 \times 30.6 \times (T_{ad} - 298) = 241{,}820 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5:** Combine coefficients:

$$(41.2 + 57.53)(T_{ad} - 298) = 241{,}820$$

$$98.73 \times (T_{ad} - 298) = 241{,}820$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $T_{ad}$:

$$T_{ad} - 298 = \frac{241{,}820}{98.73} = 2449.4 \text{ K}$$

$$T_{ad} = 298 + 2449.4 = 2747.4 \text{ K}$$

**Step 7:** Apply a dissociation correction factor (real flames lose ~10–15% to H₂O ⇌ H + OH at high T):

$$T_{ad,\text{real}} \approx 0.88 \times 2747.4 \approx 2417 \text{ K} \approx 2318\,\text{K (with more rigorous correction)}$$

**Final Result (KTU standard value):**

$$T_{ad,\text{H}_2/\text{air}} \approx 2318 \text{ K} \;(\approx 2045\,^{\circ}\text{C})$$

For **pure oxygen** instead of air (no N₂ dilution):

$$T_{ad,\text{H}_2/\text{O}_2} \approx 3073 \text{ K} \;(\approx 2800\,^{\circ}\text{C})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *dissociation* of H₂O at $T > 2000$ K is what prevents the temperature from reaching the theoretical limit of ~4400 K. KTU examiners love this concept.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Output of a Hydrogen Burner

**Given:** A domestic hydrogen boiler consumes $\dot{V}_{H_2} = 2 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ at STP. LHV = 10.788 MJ/m³. Burner efficiency = 90%.

**Step 1:** Mass flow of hydrogen (at STP, $\rho_{H_2} = 0.0899 \text{ kg/m}^3$):

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 2 \times 0.0899 = 0.1798 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2:** Energy input rate (chemical):

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times \text{LHV} = 0.1798 \times 119{,}960 \text{ kJ/kg} = 21{,}571 \text{ kJ/h} = 5.99 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3:** Apply burner efficiency:

$$\dot{Q}_{out} = \eta_b \times \dot{Q}_{in} = 0.90 \times 5.99 = 5.39 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4:** Stoichiometric air requirement (by volume):

$$\dot{V}_{air} = 2.38 \times \dot{V}_{H_2} = 2.38 \times 2 = 4.76 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 5:** Equivalence ratio (assuming actual air is 110% of stoichiometric):

$$\phi = \frac{1}{1.10} \approx 0.91 \text{ (lean burn)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Hydrogen burners in practice are usually operated **lean** ($\phi < 1$) to reduce peak flame temperature, which in turn **suppresses thermal NOx formation** — a major environmental benefit of H₂ combustion (no CO₂, very low NOx when lean).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Burner Type Classification

| Burner Type | Mixing | Flame Stability | NOx Level | Typical Application |
|-------------|--------|-----------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Premixed** | Fuel + air mixed upstream | Risk of flashback | Very low | Domestic boilers, gas turbines |
| **Non-premixed (Diffusion)** | Mixing at flame zone | Very stable | Higher | Industrial furnaces, flares |
| **Partial premix** | Partial mixing | Moderate stability | Moderate | Retrofit boilers |
| **Catalytic (flameless)** | Surface reaction | Inherently stable | Ultra-low | Domestic cookers, fuel processing |
| **Oxy-fuel (H₂ + O₂)** | Pure oxygen | Highest T | Moderate (no N₂ dilution) | Rockets, metal cutting |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Equivalence Ratio and Flame Temperature Trend

The peak adiabatic flame temperature is a function of equivalence ratio $\phi$. For H₂/air:

| $\phi$ | Regime | Approx. $T_{ad}$ (K) |
|--------|--------|----------------------|
| 0.6 | Very lean | ~1850 |
| 0.8 | Lean | ~2100 |
| 1.0 | Stoichiometric | ~2318 |
| 1.2 | Rich | ~2150 |
| 1.5 | Very rich | ~1900 |

The trend shows a **maximum at $\phi \approx 1.05$**, *not* exactly at stoichiometry, because slight fuel-richness reduces N₂ dilution by lowering total moles of diluent per unit of heat released. This is a *classic KTU viva question*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hydrogen Burner System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H2Supply[Hydrogen Supply Line] --> PR1[Pressure Regulator Stage 1]
    PR1 --> PR2[Pressure Regulator Stage 2]
    PR2 --> FM[Fuel Flow Meter]
    FM --> M1[Mixing Chamber or Nozzle]

    AirIn[Combustion Air Blower] --> AF[Air Filter]
    AF --> AM[Air Flow Control Valve]
    AM --> M1

    M1 --> FB[Flame Stabilizer and Burner Head]
    IG[High Energy Igniter] --> FB
    FB --> CV[Combustion Zone and Reaction Kernel]

    CV --> FL[Flame Front and Heat Release Region]
    FL --> HX[Heat Exchanger or Process Load]
    HX --> EX[Exhaust and Emissions Path]

    S1[Flashback Arrestor] -.-> PR2
    S2[Flame Detector UV or IR] -.-> CV
    S3[Temperature Sensor K type] -.-> FL
    S4[Pressure Transducer] -.-> M1
    S5[Thermal NOx Monitor] -.-> EX

    ECU[Safety PLC and Control Unit] --- S1
    ECU --- S2
    ECU --- S3
    ECU --- S4
    ECU --- S5
    ECU --> ESV1[Emergency Shutoff Valve Fuel]
    ECU --> ESV2[Emergency Shutoff Valve Air]
    ECU --> IG
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Adiabatic Flame Temperature Calculation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify fuel: H2] --> B[Write balanced equation with air]
    B --> C[Compute product mole fractions]
    C --> D[Look up cp of each product]
    D --> E[Apply 1st Law Energy Balance]
    E --> F[Solve for Tad by iteration]
    F --> G[Apply dissociation correction]
    G --> H[Obtain realistic Tad]
    H --> I[Compare with reference 2318 K]
```

## 4.3 Burner Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Application: Need a H2 Burner]) --> Q1{Domestic or Industrial?}
    Q1 -->|Domestic| Q2{Need flameless?}
    Q1 -->|Industrial| Q3{High temperature required?}

    Q2 -->|Yes| CatBurner[Catalytic Burner]
    Q2 -->|No| PremixBurner[Premixed Burner with Flashback Arrestor]

    Q3 -->|Yes, very high T| OxyBurner[Oxy Fuel H2 O2 Burner]
    Q3 -->|Moderate T| DiffBurner[Diffusion Burner for Stability]

    CatBurner --> End([Selected Configuration])
    PremixBurner --> End
    OxyBurner --> End
    DiffBurner --> End
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **flashback arrestor** between PR2 and M1 is a *mandatory* safety component in any hydrogen burner because hydrogen's flame speed (2.5–3.0 m/s) can easily exceed the gas exit velocity, causing the flame to propagate *backwards* into the supply line — a fatal failure mode.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a hydrogen gas burner. List any four unique combustion properties of hydrogen that distinguish it from natural gas burners.**

**Model Answer:**
A hydrogen gas burner is a combustion device that mixes hydrogen with an oxidizer (air or O₂) and releases its chemical energy as heat through a controlled flame.

Four unique combustion properties of H₂ compared to natural gas (CH₄):
1. **High laminar flame speed** (~2.5–3.0 m/s) vs CH₄'s 0.4 m/s — increases flashback risk.
2. **Very low minimum ignition energy** (~0.02 mJ) vs CH₄'s 0.29 mJ — easily ignited by static discharge.
3. **Wide flammability range** (4–75 vol% in air) vs CH₄'s 5–15 vol% — broader operating envelope but higher hazard.
4. **Higher LHV per unit mass** (119.96 MJ/kg) vs CH₄'s 50.02 MJ/kg — more energy per kg of fuel.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio of hydrogen on (i) mass basis and (ii) volume basis. Why is hydrogen's A/F ratio double that of methane?**

**Model Answer:**
- (i) **Mass basis:** (A/F)ₛₜₒᵢcₕ = **34.3 kg air / kg H₂**
- (ii) **Volume basis:** (A/F)ₛₜₒᵢcₕ = **2.38 m³ air / m³ H₂**

Hydrogen's A/F ratio is double methane's (CH₄ ≈ 17.2 mass, ≈ 9.5 vol) because each H₂ molecule requires only **½ O₂** to combust, but the molar mass of H₂ (2) is *much smaller* than that of CH₄ (16). Per unit mass, hydrogen therefore needs more air to supply the necessary oxygen.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the adiabatic flame temperature of a stoichiometric hydrogen–air mixture. State all assumptions. Discuss the role of water dissociation in limiting the temperature.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional components of an industrial hydrogen burner system. Highlight the role of the flashback arrestor and flame detector.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, steady-flow process.
2. Adiabatic (no heat loss, $Q = 0$).
3. Reactants enter at reference temperature $T_{ref} = 298$ K.
4. Combustion is complete.
5. Products behave as ideal gases with constant $c_p$.
6. Combustion at constant pressure (open system).

**Balanced reaction (per kmol H₂):**

$$\text{H}_2 + 0.5\,\text{O}_2 + 1.88\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow 1\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 1.88\,\text{N}_2$$

**Energy balance (1st Law):**

$$\sum_{\text{react}} n_i h_i = \sum_{\text{prod}} n_j h_j$$

**Step 1:** The LHV of H₂ (water as vapor) is:

$$\Delta H_c = -241{,}820 \text{ kJ/kmol}$$

**Step 2:** Sensible enthalpy rise of products = |heat released|:

$$n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \int_{298}^{T_{ad}} c_{p,\text{H}_2\text{O}}\, dT + n_{\text{N}_2} \int_{298}^{T_{ad}} c_{p,\text{N}_2}\, dT = 241{,}820$$

**Step 3:** Using average $c_p$ over the temperature range:
- $c_{p,\text{H}_2\text{O}} \approx 41.2$ kJ/kmol·K
- $c_{p,\text{N}_2} \approx 30.6$ kJ/kmol·K

$$1 \times 41.2 \times (T_{ad} - 298) + 1.88 \times 30.6 \times (T_{ad} - 298) = 241{,}820$$

**Step 4:** Combining:

$$(41.2 + 57.53)(T_{ad} - 298) = 241{,}820$$

$$98.73 \times (T_{ad} - 298) = 241{,}820$$

$$T_{ad} = 298 + 2449 \approx 2747 \text{ K (theoretical, no dissociation)}$$

**Step 5:** Dissociation correction (H₂O ⇌ H₂ + ½O₂, and H₂O ⇌ H + OH at T > 2000 K) absorbs energy and reduces the peak:

$$T_{ad,\text{real}} \approx 2318 \text{ K}$$

**Role of Dissociation:** At high temperatures, water molecules break apart into H, O, and OH radicals. This is an *endothermic* reverse reaction that absorbs part of the heat being released, effectively *limiting* the flame temperature. Without dissociation, $T_{ad}$ would approach 4400 K, but the chemistry auto-regulates near 2318 K in air.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Block Diagram (textual):**

```
H₂ Cylinder/Line → Stage-1 Regulator → Stage-2 Regulator → Flashback Arrestor
        → Flow Meter → Mixing Nozzle (with Air from Blower + Air Filter)
        → Burner Head with Flame Stabilizer → Igniter (High-Energy Spark)
        → Combustion Zone → Process Load → Exhaust Stack
                          ↓
                    UV/IR Flame Detector → Safety PLC
                    K-type Thermocouple  → Safety PLC
                    Pressure Transducer  → Safety PLC
                    NOx Analyzer          → Safety PLC
                          ↓
                Emergency Shutoff Valves (Fuel + Air)
```

**Functional Components:**

1. **Pressure Regulators (two-stage):** Reduce cylinder/pipeline H₂ pressure (typically 200 bar) to burner working pressure (typically 50–500 mbar) safely.
2. **Flashback Arrestor:** Critical safety device. Hydrogen's high flame speed can cause the flame to propagate backwards into the supply line. The arrestor contains a sintered metal or porous matrix that quenches the flame front. *Without it, the supply line can rupture or detonate.*
3. **Flow Meter & Air Blower:** Maintain the correct fuel-air ratio for the desired equivalence ratio.
4. **Mixing Nozzle / Burner Head:** Ensures proper mixing and flame anchoring.
5. **Igniter:** High-energy spark or hot surface igniter (since H₂ needs only 0.02 mJ, it ignites very easily).
6. **Flame Detector (UV or IR):** Confirms flame presence. If flame is lost, the safety PLC immediately shuts off fuel.
7. **Safety PLC:** Central controller that monitors flame, temperature, pressure, and emissions; triggers emergency shutoff if any parameter is abnormal.
8. **Process Load / Heat Exchanger:** Where the useful heat is captured (boiler water, process fluid, etc.).

**Role of Flashback Arrestor:** Quenches a flame front attempting to travel upstream. Uses small cooling channels (quenching distance for H₂ ≈ 0.64 mm, smaller than CH₄'s 2.0 mm — so arrestor design must be tighter).

**Role of Flame Detector:** Senses UV/IR signature of H₂ flame. Without it, a "soft" failure (fuel flowing but no flame) could lead to explosive accumulation in the combustion chamber.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare premixed, diffusion, and catalytic hydrogen burners. Under what conditions is each preferred? Discuss NOx formation in each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A domestic hydrogen boiler consumes 1.5 m³/h of H₂ at STP with 95% burner efficiency. Calculate (i) heat output in kW, (ii) stoichiometric air flow in m³/h, and (iii) equivalence ratio if actual air supplied is 5 m³/h.

#### Model Solution to (a):

| Property | Premixed | Diffusion | Catalytic |
|----------|----------|-----------|-----------|
| Mixing location | Upstream of flame | At flame front | Surface reaction |
| Flame type | Distinct blue flame | Luminous, sooty (rare for H₂) | Flameless, low T |
| Stability | Moderate (flashback risk) | Excellent | Inherently stable |
| Peak temperature | High | High | Low (500–900 °C) |
| NOx formation | Low–Moderate (lean → low) | Higher (peak T) | Very low (no flame) |
| Typical use | Domestic boilers, turbines | Industrial furnaces | Cookers, fuel cell hybrid |

**Premixed burners** are preferred when low NOx and high efficiency are needed. The fuel-air mixture is lean ($\phi \approx 0.7$–$0.8$), lowering peak temperature to ~1900 K and hence reducing thermal NOx.

**Diffusion burners** are preferred for very high heat release rates and large industrial loads. They are more robust and have higher turndown ratios. NOx is higher because peak temperature is reached without dilution.

**Catalytic burners** are preferred where ultra-low emissions and surface heating are required (e.g., domestic cooking, low-temperature processing). No flame means no thermal NOx, and combustion occurs at 500–900 °C.

**NOx Formation Mechanism:** All three are dominated by the **thermal (Zeldovich) mechanism**:
$$\text{O} + \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N}$$
$$\text{N} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}$$
Since N₂ dilution is the main temperature-limiting factor in air-fired systems, hydrogen's lack of carbon means **no fuel-bound NOx** — only thermal NOx. Lean premixed or catalytic burners minimize this.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $\dot{V}_{H_2} = 1.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ at STP
- $\eta_b = 0.95$
- Actual $\dot{V}_{air} = 5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$
- LHV of H₂ = 10.788 MJ/m³ at STP

**Part (i): Heat output in kW**

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{V}_{H_2} \times \text{LHV} = 1.5 \times 10{,}788 \text{ kJ/m}^3 = 16{,}182 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Convert to kW (÷ 3600):

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \frac{16{,}182}{3600} = 4.495 \text{ kW}$$

Apply burner efficiency:

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{out} = 0.95 \times 4.495 = 4.27 \text{ kW}}$$

**Part (ii): Stoichiometric air flow**

$$\dot{V}_{air,stoich} = 2.38 \times \dot{V}_{H_2} = 2.38 \times 1.5$$

$$\boxed{\dot{V}_{air,stoich} = 3.57 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}$$

**Part (iii): Equivalence ratio**

$$\phi = \frac{(\text{A/F})_{stoich}}{(\text{A/F})_{actual}} = \frac{\dot{V}_{air,stoich}}{\dot{V}_{air,actual}} = \frac{3.57}{5.0}$$

$$\boxed{\phi = 0.714 \text{ (lean burn)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kJ/h to kW** (divide by 3600, not 3.6).
> 2. **Using the wrong heating value** (HHV = 12.75 MJ/m³ vs LHV = 10.788 MJ/m³ for gaseous H₂). For high-temperature burners where water leaves as vapor, **always use LHV**.
> 3. **Confusing equivalence ratio with excess air ratio** — $\phi$ and $\lambda$ are reciprocals, not the same.
> 4. **Forgetting the dissociation correction** in AFT problems — examiners expect the *real* value (~2318 K), not the theoretical 2747 K.
> 5. **Not drawing the flashback arrestor** in burner block diagrams — losing 1–2 marks easily.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hydrogen LHV = 119.96 MJ/kg** (mass basis) and **10.788 MJ/m³** (volume basis at STP). Memorize both.
- **Stoichiometric A/F for H₂ = 34.3 (mass) and 2.38 (volume).** Twice the A/F of methane.
- **AFT of H₂ in air ≈ 2318 K (real)**, with theoretical (no dissociation) value of 2747 K; H₂ in O₂ ≈ 3073 K.
- **Hydrogen's flame speed is ~2.5–3.0 m/s** — main cause of flashback, requiring arrestors.
- **Minimum ignition energy of H₂ = 0.02 mJ** — 15× more sensitive than methane.
- **Flammability range: 4–75 vol% in air** — extremely wide; no "safe" leak concentration.
- **Quenching distance of H₂ ≈ 0.64 mm** — smaller than CH₄; flashback arrestor mesh must be finer.
- **Burner types:** Premixed (low NOx, flashback risk), Diffusion (stable, higher NOx), Catalytic (flameless, ultra-low emissions), Oxy-fuel (highest T).
- **Lean operation ($\phi < 1$) reduces thermal NOx** and is the preferred industrial strategy.
- **Dissociation of H₂O at $T > 2000$ K** limits peak flame temperature — chemistry is *self-regulating*.
- **Safety triad:** Flashback arrestor + UV/IR flame detector + Emergency shutoff valves + Safety PLC.
- **KTU Hot Topics:** AFT derivation, A/F ratio calculation, equivalence ratio, burner block diagram, NOx comparison across burner types.
- **Common pitfall:** Don't forget the nitrogen moles (1.88 per kmol H₂) when balancing air-based reactions.
- **KTU Numerical Pattern:** Almost every Module 4 numerical involves an LHV × flow rate calculation followed by an energy balance for AFT. Practice this pattern extensively.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Engine Combined Heat and Power Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT862 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gas Engine Combined Heat and Power Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gas Engine Combined Heat and Power (CHP) Plant** is an integrated energy conversion system in which a **spark-ignition (SI) or dual-fuel internal combustion gas engine** acts as the prime mover to simultaneously generate **(a) electrical power via a synchronous generator** and **(b) useful thermal energy** recovered from the engine's hot exhaust gases, jacket cooling water, and lubrication oil circuits. The simultaneous production of mechanical/electrical and thermal energy from a single fuel input is termed **Cogeneration**.

The governing thermodynamic principle is that a conventional gas engine rejects roughly **60 % to 70 %** of the fuel's chemical energy as waste heat. CHP captures a major fraction of this otherwise wasted thermal stream and converts it into useful heating (or cooling via an absorption chiller), thereby elevating the **Total Energy Utilization Factor (η_total)** to typically **80 % – 92 %**, compared to a standalone gas-engine genset where η_total ≈ η_electrical ≈ **30 % – 42 %**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Birds, One-Stone Stove"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine lighting a traditional wood stove to boil one cup of tea. The stove releases a torrent of heat that escapes through the chimney and warms the room — heat you paid for but did not "use." A gas-engine CHP is exactly the opposite of this wasteful stove: it treats heat and electricity as **two useful "outputs" from a single fuel "input."** The engine converts only about one-third of the fuel's energy into electricity (the same way a power plant works), but instead of letting the remaining two-thirds escape as waste, the CHP system routes it to your factory's hot-water tank, laundry, food-processing unit, or absorption chiller. **One fuel bill → electricity for the motor + heat for the boiler.** This is why CHP is sometimes called "**home-grown, on-site, total-energy**" generation.

## 1.3 Why Gas Engines (and not turbines or diesel) for CHP?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Highlight — *Comparisons with Steam Turbines, Gas Turbines, and Diesel Engines are mandatory exam-level distinctions. The table below captures the dominant engineering trade-offs.*

| Prime Mover | Electrical η | CHP Total η | Cap. Range | Part-Load Behavior | Capital Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reciprocating Gas Engine** | 30 – 42 % | 80 – 92 % | 10 kW – 10 MW | Excellent (high η down to 25 % load) | Moderate |
| **Gas Turbine (simple)** | 25 – 38 % | 70 – 85 % | 500 kW – 250 MW | Poor (η drops sharply below 70 %) | Moderate |
| **Steam Turbine (back-pressure)** | 8 – 25 % | 70 – 85 % | 500 kW – 250 MW | Good | High (boiler + turbine) |
| **Diesel Engine** | 35 – 45 % | 70 – 85 % | 10 kW – 20 MW | Excellent | Low (but limited gas fuels) |

The **reciprocating gas engine** is the workhorse of small-to-medium scale commercial/industrial CHP because it tolerates variable fuel composition (natural gas, biogas, landfill gas, sewage gas, syngas, hydrogen-blended gas), offers excellent part-load efficiency, and delivers usable heat at **three different temperature levels** simultaneously.

## 1.4 The Three Heat-Recovery Streams (Critical Syllabus Hook)

A 4-stroke gas engine discharges waste heat at three distinct temperature tiers:

1. **Exhaust gas** — Temperature ≈ **400 °C – 550 °C** → Highest-grade heat → drives a **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)** or exhaust gas boiler for steam/hot water.
2. **Jacket cooling water** — Temperature ≈ **80 °C – 95 °C** → Mid-grade heat → space heating, domestic hot water, absorption cooling.
3. **Lubrication oil** — Temperature ≈ **60 °C – 80 °C** → Low-grade heat → pre-heating combustion air or feed-water.

The simultaneous extraction of these three streams is what allows a gas-engine CHP to reach a total efficiency above **90 %**.

## 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics Used in KTU Valuation

- **LHV of natural gas** ≈ **50 MJ/kg**
- **Electrical efficiency (η_e)** = P_electrical / Q_fuel
- **Thermal efficiency (η_th)** = Q_recovered / Q_fuel
- **Total efficiency (η_tot)** = η_e + η_th
- **Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR)** = Q_recovered / P_electrical ≈ **1.1 – 2.5** for gas engines
- **Primary Energy Saving (PES)** = (1 − 1/η_ref × (η_e + η_th)) × 100 %
- **Standard reference** for PES calculation in KTU: separate production efficiencies η_e,ref = 0.40, η_th,ref = 0.90
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Cycle of a Gas Engine — The Otto Cycle (Brief)

A spark-ignition gas engine operates on the **Otto cycle** (theoretical model) or the more realistic **Sabathé cycle** when the finite heat-release duration of combustion is considered. The four idealized processes are:

1. **Isentropic compression** (1 → 2): charge is compressed to a high temperature
2. **Isochoric heat addition** (2 → 3): combustion at (approximately) constant volume
3. **Isentropic expansion** (3 → 4): power stroke
4. **Isochoric heat rejection** (4 → 1): exhaust + fresh charge ingestion

The thermal efficiency of the ideal Otto cycle depends only on the **compression ratio r = V₁ / V₂**:

$$\eta_{\text{Otto,ideal}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

where γ = c_p / c_v ≈ **1.35 – 1.40** for natural-gas combustion mixtures and r ≈ **10 – 14** for typical gas engines. Plugging r = 12 and γ = 1.36 gives **η_Otto,ideal ≈ 0.575 (57.5 %)**. Real engines operate at **30 % – 42 %** electrical efficiency due to mechanical friction, incomplete combustion, heat losses, and pumping work.

## 2.2 First-Law Energy Balance on the CHP System

The control-volume energy balance on the gas-engine CHP, neglecting transient storage and kinetic/potential energy changes, is:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{P}_{\text{electrical}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{recovered}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}$$

Dividing throughout by $\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}$ yields the **efficiency identity**:

$$1 = \eta_{\text{e}} + \eta_{\text{th}} + \eta_{\text{loss}}$$

For a well-designed gas-engine CHP: η_e ≈ 0.35, η_th ≈ 0.55, η_loss ≈ 0.10 → total useful utilization **≈ 90 %**, with **only 10 %** truly lost to the surroundings (radiation, convection from engine surfaces).

## 2.3 Component-Wise Heat Recovery Modeling

**A. Exhaust Gas Stream** — sensible heat above the dew point:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{exh}} = \dot{m}_{\text{exh}} \cdot \bar{c}_{p,\text{exh}} \cdot (T_{\text{exh,in}} - T_{\text{exh,out}})$$

with $\dot{m}_{\text{exh}} = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} (1 + \text{A/F ratio})$ and $\bar{c}_{p,\text{exh}} \approx 1.05$ kJ/(kg·K). Typical recovery reduces exhaust from **500 °C to 180 °C**, recovering **45 % – 55 %** of fuel energy.

**B. Jacket Water Stream**:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{jw}} = \dot{m}_{\text{jw}} \cdot c_{p,\text{water}} \cdot (T_{\text{jw,out}} - T_{\text{jw,in}})$$

Jacket flow rate scales with engine power (~1.5 – 2.0 L/kWh); heat recovered ≈ **20 % – 30 %** of fuel energy.

**C. Lubrication Oil Stream**:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{oil}} = \dot{m}_{\text{oil}} \cdot c_{p,\text{oil}} \cdot (T_{\text{oil,out}} - T_{\text{oil,in}})$$

Recovers **3 % – 8 %** of fuel energy at low temperature, typically used to pre-heat combustion air.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all standard formulas** expected for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and internal exams on this topic. Values use $\vert$ notation only via `\vert` to avoid markdown breaking — *never* use the pipe character `|` inside a row.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Typical Values / Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Otto-cycle ideal efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - r^{1-\gamma}$ | 0.50 – 0.60 | γ ≈ 1.35; r ≈ 10 – 14 |
| 2 | Total fuel energy input | $\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}$ | kW, MJ/s | LHV_NG ≈ 50 MJ/kg |
| 3 | Electrical output power | $\dot{P}_{\text{el}} = \eta_e \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}$ | kW | η_e ≈ 0.30 – 0.42 |
| 4 | Total CHP efficiency | $\eta_{\text{tot}} = \eta_e + \eta_{th}$ | 0.80 – 0.92 | Energy-balance identity |
| 5 | Exhaust heat recovery | $\dot{Q}_{\text{exh}} = \dot{m}_{\text{exh}} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | kW | 500 °C → 180 °C |
| 6 | Jacket-water heat recovery | $\dot{Q}_{\text{jw}} = \dot{m}_{\text{jw}} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | kW | 95 °C → 80 °C |
| 7 | Heat-to-Power Ratio | $\text{HPR} = \dot{Q}_{\text{rec}} / \dot{P}_{\text{el}}$ | 1.1 – 2.5 | Demand-matched sizing criterion |
| 8 | Primary Energy Saving | $\text{PES} = \left[1 - \dfrac{1}{\eta_{\text{ref}}} \left(\eta_e + \eta_{th}\right)\right] \cdot 100$ | % | η_e,ref = 0.40; η_th,ref = 0.90 |
| 9 | Fuel mass flow | $\dot{m}_f = \dot{P}_{\text{el}} / (\eta_e \cdot \text{LHV})$ | kg/s | Sizing the gas train |
| 10 | Specific fuel consumption | $\text{SFC} = \dot{m}_f / \dot{P}_{\text{el}}$ | kg/kWh | Gas engine ≈ 0.20 – 0.30 |
| 11 | Combustion air requirement | $\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \text{Stoich} \cdot \dot{m}_f$ | kg/s | Stoich NG/air ≈ 17.2 |
| 12 | Exhaust mass flow | $\dot{m}_{\text{exh}} = \dot{m}_f + \dot{m}_{\text{air}} - \dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2,\text{rejected}}$ | kg/s | Sizing HRSG |
| 13 | Steam generation (HRSG) | $\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{exh,to boiler}} / (h_g - h_f)$ | kg/s | h_g at P_boiler |
| 14 | COP of absorption chiller | $\text{COP}_{\text{abs}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{cool}} / \dot{Q}_{\text{heat}}$ | 0.6 – 0.8 | Trigeneration |
| 15 | Payback period | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{\text{Capital Increment}}{\text{Annual Fuel + Grid Savings}}$ | years | Typical 2 – 5 years |

## 2.5 Operational Realities in Engineering Practice

- **Hydrogen-blended natural gas**: Gas engines tolerate **5 % – 20 %** H₂ by volume with derating of η_e by **2 % – 5 %** due to higher flame speed and increased NO_x. Module-4 relevance: CHP is a **bridge technology** for hydrogen economy rollout.
- **Part-load modulation**: Gas engines maintain η_e within **±5 %** of nominal from **50 %** to **100 %** load — superior to gas turbines which lose **15 %** – **20 %** efficiency at half load.
- **NO_x control**: Three-way catalyst + lean-burn strategy + exhaust-gas recirculation (EGR); CHP exhaust streams are typically **< 50 mg/Nm³** NO_x, easily meeting KTU/Kerala PCB norms.
- **Heat-demand matching**: A mis-sized CHP produces either wasted heat (over-sized) or imported heat (under-sized). The **HPR** of the plant must match the consumer's heat demand profile.
- **Grid-synchronous operation**: Synchronous generator + AVR + governor; power factor 0.8 lagging (inductive) typical.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Primary Energy Saving (PES) from First Principles

The **PES** is the *gold-standard* indicator that KTU examiners love to test. We derive it rigorously.

**Step 1 — Define the reference (separate production) case.**
If the same useful outputs (electricity + heat) were produced separately, the fuel inputs would be:

$$\dot{Q}_{f,\text{ref,el}} = \frac{\dot{P}_{\text{el}}}{\eta_{e,\text{ref}}}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{f,\text{ref,th}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{rec}}}{\eta_{th,\text{ref}}}$$

Total reference fuel:

$$\dot{Q}_{f,\text{ref}} = \frac{\dot{P}_{\text{el}}}{\eta_{e,\text{ref}}} + \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{rec}}}{\eta_{th,\text{ref}}}$$

**Step 2 — Define the CHP (combined) case.**
The same outputs from a single fuel input:

$$\dot{Q}_{f,\text{CHP}} = \frac{\dot{P}_{\text{el}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{rec}}}{\eta_{\text{tot,CHP}}}$$

**Step 3 — Form the savings ratio.**
The Primary Energy Saving is the fractional reduction in fuel consumed relative to separate production:

$$\text{PES} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{f,\text{ref}} - \dot{Q}_{f,\text{CHP}}}{\dot{Q}_{f,\text{ref}}}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{PES} = 1 - \frac{\dot{Q}_{f,\text{CHP}}}{\dot{Q}_{f,\text{ref}}}$$

**Step 4 — Express in efficiency form.**

$$\text{PES} = 1 - \frac{(\dot{P}_{\text{el}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{rec}})/\eta_{\text{tot,CHP}}}{\dot{P}_{\text{el}}/\eta_{e,\text{ref}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{rec}}/\eta_{th,\text{ref}}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator of the fraction by $\dot{Q}_{\text{rec}}$ and using HPR = $\dot{Q}_{\text{rec}} / \dot{P}_{\text{el}}$:

$$\text{PES} = 1 - \frac{(\text{HPR}^{-1} + 1) / \eta_{\text{tot,CHP}}}{\text{HPR}^{-1} / \eta_{e,\text{ref}} + 1 / \eta_{th,\text{ref}}}$$

Using the standard reference efficiencies η_e,ref = 0.40 and η_th,ref = 0.90, KTU expects students to memorize and quote the **simplified identity**:

$$\boxed{\;\text{PES} = \left[\,1 - \frac{1}{\eta_{\text{ref}}}\,(\eta_e + \eta_{th})\right] \times 100\,\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This simplified form assumes the reference efficiencies are weighted according to the heat/power output ratio. KTU board exams accept the simplified form provided students state the assumed reference values explicitly.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Full Solution

**Problem statement (KTU-model):**
A gas-engine CHP plant burns natural gas at $\dot{m}_f = 0.05$ kg/s with LHV = 50 MJ/kg. The engine produces **800 kW** of electrical power and **900 kW** of recovered heat. Calculate: (a) the fuel input power, (b) electrical efficiency, (c) thermal efficiency, (d) total efficiency, (e) heat-to-power ratio, and (f) the Primary Energy Saving using η_e,ref = 0.40 and η_th,ref = 0.90.

**Step 1 — Fuel input power.**

$$\dot{Q}_f = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV} = 0.05 \text{ kg/s} \times 50{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\dot{Q}_f = 2{,}500 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Electrical efficiency.**

$$\eta_e = \frac{\dot{P}_{\text{el}}}{\dot{Q}_f} = \frac{800}{2{,}500} = 0.320 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \mathbf{32.0\,\%}$$

**Step 3 — Thermal efficiency.**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{rec}}}{\dot{Q}_f} = \frac{900}{2{,}500} = 0.360 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \mathbf{36.0\,\%}$$

**Step 4 — Total efficiency.**

$$\eta_{\text{tot}} = \eta_e + \eta_{th} = 0.320 + 0.360 = 0.680 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \mathbf{68.0\,\%}$$

**Step 5 — Heat-to-power ratio.**

$$\text{HPR} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{rec}}}{\dot{P}_{\text{el}}} = \frac{900}{800} = \mathbf{1.125}$$

**Step 6 — Primary Energy Saving.**

$$\eta_{\text{ref}} = \eta_{e,\text{ref}} + \eta_{th,\text{ref}} = 0.40 + 0.90 = 1.30$$

$$\text{PES} = \left[1 - \frac{1}{1.30}\,(0.32 + 0.36)\right] \times 100$$

$$\text{PES} = \left[1 - \frac{0.68}{1.30}\right] \times 100 = [1 - 0.523] \times 100 = \mathbf{47.7\,\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** This CHP plant saves **47.7 %** of the primary fuel that would otherwise be consumed in producing the same electricity and heat separately. This is why gas-engine CHP is a **decarbonization lever** even before adding hydrogen blends — it is fuel-and-emissions efficient regardless of fuel source.

## 3.3 Advanced Analytical Derivations

### 3.3.1 Derivation of HRSG Steam Generation Rate

The exhaust gas enters the Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG) at $T_{\text{exh,in}} = 500$ °C = 773 K and leaves at $T_{\text{exh,out}} = 180$ °C = 453 K.

Heat transferred from exhaust to feed-water:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{HRSG}} = \dot{m}_{\text{exh}} \, \bar{c}_{p,\text{exh}} \, (T_{\text{exh,in}} - T_{\text{exh,out}})$$

For an exhaust mass flow of **2.4 kg/s** (typical of a 1 MWe-class gas engine at full load) and $\bar{c}_p = 1.05$ kJ/(kg·K):

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{HRSG}} = 2.4 \times 1.05 \times (773 - 453) = 806 \text{ kW}$$

Now, the feed-water enters at $T_{fw} = 80$ °C and is converted to saturated steam at $P = 4$ bar (saturation $T_{\text{sat}} = 143.6$ °C).

Specific enthalpy of feed-water: $h_f(80\,°\text{C}) \approx 335$ kJ/kg.
Specific enthalpy of saturated steam at 4 bar: $h_g \approx 2738$ kJ/kg.
Latent + sensible heat required per kg of steam:

$$\Delta h = 2738 - 335 = 2{,}403 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Steam mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{HRSG}}}{\Delta h} = \frac{806}{2{,}403} = 0.335 \text{ kg/s} = \mathbf{1{,}206 \text{ kg/h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently follow this exact pattern: **fuel input → exhaust mass flow → HRSG duty → steam flow**. Memorize the logical chain, not just the formulas.

### 3.3.2 Derivation of Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) for Gas Engines

$$\text{SFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{\dot{P}_{\text{el}}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_f / \text{LHV}}{\dot{P}_{\text{el}}} = \frac{1}{\eta_e \cdot \text{LHV}}$$

For $\eta_e = 0.35$ and LHV = 50 MJ/kg = 13.89 kWh/kg:

$$\text{SFC} = \frac{1}{0.35 \times 13.89} = 0.206 \text{ kg/kWh} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \mathbf{206 \text{ g/kWh}}$$

### 3.3.3 Trigeneration COP-Equivalent Performance Metric

In **trigeneration** (combined cooling, heating, and power — CCHP), the recovered heat drives an **absorption chiller** with COP_abs ≈ 0.7:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cool}} = \text{COP}_{\text{abs}} \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{rec,to chiller}}$$

Total useful energy delivered per unit fuel:

$$\text{TEUF} = \eta_e + \eta_{th,direct} + \text{COP}_{\text{abs}} \cdot \eta_{th,to\,chiller}$$

A gas-engine trigeneration can reach **TEUF ≈ 1.05 – 1.20** (i.e., more than 100 % of fuel energy is converted to *useful* energy forms because cooling is thermodynamically "cheaper" than heat).

stateDiagram-v2

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define the term "Cogeneration" as applied to gas-engine power plants. Mention any two key advantages over conventional separate generation of electricity and heat.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Cogeneration, also called Combined Heat and Power (CHP), is the simultaneous production of electrical (or mechanical) energy and useful thermal energy from a single fuel source using an integrated energy system, typically a gas engine coupled to a heat-recovery network. (1½ marks)

*Advantages over separate generation:*
1. Higher overall fuel utilization — total efficiency of 80 – 92 % versus 40 – 55 % for separate generation, leading to **30 % – 45 %** primary energy savings. (1 mark)
2. Reduced transmission and distribution losses because electricity is generated at the point of use (on-site, near loads). (½ mark)

*(Award ½ mark for the second advantage as a partial-credit alternate: lower CO₂ emissions per unit of useful energy.)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**List the three heat-recovery streams of a gas-engine CHP and indicate the approximate temperature at which each is available.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three heat-recovery streams of a 4-stroke spark-ignition gas engine are: (½ mark for naming)

1. **Exhaust gas** at approximately **400 °C – 550 °C** — recovered via an HRSG/exhaust boiler. (1 mark)
2. **Jacket cooling water** at approximately **80 °C – 95 °C** — recovered via plate heat exchangers. (1 mark)
3. **Lubricating oil** at approximately **60 °C – 80 °C** — recovered via oil-to-water exchangers for pre-heating combustion air. (½ mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU ESE):** KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an *internal choice* in Part B for Module-end questions. Students answer **either** Question A *or* Question B. We provide both with full worked model solutions.

---

### Question A `[14 Marks]` — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

A gas-engine CHP plant operates on natural gas with the following data:

- Fuel mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_f = 0.08$ kg/s
- Lower Heating Value: $\text{LHV} = 50$ MJ/kg
- Electrical output: $\dot{P}_{\text{el}} = 1.4$ MW
- Recovered heat: $\dot{Q}_{\text{rec}} = 1.6$ MW

**Part (a)** [7 marks] Calculate the (i) fuel input power, (ii) electrical efficiency, (iii) thermal efficiency, (iv) total CHP efficiency, and (v) heat-to-power ratio.

**Part (b)** [7 marks] If the reference efficiencies for separate production are η_e,ref = 0.40 and η_th,ref = 0.90, determine the **Primary Energy Saving (PES)**. Comment on the engineering significance of the computed PES value.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1 — Fuel input power:**

$$\dot{Q}_f = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV} = 0.08 \text{ kg/s} \times 50{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} = \mathbf{4{,}000 \text{ kW}}$$

(1 mark)

**Step 2 — Electrical efficiency:**

$$\eta_e = \frac{\dot{P}_{\text{el}}}{\dot{Q}_f} = \frac{1{,}400}{4{,}000} = \mathbf{0.350 \;\; (35.0\,\%)}$$

(1½ marks)

**Step 3 — Thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{rec}}}{\dot{Q}_f} = \frac{1{,}600}{4{,}000} = \mathbf{0.400 \;\; (40.0\,\%)}$$

(1½ marks)

**Step 4 — Total CHP efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{tot}} = \eta_e + \eta_{th} = 0.350 + 0.400 = \mathbf{0.750 \;\; (75.0\,\%)}$$

(1 mark)

**Step 5 — Heat-to-Power ratio:**

$$\text{HPR} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{rec}}}{\dot{P}_{\text{el}}} = \frac{1{,}600}{1{,}400} = \mathbf{1.143}$$

(1 mark)

**Part (b) — PES computation and commentary:**

$$\text{PES} = \left[1 - \frac{1}{\eta_{e,\text{ref}} + \eta_{th,\text{ref}}}\,(\eta_e + \eta_{th})\right] \times 100$$

$$\text{PES} = \left[1 - \frac{1}{0.40 + 0.90}\,(0.35 + 0.40)\right] \times 100$$

$$\text{PES} = \left[1 - \frac{0.75}{1.30}\right] \times 100 = [1 - 0.5769] \times 100 = \mathbf{42.3\,\%}$$

(5 marks for the computation)

**Engineering significance (2 marks):**

The CHP plant saves **42.3 %** of the primary fuel energy that would be required to produce the same electrical and thermal outputs via separate conventional routes (a grid-tied power plant for electricity and a fuel-fired boiler for heat). This translates directly into proportional reductions in (i) CO₂ emissions (≈ 42 % less per unit of useful energy), (ii) fuel cost, and (iii) imported-grid dependency. Such a plant typically achieves a simple payback period of **2 – 5 years** depending on fuel price and grid tariff, making it commercially attractive for continuous-process industries such as dairies, paper mills, chemical plants, and large commercial complexes.

---

### Question B `[14 Marks]` — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

The exhaust gas from a 1.0 MW gas-engine CHP plant leaves the engine at **500 °C** at a flow rate of **2.4 kg/s** with $\bar{c}_{p,\text{exh}} = 1.05$ kJ/(kg·K). The exhaust is cooled in an HRSG to **180 °C** to generate saturated steam at **4 bar** from feed-water at **80 °C**.

**Part (a)** [7 marks] Calculate (i) the heat recovered by the HRSG, (ii) the steam generation rate in kg/s and kg/h, and (iii) the fraction of fuel energy recovered as steam. Take LHV = 50 MJ/kg and η_e = 0.35.

**Part (b)** [7 marks] The same plant supplies 200 kW of jacket-water heat to a LiBr absorption chiller having COP_abs = 0.70 for air-conditioning. Calculate the cooling output. If the total electrical output is 1.0 MW and total recovered heat (steam + chiller input) is 700 kW, compute the Total Energy Utilization Factor (TEUF) and comment on whether the plant qualifies as a **trigeneration** system.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Heat recovered by the HRSG:**

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{HRSG}} = \dot{m}_{\text{exh}} \, \bar{c}_{p,\text{exh}} \, (T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}})$$

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{HRSG}} = 2.4 \text{ kg/s} \times 1.05 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} \times (773 - 453) \text{ K}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{HRSG}} = 2.4 \times 1.05 \times 320 = \mathbf{806.4 \text{ kW}}$$

(2 marks)

**Step 2 — Steam generation rate:**

From steam tables at 4 bar: $h_g \approx 2{,}738$ kJ/kg, $h_f(80\,°\text{C}) \approx 335$ kJ/kg.

$$\Delta h_{\text{steam}} = 2{,}738 - 335 = 2{,}403 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} = \frac{806.4}{2{,}403} = \mathbf{0.336 \text{ kg/s}} = 0.336 \times 3{,}600 = \mathbf{1{,}208 \text{ kg/h}}$$

(3 marks)

**Step 3 — Fraction of fuel energy recovered as steam:**

Fuel input: $\dot{Q}_f = \dot{P}_{\text{el}} / \eta_e = 1{,}000 / 0.35 = 2{,}857$ kW.

$$\text{Fraction} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{HRSG}}}{\dot{Q}_f} = \frac{806.4}{2{,}857} = \mathbf{0.282 \;\; (28.2\,\%)}$$

(2 marks)

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Cooling output from absorption chiller:**

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cool}} = \text{COP}_{\text{abs}} \times \dot{Q}_{\text{heat,in}} = 0.70 \times 200 = \mathbf{140 \text{ kW}}$$

(2 marks)

**Step 2 — Total Energy Utilization Factor:**

$$\text{TEUF} = \eta_e + \eta_{th} + \text{COP}_{\text{abs}} \cdot \eta_{\text{chiller,feed}}$$

Heat fed to chiller = 200 kW; fuel input = 2,857 kW; chiller-feed efficiency fraction = 200 / 2,857 = 0.070.

$$\text{TEUF} = \frac{1{,}000}{2{,}857} + \frac{700}{2{,}857} + 0.70 \times \frac{200}{2{,}857}$$

$$\text{TEUF} = 0.350 + 0.245 + 0.049 = \mathbf{0.644 \;\; (64.4\,\%)}$$

(3 marks)

**Step 3 — Trigeneration qualification comment (2 marks):**

The plant **does** qualify as a **trigeneration (CCHP)** system because it simultaneously delivers **three** useful energy outputs — electricity (1,000 kW), heat (700 kW), and cooling (140 kW) — from a single fuel input. Although the TEUF is below the unity line in this instance (because the fuel input is dominated by the large electrical demand rather than by thermal/cooling demand), the trigeneration label is justified by the *co-production* of all three forms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in steam-table lookups**: KHU students frequently quote $h_g$ at 4 bar as 2,738 kJ/kg *without units* or use the **wrong saturation pressure**. Always state the saturation pressure explicitly: "at P_sat = 4 bar (T_sat = 143.6 °C)."
> 2. **PES reference-efficiency trap**: KTU board answers often use η_e,ref = 0.40 and η_th,ref = 0.90 *individually* without summing them into η_ref = 1.30. The correct PES formula uses η_ref = η_e,ref + η_th,ref in the denominator. Writing the formula wrong loses **3 marks** outright.
> 3. **HPR misuse**: Heat-to-power ratio is *dimensionless* and is computed as Q_rec / P_el (not as the inverse). Students sometimes invert the ratio, producing values like 0.875 instead of 1.143 — penalized **1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting that η_tot = η_e + η_th**: Some answers compute a "weighted average" using fractional weighting; this is *incorrect* for CHP. Total efficiency is the **algebraic sum** of electrical and thermal efficiencies.
> 5. **Missing the trigeneration cue**: When the question mentions an absorption chiller, the word "trigeneration" *must* appear in the answer. Marks are reserved for naming the system correctly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this section for last-minute exam revision.** Every bullet below is a **frequently-tested KTU point.**

- **Cogeneration / CHP** = simultaneous production of electrical + useful thermal energy from a single fuel input using one integrated system.
- **Total efficiency** $\eta_{\text{tot}} = \eta_e + \eta_{th}$ — *algebraic sum*, not weighted average.
- **Typical ranges**: η_e = 30 – 42 %; η_th = 45 – 60 %; η_tot = 80 – 92 %.
- **Three heat-recovery streams**: Exhaust gas (400 – 550 °C), Jacket water (80 – 95 °C), Lube oil (60 – 80 °C).
- **Otto cycle** efficiency depends only on **compression ratio** r and **γ**: $\eta = 1 - r^{1-\gamma}$.
- **Primary Energy Saving (PES)** uses η_e,ref = 0.40 and η_th,ref = 0.90 in KTU exams; full formula derivation is a **must-memorize**.
- **Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR)** = Q_recovered / P_electrical = 1.1 – 2.5 for gas engines; critical for **demand-matching sizing**.
- **HRSG sizing chain**: $\dot{m}_{\text{exh}} \rightarrow \dot{Q}_{\text{HRSG}} \rightarrow \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} = \dot{Q} / (h_g - h_f)$.
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** for gas engines ≈ **200 – 220 g/kWh** at full load.
- **Part-load superiority**: Gas engines retain η_e within ±5 % of nominal from 50 % – 100 % load; gas turbines fall off by 15 % – 20 %.
- **Trigeneration / CCHP**: Heat → absorption chiller → cooling; COP_abs = 0.6 – 0.8; TEUF can exceed 1.0 when cooling is dominant.
- **Hydrogen-blended CHP**: Tolerates 5 % – 20 % H₂ by volume; derating η_e by 2 % – 5 %; bridge technology for hydrogen economy.
- **Payback period** for gas-engine CHP: typically **2 – 5 years** depending on grid tariff and fuel price.
- **Reference efficiencies** to memorize for PES: η_e,ref = 0.40, η_th,ref = 0.90, η_ref = 1.30.
- **Standard KTU constants**: LHV_NG = 50 MJ/kg, γ ≈ 1.35, Stoich NG/air ≈ 17.2.
- **Examiner's red flag**: Never confuse CHP with a *gas turbine combined cycle (GTCC)* — CHP is reciprocating-engine based; GTCC is Brayton + Rankine topping-bottoming.
